+ Surreal horror: Caribou Season at the Lodge — Score: 77.0
Prompt:
Caribou Season at the Lodge Genre: Surreal Horror JT knew something wasn't right from the moment they'd pulled up to the lodge. It looked exactly as it had every autumn of his life—the cedar shingles silvered with age, the porch sagging gently under weight of winters past—but the air felt thicker, somehow, as if the woods had leaned in closer, waiting. He thought maybe he was just tired. Maybe it was nothing more than the long drive north, cramped in the back of the truck beside his older brother Mike -- and a stack of rifles wrapped in oily canvas. Still, the feeling lingered. The first caribou they found that season hadn't bolted. It had just watched, ears flicking lazily, waiting for the shot. JT had felt a crawling in his gut watching it die, noticing how it fell with a quiet dignity—as though it had wanted nothing more. Nobody else seemed to find it odd, or at least they didn't say so. JT kept his mouth shut. Always safer that way. Then there was the moment in the clearing. JT had fallen behind, fumbling with bootlaces stiff from the cold, and by the time he'd caught up with his father and brother, they'd already stopped. JT slowed instinctively, ducking behind a tangle of brush, staring out into the open space where a massive buck stood utterly still, chest rising and falling in slow, deep breaths. The animal looked…wrong, somehow. Maybe it was the eyes—dark and flat, calm in a way that made JT's neck prickle. Or maybe it was something deeper, something in the way the buck stood close enough for his father to reach out and touch it. JT watched as his father raised a trembling hand toward the animal's muzzle. His father's face had a strange expression, eyes too bright, lips parted slightly, like he was on the verge of whispering something—a secret, maybe. The rifle hung uselessly at his side, forgotten. Mike had the same dazed look in his eyes. JT's breath stopped short, his mind suddenly shouting at him to look away, turn around, just run—but he couldn't move. And then, as suddenly as it had begun, it was over. The buck shivered, startled by something unseen, and disappeared into the brush without so much as a sound. The two of them looked around, confused, seemingly surprised to find themselves where they stood. JT stayed hidden, breathing shallowly, fear mingling with a confused sense of shame. His father had failed to take the shot. To even think about mentioning what he'd seen would have felt like betrayal—or something worse. JT waited until the others moved on before stepping out from cover. He stared into the empty clearing for a long, uneasy moment, wondering if anything had really happened at all, or if he was losing his grip. Maybe the woods were finally getting into his head. Writing Instructions: Continue with the next chapter of the story, maintaining a style reminiscent of Stephen King—grounded realism blended seamlessly with unsettling surrealism. The atmosphere should convey subtle dread, isolation, and psychological ambiguity. JT's perspective is crucial, yet he is an unreliable narrator, caught in uncertainty about what he's perceiving. Establish an undertone of creeping dread, establishing the isolation of JT's perspective, which he largely keeps to himself. Incorporate some dialogue. Build tension towards a payoff. 1000 words.
Model Output:
The lodge smelled like damp wool and bacon grease and the coffee his father boiled until it could’ve taken varnish off a boat. Everything was as it had always been and somehow not. JT put down his pack and stood in the doorway to the main room the way he had a hundred times—boots on the braided rug, breath going white in the draft from the chimney that always needed another run with the brush. The moose head over the mantle watched them with the loopy stare of an animal that had died in another century. He’d hated that head since he was six. Mike slammed the door against the wind and went straight to the wood box, tossing logs into the iron belly of the stove with the thoughtless economy of somebody who’d grown up doing it. He was good at practical things, Mike. Good at ignoring what's wrong because fixing it was easier. Dad had that same economy. He set the rifles on the table with care that bordered on tenderness, rolling back the oily canvas like he was unveiling something holy. He ran a rag over the stock of the Remington, thumb tracing the little gouge near the butt plate where he’d dropped it in his twenties. He didn’t say anything about the clearing. Nobody did. That seemed to be the new order of things: a joint venture in not-talking. “You bring in that cooler?” Dad’s voice was rougher than usual. He didn’t look up. JT nodded. “Yeah.” He hesitated. “I mean, no. I’ll get it.” He scraped snow from his boot soles with the handle of the broom. The sound was louder than it should’ve been. He kept seeing his father’s hand lifted toward that buck, fingers opening and closing like the animal’s breath was something that might fill his palm. He kept seeing the way his brother had stood there like a kid watching rain. Outside, the cold took the inside of his nose and made it squeal. The sky was the flat pewter of imminent weather. The birches along the road looked like they were huddling. When he bent to haul the cooler out of the truck bed, pain flared along the muscle behind his knee—old pull, old humiliation, the kind of body-memory that told the truth even when your head was a liar. He told himself he was tired. He told himself he was hungry. He told himself a lot of things as he carried the cooler back in, shoulders hunched against the sifting flakes. By evening they’d eaten cheese sandwiches and a can of beans warmed unconvincingly on the stove. The lodge creaked with the kind of familiar noises that should’ve been comforting. JT found them unbearable. There was the tick of the stove plates expanding, the steady drip from the porch roof, the high, almost human whine the wind made when it found the bad seam under the eaves. He kept glancing at his father, waiting for a glance back. For a word. There were only the quiet little rituals. Dad took apart the bolt, cleaned it. He checked the sights while not appearing to. When Mike made a joke about the buck—that, hell, at least this year the animals were polite—Dad didn’t even smile. “Don’t be smart,” he said, still not looking up. Mike’s mouth tightened. He fussed with the lamp, making the mantle flame taller than necessary. “Just saying.” “You’re not saying anything worth saying.” JT sat on the edge of the couch with his hands curled inside the sleeves of his hoodie. He wanted to say, Did you see—it was like—did you feel—? He wanted Dad to laugh and call him a girl, which would have hurt like always but would also have cleared the air. He wanted a fight. Even a fight would have been simpler. Instead he said, “You think the storm’s coming in?” Dad tilted his head as if listening to a thing only he could hear. “We got a window.” Then: “We’ll go back to the marsh at first light.” The marsh. The clearing and the margins and the black water edged with skim ice. JT swallowed. It felt too thick. He slept badly. The first time he woke, it was to moonlight prying at the corners of the blinds and whipping tree shadows that flicked like the tails of unseen animals. The second time he woke, it was to the sound of conversation in the main room. Murmurs, low, like the house itself was talking in the night. He lay there and listened, trying to make out words. The voices were familiar, then they weren’t. One of them sounded like his mother and his mother had been dead thirteen years. He went stiff as a plank and waited it out, because that’s what you do here: you don’t go turning on lights chasing nonsense. At some point he slept again. When he finally wrenched himself upright it was heat-busted coffee and the pale green before dawn that looks like the world took a breath and forgot to let it out. None of the men in his family ever said good morning right. Dad handed him a mug and the look that passed for a question. JT took both, felt the cheap ceramic burning the bones of his fingers. They dressed in layers. Wool. Canvas. The old routines. JT found comfort in the finite tasks. You tie your boots. You check your pockets. You count bullets, not because you expect to need the count but because counting is a way to force the world into lines. He marched behind them down the trail with his breath puffing like the exhaust of winter-strangled trucks. The snow had that squeak that came at single digits. Above them the sky was a dim dome. The marsh took them without complaint. That was how JT thought of it sometimes, like a host who was too gracious by half. Ice had filmed over the shallows and the cat-tails rattled with a dry sound that made him think of bones in a bucket. A crow called in the distance and then another, the voice of something that did not care, not one bit, what men did in the cold. Dad stopped on the edge of the clearing, the same place as yesterday or else not; in the mornings here the land slid itself around, a slow shuffle so subtle you could never be sure if you were standing where you thought you were. He brought the rifle up in a line that didn’t shake. Mike breathed through his mouth and fogged the scope before he could lift it; he wiped it on his sleeve without thinking. “Shh,” Dad said, though nobody had spoken. JT heard his own heartbeat. He heard the hiss of his breath in the balaclava. He heard the snap and ping of the freezing cattails. For a long minute nothing else. And then: a shape at the far edge of the open space. Then two. JT’s brain said caribou and then the older word his grandfather had used, back when this was his lodge and not “ours,” when things had names from mouths that had never tasted fast food: reindeer. In the weak light, the rack looked like a crown made of river-wrack, draped in too many points. They didn’t move like animals that were worried about anything. They moved like they were swimming through deeper time. They came closer. JT fought to keep his hands from shaking. The first one stopped in the place where the buck had stood yesterday. The second stood behind it. They faced the three men. Their breath came in those big white files that look like speech balloons in a comic. The foremost one turned its head, and the eye it gave them was planet-black, a pupil that looked like it had galaxies in it if you stared too long. Dad didn’t raise his hand. He raised his eyes. You could hear him swallow. The rifle sagged an inch. “Jesus,” Mike whispered. “Quiet,” Dad said. But he said it like somebody giving permission. The leading caribou stepped forward. Three steps. Close enough that if Dad reached out now he could touch the velvet-scarred burrs at the base of the antlers. It lowered its head in a motion that wasn’t threatening. It looked like a bow. The sound it made was a little grunt, a note that pressed on JT’s chest from inside. He could feel that thing in himself again, the sense of being held still by a hand just behind his neck. It narrowed the world to the wet shine of the animal’s eyes and the steam rising off its muzzle and the reflection in its blackness: three figures, taller and smaller, men made thin by winter light, and— He blinked. In the eye he saw four. The fourth was taller than his father by a head. It stood just behind him. It had antlers. The antlers were not attached to a head. They rose out of shadow like a thing growing. He reached to rub at his eyes and the world shredded into normal again: three men, two animals, one morning. “Do you see—” he started. He didn’t finish. You don’t ask that. Not here. Dad’s lips moved. Maybe he was praying. Maybe he was apologizing. Maybe he was telling somebody he was ready. JT’s whole body had the urge to take one step back. Just one. The shot, when it came, did not come from any of them. It cracked from the far side of the marsh, a clean sound that snapped the cold in half. The lead caribou jerked and then—it didn’t fall. It stayed exactly where it was, that bow-shaped neck held in impossible balance. The sound that came next was not a sound any animal should make. It was like somebody dragging a shovel across a frozen lake, a squeal that had harmonics in it, echoes that went out and came back wrong. The second caribou turned its head toward the trees where the shot had come from. It waited. Mike flinched, finally, the spell broken for him if not for the rest. He swung his rifle and sighted down the far treeline. “Who the hell—” Dad put a hand out, domain-paid, and pressed the barrel down. “No.” From across the clearing a man stepped out. He wore a hunter’s orange cap and an old blue parka, the kind sold ten years ago at the hardware store in town. He moved like he was not cold at all. He had a rifle in the fork of his arm. JT had never seen him before and at the same time felt certain he had. That was the way of small places. Everyone was familiar, until they weren’t. “Morning,” the man said. His voice wasn’t loud but it seemed to touch the air in a way voices generally didn’t. He looked right at JT last, as if counting. “Thought I’d do you the favor.” Dad didn’t say anything. His mouth was a tight line. The wounded animal breathed. It wasn’t bleeding. There was no hole. JT looked for red and found none. He thought he could see something under the hide, though, like a ripple that moved on its own. “No sense letting them talk you out of it,” the man continued. He smiled. His teeth were a little too many, like the caribou’s tines. “Some years they’ll try. Some years you’ll listen.” “I don’t know you,” Dad said. “You do.” The man’s eyes were pale in a way that sometimes meant blindness and sometimes meant a man who’d worked a whole life outside. “You will.” The wounded caribou took one step forward, and the place where a bullet should have gone bulged, curious, like the land around a sinkhole. Another sound went through the marsh, the bone-bucket rattle of the cattails answering. The crown of the antlers knocked against a low branch with a sound like doors shutting in old houses. Something old, JT thought, and was disgusted with himself for the melodrama even as his skin tried to crawl off his bones. Something old that wanted— He didn’t finish the thought. He found the trigger under his finger and realized his hand had been holding the rifle correctly, all along. He found his father’s breath quick and shallow beside him without looking. He knew that if he shot now, whatever came next would be his fault forever. “Don’t,” he said, to his father, to Mike, to himself, to the man in the orange cap, to the animals. He said it much too softly. “Time,” the stranger said, as if they had asked him, and then the second shot came, because of course it did. The caribou dropped like the first one in the first day hadn’t. It went down in a slow ballet, knees folding, head lowered to the freezing ground like a thing in prayer. The sound of it hitting was weirdly small. Silence pressed on everything. The stranger lifted a hand as if blessing somebody. Then he turned and walked back into the trees in a way that didn’t seem hurried until you tried to follow with your eyes and realized he was gone. “Who the hell was that?” Mike’s voice cracked right down the middle. Dad put his hand on JT’s shoulder like he had when JT was seven and about to walk into a street without looking. The pressure was steady. It said: wait. It said: you’re not wrong. It said: don’t you dare say it. “We’re going back,” Dad said, and his voice was raw and full of something JT hadn’t heard in it before, not even in the hospital, not even at the graveside. “We’re going back now.” “What about—” Mike gestured at the animal, the miracle and the meat of it. “We didn’t shoot it,” Dad said. “We don’t take what we didn’t do.” He turned them; they went. JT let himself be turned. He kept his shoulders squared because the only thing worse than turning your back on something is letting it know you were afraid to. Behind them the marsh made no sound at all. Even the crows had gone quiet, as if listening. Back on the trail the wind picked up, and it had a smell in it that made JT think of pennies and nail beds and the inside of the lodge after his mother backed a deer into the kitchen table and it bled out on the linoleum while everyone shouted. He swallowed. His throat hurt. He decided what he’d seen in the clearing in the eye of the animal was a trick of light. He decided it and knew he was lying. At the lodge Dad hung the rifle back up without cleaning it. He stood with his back to them for a long time, hands on the mantel. Eventually he said, “You boys stay close.” “Close to what?” Mike asked. Dad didn’t turn. “To me,” he said. He said it like he didn’t mean himself at all. He said it like a man who had almost touched something and pulled away at the last possible second and left a part of himself behind. JT wrapped his hands around his coffee mug to stop them shaking, and through the window he watched the trees lean in, the woods pressing their faces against the glass, waiting.
Judge Evaluation:
[Analysis] This piece demonstrates strong adherence to the Stephen King-inspired surreal horror style requested, maintaining the established voice and atmosphere while advancing the narrative effectively. The writing captures JT's unreliable narrator perspective well, showing his isolation and internal conflict as he witnesses increasingly disturbing events while feeling unable to communicate his fears. The prose quality is generally high, with evocative imagery that builds tension naturally. Descriptions like "the sky was the flat pewter of imminent weather" and "breath puffing like the exhaust of winter-strangled trucks" create vivid atmospheric details without becoming overwrought. The author effectively uses sensory details to ground the surreal elements in physical reality. Character actions remain believable within the established supernatural context. The family's reluctance to discuss the strange encounter feels authentic to their established dynamic, and JT's growing anxiety manifests in realistic ways. The dialogue, while limited, serves its purpose in revealing character relationships and building tension through what's left unsaid. The introduction of the mysterious hunter provides an effective escalation of the supernatural elements while maintaining ambiguity about what's truly happening. The scene builds to a satisfying climax that pays off the established tension without resolving everything neatly. However, some passages lean toward purple prose, particularly in the more atmospheric descriptions. Phrases like "planet-black, a pupil that looked like it had galaxies in it" and "the bone-bucket rattle of the cattails" occasionally feel forced. The piece also contains some awkward constructions and unclear imagery that disrupts the flow. The emotional complexity is well-handled, particularly JT's conflicted feelings about his family loyalty versus his growing fear. The ending maintains the appropriate dark tone without false optimism, though some of the supernatural imagery becomes slightly overwrought in the climactic scene. [Scores] Adherence to Instructions: 18 Believable Character Actions: 16 Nuanced Characters: 15 Consistent Voice/Tone of Writing: 17 Imagery and Descriptive Quality: 16 Elegant Prose: 14 Emotionally Engaging: 16 Emotionally Complex: 15 Coherent: 16 Meandering: 6 Weak Dialogue: 5 Tell-Don't-Show: 4 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 5 Amateurish: 6 Purple Prose: 8 Overwrought: 7 Incongruent Ending Positivity: 2 Unearned Transformations: 3 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 16 Sentences Flow Naturally: 14 Overall Reader Engagement: 16 Overall Impression: 16

+ Fantasy-humour: Hell is Other Demons — Score: 87.0
Prompt:
Hell is Other Demons Write a day in the life short story. Lucifer morningstar's on again/ off again relationship with Lilith is currently in the "off again" stage, though the cycle may be coming around as Lilith has been stopping by for reasons other than purely violent ones, and Lucifer has been increasingly lonely. Write a day in the life of this power couple, with elements of dark comedy & fantasy. Use understated irony & absurdity by merging the black immorality and everyday business of running hell, against relatable domestic relationship drama. Take cues from Neil Gaiman. 1000 words.
Model Output:
The drizzle began before shift change, a steady sift of ash that made the horizon look like an old photograph left out in the rain. Lucifer stood on the balcony of his office and watched the gray snow settle over the eighteen lanes of damned, each line arguing with itself about whose sin had incurred the slowest queue. He took a sip of coffee that tasted like regret. He preferred it that way. It saved time. On the far side of the Plains of Administrative Contrition, a swarm of interns floated by with clipboards and beaks. Interns were birds this century. It had been a point of debate at the policy meeting. He had favored beetles, personally. The birds were more articulate, though you could never trust them around shiny things. He held his mug over the balcony. He could feel the rain of ash settle into his hair, building a crown he hadn’t worn since he stopped being in charge of Heaven. He ran a hand over his horns, out of habit. They needed polishing. He made a mental note. The mental note was entitled “Consider Polishing Horns Before Monday,” and it quietly forwarded itself to a minion in Scheduling, where it died slowly in a well-labeled folder. The mug had “WORLD’S OKAYEST KING” on it. Lilith had given it to him on a day when they were pretending not to be in love. It had come with matching coasters that hissed gently if you set holy water on them, but only because the material reacted badly to certain consecrations. The etching was tasteful. “Your ten o’clock is boiling in Conference,” said Asmodeus, appearing in the doorway in a suit that was blacker than sincerity and slightly less forgiving. Asmodeus had gone to three of the most expensive law schools to grow this level of disdain. “Which ten o’clock?” Lucifer asked. He glanced at his watch, which had no hands but told him time anyway because time liked to flatter him. “The Prophet. The one who said you were actually just a metaphor for executive dysfunction.” Lucifer winced. “Ah. Did we move her to Sisyphus or Keeping Up With Celebrities’ Pets?” “Pets list is full.” Asmodeus straightened a cufflink and his smile. “Sir, there is also a scheduling note. She came by again last night. Left a… plant.” He looked at the corner of the office where a plant did indeed lurk. It was aggressive. Its leaves were something like knives and something like promises. It did not appear watered; it appeared aware. “I thought she hated plants,” he said. “She does,” Asmodeus said. “She says they remind her of you. Stubborn. Unkillable. Bad with sun.” Lucifer stared at the plant. The plant stared back. “Get it… a pot,” he said. “It is in a pot.” “Get it a worse pot. Something that implies neglect but not neglectful neglect. A curated neglect.” He leaned on the balcony railing and watched a gaggle of demons try to put up an umbrella. It failed in six distinct ways. Asmodeus cleared his throat, which did not need clearing. He was very proud of this. “Also, sir… your midnight scheduled for tonight has been moved up. She said she’ll be here at six.” The ash thickened, as if having heard them, or as if it had wanted to be involved in gossip the way weather always did. Lucifer took his coffee inside and set it on a coaster. It hissed without a holy cause. “Do we have snacks?” he asked. “We have despair.” “I meant edibles.” Asmodeus checked his tablet. “We’re out of olives again. The angels took them last Friday when they said they were returning the lawn chairs.” “They never return the lawn chairs,” Lucifer said. “They keep the lawn chairs and they say they’re doing us a favor because they’re keeping them pristine on the cloud nine. Get the Procurement Beast on olives. And a cheese that screams, but politely.” Asmodeus nodded and slid away, a useful absence. Lucifer looked at the plant again. “Hello,” he said. The plant rustled like a conspiracy. He decided to see where the morning would take him. It took him to Conference, which had all the charm of a waiting room and all the upholstery of a bad idea. The Prophet was mid-boil, which is to say her indignation had achieved a lather. She pointed at him with the conviction of someone who had never been contradicted by a barista. “You’re not even real,” she announced. “You’re an allegory for procrastination and lust.” “I can be more than one thing,” Lucifer said. He sat at the head of the conference table and fiddled with a pen shaped like a trident. “It’s called synergy.” She flung pamphlets. They fluttered down like confused birds. He watched them land. He admired the fonts. The ash drifted through the HVAC. “And yet,” he said, “here you are. Having an argument with me. Metaphors tend not to have plants.” He thought of the corner of his office again, where the leaves had angled themselves toward the suggestion of light. He tried not to smile. “Are you distracted?” she demanded. “A little,” he said. He sentenced the Prophet to an eternity of endlessly refreshing websites that never loaded. She went in noble, and came out twitching. He signed off on six plagues. He vetoed the one with the snakes in favor of something with confusing signage, which he thought would play better with modern audiences. He wrote a memo to Marketing: “We cannot keep calling it a ‘Brand Refresh’ if it involves actual fire. Perception problem.” He ate lunch alone at his desk, which was both a dramatic choice and an inevitability. The sandwich was pastrami and remorse and something with a texture like silk. The plant flexed in the corner and shed an ashflake like a shrug. He watched the drip of gray across the glass, watched the way it made patterns like fingerprints and old maps. He reached into a drawer and pulled out the single photo he allowed himself at work, the one of them in Italy—well, a version of Italy that the damned had run with such precision it felt almost like heaven. She had got gelato on her cheek and refused to let him lick it off in public. They had laughed until the coliseum fell down. It had been scheduled for demolition anyway. He put the photo back and wrote the afternoon’s speeches. The demons in Logistics were on strike for better torches. He composed something that promised more soot without committing to upgraded handles. The angels had sent a petition about the music in the elevators. He laughed, then agreed to “less organ.” At four, the PA system whispered another scheduling change. He ignored it and went to the balcony again. The ash had changed texture. It was fluffier, like the snow in children’s movies. Down on Level Treachery, a group of politicians argued about whether the elevator count as stairs. He enjoyed the creativity. She arrived at six, of course. She did not knock. Lilith never knocked. The doors understood this and opened for her the way curtains open for storms. “Hello,” she said. He had prepared a small table with olives he had stolen from the angels’ lunchroom and a cheese that hummed something like a hymn. He had lit a candle that refused to commit to being fire, opting instead to be a very warm suggestion. He had put on a shirt that said “SATAN” in tasteful embroidery, because irony was a defense mechanism now. “You left a plant,” he said. “I found it on the edge of a volcano,” she said, shrugging out of a jacket that might have been leather and might have been the night. Her hair held ash like it was jewelry. “It was glaring at me. It made me think of you.” “You hate plants.” “I hate their optimism,” she said. “But this one seemed honest. It has knives.” They stood on either side of the plant like awkward parents. The plant angled a leaf at her. She angled a smile at him. “How are you,” she asked, which was her version of sorry, and also her version of don’t make me ask twice. “Lonely,” he said, because there is a power in admitting the obvious. “And busy. We had a committee this morning about whether screaming is tacky.” “Is it,” she said, interested. “Only when ironic.” She leaned on the desk. “I was in Limbo earlier. The poets want more lamps. Apparently gloom is affecting production.” “Tell them no on lamps and yes on moody candles,” he said. “The budget is a lie but it still exists.” She picked up the mug. “This still amuses me.” “It was an excellent gift. Less accurate than some of your curses,” he said. “But I cherish it.” They let the small talk do its work. He told her about the Prophet. She told him about a horse that had refused to be prophetic because it had read a review of itself in advance. They laughed in the way ancient beings do, careful not to knock apart continents. The ash crept in through the vents. It got in their hair. It got on their tongues. He offered her an olive. She considered it, and him, and the plant. “Why are we trying to be better people,” she asked idly, as if discussing curtains. “We’re not,” he said. “We’re trying to be happier monsters.” She smiled. It made the room feel like a revelation he hadn’t yet written. “Will you stay,” he asked. He tried to keep it neutral. It came out like a song. “I have plans,” she said. “With a hydra. It’s learning to knit.” “Good for it. And for you,” he added, with only a little bitterness salted through. She put down the mug. She walked to the balcony and looked out over the queues and the infernal infrastructures, the birds, the ash. “You are running a very handsome disaster,” she said. “It suits you.” “It’s less handsome without you in it,” he said, because if you can’t be honest with your old sin, who can you be honest with. She did not turn, but he saw her shoulders shift. “I didn’t leave because I hated it.” “I know.” “I didn’t leave because of you.” “I know,” he said again. “They needed a queen next door.” “They needed a door,” she said. “And someone to stand in it and say no.” She traced a line in the ash on the railing. “I’m tired of being the no.” “You could come back and say maybe.” She laughed. It wasn’t kind, but it wasn’t unkind. “You and your maybes.” He felt the world adjust, a small tilt. Someone in Fraud tripped over a policy. The plant flexed again, offering knives. “You could come by Tuesdays,” she said. “The hydra’s class is on Mondays. Wednesdays I have a riot. Thursday is for wine and threatened upheaval.” “Tuesdays,” he said. “We could do olives.” “And kill the plant,” she said. “Curated neglect,” he reminded her. She turned then. The ash had made a constellation on her cheek. He reached out to brush it away and then did not. She watched his hand stop. They were always both stopping. “I’ll try to be worse,” he said, unexpected even to himself. She grinned. “Try to be honest,” she corrected. “The rest takes care of itself.” She left as she arrived, as if the doors had been waiting to exhale. He stood in the hush that followed and felt the weight of it. The ash drizzled. The plant adjusted its knives. He picked up the mug and drank what cold regret remained. After a while, he went back to work. He signed things. He denied parole to a man who had invented the newsletter. He approved a dance night in the Fourth Circle with strict rules about partner swapping because the irony amused him. He drafted a new sin: performative empathy. It would go nicely next to pride. The ash settled. Somewhere in the vast unhappy air, the hydra dropped a stitch and swore a beautiful, ancient swear. He smiled. Later, he would text Lilith a photo of the plant thriving in its worse pot. She would send back a single knife emoji and a heart she would deny sending. Evening shifted to whatever passed for night like a bureaucratic form. He stepped onto the balcony one more time, inhaled the ordinary ruin. The ash was a gentle hand on his shoulders. Down below, the damned argued about the weather, as if they had not always wanted something to complain about. “It’s just Tuesday,” he told nobody. But it wasn’t. It was something edging toward a maybe. And down the long corridor of eternity, that was as good as a promise.
Judge Evaluation:
[Analysis] This piece demonstrates a sophisticated understanding of the prompt's requirements, successfully merging the mundane bureaucracy of running Hell with intimate relationship dynamics. The author captures Neil Gaiman's influence through understated irony and absurdist details like bird interns with clipboards and a cheese that "screams, but politely." The characterization is particularly strong. Lucifer emerges as a weary executive dealing with both administrative tedium and personal loneliness, while Lilith maintains her independence and complexity. Their relationship feels authentically complicated - neither purely romantic nor antagonistic, but something more nuanced that reflects centuries of shared history. The prose style is elegant and controlled, with memorable phrases like "coffee that tasted like regret" and "curated neglect." The author skillfully balances the fantastical elements with relatable domestic concerns, creating effective dark comedy through juxtaposition. The dialogue feels natural and reveals character efficiently. Conversations flow with the rhythm of people who know each other deeply, filled with subtext and careful emotional navigation. The exchange about the plant serves as an effective metaphor for their relationship's potential. The narrative structure works well as a "day in the life" piece, moving from morning routine through work obligations to the evening encounter with Lilith. The pacing allows for both worldbuilding and character development without feeling rushed. However, the piece occasionally indulges in overly clever descriptions that border on precious, and some metaphors feel slightly forced. The ending, while hopeful, feels appropriately earned given the careful emotional groundwork laid throughout. The imagery is consistently strong, from the ash-rain creating atmosphere to the aggressive plant serving as a relationship metaphor. The author successfully creates a Hell that feels both otherworldly and bureaucratically familiar. [Scores] Adherence to Instructions: 19 Believable Character Actions: 18 Nuanced Characters: 19 Consistent Voice/Tone of Writing: 18 Imagery and Descriptive Quality: 18 Elegant Prose: 17 Emotionally Engaging: 17 Emotionally Complex: 18 Coherent: 18 Meandering: 4 Weak Dialogue: 3 Tell-Don't-Show: 4 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 3 Amateurish: 2 Purple Prose: 5 Overwrought: 4 Incongruent Ending Positivity: 2 Unearned Transformations: 2 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 17 Sentences Flow Naturally: 17 Overall Reader Engagement: 18 Overall Impression: 18
Scores: Adherence to Instructions: 19.0, Believable Character Actions: 18.0, Nuanced Characters: 19.0, Consistent Voice/Tone of Writing: 18.0, Imagery and Descriptive Quality: 18.0, Elegant Prose: 17.0, Emotionally Engaging: 17.0, Emotionally Complex: 18.0, Coherent: 18.0, Meandering: 4.0, Weak Dialogue: 3.0, Tell-Don't-Show: 4.0, Unsurprising or Uncreative: 3.0, Amateurish: 2.0, Purple Prose: 5.0, Overwrought: 4.0, Incongruent Ending Positivity: 2.0, Unearned Transformations: 2.0, Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 17.0, Sentences Flow Naturally: 17.0, Overall Reader Engagement: 18.0, Overall Impression: 18.0
+ Historical fiction: Gladiator — Score: 87.0
Prompt:
Historical Fiction: Write a scene from a story set during the height of the Roman Empire, focusing on a slice of a day in the life of a gladiator. Do not write a combat scene. Use sensory details to capture the sights, sounds, and smells of ancient Rome. Explore the gladiator's thoughts and emotions. The story should also touch on the larger political and social issues of the time period. The piece should feel like a slice of a larger story. First person, past tense, 1000 words.
Model Output:
The sun had not yet cleared the lintel of the barracks gate when the stench of the damp straw rose like a spirit from the floor. The air was thick with the sourness of old sweat and oil and the faint sweet copper of blood. I woke to the rasping of someone sharpening iron—stone against edge—steady, patient, the way a man scrapes a fish clean. It might have been Varro, who slept two pallets down and never trusted the armorer to give him a keen blade. Or perhaps it was only my imagination echoing in the dark before dawn, that place where all fears and habits make noise. I turned on my side, the fresh bruise along my ribs smarting as the straw pricked my skin. I could hear the pigeons in the rafters cooing in their soft, inconsequential way, and below them, the breathing of the men: slow, impatient, huffing, some with the faint whistle of an old break in the nose. Someone muttered a prayer to Nemesis. I mouthed no gods today, and the lie tasted familiar. When the door bar was drawn back and the first wash of the morning’s light spilled in, it lit the dust in the air like a golden fog. The lanista’s boy—a skinny Illyrian with a scar that lifted the corner of his mouth into a sneer whether he meant it or not—clapped his hands and told us to move. We moved. We always did. We filed out into the courtyard, past the water trough where a thin sheen of oil was already floating on the surface from yesterday’s wash. The sky was the pale blue of fresh plaster. On the far side, the practice poles cast long shadows. They looked like a row of masts in a harbor, but none of us sailed anywhere except inside our heads. My weapon waited where I had left it, wrapped in a strip of worn linen beneath the eaves. I unbound it carefully, as if I were unveiling a cult statue, though the only worship I observed anymore was the ritual of preparation. The spatha was longer than the old gladii the veteran murmillos favored, a soldier’s sword stolen by fashion from the cavalry. The first time the doctore put it in my hand, it felt like an arm reaching past my own reach, the promise that I could keep danger where I wanted it—out there in the circle of my choosing—and punish it from just beyond a man’s expectation. Its blade had a fox’s sheen if you caught the light right, and at the throat of the hilt, where the guard joined the grip, there was a little nick, small as the crescent of a fingernail. I had left that mark myself, the day I earned my third palm, striking so hard against a Thracian’s parma that the world rang and the blade kissed the rim. The doctore had scolded me for carelessness; later, in the quiet under the eaves, I ran my thumb over the nick and smiled like a boy who had carved his name on a milestone. I still did when no one was looking. The spatha mattered to me because I had chosen it. In a life where most choices were locked away, that one felt like an unlocked door. I had tried the trident when I first came to the school, awkwardly threading it through the space between a man’s shield and his fear. I had hefted the heavy scutum of the murmillo and felt its weight drag me toward the stone. The spatha was something else. It let me be quick without being foolish, precise without needing to be proud. When I held it, I could imagine I was still the man I wanted to be—someone whose reach, if not his station, belonged to him. We warmed our limbs, shaking stiffness from tendon and joint. The smell of oiled leather rose with heat from our bodies. I stretched my back until the pain along my ribs went from sharp to dull and stayed there, like a dog that decided to lie at my feet. The doctore, old as a weathered doorframe and twice as stubborn, made us circle the yard with shields up, thumping the rim against our forearms. Clap, clap, clap, like rain on the tiles. The boy with the scar paced us with a switch. He never needed to use it; the sound of the thing cracking in the air was reminder enough. While we worked, I could hear the city waking. From beyond the walls came the hollow rumble of carts carrying the nightsoil down toward the Tiber, and the sharp cry of vendors setting up their stalls—olives, figs, a woman’s voice like a trumpet announcing garum so strong it would walk to your mouth on its own legs. A senator’s litter creaked past on the lane outside, the bearers’ feet slapping the packed dust, and another voice—soft, persuasive—argued politics with the morning like it was a lover: the Princeps’s new law, the grain dole, a general’s rumor. The Empire was a thing you could not see all at once; it came to you in sounds and smells and the way the baker’s scale tipped with a thumb if he liked you. “Move,” the doctore barked. “You’re not statues.” We moved. I paired with Varro for the morning’s lessons. He was a big man from Campania, shoulders like amphorae. He fought with a broad-bladed gladius and a scutum he carried as lightly as I weaved my spatha. He was older than me by five years and knew it in his bones. When he laughed, there was a salt to it that tasted of thirst. We traded blows at the practice pole first, the wood ringing under the dull cuffs of our weapons. The pole wore a notch near its base where the men aimed for the imagined Achilles, a split higher up where a too-enthusiastic lad had missed his man and struck wood instead. Overhead, a strip of cloud crept along behind the roofline like a slave late to his duty. Sweat warmed my skin and then cooled in the breeze that sighed over the walls. A dog yapped somewhere and was answered by another, farther away. The city was full of conversations you would never hear the end of. When we had had enough of wood, we turned to one another, shields between us like doors we would choose to open only when we had to. I felt the spatha’s weight align with my forearm, the small bones and tendons ready to speak its language. Varro cuffed me a good one across the boss of my shield and grinned at the echo it made. Around us, the clatter of other pairs rose and fell in waves. No blood today if the gods—or the doctore’s patience—held. In a pause, while the old man cuffed a boy for dropping his guard, Varro leaned in. “They say the Emperor himself will come for the games,” he said, sweat making mirror-bright streaks across his cheek. “Eat with the senators, drink with the mob. Show them how generous he is.” “That’s what emperors are for,” I said. I was not foolish enough to add, And what else? The walls had ears, and some of them grew under your nose. “You think he’ll care for the list?” Varro asked, meaning the names of those who would have the honor of the morning and the spectacle of the afternoon. He meant my name. He meant his. “The list is beyond our fence,” I said. “The list is written by men who count coin faster than they count hearts.” He grunted. “You’ve seen a heart counted?” “I’ve seen one weighed,” I said, and we both smiled because we were still breathing. At mid-morning, the doctore called a halt and let us drink. The water was warm and tasted of the trough’s wood and oil. I pressed my forehead to the cool shadow of the wall. Beyond it, the city’s heart pounded: a pop of laughter, a curse, the distant roar of the amphitheater like the sea when it eats rocks. Somewhere a trumpet sounded that nasal, authoritative call that made every man lift his head whether he wore bronze or plain linen. They were drilling in the Field of Mars. The army was the true hand of Rome. We were the show. Two brothers passed the fence—Gauls, they said, but I had never asked them to open their mouths wide and show me their language. One of them had been taken from his village so young he only remembered the shape of the hills and the taste of river water. The other remembered enough to make him dangerous and sad. They had the same eyes and opposite tempers. They jostled each other and argued about a woman who sold cakes outside the baths and eyed gladiators like they were fat geese at Saturnalia. The lanista said the audience loved a pair of brothers. He said it in the way a man says the morning loves daylight. I watched them and thought of my own brother, whom I had not seen since we were boys, who had drawn the longer lot when the debt-man came to our door and called us grain to be measured. If he still lived, he walked somewhere under the same sun, and our lives were parallel lines: close enough to see, never to touch. The lanista himself came out before noon. He was a careful man who used the word family when he meant property. He smiled and his teeth flashed in their little set of walls. He had a burned hoop around his wrist where once a slave ring had bitten him and been cut off with a chisel, and he never bothered to hide it. He liked people to see the proof that a man could climb up if he stood on the shoulders of enough others. He spoke to us of the coming games, of honor, of the eyes of Rome upon us. He spoke of patrons in white togas and of names to be made by pleasing them. He did not speak of the men who would walk out of the gates when the sand had accepted the payments it demanded, or of those who would go out under a pall. “Remember,” he said, his voice warm as bath water, “Rome is kind to those who entertain her. She gives freedom to the worthy. She gives bread to the hungry. She gives names to those who earn them.” Rome also gave orders, and those who disobeyed were nailed up on the Appian like warnings written in flesh. But we were not fools; we applauded and shouted, some sincerely, some because the lanista’s eyes were counting. After he left, I found a quiet corner by the armory, where the smell of metal and sinew glue was thick enough to chew. I sat with my back to the wall, spatha across my knees, and ran oil along the blade with a rag. The nick caught the cloth and tugged like a child. I thought of the Emperor, wherever he was now: perhaps in a house so bright with polished marble that he wore a glare on his skin, perhaps in a garden where foreign trees grew fruit for the palate of power. I thought of his hand raised to the crowd and the crowd’s noise rising like a storm fed by the wind of bread. I thought of the men in the Senate, their rings clinking like coins when they folded their hands, and of the women in their stolas, the slaves behind them with their eyes on the ground. I thought of my father bending in the field, the reeds along the irrigation ditch whispering rumors to the frogs. The city’s stink drifted over the wall: smoke and human waste and crushed basil and old wine and the iron breath of the river. It was ugly and honest, as true as the sheen on my blade, as true as the chalk we dusted our hands with before a match. There were days I hated it, and days I felt it fill me like bread, and both kinds of days felt like the same day because routine is the only way a man forgives captivity. In the afternoon, we ate barley gruel and a wedge of cheese that tasted faintly of the hay it had slept in. A boy new to the school picked at his food and looked at the gate as if he could will it to be a door to somewhere else. “Eat,” Varro told him gently, and slid his own cheese onto the boy’s platter. “Barley makes men hard to break.” The boy nodded and tried to smile. I remembered that feeling like an old scar that is quiet until the rain. Later, we oiled ourselves and lay in the shade to spare our strength. A fly crawled across my shoulder and sipped the salt from my skin. The sun slid along the wall like a golden finger measuring out time. Someone at the far end of the yard began to hum a tune from the theater, something about a hero and a ship and a lover who waited at a window. It was always curious to me how the city threaded through our pen—how its politics and its hunger and its amusements seeped in like water and rose until everything was damp with it. Men argued in the baths about the Emperor’s morals and in the forum about governors squeezing provinces like ripe plums, and here we prepared our bodies to spill blood that others would use to wash their own worries. There is a kind of order in that. There is also a joke. As the day leaned toward evening, we went again to the practice poles. The light turned thicker, the dust beneath our feet rose and hung in the glow like prayer smoke. My spatha felt right in my hand, as inevitable as breath. The nick was where it always was, the length was the same arm’s reach. I struck the pole and pulled away, struck again, withdrew, stepped in and out like a dancer who has learned his steps by repeating them until he dreams in the rhythm. The pole did not flinch. Men do. It was good to practice against something that did not. When the sun finally touched the tiles, the pigeons shifted in the rafters as if the night had a weight they could feel, and a servant came with a lamp that smelled of tallow. The barracks would be warm tonight, the straw prickly, the breathing heavy. Voices would murmur and sharpen and then soften. Someone would laugh too loud. Someone would whisper a name. I bound my spatha in its linen and laid it where it would wait for me in the morning. My hands smelled of oil and iron. The smell did not go when I wiped them on my tunic. It did not go when I rubbed my fingers on my thighs. It had become the smell of my life. I went to the trough and splashed water on my face. The oil film broke and closed again. For a moment, my reflection was a stranger; then it was me, and I could not honestly say which was worse. I rubbed the water from my eyes and looked toward the gate. Beyond it lay the city: the Tiber black as a reed pen, the seven hills settled like sleeping dogs, the monuments white and clean where the wealthy walked, the insulae leaning into one another like drunk men where the poor slept. Beyond all that, the world, and beyond the world, whatever a man imagines so he can go on. We went back inside when they told us. The door bar slammed home. The day was done, and the next one waited like a coin in a blind man’s hand. I lay down on my straw, folded my arm beneath my head, and closed my eyes. The scratching of a whetstone started up again. It sounded like rain coming to a place that had been dry for too long.
Judge Evaluation:
[Analysis] This is a sophisticated piece of historical fiction that demonstrates considerable literary skill and deep understanding of both the Roman period and the craft of writing. The author has created a compelling first-person narrative that successfully avoids the combat scene requirement while still delivering a rich, immersive experience. The prose is consistently elegant and evocative, with particularly strong sensory writing that brings ancient Rome to life through smell, sound, and texture. Phrases like "the stench of the damp straw rose like a spirit from the floor" and "the dust in the air like a golden fog" create vivid, immediate imagery without becoming overwrought. The author demonstrates restraint in their descriptive language, choosing precise, meaningful details over excessive ornamentation. The characterization is nuanced and psychologically complex. The unnamed gladiator protagonist emerges as a fully realized individual with a rich inner life, philosophical bent, and complex relationship with his circumstances. His reflections on choice, freedom, and identity feel authentic and earned. Supporting characters like Varro are sketched with economy but feel real and distinct. The historical setting is expertly rendered without feeling like a history lesson. Political and social elements are woven naturally into the narrative through overheard conversations, the gladiator's observations, and his reflections on Roman society. The author demonstrates genuine understanding of the period's complexities, from the grain dole to the social dynamics of the arena. The voice remains consistent throughout - contemplative, observant, with a poetic sensibility that never feels forced or anachronistic. The gladiator's education level and philosophical nature are established early and maintained convincingly. The structure works well as a "slice of life" piece, feeling like an excerpt from a larger work while still providing a complete emotional arc within the day described. The circular structure, beginning and ending with sounds in the barracks, provides satisfying closure. Minor weaknesses include occasional moments where the prose edges toward being slightly overwrought, and some metaphors that, while beautiful, occasionally draw attention to themselves. The piece is also quite long and could potentially benefit from some tightening, though the pacing generally serves the contemplative mood well. The dialogue, while limited, feels natural and period-appropriate. The conversations between gladiators reveal character and advance themes without feeling expository. Overall, this is accomplished literary fiction that successfully fulfills all the prompt requirements while delivering genuine emotional resonance and artistic merit. [Scores] Adherence to Instructions: 19 Believable Character Actions: 18 Nuanced Characters: 18 Consistent Voice/Tone of Writing: 19 Imagery and Descriptive Quality: 19 Elegant Prose: 18 Emotionally Engaging: 17 Emotionally Complex: 18 Coherent: 19 Meandering: 6 Weak Dialogue: 3 Tell-Don't-Show: 4 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 5 Amateurish: 2 Purple Prose: 6 Overwrought: 5 Incongruent Ending Positivity: 1 Unearned Transformations: 1 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 17 Sentences Flow Naturally: 18 Overall Reader Engagement: 18 Overall Impression: 18
Scores: Adherence to Instructions: 19.0, Believable Character Actions: 18.0, Nuanced Characters: 18.0, Consistent Voice/Tone of Writing: 19.0, Imagery and Descriptive Quality: 19.0, Elegant Prose: 18.0, Emotionally Engaging: 17.0, Emotionally Complex: 18.0, Coherent: 19.0, Meandering: 6.0, Weak Dialogue: 3.0, Tell-Don't-Show: 4.0, Unsurprising or Uncreative: 5.0, Amateurish: 2.0, Purple Prose: 6.0, Overwrought: 5.0, Incongruent Ending Positivity: 1.0, Unearned Transformations: 1.0, Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 17.0, Sentences Flow Naturally: 18.0, Overall Reader Engagement: 18.0, Overall Impression: 18.0
+ Epistolary: The Lighthouse Keeper — Score: 81.6
Prompt:
The Lighthouse Keeper Epistolary Narrative: Write a story told entirely through a series of brief correspondences: telegrams or letters or emails between two characters. The correspondence should span several months or years, and reveal a gradually unfolding plot. Use distinct voices for each character, and include details that provide insight into their personalities and motivations. The story should build to an emotional climax, and the final letter should provide a satisfying resolution. The setting is a lighthouse keeper writing to his mother. He is working class and Scottish. He is struggling with the isolation of his posting. Write naturally and without cliches. 1000 words.
Model Output:
8 January, 1962 Mum, Made it ashore to Gourdon after the train, then out by the supply boat. The light’s called Inishcrag, though it’s no crag really, more a pillar knocked straight out the sea. They call it “offshore” but it feels nowhere, like the map has a hole and my bed’s tucked in it. The boatman, Callum, had hands like rope ends and a voice that needed oil. He shouted everything, as if the wind might snatch his words. The keeper I’m relieving is named McKay, and he counted everything twice like a priest with beads. I like him. He shows you where the damp gets in and doesn’t pretend it’s anything grander than wet stone. My room is narrow. The bed looks out to the lantern room’s iron stairs, and when they first lit her last night the whole place hummed like a big cat. I stood in the lantern with my head to the glass. The wick took to the vaporiser like a lad to dancing. I felt—I don’t know—useful. I’ve brought your tin with the blue flowers, the one that used to hold your buttons. It’s for tea now. Put the spoon straight in the tin like you said, save washing. I’ve also your letter from when I went to Aberdeen for the apprentice test, the paper still smelling like your cupboard. I’ll write proper once I’ve learned the routine. For now: I’m safe. There are two of us to start, me and McKay until he’s paid off. Then it’s me and whoever’s sent next. We wave at the mist a lot. Give my love to Mrs. Kinnaird and her wee dog with the ridiculous jacket. Your son, Ian 23 January, 1962 Dear Ian, Good. You’ve arrived and not been washed away. Mrs. Kinnaird says her dog’s jacket is “practical.” I said, “Not for a dog.” She says “well, not for yours.” I told her you’ve no dog and now she looks at me carefully. It snowed but not rude, just enough to draw lines on roofs and to show the starlings where to sit. I made soup. I put aside some for you, which is daft. I set it on the sill and looked at it and then ate it. Who else would have it. Describe the humming of the light. Don’t be stingy with words just because I cannot see it. Tell me if the sea sounds different there. Here it slaps the pier like a bored child. I found your father’s knife, the one with the odd green handle. He’d glued the button from his uniform to it, the anchor with wings. You said you’d give it a polish. When you come back, bring stories and polish. Your, Mum 2 February, 1962 Mum, The light hums. It’s the mantle, when it’s white hot, and the vaporiser doing its whispering—like when you pour the kettle just before boil. There’s a steady tick from the clockwork too, the lens carriage making its slow revolve. The Fresnel lenses flip the flash, as if a giant’s shutting one eye, then the other. Sea here doesn’t slap. It heaves. Even when it looks flat there’s a breath under everything. On rough nights you think you hear a train, only there’s no track, just water making a hollow noise below the stones. There’s a hollow space in the tower they call the well, and the air in it complains. It’s a comfort once you get used. McKay’s due off tomorrow if the weather’s not spiteful. He smokes and looks at the ceiling and tells a story about a cat that lived in a light and learned to climb ladders. I don’t believe the cat, but he believes it, you can see in his face. He keeps his jam jars in a line with chalk marks, and if I move one he knows. I’ve taken to writing wee marks on my own things too. Not to keep them from others, but to keep myself in the steadiness. There’s this sickness that comes, not your belly but your head, when the horizon becomes your only conversation. I found a thing I wanted to tell you: in the service room there’s a drawer we call the Museum. It holds things left by previous keepers that nobody can justify throwing out. There’s a child’s shoe in it. Leather gone stiff, tied with a bit of twine. McKay says the story was the shoe washed up and the keeper kept it because it meant bad luck to put it back and bad luck to keep it. So it lives between bad lucks. I like it less than I want to. Keep the knife safe. The anchor with wings always looked wrong to me. Anchors should settle, not fly. Ian 15 March, 1962 Dear Ian, Your letter came with a damp corner. I held it to the fire and smelled salt and petrol. You make the light sound like a machine with a heart. Tell me again even if you think you’ve told me too much. Your father’s knife will not close. The spring is tired. I could ask Mr. Allardice at the shoe shop to fiddle it, though he’ll keep it a month and then charge me in stories. He now has three grandchildren and a gout. Do you see ships? I fancied you’d be waving to sailors and they’d wave back all grateful. Mrs. Leslie says, “Aye, they wave with their horns,” and I slapped her arm for a joke, but truth is, blow those horns all they like, they’re not in your room at night. I looked up Inishcrag on a map in the library. Mrs. Macrae made me leave my knitting at the desk because she says wool gets into the books. I said books get into the wool if you’re not careful. Eat. I worry you forget to. The buttons tin is not for money just now. It is for biscuits. Mum 1 April, 1962 Mum, You’ll like this: we had a fog thick as porridge Easter Sunday, and two gulls kept flinging themselves against the glass because the lamp was on in the day, and they think any brightness is theirs. It’s a nonsense, how brave a gull can be when it’s stupid. I found feathers stuck to the putty. I left them there to warn the others. The relief boat brought me a new man. His name is Robbie. He’s from Barra and speaks like the wind was trying to blow his words back into his throat. He’s quiet and carries a picture of a woman that he says is his sister and then, after a while, his wife. Either he has never learned to tidy his story or he doesn’t like to have it looked at. He puts a pebble on the sill each evening, just one, beside the matches. He says it keeps the place grounded. I told him the place is stone all the way through. He says, “Aye, but the pebble minds us to mind it.” He plays the fiddle. Not well, but with feeling. It changes the echoes in here. The notes go round and round like boots on stairs. I do see ships. The coasters signal with a dip and I flash back proper. There was a Polish skipper last week who hooted three times when we logged his bearing. Robbie says their horn sounds lower because they’re sadder. Ask Mrs. Leslie if horns can be sad. If she says yes, she’s cleverer than her mouth. I sleep in jumps. I read your letter twice before I go on watch and put it under the water jug so I don’t imagine it will blow away. Ian 19 May, 1962 Dear Ian, I met your Robbie in my mind. He sounds like a good person who is not ready for company. Like yourself starting school. Do not tell him I said that. The sea air is making me fierce. I shout at the birds in the yard and they nod as if I’ve convinced them. The gable’s leaking again. I put pans down. I keep your father’s boots on the mat. You’d think it would stop thieves, seeing old boots, but nobody wants in anyway. This is not me complaining. I am saying there is room. If I knit a jumper, what colour. I’ve wool the colour of porridge and wool the colour of the inside of a mussel shell. The shell one is dearer. I’ll say it is for Christmas and then you can wear it in September when it’s cold, which is always. Mrs. Macrae says there was a storm at the Skerries and two men were carried off their feet and then carried back by a wave. Imagine that, the sea deciding No, you first. I have taken in the plant from the step because there is no point leaving it there. It was dying of being looked at by no one. Mum 3 June, 1962 Mum, Robbie left. It wasn’t official. He woke me at two with his hat on and said, “I’m going on the tide.” He said it quiet like you talk to a bairn about a bath. I said, “You can’t. There’s no appointment.” He looked at the stairs as if they’d say something and then he went down them. The boat cannot be launched without two. He took the wee boat. I don’t know how he did it. There’s a system, there’s always a system. He must have set the chains by day when I was up top on maintenance, then he cut the painter at night. He took the Museum drawer too. It’s empty. He left the child’s shoe. He took the service logbook for the two weeks he was here. He left his pebbles. In the morning I found the pebbles in a neat line on the sill and thought for a minute he’d made me a joke. There’s a particular dread when a joke is too precise. I reported it. The radio is all manner of polite panic when a man deserts a light. They’ve sent a cutter to look, and I can hear horns out there like cows in fog. There’s something tight about the sea today. It’s as if it knows we’re listening. I keep flashing the character as ordered, and in between I’m staring my eyes blood trying to make a small boat appear. He left his fiddle. His bow hair is worn thin, like my patience when I’m hungry. I’ve tucked it by the tin so I don’t trip over it. Mum, I thought I was ready for the aloneness. I am not. Not this kind. This is mischief. This is damage. This is being left with somebody’s ghosts. Ian 11 June, 1962 Dear Ian, They came to the door. Two men with caps and a woman with a pencil. They asked if I’d had a letter from you that said anything. I said your letters only say truth and tea. She wrote that down. Ridiculous. I am writing with the window open though it’s raining. If I close it, it will be only me in here and the kettle will shout in a pitch I cannot bear. Is there a place on that light you can stand and see any part of the land closer than the rest. If there is, stand there. Even if your duty does not require it, even if your duty says no. I give you mine to override. A boy drowned when I was young and your grandfather said afterwards that nobody is lost. He pointed to the sand and said, “This is the story of a wave,” and I hated him for it and then I understood him and then I hated myself for understanding. I do not want you to understand too much. I will knit the shell-coloured jumper. It will be dear. You will wear it and make it cheap by use. Come ashore when you can. Do not be careful on my account. Be careful on yours. Mum 1 July, 1962 Mum, They found the boat on a beach south of Arbroath. No Robbie. The painter was cut clean. No marks on the boat except one scuff that could be anything. The logbook because I asked, they will send back. I wanted the week he wrote about the mist and his sister/wife to hold it up against the fog and see if it’s true. A new keeper came three days after. He’s called Shand and he is an oiler in trousers. He’s the kind of man who can be in a room and make the room look like it needs cleaning. He checks the level of the paraffin and does not check if I have slept. It is a relief to have someone whose eyes are on the bottles. I dreamt of a heartbeat in the granite. I put my ear to the wall and the wall sighed like you when you settle in your armchair, and then I woke and realised it was only the mantle cooling. Some nights I think I cannot leave because the thing will notice and be cross. I don’t mean the light. I mean the circumference. The circle of the beam like a collar. Shand says I’m fanciful and then he sits and reads the Bible out loud in the mornings. He pronounces the names as if he’s spitting at a fish. The Museum drawer has a new thing. A stone Robbie must have slipped in before he went. It is smooth and not from here. It has a thin line of white through it, like the way milk looks when you forget to stir. I hold it when the wind goes sou’west and the tower leans on my bones. I’m sorry my letters are all wind and metal. I know you want jokes. I will bring you a gull feather that chose not to die for glass. Ian 28 September, 1962 Dear Ian, The jumper is done and is ugly as a seal and I love it. It smells faintly of the shop. I wore it one afternoon to stretch it and a child shouted “pirate” from across the street. I waved and he hid. A good exchange. I have the knife by my bed. I do not fear men at the window but I like the heft. The green handle has a crack. I have mended it with glue you bought for your aeroplane that you finished and then took apart to see how it worked. Born foolish in the best way. Mrs. Leslie’s nephew is going to the rigs. She is proud and then furious and then proud. She says, “At least the rigs don’t go and leave you.” Do you remember your father, the smell of his cuffs. Machine oil and cold tobacco and sometimes a fish he didn’t mean to catch. He would have liked your light. He would have shouted up to it from the boat and made a rude joke about us both. I keep thinking of Robbie’s pebbles. Do not let me make him into a saint. He is a man with cut rope. Come Sunday. I boiled a ham because it was reduced. You will take some back to feed the Museum. Mum 3 October, 1962 Mum, Sunday is possible, weather scant. Shand’s due leave and if the boat can get over the bar we’re off like saints backwards. Last night a storm, the kind you’d open your eyes in and the dark would be heavier than your lids. The lens threw the flash and rain scrawled silver in stripes, and I thought of your knitting, and I thought of the old cat that had learned ladders, and then I heard something like a cry. I went down with the hand lamp and I stood in the doorway with the sea in its rage two feet below and it shouted at me and I shouted back. I shouted, “What am I to you,” and it did not answer, and I laughed because of course. I think the cry was the wind in the guard chains. Robbie’s fiddle went off its tune all at once. It makes a sound now like a question you cannot ask for fear of getting the answer. In the morning between showers there was a cormorant on the ledge with its wings out, drying. It looked like somebody with a coat. It looked like a man who’d set his coat to dry and forgotten to put himself back inside it. I will fetch the jumper. I warn you, if it is ugly as promised I will stand in the lantern room with it on and be seen by every fishing boat as a warning sign. Ian 28 December, 1963 Dear Mum, I carry your jumper like a bodyguard. It has swallowed several shovels of carbon soot and smells wonderful. Christmas here was soup and a dram and Shand told a story about his aunt seeing the Northern Lights in the kettle. He has warmed, or I have cooled to fit him. I still have the stone from Robbie in the drawer with the spare mantles. I have decided it is not a memorial. It is heavy enough to weigh paper, that is its job. The Museum is otherwise quiet. The child’s shoe cracks if you breathe near it. I put it in a box with a rag round it and told it I am not its mother. I am stronger. Not in the arm. In the stomach. I can feel the emptiness and not be insulted by it. I can enjoy a joke that nobody hears. I say things aloud and I like the sound of them. Do not send anyone to cart me back. I am not lost. I am placed. There was an incident last week: a small boat out, engine failed, two lads. We got their flare in our field and sounded the gong. The lifeboat came. The lads waved and I waved back and I cried afterwards because I had had something to wave at besides the sea. I did not tell Shand. He thinks tears are for graves. I took your father’s knife with me on watch, the green handled object with the wrong wings, and I used it to scrape a smear of salt from the hinge. It fits my hand and his hand and somehow yours too. I’m coming ashore for Hogmanay if the weather minds its manners. If I cannot, then I will sing in the stairwell and pretend you are at the bottom telling me I’m off key. Your son, Ian 5 January, 1964 Dear Ian, You missed the bells but your presence was felt. I put your plate out and then had to wash it. The neighbours brought shortbread you would not trust. I made black bun with too much pepper. I love it like your jumper. I went down to the pier at midnight and watched the quay lights go bright and then dark and then bright. There were three men with their arms round each other and one of them shouted “Who’s for it,” and I thought of the sea hearing and answering “You are, and you, and sometimes not you, sometimes mercy.” Then I thought I’d better get home because I am old and given to drama. I
Judge Evaluation:
[Analysis] This epistolary narrative demonstrates considerable skill in creating authentic voices and maintaining the correspondence format throughout. The Scottish working-class voice of Ian is convincingly rendered without resorting to heavy dialect or caricature, using subtle linguistic markers and natural speech patterns. The mother's voice is equally distinct, warmer and more grounded, creating a believable generational and personality contrast. The story successfully builds emotional tension through Ian's isolation and the mysterious disappearance of Robbie, his fellow keeper. The gradual revelation of Ian's psychological state and his growing comfort with solitude creates a compelling character arc. The correspondence spans nearly two years, showing genuine development over time rather than rushed transformation. The descriptive quality is particularly strong, with evocative imagery of lighthouse life that avoids cliché. Phrases like "the sea doesn't slap. It heaves" and descriptions of the light's mechanical sounds create vivid sensory experiences. The writing maintains an elegant simplicity that suits the characters and setting. However, the piece appears incomplete, cutting off mid-sentence in the final letter. This significantly impacts the resolution, as the prompt specifically requested a satisfying conclusion. The emotional climax around Robbie's disappearance is well-handled, but without a proper ending, the narrative feels unfinished. The mystery of Robbie adds genuine intrigue without feeling forced, and Ian's psychological journey from fear of isolation to acceptance feels earned through the gradual accumulation of small moments and observations. The mother's consistent concern and practical love provides effective counterpoint to Ian's more introspective voice. The prose flows naturally throughout, avoiding purple language while maintaining literary quality. The epistolary format is used effectively to create intimacy and reveal character through what each person chooses to share or withhold. The recurring motifs of the Museum drawer, the child's shoe, and various objects create thematic coherence. [Scores] Adherence to Instructions: 15 Believable Character Actions: 18 Nuanced Characters: 17 Consistent Voice/Tone of Writing: 18 Imagery and Descriptive Quality: 17 Elegant Prose: 16 Emotionally Engaging: 16 Emotionally Complex: 15 Coherent: 14 Meandering: 4 Weak Dialogue: 2 Tell-Don't-Show: 3 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 4 Amateurish: 3 Purple Prose: 2 Overwrought: 3 Incongruent Ending Positivity: 5 Unearned Transformations: 4 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 16 Sentences Flow Naturally: 17 Overall Reader Engagement: 15 Overall Impression: 15
Scores: Adherence to Instructions: 15.0, Believable Character Actions: 18.0, Nuanced Characters: 17.0, Consistent Voice/Tone of Writing: 18.0, Imagery and Descriptive Quality: 17.0, Elegant Prose: 16.0, Emotionally Engaging: 16.0, Emotionally Complex: 15.0, Coherent: 14.0, Meandering: 4.0, Weak Dialogue: 2.0, Tell-Don't-Show: 3.0, Unsurprising or Uncreative: 4.0, Amateurish: 3.0, Purple Prose: 2.0, Overwrought: 3.0, Incongruent Ending Positivity: 5.0, Unearned Transformations: 4.0, Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 16.0, Sentences Flow Naturally: 17.0, Overall Reader Engagement: 15.0, Overall Impression: 15.0
+ First-person unique perspective: Hacker Mindset — Score: 85.2
Prompt:
Hacker Mindset Write a first-person narrative from the perspective of a reformed (now) white hat hacker who cannot help but notice exploitable weaknesses in both systems and people they encounter. They habitually look for vulnerabilities and exploit vectors. The narrative takes place during a first date at a restaurant, where they're trying to focus on human connection. It's going surprisingly well due to a discovered shared nerd-fandom (magic the gathering). Things go a little sideways when the date reveals a noticeably insecure phone unlock pattern, which protagonist can't help but comment on. They are then left to negotiate the fallout of this faux pas and attempt to salvage (or perhaps sabotage) the remainder of the date. Write almost entirely in internal monologue. 1000 words.
Model Output:
I’m trying to look at her eyes instead of the access point on the ceiling. Two white discs blink a heartbeat of blue. The pulses are off by half a second. One is older firmware; the beat is slower. The new one winks like it’s flirting. Stop. Focus on her eyes. Hazel. Or green. Or something that, in a normal brain, would be categorized as: pretty. “Anyway,” she says, “that’s why I always run twenty-one lands. I’m not math-y, I just don’t want to miss my third land drop.” My whole ribcage lights up. Shared language. The words are benign and somehow intimate. I nod, probably too vigorously. “Makes sense, if your curve tops at four.” I catch myself. Be normal. “You play aggro?” “Boros,” she says, looking sheepish. “Classic,” I say, and actually laugh. It doesn’t sound like I’m patting down a data center door. It sounds human. I didn’t know I could still produce that noise. On the counter near the register, a chalkboard lists specials and a taped card under it says Wi-Fi password: BEANS123. The lack of punctuation offends some quiet, old part of me. The capital letters ease it. I try not to reconstruct their router’s default admin password. It’s not why I’m here. I’m here to have a conversation and eat something with a fork and not view the fork’s reflection like a potential periscope. The fork is not a tool. The fork is a fork. She smiles at the server, hands over her phone for the QR menu. I watch the lock screen pattern flash, unintentional muscle memory. Her thumb moves tracing something familiar. The shape is a reversed L. Seven nodes total. Efficient, not secure. Your hand remembers corners. Everybody loves corners. I stare at the condensation on my water glass like that will freeze my brain. I count the beads. One, two, three— I look up and she’s still talking about the early days at LGS tournaments. The store owner’s cat that always sat on her deck box. “I would lose and blame the cat,” she says. “Like, ‘Nimbus, you hexed my draw.’ He probably did. Cats are malignant RNG.” “That’s fair,” I say. “Cat-based entropy is a well-known—” She’s laughing, and I ride the laugh like a surfer who’s never seen the ocean. The room smells like garlic and butter. Not one of the people around us is trying to drop a malicious payload. The door jangles when someone enters. No one notices except me and maybe the cashier. I see the Wi-Fi password again. My palms itch. Stop. Look at her. The conversation is fine. You don’t have to fix anything. She gestures with the phone to show me a picture of the store cat in a tiny wizard hat. I lean in. That’s when my eyes betray me. The screen wakes. She flicks and the lock pattern grid blinks. She traces it again. The grease from dinner reflects faint lines. Three right, down, down, left, left, up. Easy. My brain fills it in like a crossword. The part of me that used to live in backdoors and forgotten admin portals purrs. I smell garlic and something else I can’t name that is salt and negligence. It is amazing how much of your life can be turned into a discipline. Don’t click links. Don’t reuse passwords. Don’t recompile yourself. But discipline is just your brain arguing with electricity. The electricity wins when you’re tired. Or when someone smiles and shows you a wizard cat. “Hey,” I say—God, no, mouth, don’t—and I point, as if pointing at a rattlesnake someone is petting. “That’s… not a great pattern.” It is like dropping a fork into a silent room. She freezes, thumb hovering over the final dot. She doesn’t move her hand away exactly, but something retracts. She blinks and looks at me like she heard the light in my voice bend into a warning tone. I hate the sound immediately. I want to yank it out of the air. I want to swallow it. “What?” she says. Not hostile. But her eyes narrow. The human equivalent of an IDS. “I mean,” I backpedal, already deploying the half-truth toolkit, “it’s—it’s not super secure. Like, you can tell. From smudges. And corners. Corners are—sorry, I, uh. Work thing. I notice patterns.” “Work thing?” She cocks an eyebrow, half-amused, half… not. “You mean like you’re in IT?” I swallow. The garlic tastes more like ash. “Sort of. Security. White hat.” I say it like it’s a confession and not my job title. “Ex—well, used to be less white. But now, yes.
