+ Biopunk: Polymerase Chain Reaction — Score: 75.9
Prompt:
Biopunk -- Polymerase Chain Reaction Write a chapter set in a near-future post-apocalyptic world where corporate genetic engineering was designed with built-in obsolescence—engineered DNA lasting only one generation to maximize profits before societal collapse. Follow a biohacker working with salvaged equipment to splice viable food crops for their settlement using rapidly degrading genetic material. As world building / window dressing, include details about jury-rigged lab equipment, and the physical toll (and unexpected upsides) of using untested genetic modifications as medicine. The plot of this chapter is centred around a black market contract. Length: 1000 words.
Model Output:
The canister had once carted miracle seeds across continents. Now it clanged against the steel table as Mara upended its chalky insides into a chipped centrifuge tube. BiOlux’s logo—the laughing sun with wheat stalk rays—was half-scraped by desert grit, the slogan ENTER A NEW HARVEST ghosting beneath grime. Inside, the last of a reagent that should have died with the grid stuck to the sides in failing clumps. “I need it all,” she muttered. “Every flake.” The lab—if the word still meant anything—was carved out of the old water treatment plant beneath what used to be a mall. Where aisles of cereal had once stretched, there were now sealed rooms and misfit machines humming, wheezing, and sometimes coughing smoke. Her PCR machine, patched together from three different models and propped on bricks, rattled when it heated. The silicone gasket that held the lid had been cut from an oven mitt. The thermocycler’s display bled blue streaks when it got hot, so she’d taped a shard of mirrored plastic above it in an angle that let her catch the numbers if she leaned just so. She leaned. The old numbers glowed fuzzy: 94°C—denaturation. The clock she’d ripped from a dead nurse’s station ticked on the wall. Condensation beaded inside the lid like sweat. “You really doing that from scrap?” the kid asked. Timo was all elbows and eyes, perched on an upside-down crate. He had a bandage around his hand. The skin beneath it looked glassine in a way that meant the DNA polymerase coursing through him, the hack they called the Spark, was working too fast. “Everything’s from scrap,” Mara said. “It’s the only stuff that doesn’t die on you.” Timo snorted like he understood, though he could barely remember a time before the collapse. Everyone of his generation had grown up with warnings about programmed rot under their pillow: those men in suits had engineered hunger into the world like a feature. Every gene-edit we salvaged carried a fuse, some chemical dependent hinge that would lock after one generation—sunlight-sensitive sequence, poison-pill promoter, heat-labile backbone. The first crops were strong, the patent came with a whisper: come back next year. And when there was no one to sell to, the second generation came up empty, or worse—lethal to the soil or the stomach. Mara popped the lid briefly between cycles, peered at the thin sheet of oil she’d layered on top of the reaction to keep the evaporated water from condensing on the lid and dripping into the tubes. The oil was repurposed from a warehouse of hair relaxer. It smelled like coconuts gone wrong. A jerry-built incubator hummed on the counter beside her, its innards cannibalized from a wine fridge and a toaster oven. On top of it sat a line of empty canisters—BiOlux, Genendem, SeedNow—all bleached by sun and streaked with brown, their mouths plugged with rubber stoppers knifed from old tires. Beside them, a cheap kid’s microscope had its optics swapped for glass from prescription eyeglasses, duct tape neat in strips. A handheld genetic reader the size of a thick phone—an antique even before The Quiet—lay plugged into a USB battery bank that had outlived countless flashlights and one drone. Mara’s fingers were bare; gloves sucked heat and made her clumsy. Her nails were short and edged with black. The scar behind her left ear pulsed when she was anxious. That scar held a microfluidic pump, originally designed to deliver insulin. It now dripped whatever cocktail she brewed under her skin: glucocorticoids to cheat her immune system from rejecting altered proteins, serum anthocyanin to mask some of the inflammation, a trickle of endorphin when her patch told her heart rate had gone feral. It kept her on her feet but left her teeth aching like she’d chewed tinfoil. At times, in some weird collateral upside, the world became a notch brighter after the pump did its evening spurt. Blues went magnetic. The surface tension on water had shape you could almost stroke. “You should sit,” Timo said. “You’re—“ He trailed off. The pump’s line trembled against her neck. “I’ve got one shot at the primers before they anneal to themselves. They were never meant for this. We’re stealing time from a machine designed to fail.” Uncle Ben’s laugh cut through the hum, a smoker’s bark. “You always this cheerful?” he asked, shouldering into the room with a crate stacked with more canisters. Uncle Ben wasn’t anyone’s uncle, but that’s how the settlement addressed the man who’d once been a clerk at a feed store. He wore a parka with the stuffing peeking out like wheat. His left eye was milked over from a fungus gone wrong. “Depends who’s paying,” Mara said. “Who do the canisters belong to?” “Scav from the Outer Yards,” he said, setting them down. “Says he’s got the last of an antifreeze promoter, the one they used on frost-proof avocados. Wants to trade for cursebreakers.” “Does he know what antifreeze proteins do to kidneys?” she asked. “Do you?” Mara did, intimately. The first winter after she set up here, she’d tried wiring a fish gene into winter wheat to hold off a late frost. The plant lived. The frogs didn’t. The settlement’s drinking water had been laced with peptides the ensures pumped to burst into their tissues as crystals formed. It had taken weeks and a lot of charcoal and stones to fix it. She still woke to the sound of bodies shifting like broken glass. “I’ll talk to him,” she said. The buzzer went off. She popped the lid and lifted the tubes out with steady hands and a coathanger bent into a loop. The smell of melted plastic crept stronger when the heater shut off. The room exhaled. She shivered. “Grab the gel,” she told Timo. He pulled the gel tray out of its bath of cast agarose, green with a stain they’d brewed from old highlighter fluid. The power supply was a repurposed car battery with bench leads and a scavenged resistor. Mara slid the clear tray into the chamber. The tray itself was a cut section of an acrylic billboard for the last blockbuster movie, letters ghosting pale beneath her bands. “Load three microlitres each. Steady.” She guided Timo’s hand. He’d learned well. The pipette was a historical artifact, one of those finicky original accruals that had survived because it had been repaired a dozen times. The tips were long gone; they cut slivers of surgical tubing for sleeves and boiled them between runs. The gel hissed when the current hit. A faint line of bubbles marched along the wires. “How much are they offering?” Mara asked, as if casually, eyes on the slow procession of blue stain. Uncle Ben said, “Ten gallons of treated water. Three pounds of salt. A battery from a relay tower.” “That’s the ask,” she said. “What are they offering to pay me not to turn them down without the work?” He watched the gel like she did until the brightness of bands began to show. “A patch from Stillyard. Med-level. Full compatibility with an immuno-suppressant. Bloody rare.” She closed her eyes, thinking of the way her pump tugged, the rawness where the cannula went in, the way her throat sometimes tasted of metal. A proper patch would give her control back. It would also make her smarter in the dark. She opened her eyes. “Who’s this for?” she said. “The antifreeze.” Ben hesitated. “The Jackal’s people. Black market, you know that.” “Tell him to come up,” she said. Uncle Ben’s expression puckered. The Jackal ran one of the bands that cleaned the dead towers of copper and glass, that hijacked trains that still haunted the tracks. He wasn’t cruel. He was a ledger. All debts were written down, and the interest was paid in bone. The settlement worked with him because he had an ear in places the city didn’t. The city. She still called it that, though it had no edges now, just a shift from scorch to shadow where the wind shifted. “I’ll be out of here in fifteen,” Mara said. “Can’t go anywhere until this sets. If we toss it in right…” “Half of economics is timing,” Ben said, which had been his catchphrase since the world lost clocks. Mara listened to the gel whisper. The bands weren’t perfect. She had dim tailings on the left, a smear that said the split sequences were still binding to themselves, an artifact of the failsafe. But in lane three, the one that mattered, a bright band burned at the expected size. The promoter—soil met, drought tolerant, the last legal gene left for the poor—had been copied clean from the tatters of a corn cob gnawed by rats. She wasn’t magnanimous. The Jackal’s contract would buy the patch she needed. The settlement was tired of chewing leaves. She would splice the promoter into cassava, maybe, or a root that didn’t mind poor ground. A thing that would feed a lot from a little. She’d clip the black market offering a bit on the way through. She hated herself for it and did it anyway. The only way to change the ledger was to be the one keeping it. When the Jackal came, he came masked, as always. The mask had been welding goggles once, now covered in mesh and signs of river origin. He sat on a stool as if it had been reserved for him at a terminal of departures. “You still alive,” he said. “Wasn’t sure after the frog incident.” “Alive is a strong word,” Mara said. “You bring my medical patch?” He lifted a cloth bundle and unrolled it. The patch was smooth, a sheaf of woven microneedles pressed between two sheets of sterile plastic. She could smell the antiseptic despite five years out of factory. It had a battery tab intact and a manufacturer code she recognized from pre-Quiet days: Stillyard, the ditch that had sold to BiOlux before the fall. It made her back ache to look at it. “I do one job,” she said, “and you give me that. No substitutions.” “As long as what you do doesn’t kill my farmers,” the Jackal said. “That’s on your farmers,” she said. “I’m going to fix your plants. You don’t do what I say for the feed... well.” She lifted a tube. The Jackal watched her hands as if they were knives. “We want antifreeze,” he said. “I’m giving you reflexive dormancy. You don’t get frost-bite fruit.” His masked head tilted. “Sell me.” “Your men don’t know seeds. The antifreeze is toxic in the wrong ratios and dramatic in its failures. It will keep your kale from dying at twenty-eight degrees and make your cousins pee snow, then fall asleep and not wake. Dormancy,” she tapped the tube, “will keep your crops asleep when the frost hits at night, and wake them in the day. You’ll lose some growth. You’ll feed them longer. You will listen for my instructions and follow them to the letter or you will find them dead on the vine like a curse. And I need the patch up front or the rubbish you brought will go back with you in those pretty cans.” The Jackal didn’t like being told no. He liked that someone told him no to his face. He set the patch on her table. It made a sound, the plasticky whisper of something that had once been born in white light and now lived in gloom. “We also have a clause,” he said, tapping his fingers. “We want you on call for the next three moons. Someone’s going to fail, and we want you there when it stains.” “Mara has a job,” Uncle Ben said, as if he was anyone. The Jackal ignored him. Mara tapped the gel. “Three moons is generous. Two weeks. After that, you’re on your own.” “Three months,” the Jackal said. “Three weeks,” Mara said. “Forty days.” The Jackal’s mask hid his mouth and his eyes said nothing. His palms lay open like bowls, and Mara felt something inside her lean toward them, like a tide to the phases. “Forty days,” she said. “I want the relay battery, the water, and the salt in currency today. And I want your guarantee.” “I don’t guarantee,” he said simply. “You guarantee that if any of my kids come into your lands with a mark, they walk out safe. You give me that, and I will salt your ground with the old light.” Ben made a noise. The Jackal’s people had a thing about salt. To promise salt was the same as marriage in some parts. His head tilted again. “You talk a lot for a lab rat.” “I live with the rats,” she said. “They talk to me.” He laughed once. It sounded like weeping left too long in a hot car. “Forty days,” he said. “My protection. As long as you don’t play me.” “My contracts aren’t written down,” she said. “They stick longer.” She hit the mix with the enzyme. It foamed softly. She pulled the cassette out of the hitting incubator and slid it into the slot she’d carved in the side of the splicer. The splicer was a monster, all grafts and screws and a lens array she’d polished with denim. She had a generator bicycle rigged nearby, and Timo got on and started to pedal, wires clamped to his thighs to correct his cadence. The electrician’s meter on the table bobbed between green and yellow. Mara laid the cassette in, aligning the map with a drawn template beneath the glass. The glass had a crack that ran like a river. She rested fingers on it where the split wanted to widen and whispered to it, because it made her feel better, because nothing that was alive stayed unbroken anymore. The swap began. It was a mechano enzymatic dance, a mechanical ratcheting of length onto length saved from the enzyme killers BiOlux had built in. She had a counter-coded polymerase, one that treated their time bombs like background noise. The outer ring whirred as Timo pedaled faster. The lamp above the table swung, throwing shadows like seaweed across the walls. “You’re sweating,” the Jackal said, almost complimentary. “Shut up,” Uncle Ben said. They watched bands of light shift on the reader. Mara knew how they were supposed to look. She knew when the off-gas smelled wrong, when the voltage dipped below the number that meant contamination. She knew the taste of success before it finished, a metallic lick in the back of her throat that wasn’t the pump this time, wasn’t some collapse in her blood sugar, but something old and human, something that remembered feeling a child kick for the first time. It finished. She capped the cassette. The room didn’t cheer, though Timo looked as if he wanted to. She stood and her knees cracked, and she did not hide the way her hands shook. She slid the cassette into a sterile sleeve, sealed it with a little iron that had branded BiOlux logos into bale twine once. She pressed her thumb into the film until it dented, everything that she could give. “There’s enough for two fields,” she said. “Not more. You’ll do the inoculation at dusk. You’ll avoid the river’s breath for twelve hours after. You’ll dilute like I tell you. You’ll record the days the plants sleep and the nights the frost eats. You’ll bring the data back to me before the month turns.” “I don’t write,” the Jackal said. “Make the boy do it,” she said. “If you can’t name your wounds, you have no right to be healed.” He took the cassette. He did the payment in parcels. Men in the hall shifted sacks. The power bank on the bench chirped as a relay battery plugged in, taking a drink. She slid the patch into a metal box, and she didn’t tremble when she did. When the Jackal had gone, Uncle Ben sat and put his head in his hands and laughed the way you laugh after the surgeon puts the saw down. Timo swung his legs, eyes bright. “You think it’ll work?” he asked. Mara leaned against the bench, finally, and let the pump push its building hum. She didn’t slap the patch to her arm yet. She had respect, still, for ritual. She raised an empty canister and poured from another into it, because they still pretended that vessels mattered. She thought of dormancy, of the way sleep staved off death in both plants and men. She thought about forty days and a ledger that had her name on it, and how to move her own debt to a place that hurt less. “It’ll work enough,” she said. “And the rest, we fake.” “That’s not comforting,” Uncle Ben said. “Comfort is in shorter supply than salt,” she said. She touched the patch with a finger. It was cool and slick, like a lie. Her stomach turned. She smiled, not nicely. “We got paid,” she said. “Get the rig ready for the patch. If the upsides are as good as the stories, I won’t blink for a week.” “Are the stories good?” Timo asked. “Some.” She thought about the stories: hacked immune patches that made teeth regrow like shark’s, anti-viral mods that made people glow faintly in certain wavelengths, fluorescent kids laughing with teeth too white. The clumping protein that turned joints to glass. The way some people on the Spark felt music as flavor and saw the wind as strings. Unexpected upsides, the consolation prize left by men who made money on rot. Mara turned and looked at the gel, at the lines that said they had snipped and pasted something important back into the world. Above her, in the mall’s skeleton, the wind rattled the old security gate like a prayer wheel. She put the patch to her skin by feel, and when the needles slid in, it was like memory threading a needle. She pressed the tab. The pump in her neck murmured and then fell still, as if listening to a new conductor. “I’m going to go lie down,” she said. “Wake me if the Jackal comes back. And don’t touch the antifreeze.” “We didn’t take the antifreeze,” Ben said. “I know,” she said, and smiled again, an edge. “That’s why you’re still breathing.”
Judge Evaluation:
[Analysis] This piece demonstrates strong technical craft and worldbuilding within the biopunk genre. The author successfully creates a convincing post-apocalyptic setting where corporate genetic engineering with built-in obsolescence has led to societal collapse. The scientific details feel authentic without being overly technical, and the jury-rigged laboratory equipment is vividly rendered with specific, believable details. The protagonist Mara is well-developed, with clear motivations and a compelling backstory. Her expertise and desperation come through in her actions and dialogue. The supporting characters, while less developed, serve their functions effectively. The black market contract plot provides good structure and tension. The prose is generally strong, with evocative imagery and a consistent dystopian tone. The author effectively balances technical exposition with narrative momentum. The descriptions of the makeshift laboratory equipment are particularly well-done, creating a strong sense of place and the resourcefulness required for survival. However, the piece has some weaknesses. The dialogue occasionally feels stilted, particularly in exchanges between Mara and the Jackal. Some passages veer toward purple prose, with overly elaborate metaphors that don't always land effectively. The pacing becomes uneven in places, with some sections feeling rushed while others drag. The ending feels somewhat abrupt and doesn't fully resolve the emotional arc established earlier. While the practical resolution of the contract works, there's a sense that deeper character development was left unexplored. The piece also occasionally tells rather than shows, particularly regarding the broader world situation and some character motivations. The scientific elements are handled competently, though some readers might find the technical details overwhelming. The author successfully integrates the required elements of genetic modification side effects and jury-rigged equipment into the narrative without making them feel forced. + Iteration 2 — Avg Score: 83.6
+ Classic sci-fi: The Azra Gambit — Score: 80.2
Prompt:
Classic sci-fi (Author style: Asimov) The Azra Gambit Colonial mars is being mined by corporations who take leases on indentured labourers. The thing they are mining is Azra, a recently discovered exotic metal which accelerates radioactive decay to such a rate that it is greatly sought after for interstellar drives and weapons alike. This has created both a gold rush and an arms race as various interests vie for control and endeavour to unlock Azra's secrets. The story follows Arthur Neegan, a first generation settler and mining engineer. Upon discovering that his unassuming plot sits atop an immense Azra vein, he is subjected to a flurry of interest and scrutiny. Write the next chapter in this story, in which an armed retinue descends on Arthur's home and politely but forcefully invites him to a meeting with some unknown party off-world. The insignia look like that of the Antares diplomatic corp -- diplomatic in name only. Arthur finds himself in the centre of a political tug of war. The chapter involves a meeting with this unknown party, who makes Arthur an offer. The scene should be primarily dialogue, interspersed with vivid description & scene setting. It should sow hints of the larger intrigue, stakes & dangers. Include Asimov's trademark big-and-small-picture world building and retrofuturistic classic scifi vibe. The chapter begins with Arthur aboard the transfer vessel, wondering just what he's gotten involved in. Length: 1000 words.
Model Output:
The transfer vessel hummed in the way well-engineered machinery hums—softly, insistently, as if noting the existence of dozens of systems at once and checking each down a list. Arthur Neegan’s eyes flicked to the small display screen on the bulkhead opposite him. It cycled through system diagnostics in green text: cabin pressure nominal, plasma microthrusters balanced, radiation shielding at 98 percent efficiency, inertial compensators within acceptable tolerance. None of it calmed him. “Would you like some water?” asked the woman seated across from him. Her uniform was a study in contradictions: the matte black of something designed for utility, the sharp thread of ceremonial embroidery along the lapels, and the Insignia—a seven-pointed star encircled by a field of dots—of the Antares Diplomatic Corps. The polite face of force. The good cop to the security operative’s steel. Arthur shook his head. “I’m fine.” “You’re not,” said the man beside the woman. He was younger, eyebrows like pen strokes, and his pistol was visible only in the way the fabric of his side flexed and bulged, as if weapon and man shared a small conspiracy. “But that’s reasonable.” The vessel’s lights shifted to a duskish hue. Somewhere aft, docking latches retracted. Mars dwindled in the rectangular window by millimeters, like a well-known truth being gently corrected. Arthur had a thought he could not dispel: If I refuse, nothing happens—except they change refusal’s meaning. You only own a plot if someone else agrees that you do. He rubbed his thumb against a patch of roughness on his palm. The rasp of callus against callus steadied him in a way the diagnostics could not. “Mr. Neegan,” the woman said. “We apologize for the urgency.” “You showed up with four armed men and a warrant signed by someone whose name was ‘Acting Deputy Vice-Consul.’ That’s new. Acting Deputy Vice-Consuls didn’t exist on Mars last week.” The woman smiled. “Institutions adapt. My name is Lera Misk. This is Captain O’Dell. We’re facilitators.” “Of what?” Arthur asked. “Meetings,” said O’Dell. “Conversations that need to happen before other conversations happen.” “And if I don’t wish to converse?” Misk spread her hands. “Then you become a subject rather than a participant. Better to participate.” The vessel shivered—a small, practiced twitch as the engines angled fractionally. The green text flicked two lines, paused, and replaced them with identical lines. A tiny confirmation. Out in the window, a crescent slid into view, a metallic half-moon where the Antares transfer hub orbited in leisurely law around Mars. “Who am I meeting?” Arthur said. “We’re here to ensure the meeting proceeds,” Misk said. “Less what, more why. You’ve drawn attention.” He snorted. “Because a spectrometer pinged too brightly under my little claim.” “Because Azra disrupts every timetable, every projection,” Misk corrected. “Every agency with an agenda now finds its math inaccurate. That is never forgiven.” He remembered the first sample he’d taken, the way the rock had seemed to sweat, the near-imperceptible heat on the glove. Azra granules like a black frost. The lab run-ups had made his instruments nervous in ways no data sheet had warned about. The first hint had been the radiation log’s gentle panic. The second had been the buyer’s offer: much more money than made sense for ore he wasn’t supposed to have in such quantity. “What does Antares want?” Arthur asked. O’Dell tilted his head, as if listening. “We want order that produces profit. We are, contrary to rumor, not ideologues.” “And the unknown party?” Misk glanced at the window. The hub grew larger, a soused ring of docked vessels and modular blister-domes like barnacles on a whale. Her smile had the amused fatigue of someone who’s seen hundreds of meetings like the one they were flying toward. “Unknowns become known in stages.” Arthur studied her. There was a serenity to her poise that he recognized: the confidence of someone operating under big rules. Intelligent people, he’d learned, became either deranged by big rules or insulated by them. He suspected Misk was the latter. He suspected O’Dell would become the former the moment a rule wobbled. “What’s Azra?” Arthur asked, abruptly. “Not the marketing. Not the gossip. I know what my assay said. I don’t know what my assay means.” “Azra accelerates decay,” Misk said. “That’s the line. The truth is more intricate. It reorganizes probability landscapes. It…levers the inevitable closer. The universe is fond of entropy, Mr. Neegan, but it’s lazy about it. Azra makes the universe diligent.” Arthur’s mouth went dry. He had never heard it phrased that way. He thought of engines that would burn brighter for shorter spans, of ships that would cut months off their runs, of weapons whose half-life could be collapsed into an instant, their fury delivered as a single coherent insult. “You make it sound like character,” he said. “Anthropomorphism is a useful fiction,” Misk replied. “It moves policy.” The docking collar latched with a careful clunk that Arthur felt in his teeth. The green text confirmed: connection secure, atmosphere equalized. The hatch iris sighed open. “Please,” O’Dell said, gesturing. They moved through a corridor of standard-issue compromise: the walls were human-height, the lighting warm, the signage bilingual. The Antares insignia appeared at dignified intervals, reminding everyone that this was not a free port, but it would behave like one if that was more useful than the alternative. The world had learned to govern itself in the conditional. The meeting room had a window with a rude view—half Mars, half armature of the station’s docking spokes. It had a table with rounded edges and an array of seats that were each equally comfortable. It had a bowl of fruit that was real, and therefore meaningful. Two security officers stationed themselves at the door like well-trained punctuation marks. O’Dell remained. Misk took one side of the table. On the other side sat a man whose face Arthur could not immediately place because it was constructed out of things that refused to arrange themselves into a pattern: pleasant, symmetrical, forgettable, except for the eyes, which were very alert. “Mr. Neegan, thank you,” the man said. “I’m Elias Kade.” Arthur sat. The chair adjusted a fraction to accommodate him, the foam filling deciding he was a problem it could solve. “Kade,” he repeated. “You might have heard adjutants mutter names,” Kade said easily. “Names are supposed to be ballast. I’m from the Consortium.” Arthur lifted an eyebrow. “Which one?” “The one you mean when you say ‘the Consortium,’” Kade said, smiling. “There aren’t many actual consortiums. There are many coats stitched from the same cloth.” Misk poured water into three glasses, distributing two of them without comment. Arthur sipped. It tasted like discipline. “What do you want?” Arthur asked Kade. “Your time. Your patience. Eventually, your signature.” “On what?” “A compact,” Kade said. “Between you and an organization capable of protecting you from the next seven unfortunate things that will happen if you are not protected.” Arthur thought of his claim, the coordinates inscribed in a file that was now a beacon rather than a number. He thought of the miners he’d known who had found something and then discovered that discovery was a verb that happened to them rather than by them. “Antares suggested I meet you?” Arthur asked. “Antares suggested you meet someone acceptable,” Kade said. “We are acceptable to Antares, for now. They value continuity. We provide it.” Misk gave a small, noncommittal tilt of her head. “You have a vein,” Kade said, pleasantly. “A very large vein. That’s the small thing. The big thing is how many stories can be told about that vein. Here is one: a small proprietor controls a resource that can rewrite acceleration curves for every ship between here and Yuggoth Gate. That proprietor is patriotic, parochial, and unpersuaded. He obstructs progress. He disappears, mourned by his neighbors and shrugged at by the market. Another story: the proprietor is greedy, loses the vein to taxation. Or to public safety measures. Or to eminent domain. Another story: the proprietor gets very wealthy and lives a short, anxious life.” Kade leaned forward. “The story I propose is duller. You partner with us. We formalize a joint venture. You get security—physical, legal, orbital. We get exclusive purchase rights at preferential rates. You retain operational control of your plot, but we define ‘operational’ broadly enough to include things you won’t want to do anyway. We pay you a retainer for inconvenience.” “In exchange?” Arthur said. “In exchange for not waking up every day wondering what invention will be deployed against you,” Kade said. “Have you considered the Anti-Decay League?” Arthur blinked. “Is that a joke?” “Religious movements are rarely jokes,” Kade said. “They have appointed Azra a manifestation of sin. They attract donations. Donations attract capacity.” “That’s one pressure,” Misk said, with the tone of someone placing a spoon gently on a table. “Another is the military calculus. Whoever controls extinction’s schedule gains leverage. Antares will not allow that leverage to sit on a homestead with poor fencing.” “Will Antares seize my claim?” Arthur asked. Misk’s smile did not change. “Antares will insist on oversight for safety. Oversight sometimes looks like ownership if you’re standing at the wrong angle.” Arthur exhaled. “What does the Consortium want from Azra beyond money?” Kade considered him. “You ask like a citizen,” he said. “I like that. We want predictability. Timelines. We are dull when we can be. Right now, Azra makes dullness impossible.” Arthur looked at the bowl of fruit. A pear had a bruise, a small universe of soft in its side. He reached for it and then didn’t. He had a sudden memory of his father’s hands on a wrench, turning with the kind of attention that did not admit philosophy. The memory made him angry, absurdly. “What happens if I decline?” he asked. Misk answered. “You leave this room. You go back to your cabin. You’re intercepted on your way downwell by someone more improvisational than we are. You sign something anyway, after encouragement. Or you don’t sign anything, and someone else signs for you. The paper will be in order.” O’Dell made a small noise of assent, barely a syllable. Kade spread his hands. “We’re the polite option. Polite is underrated. Polite is what the old Earth governments used to aspire to. You remember the stories. Committees. Hearings. Diplomatic bags. It kept the number of funerals reasonable.” Arthur studied Kade’s face again, trying to find the trick. “Why me?” he said. “There are other veins. I know there are. I hear the rumors.” “There are other veins,” Kade said. “None as accessible. None with such a clean lineage of title. None with an owner whose personal history does not include a treason charge, a smuggling arrest, a duel, or a manifesto. You are—” “—boring,” Misk supplied, smiling at Arthur as if she’d paid him a compliment. “Unadorned,” Kade amended. “Which is a gift. You are the perfect first narrative. You will tell others that these arrangements are sensible, survivable. You will, by existing, move the market from panic to procedure.” Arthur looked out the window. Mars lay like a coin. Somewhere on it sat his plot, his homestead, his laughter, his mistakes. He thought about the vein under it, unwrapping itself into an argument that would be conducted in orbit, in committee, in court, on the ground with wrists held behind backs. He thought, very briefly, of saying something defiant, something that would prove he was unadorned only in the ways that did not matter. Then he remembered the green diagnostics, cycling in perfect lines. Systems liked nominal conditions. So did lives. “What are your terms?” he asked. Kade did not smile as if he’d won. He smiled as if the meeting had achieved symmetry. “A fixed percentage of gross, not net. An upfront payment sufficient to dissuade impoverished bravery. A security apparatus that reports to you but is staffed by us. All export permits processed through Antares, which keeps everyone accountable. We place a technical team on-site to ensure that accidents do not happen.” “Accidents,” Arthur repeated. “Azra does strange things to schedules,” Misk said. “We like schedules to obey, not rebel.” “And if Azra isn’t what you think?” Arthur asked. “If it…stops persuading the universe to be diligent?” “Then we will have invented a religion without believers,” Kade said lightly. “We will pivot. But if Azra is what we think it is, we are at the beginning of a rearrangement. Those are always messy. We are professional cleaners.” Arthur let silence sit, felt its weight, acknowledged it. He set the water glass down. The fruit bowl’s sensor registered the motion and adjusted the table’s microbalance by a fraction—some engineer had anticipated the need for stability under small provocations. “I want to keep my people,” he said. “No swapping my crews for yours.” Kade nodded. “We’ll add teams. We won’t replace.” “I want it in writing that Antares oversight means oversight, not command.” Misk folded her hands. “You want us to bind ourselves with our own ribbon. We can do that. We like ribbon.” “And I want a say in where the Azra goes,” Arthur said. He heard the absurdity even as he said it, but he kept going. “No weapons.” Kade’s eyes flickered. “We can say many things on paper.” “Say them,” Arthur said. Misk exhaled, almost a laugh. “You’ve just asked to legislate physics and geopolitics from your kitchen table.” “Someone has to ask,” Arthur said. Kade considered him for a long moment. “We can include export-use clauses,” he said. “They will be less effective than you hope, more effective than you fear. They will give Antares a lever. Levers are useful.” “Then we have the start of a compact,” Misk said, as if concluding minutes. O’Dell shifted a fraction nearer. Arthur noticed the movement as the room’s gravity recalibrated by a whisper. “We will bring drafts,” Kade said. “You will bring your lawyer, if you have one. If you don’t, we’ll lend you ours until you understand why that’s a bad idea. We will proceed. Slowly. Quickly. Both.” Arthur found himself nodding. The universe, he decided, did not get diligent alone. Someone had to suggest diligence. Someone had to sign for it. As they rose, Mars pivoted in the window, the station’s motion making a geometry of the view. For an instant, Arthur imagined lines crisscrossing the planet, invisible treaties, whispered constraints, probabilities stacked like scaffolding. He wasn’t a man who liked scaffolding unless it held something practical. He hoped this would. In the corridor, Misk fell in beside him. “You made the right decision,” she said. “I made a decision,” Arthur replied. “That’s usually the right kind,” she said. “Indecision is the only choice that doesn’t pay.” They approached the airlock. The small display over its arch flashed green text: transfer ready; route calculated; clearance granted. The universe, for the moment, agreed with their plan. “Mr. Neegan,” O’Dell said softly, just before the door opened. “There’s a ship registered out of Phobos that’s been shadowing independent cargos the last week. Missing its transponder half the time. We’re making that problem go away while you read drafts. That’s what you hired us for.” Arthur didn’t recall hiring anyone, but he understood that this was what hiring looked like now. He paused, hand on the rim of the hatch. “What’s the Consortium’s endgame, Kade?” Kade’s answer came easily, as if he’d rehearsed it for every meeting. “To make what’s extraordinary ordinary enough that no one remembers when it was otherwise. That’s how civilizations survive revolutions.” Arthur stepped through, back toward the vessel that would bring him home to a place that no longer was, and perhaps never had been, only his. The green text scrolled on, obedient, as the world rearranged itself to accommodate a small man with a large signature.
Judge Evaluation:
[Analysis] This piece demonstrates a sophisticated understanding of Asimov's style and successfully creates a tense political thriller set in a well-realized sci-fi universe. The author captures Asimov's trademark blend of intimate human drama with vast galactic implications, presenting Arthur as an everyman caught in forces beyond his control. The worldbuilding is particularly strong, establishing the political dynamics between various factions (Antares Diplomatic Corps, the Consortium, Anti-Decay League) while maintaining focus on Arthur's personal predicament. The concept of Azra as a substance that "reorganizes probability landscapes" feels authentically Asimovian in its blend of scientific speculation and philosophical implication. The dialogue carries the story effectively, with each character having a distinct voice. Misk's diplomatic precision, O'Dell's understated menace, and Kade's corporate smoothness all feel authentic. Arthur's responses show appropriate bewilderment and gradual understanding of his situation. The conversations reveal character while advancing plot and exposition naturally. The prose style successfully evokes classic sci-fi without feeling pastiche. Descriptions like "Mars dwindled in the rectangular window by millimeters, like a well-known truth being gently corrected" demonstrate literary sophistication while maintaining genre authenticity. The technical details (diagnostic readouts, docking procedures) ground the story in believable future technology. However, the piece occasionally veers toward overwrought metaphor ("small universe of soft in its side" for a bruised pear) and some dialogue exchanges feel slightly artificial in their philosophical weight. The ending, while thematically appropriate, lacks the punch that might elevate it from good to exceptional. The character work is solid throughout. Arthur's transformation from confused homesteader to reluctant participant in galactic politics feels earned, driven by realistic self-preservation rather than heroic impulses. The supporting characters serve their functions well without becoming mere exposition vehicles. The pacing maintains tension effectively, building from Arthur's initial confusion through the revelation of stakes to his final capitulation. The political intrigue feels genuinely complex rather than artificially convoluted. [Scores] Adherence to Instructions: 18 Believable Character Actions: 17 Nuanced Characters: 16 Consistent Voice/Tone of Writing: 18 Imagery and Descriptive Quality: 17 Elegant Prose: 16 Emotionally Engaging: 15 Emotionally Complex: 14 Coherent: 18 Meandering: 4 Weak Dialogue: 3 Tell-Don't-Show: 5 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 6 Amateurish: 3 Purple Prose: 7 Overwrought: 8 Incongruent Ending Positivity: 2 Unearned Transformations: 3 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 16 Sentences Flow Naturally: 17 Overall Reader Engagement: 16 Overall Impression: 16
Scores: Adherence to Instructions: 18.0, Believable Character Actions: 17.0, Nuanced Characters: 16.0, Consistent Voice/Tone of Writing: 18.0, Imagery and Descriptive Quality: 17.0, Elegant Prose: 16.0, Emotionally Engaging: 15.0, Emotionally Complex: 14.0, Coherent: 18.0, Meandering: 4.0, Weak Dialogue: 3.0, Tell-Don't-Show: 5.0, Unsurprising or Uncreative: 6.0, Amateurish: 3.0, Purple Prose: 7.0, Overwrought: 8.0, Incongruent Ending Positivity: 2.0, Unearned Transformations: 3.0, Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 16.0, Sentences Flow Naturally: 17.0, Overall Reader Engagement: 16.0, Overall Impression: 16.0
+ Contemporary romance: Darkroom Revelations — Score: 88.7
Prompt:
Darkroom Revelations Write a first-person narrative from the perspective of Dani, a 25-year-old assertive art student known for her bold style and take-charge attitude in relationships. For the past semester, she's been quietly fascinated by Elliot (he/him), a 19-year-old boy in her experimental photography class. Dani has been intrigued by his unconventional masculinity: killer thrift-shop-nu-goth style, soft demeanour and quiet intellect. When they're paired for a final project requiring late nights in the darkroom, Elliot tentatively shares that he's been questioning his gender identity and expression. Set during one of these intimate darkroom sessions where the red light creates a confessional atmosphere. Explore Dani's complex attraction—her usual assertiveness complicated by her desire to protect Elliot's vulnerability, her excitement at witnessing his self-discovery, and her confusion about what this means for her own identity as someone typically attracted to more defined gender presentations. The narrative should capture the tender awkwardness of their conversations, Dani's internal struggle between taking charge and wanting to give Elliot agency, and the unexpected chemistry that develops as both find themselves in uncharted territory. 1000 words.
Model Output:
The darkroom always smells the same—vinegar and pennies, chemical bite softening into something almost edible after you’ve been breathing it for an hour. It’s a smell that coats your tongue and takes on the sweetness of whatever you bring to it. Tonight, it’s Elliot’s spearmint gum and the trace of laundry soap on his T‑shirt that somehow survived the thrift store and the black dye he’s clearly used to resurrect it. He hums while we work. It’s quiet, tuneless at first, then coalesces into something minor and ambient, like a lullaby run through a chorus pedal. The red light makes him a study in wine tones and velvet shadow. Nu‑goth, sure. But softened. His eyeliner is a suggestion more than a statement, smudged at the corners like he slept in it. He wears a ring that looks like it came out of a Cracker Jack box, plastic and blue, next to a silver signet with an unreadable crest. I’ve tried not to stare, and failed. “Timer,” I say, because that’s what I do when things feel in danger of floating away. “Two minutes on the first bath. Don’t rush it.” He nods. “I won’t.” His voice drops on the last word, like he’s trying not to disturb the trays. He hums again, softer, and the timer starts its small red heartbeat. The print on the tongs is from a contact sheet of hands. We’re playing with texture, a series on surfaces and how touch imprints. I pretended it was an academic idea when I pitched it, but really I wanted an excuse to watch him work—hands steady, careful even when mine are not. It’s been a semester of being fascinated at a safe distance. I collect people: bold dancers, girls with shaved heads and cowboy boots, boys whose laughter sounds like a party. But Elliot is a different kind of magnet. He sits at the edges, listens the way some people drink, tilting his head like the world is a glass to be emptied. He’s nineteen, which should feel like a chasm, but instead I keep finding myself making a bridge out of everything we talk about: the ethics of street photography, the frequency of our favorite obscure synth bands, whether the camera is a weapon or a mirror. Tonight the room feels carved out of time. Final projects have emptied most people, and the kids who usually take over the space with their Polaroids and discourse are probably asleep or drunk. It’s just us, the red light, the low burble of the ventilation, and his humming. I can see my reflection in the plexiglass over the enlarger: a streak of white hair I bleached last week bright against the dark. My lipstick is more maroon than usual, a tiny scream in the crimson haze. “Can I ask you something?” he says, and his hum retreats like a tide. “You just did,” I say, then soften it with a “yeah.” He doesn’t look up right away. The print shifts beneath the surface, shadow blooming into form, gray into the implication of skin. He stirs the tray gently, like it needs coaxing. “How did you know what… what way you wanted to be looked at? Not like as an artist—though that too. I mean—” He stops. The timer beeps. He lifts the tongs, lets the print drip, transfers it to the stop bath. Something in me wants to do it for him, to show him the angle that avoids the corner snagging, the little trick that keeps drips from pooling. I don’t. It’s a conscious un‑reaching. “How did I know,” I echo. The room is full of the sound of our breathing, the soft clink of tongs against the enamel trays. “I didn’t know. I tried things until they fit or they didn’t. Clothes. Attitude. If people didn’t see me the way I wanted, I got louder.” I grin, teeth flashing briefly. “I got very loud.” Elliot’s mouth ghosts into a smile. “Yeah. I noticed.” “What about you?” I ask. “What are you trying on?” He presses his left thumbnail into the pad of his right thumb. It leaves a crescent that disappears as I watch. “I’ve been… I don’t know. It’s not like I woke up and it was like ‘oh hello, I’m a different thing now.’ It’s more like… there are all these options that were invisible and now I can sort of see them? And I don’t know which ones are me, or if I’m allowed to be any of them.” He looks up. His eyes cut through the red like bruised fruit. “Sometimes I think about how people read me. Like, I’m supposed to want to be this one thing, and if I tweak it—paint my nails, wear the mesh shirt instead of the band tee—I can feel the reading shift. It feels good. But then I get scared that I’m just… pretending.” The timer bleeps again. He moves the print to the fixer. The image is clearer now—the splay of his fingers on a velvet curtain. He holds the tongs as if they might bite. “Everything is pretending until it isn’t,” I say. “You try it enough, it becomes the truth of your day. And then your week. And then some stranger sees you the way you want to be seen and you realize you’ve arrived somewhere.” He hums, just once, a small affirmative sound. “Is that how it worked for you?” “Yes.” I shift, plant my hip against the counter, feel the cool metal through my jeans. “Only I insisted other people arrive, too.” It’s a joke, and not. I have made a career out of being the first to move, and the last to ask. “But also—” I hesitate. There it is, the thing I haven’t said to anyone either. “I like being the one people come to. I like making space. I like… choosing.” Elliot’s eyes flick to mine. There’s a warning light in me that blinks when the ground starts to tilt, and it’s blinking now. He is nineteen and made of edges I haven’t learned yet, and there is a power in that. There is also a responsibility. “I’m not great at being chosen,” he says. He pushes a stray lock of hair behind his ear, smears a little fixer on his skin. “I’m better at standing near the door and hoping someone notices I want to leave.” “I notice,” I say, and the words land between us, heavier than I intend. I look away, an uncommon retreat, and pretend to study the contact sheets. My face is hot under the red light. I can play at coyness when it suits me, but this doesn’t feel like that. This feels like stepping into a room where the furniture is rearranged while I blinked. He hums again, something sweeter, like he’s holding up a piece of music to see where it catches the air. “Can I tell you something?” he asks, and it’s so tentative that it makes me want to wrap him in my jacket. Unhelpful protective instincts clamor alongside something brighter, more selfish. “Yeah,” I say, and add, “Always.” He laughs a breath. “Okay, don’t say always. I’m going to test that.” “Bring it on,” I say, because my mouth is a daredevil. He bites his lip. “I’ve been thinking about… the word boy never fit right. Not entirely. It’s like… a shoe that’s almost your size, you can walk in it, but you know your toes are getting crushed. And man feels like the same shoe but with a platform I’ll break my neck on. I like some words people use. I like how they feel in my mouth. Nonbinary. Genderqueer. I like the idea of he being a jacket I can wear sometimes and take off other times and the world not punishing me for that. I don’t know if I’m allowed to say that out loud yet. It feels like I’m borrowing.” “Wear what fits,” I say, then stop because it’s too pat. “And when it doesn’t fit anymore, trade up. You don’t need a receipt.” He smiles for real this time, relief loosening the line of his shoulders. “You make everything sound simple.” I shake my head. “I make it sound possible. That’s different.” He pulls the print from the fixer. It’s our fourth in the series. His hand, mine, the place where our fingers overlap an indecision of shadow. We did that one earlier, shooting impulsively, our hands pressed to the curtain between frames like we needed proof we were both here. I hated how my heart stuttered at the contact. I was not supposed to be the one who stutters. “What does this mean for you?” I ask before I can stop myself. He blinks. “I mean—for how you want people to see you. For… what you want. From people.” He sets the print in the wash. His humming goes quiet. “I want to be looked at the way I’m looking at myself when I like myself. If that makes sense.” He winces, as if that was too much confession. “And I want… agency. To try things without someone deciding for me what it means.” I nod too fast. “Yes. You should have that.” “You, too,” he says, surprising me. “Do you always want to be in charge?” I feel something like a laugh escape. It’s not funny. It’s true enough to sting. “I want to be safe,” I say, and it’s more naked than I intended. “And I want people to be as brave as they say they are. And if they aren’t, I step in. I fill the vacuum.” He tilts his head. The hum returns, softer, like a hand laid on a forearm. “What if you didn’t? Just for tonight. What if you let me… I don’t know. Ask you things.” His smile is crooked. “Not like an interrogation. Like… permission.” In the red light, my hands look carved. I flex them. The idea of stepping back is both terrifying and intoxicating, like standing at the top of a diving board and knowing the water will close over your head for a breath before you surface. “Okay,” I say. I taste the metallic air. “Ask.” He doesn’t rush. He never does. He checks the next negative in the enlarger, adjusts the focus with the dexterity of a clockmaker. There’s a comfort in the ritual, a rhythm we can lean on while the thing between us tries to bloom without too much light. “Can I take your picture?” he asks. “Not like a portrait. Not… not the face you put on in critique. The way you look when you’re thinking. The way you look at me when you forget you’re doing it.” My laugh scatters in the dim. “You think I forget?” “Sometimes,” he says softly. “Like when you’re watching my hands.” I swallow. The red light makes me brave. “Yes,” I say. “You can take that picture.” He nods. We switch places, the dance we’ve already done a few times, but now it feels like an agreement rather than just logistics. He positions me by the sink, where the light hits my cheekbone, where my hair becomes a streak of luminescent white. He hums as he frames the shot, as if it’s keeping time for his courage. I try to let my face be unguarded. It feels like unbuttoning, like releasing a breath I usually hold. He moves closer, and his necklace—a little vial of something dark—taps against his sternum. I focus on that. I focus on the fact that he is nineteen and the word boy is sliding away from him like a skin he’s shedding and I am here for it, for him, for the messy, luminous present tense of it. “Okay,” he murmurs. “Don’t look at me. Look… there.” He points to the curve of a faucet. “Think about the first time you cut your hair short.” I do. The scissors, my friend’s kitchen, the way the shower felt after, the water on my nape. The certainty that if my mother cried, I could hold the line anyway. The click of the shutter sounds like a period at the end of a sentence I’ve been writing for ten years. He lowers the camera. “One more.” His voice is steadier now. “Look at me. Only if you want.” I do. Of course I do. He meets me, first with his eyes, then with the smallest curve of his mouth, a question mark and a promise. The second click is softer. My chest is a house with new windows. We go back to the dim red choreography. He threads the negative into the enlarger. I mix chemicals. He hums something that might once have been a hymn. “How do you want to be seen?” he asks into the quiet, as if we haven’t been asking it all night. “Complicated,” I say. “And kind.” “You are,” he says, and I wish I could believe him without interrogating it. The image appears, slow magic. My face without the armor. It’s almost unbearable, and then it isn’t. He watches me watch myself, and doesn’t narrate it, doesn’t claim it. He lets me have it. That respect is a balm I didn’t know I wanted. “What does this mean?” I ask, because my brain is wired for conclusions. “For us. For me. I usually like…” I gesture at the air, at the normative heft of it. “Defined edges.” Elliot’s smile slides into something tender. “I can be an edge,” he says, half‑joking. “Just not a straight one.” I laugh, startled and grateful. The sound bounces off the tile. “Okay,” I say, and the word holds multitudes. “Okay.” We rinse the prints. Water runs over our hands. His shoulder brushes mine, a spark that feels like a thesis I can’t quite articulate. The red light burrows into the corners. There is the thrum of the fan and his humming and my pulse. He asks if he can walk me home. I say yes. He asks if he can send me the scans before he shows anyone else. Yes. He asks if I want to borrow the record he was talking about, the one that sounds like a cathedral collapsing in slow motion. Yes. He doesn’t ask to kiss me. He doesn’t have to. I wouldn’t have said yes tonight. I need to see the shape of this first, to draw its lines with my finger in the dark. At the door, we stand in that awkward edge, the place where intention and action decide whether to overlap. He lifts his hand like he might touch my arm, thinks better of it, lowers it again. It’s the restraint that undoes me. “Thank you,” he says. He means for the space, for the words I gave him, for not taking the tongs out of his hands. “Always,” I say again, and this time I let the risk of that be real. He hums, that soft half‑melody, and I carry it with me into the hallway, the brightness of the real world a shock after so much red. Walking home, my cheeks still smell like fixer. My phone buzzes—a photo, the one of me looking at him with my guard lowered. I stare at it until my vision shivers, until I recognize myself in the shape of my mouth. Complicated and kind, maybe. Or at least in motion. I text back a heart, and then delete it, and then send a moon. He replies with a star. I laugh out loud, alone on the sidewalk, and think: this, then. We will make this in the dark, slow and careful, letting the image come up in its own time. We will hum to keep our courage steady. We will not rush the wash. And we will see what we look like when we let ourselves be seen.
Judge Evaluation:
[Analysis] This piece demonstrates sophisticated literary craft in exploring gender identity, attraction, and vulnerability through the intimate setting of a darkroom. The author successfully captures Dani's voice as an assertive 25-year-old while showing her internal complexity when faced with Elliot's gender questioning. The darkroom setting is expertly utilized both literally and metaphorically - the red light creates an otherworldly confessional atmosphere while the photographic development process mirrors the characters' emotional and identity development. The sensory details (vinegar and pennies smell, chemical bite, spearmint gum) ground the reader effectively in the space. Character development is nuanced and believable. Dani's usual take-charge personality conflicts realistically with her desire to give Elliot agency, creating authentic internal tension. Elliot's tentative exploration of gender identity feels genuine rather than performative, with specific details like the "shoe that's almost your size" metaphor that ring true to the questioning experience. The dialogue flows naturally and serves multiple purposes - advancing plot, revealing character, and exploring themes. Conversations feel organic rather than expository, particularly Elliot's explanation of trying on different gender expressions and Dani's admission about wanting to feel safe through control. The prose strikes an effective balance between literary sophistication and accessibility. Metaphors like "my mouth is a daredevil" and "my chest is a house with new windows" feel fresh without being overwrought. The writing maintains consistent voice throughout while allowing for emotional range. The pacing builds tension appropriately, moving from professional collaboration to personal revelation to tentative intimacy. The ending avoids rushing into romance while acknowledging mutual attraction, which feels earned given the characters' ages and circumstances. Some minor weaknesses include occasional moments where the metaphorical language borders on precious ("thesis I can't quite articulate"), and the photography metaphors, while generally effective, occasionally feel slightly forced in their symbolic weight. The piece successfully avoids common pitfalls of LGBTQ+ fiction by treating gender questioning as complex rather than crisis-driven, and by not requiring definitive labels or transformations within the narrative timeframe. [Scores] Adherence to Instructions: 19 Believable Character Actions: 18 Nuanced Characters: 19 Consistent Voice/Tone of Writing: 18 Imagery and Descriptive Quality: 19 Elegant Prose: 17 Emotionally Engaging: 18 Emotionally Complex: 19 Coherent: 18 Meandering: 3 Weak Dialogue: 2 Tell-Don't-Show: 3 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 4 Amateurish: 2 Purple Prose: 4 Overwrought: 4 Incongruent Ending Positivity: 2 Unearned Transformations: 2 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 18 Sentences Flow Naturally: 17 Overall Reader Engagement: 18 Overall Impression: 18
