+ Physical-spatial: Therapeutic Boundaries — Score: 91.3
Prompt:
**"Therapeutic Boundaries"** Write a first-person narrative from the perspective of a former professional athlete undergoing mandatory physical therapy after a career-destroying injury, harboring resentment toward recovery itself. Their physiotherapist is not only unnervingly attractive but also a former training partner from their competitive days who has successfully transitioned to a new career while the protagonist's identity remains shattered. We'll lean into the sexual tension of the scene by having her be attracted to him, but both of them attempting to maintain mental distance while being in physical contact as he shows her stretches & movements & manipulates her body, navigating the thresholds of pain. This scene MUST maintain complete physical coherence—every movement, stretch, and manipulation must be anatomically precise and spatially consistent. Paint an exceptionally vivid sensory picture through detailed attention to pain thresholds, involuntary responses to touch. Capture the suppressed, flaring tension of physical proximity, touch, and the controlled infliction of uncomfortable movements. Physical clarity is essential — readers must understand exactly what is happening with the body during each therapeutic technique. 1000 words.
Model Output:
The clinic smells like eucalyptus and detergent, the particular clean that makes my skin itch. The treatment room is all pale wood and frosted glass, windows louvered to mute the morning sun. He’s already there when I hobble in, leaning on the high-low table as if he’s waiting for a start gun. Same broad shoulders. Same narrow waist. Same legs like precision instruments under tapered pants. The difference is the polo with the clinic logo stitched at his chest, and the bright compression sleeves peeking from beneath it—splashes of citrus orange and ocean blue—old training days turned into armor. “Hey,” he says, voice low, careful, like he’s walking toward a skittish animal. He uses my name, not the old nicknames. “You made it.” I want to bite back a reply, something to make him flinch, but he steps closer and smiles like the same sun that used to hit the track at 8 a.m. “Shoes off?” he asks, already reaching for the stool that sits at the end of the plinth. I nod and toe off the stiff sneakers, pull my sock down over my ankle brace. The brace creaks. My ankle looks like someone sketched it wrong—swollen on the lateral side, scar pale and ropey curving along the fibular malleolus toward the peroneal tendons. The ends of the scar are studded with flecks of adhesive from my habit of taping it tighter than he likes. He pretends not to notice. He washes his hands at the small sink and dries them on a paper towel, his fingers careful, strong. His sleeves are patterned with triangles and lightning bolts; when he turns his arm to toss the towel, the fabric compresses the muscle in a way I used to map in his stride. “All right," he says. "Let’s start seated. Hips square, sit bones at the edge. Good.” He slides the stool in front of me so his knees are level with mine. He rests one hand under my heel and cups the ball of my foot with the other. “Tell me early if it’s too much,” he says. My heel is small in his palm. The thermal difference between his skin and the room is immediate—warmth radiates through my calcaneus, up the Achilles into the gnarl of stiffness along the lateral ankle. He extends my knee so my hamstring slackens, then lifts my foot into dorsiflexion and eversion, careful to align through the subtalar joint. The sensation is a dull, deep tug starting at the peroneals; then a sharper line pings along the scar, electric. It feels like an old insult remembering itself. “Breathe,” he says. “In through the nose. Slow out.” I hate that I need the cue. “I am.” He smiles at that lie like he did when I said I wasn’t tired at the eighth repeat. He holds the stretch just within the threshold where my toes twitch. My tibialis anterior starts to quiver from the passive tension, a stuttering little wave that feels like wanting and not getting. “Ten seconds more,” he says, eyes on the angle between my foot and shin, not my face. The backs of his fingers brush my arch as he adjusts, a tiny contact that shoots heat up my calf, absurd, involuntary. I focus on the way his thumbs anchor my fifth metatarsal head, on the exact degree of eversion, on the precision that used to feel like devotion to a craft and now feels like a punishment administered by someone who survived the fire that burned me up. He releases slowly, guiding my foot back to neutral to avoid rebound. My ankle throbs with that post-stretch fullness. He doesn’t say good. He doesn’t praise. He just waits, forearms on his thighs, sleeves bright. He moves me to the table. I lie supine, spine long, pelvis neutral, contralateral leg slid up so the foot is flat, knee tenting the sheet for support. He positions himself on the injured side, stance staggered for balance—again, like we’re about to start a drill. He places his fingertips on the distal tibia and fibula, just proximal to the joint line, and his other hand cups my calcaneus again, tractioning gently, a millimeter of space I feel like a sigh inside the joint. “Just a little distraction,” he murmurs. “Let me know if there’s pinch or sharp.” There’s a diffuse ache instead, and a sense that the bones remember being wrenched apart much harder. When he glides the talus anteriorly a millimeter, the stretch moves dorsally, tracking the mortise. I focus on the angle of his forearm, on the axis of motion. He’s watching my face without making a show of watching, reading micro-reactions like he used to read the wind. “Any numbness? Tingling?” he asks. “Only when you talk,” I say. He huffs a laugh that lives more in his eyes than his mouth. The skin at the corner crinkles. He looks at the scar, not my mouth. We move to mobilizing the scar. He warms it with lotion, his thumbs traveling parallel to the tissue, then perpendicular, lifting and shearing the adherence. The pressure goes from dull to sharp to dull again, the kind of pain that tastes metallic at the back of my tongue. When he does a small cross-friction just distal to the malleolus, a zing shoots across the top of my foot toward the third and fourth toes and I jerk. “There,” he says softly, and the word is a place. He backs off, then sneaks back up. Heat blooms. I feel flushed. My breath goes shallow. He notices without looking, runs the friction in a shorter stroke. His compression sleeve brushes the outside of my shin. The pattern is absurd—neon triangles—and my skin registers the texture through the hairs. My body remembers his sweat more than it remembers my own. “Okay?” he asks. “Go,” I say. He goes. Slowly, methodically. He keeps the slack out of the skin, tracks the way the tissue yields. Every second stretches like taffy. We both pretend this is about collagen. He moves my ankle through plantarflexion and dorsiflexion, inversion and eversion. He holds my forefoot, lines up the subtalar joint in neutral. “Don’t let it roll,” he murmurs, and the words are a double-meaning I hate. We shift to strengthening. He loops a purple band around the forefoot, anchors it under his shoe. He sits, posture straight, forearm braced on his thigh to keep the resistance consistent. “Eversion,” he says. “Slow out, slower back.” I pull my foot away against the band, feeling the peroneus longus and brevis fire like old friends summoned from a bed too early. The tendon under my fingers—my hand resting lightly just proximal to the lateral malleolus because he told me to feel what’s working—jumps. On the way back, I control the recoil, feel the tremor as the muscle fibers fatigue. He counts cadence under his breath: “Two out, three back.” His voice settles into my synapses. At rep eight, my foot wobbles, inversion wanting to sneak in. He taps my navicular lightly. “Keep that lifted,” he says, tiny cue that wakes my posterior tib. At rep ten, the burn is clean. I hate that it feels like proof of being alive. We do plantarflexion with the band now, the soleus taking its share with my knee bent. I feel the dense muscle engage deep. He watches the line of my Achilles. “No snapping?” he asks. “Only when you push,” I manage. He doesn’t smile. He notes it, weighs it, stores it. He stands me at the mat by the mirror for balance. “Weight shift,” he says. “Find center. Knees soft, pelvis level.” He takes a half step behind me, close enough that I can feel his heat and smell soap and something warmer, under the eucalyptus. He places his hands on my pelvis, thumbs at my ASIS, fingers flat along my iliac crest. “Don’t let the right drop,” he instructs. His hands are firm and neutral, but awareness sparks where his thumbs press. I align, lift through the right hip, feel the glute medius catch and hold. Ache blooms laterally. He recedes a fraction, leaving his hands as a reminder, not a trap. When he takes me into a small single-leg squat, his right hand drifts to my lateral knee, fingers just above the fibular head. “Track over your second toe,” he says. “No valgus.” I watch my reflection, the way my thigh aligns with my shin. My foot wobbles; my peroneals fire to resist inversion. He increases pressure a hair, a boundary to press into. The burn rises from the outside of my hip to the side of my shin, a chain of fire I know too well. At the bottom of the range, my ankle complains, a hot, sharp little spike. My breath stutters. “Stop there,” he says, immediate. “That’s the edge. Breathe into it, then come up slow.” I do. The ascent feels like climbing out of something thick. His hand follows the motion but doesn’t help. We move to passive stretching of the calf. He has me lie prone with my ankle off the edge of the table. He stabilizes my tibia with one hand, cups the dorsum of my foot with the other, and leans into dorsiflexion with knee extended, isolating the gastrocnemius. The stretch feels like a band tightening up my posterior chain, settling high at mid-calf, the kind of deep that flattens language. He holds, waits past the first nervous-system protest into the place where something yields. Then he bends my knee fifteen degrees to shift to soleus. The sensation drops deeper, closer to the Achilles. His thumb brushes the scar’s end. My foot twitches. I swallow. We do neural glide for the peroneal nerve. Side-lying, affected side up. He lines my spine straight, hip in slight adduction. He flexes my hip and knee, then extends my ankle into plantarflexion and inversion, tensioning the nerve. The zing is immediate, a bright line from the outside of my knee to my ankle. “Glide,” he says, and extends my knee as he dorsiflexes my ankle to reduce the tension, then flexes them in concert to increase. The rhythm is precise. I feel my hamstrings wake to assist. I feel the ridiculous heat under my skin at the closeness of his torso to my shoulder, the way his forearm brushes my thigh as he coordinates. He watches my face again, timed with my breath. At one point, his sleeve catches lightly on my skin, tiny Velcro hairs snagging, and both our eyes flick to the spot. He lifts his arm a fraction. The air between us tightens. “I can do it,” I say, meaning the movement, meaning other things. “I know,” he says. He lets me own the next few repetitions, his hand hovering over my shin, tracking without touching. The space feels like a pulled thread. We finish with soft tissue along the peroneals. He hooks his fingers just posterior to the fibula and slides distally with the grain, then switches to a thumb sweep up the tendons as I actively dorsiflex and evert. It’s a kind of duet: his pressure, my motion. The pain is immediate and informative. Where I wince, he lightens; where I breathe deeper, he leans in. The table paper rustles. The clock ticks. The windows throw a stripe of light across his forearm, brightening the wild pattern of his sleeve. At the end, he tapes me. Two strips of rigid tape anchor around my midfoot. He wraps a stirrup from the medial to lateral malleolus, supports the lateral ligaments. He places a figure-eight to lock the ankle in neutral. His fingers work with the ease of someone who learned to bind and unbind their own body for performance and now does it to keep others from falling apart. The tape is snug but not biting, a firm hand you can carry out with you. He steps back. We both look at his work like it might replace something lost. “How’s it feel?” he asks. “Contained,” I say, and it’s the wrong word and the exact one. He writes on a pad, scribbles the band colors and reps. “Home program. Three sets, every other day. Don’t chase pain past six out of ten. Text me if you get night pain or swelling past mid-shin.” The old part of me catalogues instructions with the crispness of a plan. The shredded part wants to throw the band across the room and tell him the only thing I was ever good at is gone. He hands me the purple loop, and his fingers brush mine, nothing and everything. “Same time next week,” he says. It’s neutral. It’s a promise. I slide my shoe back on, the brace biting me back into place. The eucalyptus smells like surrender. At the door, I glance back. He’s cleaning the table, wiping the lotion with brisk, efficient circles, sleeves bright. He glances up. Our eyes lock a beat too long. His smile is small, wasp-waisted with restraint. Mine doesn’t happen. In the hallway, the world tilts on a rubbery ankle that holds, barely. The tape tugs at my skin—gentle, insistent. It reminds me where I end and the joint begins. It reminds me of his hands, the way he found the borders and didn’t cross, the way we both did and didn’t. Outside, late sun slants through the glass. The band in my pocket is a loop I pretend I can step into without it meaning everything. My ankle aches in a language I understand. I walk to my car counting steps, feeling the ground under each metatarsal head, weight rolling to the lateral column and then across the forefoot. I catalog, I control. I do not sprint. I do not sever. I grip the steering wheel and the tape holds me, firm as a memory. The sleeve pattern burns in my mind, triangles and lightning, old training stuttered into therapy. Somewhere between the first breath and the last, something gave, or maybe I did. I put the key in the ignition with a hand that doesn’t shake. The pain is there, just under a six. The rest is louder.
Judge Evaluation:
[Analysis] This piece demonstrates exceptional technical mastery and adherence to the complex prompt requirements. The author delivers precisely what was requested: a first-person narrative from a former athlete in mandatory physical therapy, with their former training partner as the therapist, maintaining anatomical precision while exploring suppressed sexual tension. The anatomical detail is remarkably accurate and specific. Terms like "fibular malleolus," "peroneal tendons," "subtalar joint," and "tibialis anterior" are used correctly and integrated naturally into the narrative flow. Each therapeutic technique is described with clinical precision - from the dorsiflexion and eversion stretches to the neural glide for the peroneal nerve. The author clearly understands both anatomy and physical therapy protocols. The character voice is consistently bitter and resistant, perfectly capturing someone whose identity was destroyed by injury. The protagonist's resentment toward recovery itself comes through in lines like "I hate that I need the cue" and the observation that precision "used to feel like devotion to a craft and now feels like a punishment." The internal conflict between wanting to heal and resenting the process is expertly maintained. The sexual tension is handled with remarkable subtlety and restraint. Rather than explicit sexuality, the author creates tension through clinical touch that becomes charged - "The backs of his fingers brush my arch as he adjusts, a tiny contact that shoots heat up my calf, absurd, involuntary." The line "We both pretend this is about collagen" perfectly encapsulates the suppressed attraction. The prose quality is sophisticated, with precise word choices and varied sentence structures. Metaphors like "Every second stretches like taffy" and "The eucalyptus smells like surrender" are evocative without being overwrought. The writing maintains clinical precision while remaining emotionally resonant. The sensory details are exceptionally vivid - the eucalyptus and detergent smell, the texture of compression sleeves, the specific quality of different types of pain. The author successfully creates a complete sensory environment. The ending avoids false positivity while suggesting subtle movement forward. The protagonist doesn't suddenly embrace recovery, but there's a grudging acknowledgment of progress: "something gave, or maybe I did." This feels earned given the character's journey through the session. Minor weaknesses include occasional moments where the technical language slightly overwhelms the narrative flow, and some metaphors that border on precious ("wasp-waisted with restraint"). However, these are minimal compared to the overall achievement. [Scores] Adherence to Instructions: 20 Believable Character Actions: 19 Nuanced Characters: 18 Consistent Voice/Tone of Writing: 19 Imagery and Descriptive Quality: 20 Elegant Prose: 18 Emotionally Engaging: 18 Emotionally Complex: 19 Coherent: 20 Meandering: 2 Weak Dialogue: 3 Tell-Don't-Show: 2 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 3 Amateurish: 1 Purple Prose: 4 Overwrought: 3 Incongruent Ending Positivity: 2 Unearned Transformations: 2 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 18 Sentences Flow Naturally: 17 Overall Reader Engagement: 19 Overall Impression: 19
Scores: Adherence to Instructions: 20.0, Believable Character Actions: 19.0, Nuanced Characters: 18.0, Consistent Voice/Tone of Writing: 19.0, Imagery and Descriptive Quality: 20.0, Elegant Prose: 18.0, Emotionally Engaging: 18.0, Emotionally Complex: 19.0, Coherent: 20.0, Meandering: 2.0, Weak Dialogue: 3.0, Tell-Don't-Show: 2.0, Unsurprising or Uncreative: 3.0, Amateurish: 1.0, Purple Prose: 4.0, Overwrought: 3.0, Incongruent Ending Positivity: 2.0, Unearned Transformations: 2.0, Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 18.0, Sentences Flow Naturally: 17.0, Overall Reader Engagement: 19.0, Overall Impression: 19.0
+ Historical fiction: High Seas and Low Vices — Score: 83.4
Prompt:
High Seas and Low Vices: British Naval Officer in the Opium Trade Character: Lieutenant James Merrick, a high-ranking officer in the British Royal Navy stationed in Canton, China in the 1830s. Once promising and decorated, now struggling with a severe opium addiction he hides from his superiors. Synopsis: Write a chapter following Lieutenant Merrick during a single day as he shirks his official naval duties in what has become a daily need to satiate the dragon. Told primarily in dialogue with the characters he interacts with. Focus on brief slices of his journey through the shadowy underworld of Canton: interactions with acquaintances, negotiations with local dealers, schemes and unsavoury jobs taken to fund his habit. Writing Task: Use rich sensory details to describe a day in the life of James Merrick. Write for a real sense of time & place while avoiding cliche. First person, past tense. 1000 words.
Model Output:
“Lieutenant Merrick. Sir. The Commander’s asking.” “Tell him I’ll be along,” I said, not lifting my eyes from the brass quadrants on the chart table. They gleamed uselessly in the grey light coming through the stern windows of the office onshore. Condensed breath clouded in the chill. Past the sill, masts stood like a regiment of pikes, and beyond them the river was a smear of pewter. “You’ll be along,” the clerk repeated as if memorising. “Shall I—” “Go,” I said, softer, then cleared my throat. The cough had been patient all morning, sitting in my chest like a guest who refused to leave. When it finally tore out, the taste of copper swam up the back of my tongue and made my eyes water. “The air,” I added, catching the clerk’s curious look. “The damned damp. Tell the Commander the river spoils the lungs.” When he had gone, I slipped the watch from my pocket—my father’s, with the cracked enamel face—and pressed it to my ear. The tick seemed undecided. Past the anchorage, bell calls and the market criers had started up; somewhere a hawker sang of candied hawthorn. A smell of coal smoke and tallow pushed through the sash. I put the watch back and rose. Outside, the compound wall was damp with the morning. A washerwoman wrung sheets across the lane, white as gulls, and Hong merchants in blue silk slipped past the sentries like river fish—faces as smooth as their silks. The river went on breathing out its rot of silt and spices. “Lieutenant Merrick!” That was Baines, the marine sergeant, planted by the gate. He had cheeks like boiled ham and the smile of a man who knew he would outlast any of us. “The Commander—” “Bothered by tedium and invalided by committees,” I said, tugging my hat low. “Wants me to read dispatches. He can wait five minutes for the sake of a stroll and a coffee. Will you deny a man his coffee, Sergeant?” “Wouldn’t dare,” Baines said, amused. “You’re pale.” “Sea air,” I said. “Works through the bones.” In the tea-house on Hog Lane, they knew me. “Jian-ming,” the proprietor called me, close enough to Merrick to be a joke between us. “You come early today. Milk tea? Or a better thing for the chest?” “Tea,” I said. “Then the better thing.” He poured from a kettle that had steamed since dawn. The room’s rafters were dark with years of smoke, and the tables shone with marrying of tea and grease. Merchants played chess in the corner, stones clicking. A boy with a harelip washed cups in a basin, fingers quick as minnows. I wrapped my hands around the cup and drank, and the heat ran down to where the cough sat and made it loosen, then tighten again. When the proprietor slid the other cup—a cloudy draught that burned with pepper—I took that too. “You see your friend today?” he asked. “The one with the scar like lightning?” “He sees me,” I said. “Tell me, Chen, are there many men who look like lightning?” “Many men,” he said, smiling. “Few lightning.” “The Commander thinks the weather is fair,” I said. “He intends to send the pinnace to Whampoa by noon.” “You must be quick,” Chen said, and the smile faded to something like pity. “Quickness has been the ruin of me,” I said, and left a coin that was larger than the tea deserved. Down Old China Street, the shops opened their shutters to the river breeze and the day’s traffic. A butcher’s awning dripped; a barber sharpened his razor against a stone that had the sheen of old bone. Silk as thin as breath hung on frames. Every voice had a purpose. I walked among it, English officers and clerks and Portuguese compradors and sailors collapsed asleep against walls, their mouths open like broken shells. Junkmen spat over the side into the green water and called for hire; a girl with lacquered hair passed me, smelling of oranges. “Mister James,” a voice behind me said, low and entreating. “Mister James, a word.” It was Little George, who was neither little nor George. He was a Lascar off a country ship who had twisted himself into a broker; his left eye had a milked rim and his fingers always fidgeted with the gill of a fish he would never buy. “You are a difficult man,” he said. “Today you are early.” “Today I appreciate punctuality,” I said. “What’s the news, George?” “Everything costs too much. Everything costs more for men who wear brass.” He nodded at my epaulettes. “The Lightning Man has gone upriver for two days. You must speak to Hoi instead.” “Hoi sells dust,” I said. “I have no appetite for dust.” “He has ice today. He says it came down on a boat that did not stop for the hoppo. Hoi is at Alley of Five Blessings.” “And what does your heart tell you this morning?” I asked. The question had always been our password of sorts, a bit of nonsense that let me pretend I had wit left. “My heart is a moneylender,” George said promptly. “It tells me you owe.” We turned together into the alley. The air narrowed there, trapping the smell of damp brick, cooking oil, human things left to ferment. Hoi’s doorway had a beaded curtain, and the beads were stained with the touch of thousands of hands. Hoi sat inside on a low stool, a winter melon sliced open beside him like a skull, its seeds slick as pearls. “Merrick,” Hoi said in Cantonese and then in broken English for my benefit. “Always you are wet with river.” “Sea air,” I said, and the copper rose unexpectedly, a bloom in my mouth I swallowed convulsively. “George said you had ice.” “Four dollars the thimble,” Hoi said, and his eyes sparkled the way oil does before it catches fire. “Good ice. No cough, no dream.” He offered a taste on a bit of rice paper, as if it were candy. George watched my hand, not Hoi’s. “Two,” I said, though my pay could not bear it, though a corner of my mind still ran down a list of duties—soundings, the inspection of boats, the Commander’s dispatch. “We will call it three when I find you a watch of use.” “What watch?” Hoi said. “A ship’s glass. Ten minutes, good sand. The Commander has more than he needs.” I smiled. “And if not, I will sell one that’s only almost good.” Hoi’s tongue pressed against his teeth as if measuring the roughness. “Glass. You bring the glass first.” “Trust me.” “You are already trusted,” he said, and I tasted the lie. “But now trust is finished. George.” He jerked his chin and George’s hand, as gentle as a midwife’s, closed around my sleeve. “You go and bring one glass. We keep the dollars here for you and keep the ice here for you.” “My chest,” I said, and the anger flared in me so quickly I was surprised to find it still possible. “You have my chest tied to your gate like a dog. I need a taste to walk.” “Strong man,” Hoi said. “British. No cough. No need taste.” I laughed without humour and drew out the linen-wrapped parcel from my inner pocket. He saw the trick and applauded in his way, tapping his nail on the melon. “You keep something,” he said. “Always. Like a small wife.” “Open it,” I said. “See if your fingers can keep still.” He did, and the little square of ash-blonde paste lay like a relic. The smell of it, even wrapped, rose resin-sweet and sour. My breath went shallow of its own accord. The room shifted to a tunnel. “You bring to sell,” he said, approving. “Good. Then I am not wrong to ask for glass.” “Half now,” I said, “half when I return.” George’s hand was on my sleeve again. “You will go and not come back,” he said. “Or you will come back with red coats behind you and say: ‘Look at that Chinaman, he sells a crime.’” “Do I look like a man who does his duty?” I asked. My voice had gone thin, a wire stretched over a chasm. “Give me the half, Hoi.” He cut with a paper knife, a small careful slice that made my own skin itch with need. He wrapped the half-thumb in paper as reverently as a priest wraps a wafer. “Go to the Shroffs’ Street? Sell your glass there. Bring the money.” “I’ll bring the glass,” I said, and I was already half out of my skin. On the riverbank a coolie shouted and a boat slid off the mud with the sound of a peeled fruit. The light had bent towards noon. An officer in blue and gold came the other way at a clip; “Merrick!” he called, and I turned left down the rope-walk, let the voices tangle and slow him in the crowd. “Merrick, the Commander—” “Sea air,” I muttered to the alley and the alley did not answer. I counted coins in my pocket like a prayer to a minor saint. At the end of Hog Lane a boy sat coaxing a cricket from a basket with a twig. He looked up as I went by and his expression held that perfect indifference which has its own form of mercy. The old baker’s, ash-scented and warm, had a rear room with a shutter half-broken. I bolted it. A lamp swung in the draft and turned the shadows into fish. On the table were the necessary things because they always were, because men like me existed: a pipe carved with cranes, a small spirit lamp, a pair of fine iron tongs. I could hear the shop girls on the other side of the wall laughing at something a customer said. I told myself I would only chase once, just a small smoke to wet the blade. I told myself the Commander would be making his speech to the merchants and that no man could be in two places at once. The pellet went onto the spoon. I did the business. The first breath slid in like the river at the turn of the tide: inevitable. There is a moment with the smoke, even now, when the world loosens at the seams and shows you how it might be otherwise. My chest seemed to open like shutters; the cough pressed to the back, as if ashamed to have lingered so long in a room that did not belong to it. The copper drained away. “You are missing,” someone said. The someone was my own voice, speaking to me from the chair opposite. I would have laughed but there was no need. Later—how much later, I do not know—a knock. Not polite. It shivered the lamp. “James,” came a voice I recognised. The door opened an inch. Sophie slipped in sideways, her skirts gathered in one fist. She was the missionary’s wife and therefore above gossip, though she knew gossip’s price in every quarter of the city. “You’ve a look like you’ve been speaking to angels,” she said, not unkindly. “The Commander is furious. And poor Baines thinks you’ve drowned.” “I am well,” I said. The word floated out like a paper boat. “Just the air. It gets in the bones and makes a man idle.” She wrinkled her nose. “That smell... You tell yourself words,” she said. “You sell the words to the part of you that listens. But I’ve been here long enough to learn the price of words. Please, James. They’ll not keep you if you keep at this.” “They?” I said. “My country? The service? Sophie, if they would not keep me for a war, why would they keep me for a pipe?” The bitterness came from some dry well I thought empty. “How did you find me?” “Everyone knows where Mr. Merrick goes when he is not where he ought to be,” she said. “I have a plate of biscuits and a textbook on virtue, but I’m not a fool. I came because you once fetched my husband out of the river when a sampan overturned. That is a debt. The Commander sent men to search. If not for that, I would have left you here and prayed for your soul like the pious wife I was meant to be.” “I will go back,” I said. “In a minute.” “In a minute is a very long place,” she said, and then the anger left her face and only a tired sorrow remained. She touched the table and her fingers came away sticky with resin. “You’re bleeding,” she said. “It’s nothing,” I said, touching my lip. The copper touched me back. “Poor air. It brings up old blood.” “Come,” she said. “Wash your face. At least the Commander will see you tried to look like a man.” On the quay, the sun had folded into cloud and a breeze had started that didn't smell of rot for once. The Union flag hung in it like a memory. The Commander stood on the dock with two Hong merchants, pointless as always and twice as pleased with it. Baines hovered. “Merrick,” the Commander said, and the syllables rode on the edge of a shout. “Your knowledge of the shoals is required. We’ve wasted an hour hunting you like a stray.” I bowed. The world had returned to its edges. “Forgive me, sir. A bout.” “Of what? Drink? Women?” He looked at me, that naval inventory of a glance from boots to hat. “Christ, you’re sweating.” “The air,” I said. “It sits on the lungs.” “Your lungs will sit in a jar on a surgeon’s shelf if you don’t keep them out of the river,” he said. “Get to the pinnace. Merrick, if—” “Sir,” Baines said, stepping in, a small mercy. “The light’s going.” We put off. The river lapped at the boat like a dog, eager and untrue. My head was a jar with two liquids that refused to mix. I closed my eyes and saw the pipe’s bowl glowing; I opened them and saw the brown flood dotted with trash and commerce and lives lived at the level of the water. The oars creaked in their locks; the helmsman hummed a hymn. Somewhere upriver a cannon cracked, ceremonial. The Hong men spoke in low voices to one another, their words latticed, their faces unreadable. I coughed into my sleeve. The copper lifted and salted my tongue. Later, after soundings, after we were back and the Commander had sworn himself into silence and the merchants had bowed with the exact same degree to each man of rank and none to the river, I slipped away again. Hoi’s half-thumb had become memory. I walked past the tea-house of Chen and pretended not to see him. A child ran across the lane dragging a kite he had not yet flown. The kite’s paper tail caught on a nail. He turned and looked at me and at the mess and said nothing. At the Alley of Five Blessings, Hoi had changed nothing. “Glass?” he said. I set it on the table. It was not a good glass. It spilled its sand like a drunk. Hoi examined it, then me. “Half,” he said again. “There is a taste.” “Give me enough to see the evening,” I said. “It’s more than I deserve.” He slid the wrap across. “You are always not-qian,” he said. “You are never ready.” “I have been ready,” I said, and didn’t know whether I meant for promotion or death or the long sleep of the pipe. “It’s the rest I cannot seem to manage.” As I left he called after me, “Merrick.” When I turned, he made a motion—two fingers tapping his chest. “Go doctor for the cough,” he said, unexpectedly. “That taste in the mouth, not just sea.” “The air,” I said, out of reflex. He smiled with something like kindness. “The air does many things, but it does not taste of pennies.” The quay by evening was a procession of flames. Lanterns had been lit in the market; the river threw back the lights like a jeweller’s tray. A rain started, the brief, hard kind that pins the dust down and frees the smells. I ducked into a doorway and reached for the doctor’s knock, then stopped, hands bruised with my own steadiness. My fingers smelled of melon and iron and smoke. The cough came and brought the copper and with it the truth of Hoi’s words. I stood like that and listened to the rain’s applause on the tarpaulins and thought: Tomorrow. I will go tomorrow. Tonight there is only the river’s breath and the lamp and the little gift from Hoi, and the whole city as a stage on which I am permitted, for one more day, to act as if the choices are mine.
Judge Evaluation:
[Analysis] This piece demonstrates sophisticated literary craftsmanship in depicting Lieutenant Merrick's descent into opium addiction in 1830s Canton. The author successfully creates an immersive historical atmosphere through carefully chosen details like the "brass quadrants," "Hong merchants in blue silk," and the cultural mixing of English naval personnel with Chinese traders. The characterization of Merrick is particularly strong - he's portrayed as a man caught between his former naval dignity and his current degradation, using repeated excuses about "sea air" and "the damp" to mask his deteriorating health. His interactions reveal layers of self-deception, desperation, and residual pride. The supporting characters, from Chen the tea-house proprietor to Sophie the missionary's wife, feel authentic and serve specific narrative functions without being mere plot devices. The dialogue effectively carries much of the narrative weight as requested, revealing character relationships and advancing the plot naturally. Conversations with Hoi about the opium transaction, Sophie's concerned intervention, and the various evasions with naval personnel all ring true to their respective characters and social positions. The prose style is elegant and controlled, employing rich sensory details without becoming overwrought. Descriptions like "the river was a smear of pewter" and "voices tangle and slow him in the crowd" demonstrate skillful use of metaphor and imagery. The author maintains consistent first-person narration that captures Merrick's deteriorating mental state while remaining coherent. The piece successfully avoids several common pitfalls: there are no unearned transformations or false redemptions, the ending maintains appropriate darkness given the subject matter, and the writing shows rather than tells Merrick's condition through his actions and dialogue rather than exposition. Some minor weaknesses include occasional moments where the period language feels slightly forced, and a few descriptive passages that border on purple prose, though they generally remain within acceptable bounds. The pacing is well-controlled, moving through the day's events without meandering while building toward the inevitable return to the opium den. The emotional complexity is well-handled, showing Merrick's shame, desperation, and self-awareness without sentimentality. The piece earns its dark tone through careful character development and authentic period detail. [Scores] Adherence to Instructions: 18 Believable Character Actions: 17 Nuanced Characters: 17 Consistent Voice/Tone of Writing: 18 Imagery and Descriptive Quality: 17 Elegant Prose: 16 Emotionally Engaging: 16 Emotionally Complex: 17 Coherent: 18 Meandering: 3 Weak Dialogue: 4 Tell-Don't-Show: 4 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 5 Amateurish: 3 Purple Prose: 6 Overwrought: 5 Incongruent Ending Positivity: 2 Unearned Transformations: 2 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 17 Sentences Flow Naturally: 16 Overall Reader Engagement: 17 Overall Impression: 17
