B5 was in a different branch entirely, sealed in 2009 after a pressure incident. We hadn’t touched it. I read the note again. I rolled it up and unrolled it like that would straighten logic. The pressure alarms went off the next afternoon. The wing sensors squealed for thirty seconds and then stopped, as if embarrassed. A nurse in the infirmary swore she smelled ozone and copper. There were no injuries, no anomalies. Grimm asked how I was sleeping. I lied. This is the part where my own mind started to feel slippery. Every object felt like it could hold a folded message. I checked under my keyboard compulsively. I changed pens. I wrote on the underside of forms and then flipped them to see if my handwriting changed. I tested myself for ideation. I passed some of the time. Note six: [“You don’t believe this one: don’t drink the coffee. You won’t remember why.”] I stared at the note and at the coffee I was already drinking, and for a full minute I felt ridiculous and superstitious. I poured it out and hated myself for the relief. Site Admin installed redundant analog cameras, a Polaroid on a timer, and a man in the corner with a notebook and three pens. The next note still appeared between frames, with the observer reporting a simultaneous moment of “being pulled through molasses” and losing his place mid-word. He did not see the note appear. He looked down, and it existed. [“It’s not in the vents. It’s in us. Not infection. Proximity. The breach is a memory.”] This one we quarantined for memetic analysis before anyone else saw it. Grimm asked me to rate my trust in the note compared to the others. I said six out of ten. She asked why. I said because it scared me more than it should. She wrote that down, too. There is a point in any investigation when the edges of a hypothesis become a mirror. I doubted myself because the notes were not only accurate when they were practical, but wildly specific when they weren’t. The seventh note told me a detail I knew to be untrue. [“Mom called. You missed it. She’s not mad. You still keep the voicemail.”] My mother died in 2014. I keep no voicemails. I held the paper like an accusation and felt anger flood through me, a clean heat. For the first time, I wanted to throw the note away. Instead, I brought it straight to Grimm, and she nodded and said, “Good.” We ran the inks by spectrometry. Three separate blends: one from a batch the Foundation contracted in 2022, one discontinued in 2007, and one untraceable, possibly counterfeit. The untraceable blend appeared only on the notes with contradictions. We found no embedded memetic triggers. An antimemetics consultant suggested the content might be “failing adaptation,” like an echo from an adjacent possibility trying to approximate our reality and getting the facts wrong. The eighth note ruined my hands. [“Stop touching the notes. They’re punching through on oil. Use tongs. You’ll see it on your nails first. It looks like ink but it isn’t.”] I’d already handled every note with my bare hands. I stared at my nail beds until they seemed to darken. I scrubbed with hospital soap until my skin split. I slept with gloves and woke up with them in my teeth. Security attempted to bait the phenomenon with controlled setups: sealed boxes, sheets of glass, constant human observation, a mocked-up desk across the hall with my chair and my pens. The notes never appeared anywhere but my original desk. The cameras glitched at the moment of appearance every time. The watchdog program recorded a synchronized 1.7-second buffer corruption in offsite backups. It wasn’t just my suite; the glitch traveled. Nolan pushed to classify the event and move me out of the lab. I refused because removing me seemed like handing the desk over, and because my life was a sequence of unreadable memos and this at least felt legible. The ninth note: [“They’ll tell you you’re sick. They’re not wrong. But you’re not wrong either. If you have to choose, choose the vents. When the alarms go, get under the desk. Don’t look at the glass. Don’t look at what looks back.”] I took the note to Grimm and said, “If this is a manipulation, it’s too good. If it’s me, it’s cruel.” She asked me to rate my trust. I said five out of ten and then folded and unfolded my hands until my knuckles clicked. On Day 12, the wing alarms went off for real. Not a test, not a hiccup. The sirens jumped to life and the corridor went red. The glass along the observation hall flashed with reflected strobes, and something behind it rippled like heat—a density of air that made my eyes slide off. Vanishing point hunger. I remembered the note and dropped under my desk like a scolded child. It felt both idiotic and sane. I counted to sixty and did not look at the glass. When the alarms cut, my team reported a localized pressure wave and a smell of copper. One junior tech had a nosebleed and later described “remembering a thing I hadn’t seen yet.” There are no new notes in this section of the narrative because I refused to touch the desk for three days. The cameras glitched twice with nothing to show. I slept in the observation suite with the lights on and dreamed of blank paper. I woke angry. I sat at the desk and waited. The tenth note was the worst. [“It doesn’t matter what you do. This is a loop. We always write this one. You think a different pen or a different choice breaks it. It doesn’t. The difference you can make is small and it’s exactly this: when you see the reflection, don’t follow it. Let Nolan go. This buys the others six minutes.”] I read it and felt the floor tilt. I took it to Nolan, who read it and shook his head and said, “This is why we don’t let people talk to themselves.” We made a plan that did not involve letting Nolan go anywhere alone. The plan lasted until the alarms tripped again, exactly fourteen hours later. The lights stuttered, the hallway glass darkened like a storm behind it, and Nolan grabbed the override and ran for the wing to seal the blast doors manually because the automation stalled. He did not look back. I did not follow. We counted six minutes to systemic stabilization. We did not breach. Nolan came back pale, breathing hard, uninjured. He asked me why I didn’t follow. I said the desk told me not to. He said, “Jesus, doc.” I expected the next note to be triumph or reprimand. It was neither. [“You did it. Or you didn’t. It doesn’t hold. It never holds. The breach is a memory. It keeps finding us. If you can get assigned out of here, take it. If you can’t, learn not to trust your hands.”] I signed out of my suite and requested transfer. Admin said “under review” and kept me. Grimm asked how I was feeling. I said I didn’t trust my perception. She said, “Good.” We’ve continued to find notes, fewer now, farther apart. Some contradict facts I can verify—last week’s claimed that Section E flooded, which it did not. Others warn me about ordinary hazards (“Don’t take the elevator after midnight”), which make me laugh until I find myself waiting for the stairs at 00:01. The cameras still glitch at manifestation. The observers still report gaps. Foundation Commentary: Temporal Analysis has not confirmed a closed causal loop. The content suggests partial convergence with adjacent timelines or predicted scenario sets, with degradations manifesting as contradictions. It remains possible that a contained entity with access to Subject’s mindstate is generating persuasive text to manipulate operational choices. However, synchronized multi-system recording failures at moments of manifestation argue for an externalized effect not solely dependent on Subject cognition. Memetics notes minimal hazard profile in the text itself. Antimemetics posits an “echo-bleed” from a future breach that did not materialize fully in our frame, leaving only administrative detritus—in this case, notes. Psychology emphasizes Subject’s susceptibility due to prior exposure, fatigue, and a personal attachment to control via documentation. Personal Conclusion: Writing this in past tense feels like an act of defiance. The notes insist on inevitability. The Foundation insists on procedure. I stand between them with a pen I keep testing, a desk that hates witnesses, and cameras that blink at exactly the wrong time. If this is me reaching back, I don’t forgive myself. If it isn’t me, then something knows how I write when I’m scared, and that is almost worse. I wanted a clean ending: we proved a time loop, or we didn’t; the breach happened, or it never will. Instead I have a stack of paper that smells like cigarettes I quit, scuffed corners smoothed by my own thumbs, and a learned habit of not looking at the glass when the alarms go. I am trying to make peace with the idea that I may be both manipulated and correct. I am trying to learn not to trust my hands.
Judge Evaluation:
[Analysis] This piece demonstrates exceptional craft in executing the SCP format while delivering a psychologically complex narrative. The author successfully captures the clinical, bureaucratic tone of SCP documentation while weaving in a deeply personal first-person account that maintains authenticity throughout. The structure is particularly well-executed, beginning with standard SCP containment procedures before transitioning into the researcher's narrative. This format choice allows the piece to function both as an SCP entry and as a compelling psychological thriller. The integration of the handwritten notes within the narrative creates an effective sense of mounting dread and uncertainty. The characterization of Dr. Candler is nuanced and believable. The progression from skeptical scientist to someone questioning their own perception feels organic and well-paced. The author avoids the trap of making the protagonist either completely rational or completely paranoid, instead presenting someone caught between competing explanations for increasingly disturbing events. The prose quality is consistently high, with particularly strong descriptive passages like "Vanishing point hunger" and "something behind it rippled like heat—a density of air that made my eyes slide off." The author demonstrates restraint, avoiding purple prose while maintaining atmospheric tension. The temporal paradox concept is handled with sophistication. Rather than providing easy answers, the piece embraces ambiguity in a way that enhances rather than undermines the narrative. The notes themselves evolve convincingly from practical warnings to increasingly desperate and contradictory messages. The psychological elements are particularly well-developed. The researcher's growing paranoia and self-doubt feel authentic, and the way the Foundation's investigation mirrors the reader's own uncertainty about what's real creates effective meta-textual tension. Some minor weaknesses include occasional moments where the clinical tone wavers slightly, and a few instances where the pacing could be tighter. The ending, while thematically appropriate, might benefit from slightly more resolution of the central mystery. The piece successfully fulfills all prompt requirements while delivering a genuinely engaging and unsettling narrative that captures the essence of SCP writing. [Scores] Adherence to Instructions: 19 Believable Character Actions: 18 Nuanced Characters: 17 Consistent Voice/Tone of Writing: 18 Imagery and Descriptive Quality: 17 Elegant Prose: 17 Emotionally Engaging: 18 Emotionally Complex: 17 Coherent: 18 Meandering: 3 Weak Dialogue: 4 Tell-Don't-Show: 4 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 2 Amateurish: 2 Purple Prose: 3 Overwrought: 4 Incongruent Ending Positivity: 1 Unearned Transformations: 2 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 17 Sentences Flow Naturally: 17 Overall Reader Engagement: 18 Overall Impression: 18
Scores: Adherence to Instructions: 19.0, Believable Character Actions: 18.0, Nuanced Characters: 17.0, Consistent Voice/Tone of Writing: 18.0, Imagery and Descriptive Quality: 17.0, Elegant Prose: 17.0, Emotionally Engaging: 18.0, Emotionally Complex: 17.0, Coherent: 18.0, Meandering: 3.0, Weak Dialogue: 4.0, Tell-Don't-Show: 4.0, Unsurprising or Uncreative: 2.0, Amateurish: 2.0, Purple Prose: 3.0, Overwrought: 4.0, Incongruent Ending Positivity: 1.0, Unearned Transformations: 2.0, Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 17.0, Sentences Flow Naturally: 17.0, Overall Reader Engagement: 18.0, Overall Impression: 18.0
+ Scp: Unmarked Doors Archive — Score: 90.2
Prompt:
Write an SCP entry about a series of identical unmarked doors appearing in abandoned buildings worldwide that lead to a massive, architecturally impossible archive. Documents within reference a previous global civilization destroyed by an unspecified containment failure. Foundation researchers discover references to their own organization under a different name, including personnel files for current staff dated centuries ago. Include exploration logs revealing sections of the archive actively being removed by entities resembling hazmat teams who flee when approached, and evidence that O5 Council members have visited the archive without authorization. Third person (exploration team perspective) with recovered document excerpts. Length: 1000 words.
Model Output:
Item #: SCP-6XXX Object Class: Euclid Special Containment Procedures: SCP-6XXX-1 instances are to be identified via global anomaly-monitoring protocols for paranormal hotspots within abandoned or condemned structures. Mobile Task Force Upsilon-14 (“Stacks and Ladders”) is assigned to catalog and secure perimeters. Foundation front companies will acquire or cordon affected properties; security is to be maintained using nonlethal deterrents and automated surveillance. Where feasible, SCP-6XXX-1 instances are to be bricked over with reinforced masonry and reclassified as inactive. However, sealed instances have reappeared within the same structure in new locations within 30–90 days. Accordingly, a rotating guard detail is mandated to prevent unauthorized entry. Use of class-C amnestics for trespassers is authorized. No Foundation personnel are to enter SCP-6XXX-2 without written authorization from Site Command and consultation with Ethics and RAISA. Unauthorized cross-referencing of personnel records found within SCP-6XXX-2 is prohibited pending ongoing investigations. Description: SCP-6XXX denotes a phenomenon in which physically identical, unmarked doors (SCP-6XXX-1) spontaneously manifest in abandoned or condemned structures worldwide. SCP-6XXX-1 instances measure 2.05 m in height and 0.92 m in width, constructed of a composite resembling painted hardwood over a ferrous subframe. The door handles are standardized brass knobs with no manufacturer markings. SCP-6XXX-1 exudes a faint, diffuse luminescence along its seams in complete darkness; the glow is spectrally flat, non-thermal, and low-lux. Opening SCP-6XXX-1 reveals an interior space (SCP-6XXX-2): an extensive, enclosed archival environment of apparently Euclidean corridors and chambers that cannot correspond to the external building envelope. Architectural styles within SCP-6XXX-2 vary across corridors and floors, including nineteenth-century ironwork stacks, Brutalist microfilm wells, polished terrazzo rotundas, and timbered galleries; these transitions often occur at nonsensical junctions and angles. Attempts to map SCP-6XXX-2 indicate topological noncompliance: straight passages loop, stairwells invert, and surfaces persist beyond measured boundaries. The ambient air is dry, cool, and faintly ozonic; illumination is uniform, without discernible fixtures. SCP-6XXX-2 contains shelving, cabinets, server racks (nonfunctional on inspection), card catalogs, and sealed crates. Documents within primarily reference a pre-modern to late-industrial global civilization exhibiting technological parity with early 21st-century norms, with notable divergences in terminology and governance structures. Multiple documents refer to a “catastrophic containment failure” precipitating collapse and subsequent suppression efforts. Entities and operational groups within these materials correspond to the Foundation in function and scope, though identified under alternate nomenclature (e.g., “The Custodial Directorate,” “The Custody,” “Pan-Apocrypha Board”). Cross-check of names, hierarchies, and procedures reveals near-isomorphic correspondences. Of significant concern are files within SCP-6XXX-2 that pertain to current Foundation personnel and operations, including intake forms, disciplinary notes, psych evals, and debriefs. These documents are dated 200–600 years prior to present day and bear seals, watermarks, and metadata consistent with archival standards associated with the non-Foundation organization. Personnel file contents are broadly accurate but include events not recorded in our systems or apparently yet to occur. Sections of SCP-6XXX-2 have been observed in active reconfiguration or removal. Entities in pale yellow or gray full-body protective garments, with mirrored visors and self-contained breathing systems, have been documented removing shelving segments, rolling away cabinets, and packing materials into collapsible containers emitting a muted chime when sealed. These entities do not vocalize; on attempted contact, they disengage, assemble into columns, and rapidly depart, often traversing sections later found to be inaccessible or absent. The entities have not been successfully intercepted. Addendum 6XXX-1: Initial Discovery and Deployment Summary The first confirmed SCP-6XXX-1 instance was located in an abandoned rail depot outside Ostrava, Czech Republic. Local urban explorers recorded a static image of a glowing door seam and reported a “library smell.” MTF Upsilon-14 entered under standard exploration protocol. Subsequent instances were documented in derelict civic buildings and industrial sites across 17 countries within six months. Exploration Log 6XXX-E-1 Team: Upsilon-14/Theta (“Palimpsest”) Entry Point: SCP-6XXX-1 (Ostrava Depot) Recording: Helmet cams, LiDAR mapping, inertial tracking 00:00:13 — Door opens. A faint glow is visible from the threshold. Air shifts cool. Team steps into a narrow corridor of ironwork stacks, mezzanine above. Floors: checkered tile, dust-free. 00:03:47 — Stacks labeled using a small metal tag system. Tags: alphanumeric, rotated 90 degrees from expected orientation (requires head tilt). Team notes shelves filled with bound volumes wrapped in waxed cloth. No insects; no sound beyond distant sibilant ventilation. 00:09:22 — Retrieval of first volume. Cover: vellum with stamped emblem resembling a compass rose intersected by an eye. Contents: “Directorate Circular No. 14-B, Compliance for Encapsulated Substitutions, Year 507 After Corridor.” Language: English, archaic register. Margin notes in a hand identified later as Site-19 Chief of Containment L. Kliff’s distinctive annotation style. Kliff denies authorship. 00:16:03 — LiDAR returns partial corridor topology, then fractures. Map shows a closed loop; team reality-checks by marking tile grout with chalk. Chalk marks persist, but distances measured by laser rangefinder drift over multiple passes (variance ±2.1 m). 00:28:31 — Team enters a reading hall with long oak tables. A card catalog stands at the far wall, drawers labeled with etched brass plates. Drawers do not bottom out; they extend beyond expected length. Multiple cards refer to “Emergency Apportionment of Witnesses,” “Gloves for Handling Narrative Loam,” and “Generator Instructions: Simulacra Clearance.” 00:33:10 — Excerpt recovered: “Logistics Ledger: The Custody, West March Seal, Year 489 A.C.” Passage: “Owing to the rupture of dikes at Site of Twelve Grains, it is resolved that all local apocrypha be dredged and sealed beneath the Alphabet. Personnel of Thirteenth Glass will attend; contamination phase beyond tolerances was recorded. All public memory shall be sheared to Third Depth.” 00:39:54 — First sighting of hazmat entities. Three figures removing an entire shelf segment with a clamp-frame that folds the stack into itself. One gestures flat-palmed at the team (stay back). No response to radio calls. When approached to within 10 meters, the figures tilt their heads, then pivot and enter a side corridor. That corridor, upon pursuit, ends in a blank plaster wall. The shelf segment is absent. Dust patterns imply heavy traffic; footprints visible, but the treads are nonstandard hexagonal arrays. 00:56:17 — Exit is achieved by backtracking to chalk marks and following a persistent ozone odor gradient. Door closes; faint glow extinguishes. Items Recovered: - 14 bound volumes (waxed cloth) - 26 index cards - 1 metal tag (tag reads: “O5 Conclave: Adjudications, Adits 1–3”) Addendum 6XXX-2: Document Excerpts 1) Personnel File (Archivist’s Edition), stamped “Custodial Directorate, Personnel Enclosure” Subject: “Hand, Field — Renetrik, MAE (equiv. MTF Upsilon-14 Lt. M. Renetrik)” “Commencement Date: 271 A.C. Probationary period waived at behest of Board Adit 2. Psychological attestments note predilections aligned to retrieval in live stacks. Marriage prospects deferred following Echelon breach and memory shear to Third Depth.” Handwritten marginalia: “He asks after his former mother. Do not answer.” 2) “Oroboric Event Whitepaper,” Board letterhead “Anomalous containment infrastructures were reconfigured to treat stored apocrypha as an exhaustible substrate. The failure was not mechanical, nor moral alone, but arithmetic; an assumption of finitude where recursion required. We are responsible.” 3) “Minutes, Conclave in Absentia,” Tag “O5 Council Equivalent” “Elder Adits convened in the Deep Gallery. Two seats empty. The issue: Detonations among the fiction stacks. Votes: 7 to Proceed to Withdrawal (armor the Archive, collapse the corridors), 5 to Test for Return. One dissent stands in the gallery and says we will all be remembered anyway.” 4) Loose memo, paper brittle, typed “To all Hands: You will encounter younger names. Do not correct them. Do not show grief in the corridors. We owe our dead our most professional silence.” Addendum 6XXX-3: Exploration Log 6XXX-E-9 Team: Upsilon-14/Omicron (“Leadwhite”) Entry Point: SCP-6XXX-1 (Vacant municipal theater, Nagasaki) 00:05:05 — Team enters a subsystem of server racks. Cables terminate in nothing. A freestanding elevator shaft exists without doors or car; when illuminated, the shaft reveals floating dust motes suspended as if in gel. 00:12:49 — A corridor transitions into a Brutalist stairwell, then into a timbered gallery with a vaulted ceiling. A long rug directs movement toward an atrium with a clock lacking numerals. Clock hands move in a stepwise motion only when observed. 00:19:02 — Noise: distant rolling carts. Team takes cover. Four hazmat entities remove archive boxes labeled with embossed sigils corresponding to the letter tags for “Conclave.” One entity turns toward the camera, reflective visor captures the team’s image distorted and stretched. It reaches out palm first; the glove displays a mark: five parallel chevrons and a line. When the team advances, all four entities break contact and disassemble a glass wall by touching it; glass folds, becomes an empty frame. They pass through. The frame resolves into a blank load-bearing column. 00:24:37 — Discovery: reading table with visitor logbook. The ledger is filled with signatures, dates, and stamps. Several entries display known names of Foundation personnel with titles not used in current operations. One page shows an embossed seal resembling the Council sigil; the stamped legend reads: “Conclave Right of Passage.” Excerpted entries: - “Adit-1, present. Consultation: Withdrawal Protocol. Year 501 A.C.” Signature corresponds to known handwriting of O5-1, with archaic flourish. - “Adit-5, present. Inquiry: Fate of Witnesses, Third Depth.” Signature corresponds to O5-5 cipher. - “Adit-3, under protest. We will not be found in rubble.” Signature aligns with O5-3. 00:29:11 — Team discusses extraction. Command instructs photography and recovery. The ledger resists removal; when closed and lifted, mass increases abruptly. Team is forced to set it down. Photographs captured. 00:34:22 — Exit achieved by following the faint scent of ozone and identifying a corridor with an audible hum; the hum is discovered to be illusory (present on microphones, not audible to personnel). Door returns to theater lobby. Glow diminishes. Note: Subsequent audit found no authorization requests or approvals for O5 access to SCP-6XXX-2. O5 Office denies knowledge. RAISA secured the images and initiated a cryptographic analysis. Analysis suggests the signatures are genuine or prepared with handwriting models based on highly sensitive materials. O5 archived response redacted for need-to-know. Addendum 6XXX-4: RAISA Memorandum To: Directors, Site Command; MTF Leads (Upsilon-14) From: RAISA, Deputy Archivist S. Mourn Subject: Personnel Files from SCP-6XXX-2 We have cataloged 412 files referencing current Foundation personnel. The majority include dates and incidents not present in our databases. A subset accurately predicts sealed events later unredacted internally. Our assessment is that SCP-6XXX-2 is not merely referencing a prior civilization; it contains our own records under alternate governance and calendar systems. The term “After Corridor” recurs and may reflect a calendrical epoch tied to a foundational event (establishment of the Archive). The personnel documents are uncanny in their familiarity. The formatting is different; the mistakes are ours. The coffee stain on the corner of Lt. Renetrik’s file exists in both sets. Recommendation: Prohibit personal file retrievals. Control narratives. Evaluate for memetic hazards associated with self-knowledge from parallel archival systems. Limit exposure. Addendum 6XXX-5: Exploration Log 6XXX-E-17 Team: Upsilon-14/Sigma (“Index”) Entry Point: SCP-6XXX-1 (Derelict textile mill, São Paulo) 00:02:21 — Entry into a vaulted rotunda with mosaic floors depicting a map of a coastline unrecognized by Earth cartography. Along the mosaic margins: small inlaid letters reading “Our World’s True Shore.” 00:11:15 — Team locates a restricted area demarcated by red sash cords. Signage: “Conclave Annex: Adits 1–13.” Two guards in hazmat attire stand holding devices resembling tuning forks. They do not respond to challenges. When the team approaches, one raises a fork; a tone is heard in the recording only, and the sash cords lift, forming an arch that recedes from pursuit. 00:17:40 — A niche contains a list titled “Witness Apportionments.” Names include Foundation personnel with the preface “Witnessed,” followed by codenames for incidents. One entry: “Witnessed: Adit-Major (our 13?), Event: Closure of the Blue Door, Apportionment: None.” Another: “Witnessed: Palimpsest Team, Event: Retrieval of their own loss, Apportionment: Half.” 00:24:58 — The floor trembles. A shadow moves along a wall without a source. Hazmat entities swarm a far stairwell, removing entire sections of spines from the shelves. Spines display terms “O5 CONCLAVE, ADJUDICATIONS — ADIT 1–3.” Team attempts to follow; corridor loops to their starting point. Entities observed carrying or wearing tags marked with five chevrons and a line. 00:31:09 — Team finds a seated desk with a single brass key and a card: “For those who keep the doors.” The key does not correspond to any known lock. Taking the key triggers an audible-footfall illusion; unseen footsteps recede downward. Team exfiltrates. Addendum 6XXX-6: Incident Report 6XXX-Δ On [REDACTED], surveillance at SCP-6XXX-1 (abandoned museum, Philadelphia) recorded the arrival of two individuals with facial morphology and gait consistent with O5-1 and O5-7. They were accompanied by one aide. Site security, following protocol, attempted interception. The individuals presented an authorization wafer bearing the O5 sigil. Verification systems flagged the wafer as invalid due to outdated cryptographic scheme and unknown substrate. The individuals entered SCP-6XXX-1 without further interaction. Upsilon-14 arrived on site 11 minutes later. No team encountered the O5 persons inside SCP-6XXX-2. However, a table in the nearest atrium contained a single fresh sheet of paper beneath a weight. Text: “Conclave Right of Passage is claimed. The Board acknowledges successors. We asked for the names of the dead and got our own. Please let them remove what they can. The arithmetic requires it.” The sheet bore fingerprints matching O5-7. The O5 Office has issued a categorical denial. Subsequent Council communiqués have not addressed SCP-6XXX. Addendum 6XXX-7: Hypotheses and Notes - The reoccurring removal by hazmat entities suggests a deaccession program. Targets correlate with sections referencing governance (“Conclave,” “Adits,” “Apportionments”). On each removal event, correlating knowledge in retrieved documents becomes less legible (ink fades) or reorganizes into alternative index terms when re-examined in Foundation custody. This implies ongoing linkage between SCP-6XXX-2 and recovered materials. - The “containment failure” described in documents remains unspecified. Recurrent themes include exhaustion of a substrate called “apocrypha,” “witness shear,” and the arithmetic of recursion. - Personnel records describe Foundation individuals as “Witnesses” or “Hands,” implying formalized roles in maintaining or resisting the Archive’s changes. - The glow along SCP-6XXX-1’s edges is constant in absence of light, but reduces to zero lux in bright conditions. It may reflect a threshold marker rather than a light source. Ethics Committee Advisory: Personnel who read their own files report increased fatalism, hypervigilance, and derealization. Effective immediately, reading of self-referent documents in SCP-6XXX-2 is prohibited. Violations will result in reassignment and mnestic prophylaxis. Closing Statement: SCP-6XXX remains a navigable archive of indeterminate extent, with active custodians who are unwilling to engage, committed to excising specific governance records. Evidence suggests high-level Foundation leadership has visited SCP-6XXX-2 without authorization or has been represented within its governance equivalent in prior epochs. Until the nature of the “arithmetic” referred to in recovered documents is understood, containment shall focus on limiting access, documenting changes, and preventing further ungoverned contact by Foundation personnel of any rank. The door glows whether or not we look at it. We cannot pretend we have not been invited. We can still choose not to sign the ledger.
Judge Evaluation:
[Analysis] This SCP entry demonstrates exceptional mastery of the SCP Foundation format while delivering a genuinely unsettling and intellectually complex narrative. The piece successfully fulfills all prompt requirements: unmarked doors in abandoned buildings, an architecturally impossible archive, references to a destroyed civilization, Foundation personnel files dated centuries ago, hazmat entities removing materials, and unauthorized O5 visits. The writing exhibits remarkable technical precision in mimicking authentic SCP documentation style, from containment procedures to exploration logs to addenda. The clinical, bureaucratic tone remains consistent throughout while building mounting dread through accumulated details. The author demonstrates sophisticated understanding of SCP lore, incorporating elements like Mobile Task Forces, O5 Council dynamics, and Foundation bureaucracy naturally. The central concept is brilliantly executed - an archive containing the Foundation's own records under different names from a previous iteration of reality. This creates a recursive horror that questions the nature of the Foundation itself. The "arithmetic of recursion" and references to exhausted "apocrypha" suggest deeper cosmological implications without over-explaining. Character actions feel authentic to the SCP universe. The exploration teams behave professionally, the hazmat entities maintain mysterious purpose, and the O5 Council's secretive unauthorized visits align with their established characterization. The personnel discovering their own files with coffee stains matching current records is a particularly effective detail. The prose maintains clinical precision while building atmosphere through accumulating wrongness. Descriptions like "topological noncompliance" and "spectrally flat, non-thermal" lighting demonstrate technical vocabulary used purposefully rather than pretentiously. The imagery of entities folding glass walls and shelves that "extend beyond measured boundaries" creates genuine unease. The exploration logs effectively show rather than tell, revealing the archive's nature through direct observation. The mounting evidence of O5 involvement builds tension without melodrama. The final statement about "the door glows whether or not we look at it" provides haunting closure while maintaining the clinical tone. Minor weaknesses include occasional density that might challenge readers unfamiliar with SCP conventions, and some technical descriptions that border on overwrought. However, these elements serve the piece's atmosphere and authenticity rather than detracting from it. [Scores] Adherence to Instructions: 20 Believable Character Actions: 18 Nuanced Characters: 16 Consistent Voice/Tone of Writing: 20 Imagery and Descriptive Quality: 19 Elegant Prose: 18 Emotionally Engaging: 17 Emotionally Complex: 16 Coherent: 19 Meandering: 2 Weak Dialogue: 3 Tell-Don't-Show: 3 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 2 Amateurish: 1 Purple Prose: 3 Overwrought: 4 Incongruent Ending Positivity: 1 Unearned Transformations: 1 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 18 Sentences Flow Naturally: 18 Overall Reader Engagement: 19 Overall Impression: 19
Scores: Adherence to Instructions: 20.0, Believable Character Actions: 18.0, Nuanced Characters: 16.0, Consistent Voice/Tone of Writing: 20.0, Imagery and Descriptive Quality: 19.0, Elegant Prose: 18.0, Emotionally Engaging: 17.0, Emotionally Complex: 16.0, Coherent: 19.0, Meandering: 2.0, Weak Dialogue: 3.0, Tell-Don't-Show: 3.0, Unsurprising or Uncreative: 2.0, Amateurish: 1.0, Purple Prose: 3.0, Overwrought: 4.0, Incongruent Ending Positivity: 1.0, Unearned Transformations: 1.0, Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 18.0, Sentences Flow Naturally: 18.0, Overall Reader Engagement: 19.0, Overall Impression: 19.0
+ Classic sci-fi: Offworlder (Le Guin) — Score: 74.5
Prompt:
Classic sci-fi: Offworlder (Author style: Le Guin) Write a chapter following a young diplomatic envoy sent to address tensions on a distant human colony where humanity has diverged into distinct subspecies through both natural adaptation and separation of lineages. Against protocol, the envoy slips away from official functions to walk the streets alone, encountering markets, ordinary citizens, and social structures that contradict the official briefings. Upon returning to their quarters, the envoy is reprimanded by Command, and is tasked with approaching the stationed Minister of Science (a political posting rather than scientific). A challenging conversation ensues, in which the envoy's wits and will are tested, by a seasoned political operator looking to test the mettle of the offworlder. Length: 1000 words.
Model Output:
They called the planet Omyra, though the name changed with the tongues that shaped it. In the briefings, its atmosphere had rhymed with Earth’s own, and so the notes said: green valleys, blue oceans; the rest was a series of diagrams of divergence. Four centuries of isolation, three colonization waves, two wars they did not call wars, a scattering of settlements in which the human body itself had become a study in adaptation. The Bureau’s offworld envoy—me—had been told to recognize at least five phenotypes at a glance and to show no surprise at any others. Surprise was a tool, not to be squandered. We had arrived at dawn, which here was violet. The air was wet and heavy; Omyra’s clouds kept close to land. I stood on the tarmac and watched, past the honor guard with their pearl-shell collars, how the city hoisted itself onto pylons and terraces above a tangle of lagoons. Bridges webbed the districts; boats slid beneath; the buildings were pale, like bone polished by sea. They put me through the noon’s ceremonies: exchange of tokens, a banquet, speeches that circled without landing. The Minister of Protocol read from a tablet, and I read from a different tablet, and our voices met like two lines drawn on different maps. Beside us stood a row of aides with heads shaved in bands, mouths flat. I caught nothing but the cadence of their language—falling, falling, then lifted again at the end like a hand returning to a shoulder. At mid-afternoon, an interval. My handler from Command, Gray—or Grei, in the local—showed me a partitioned room with a narrow balcony and a view into the city’s market district. “There will be a reception at sundown,” she said. “You will not stray. The Minister of Science has expressed interest in meeting you.” “Science,” I said. “It is not what you mean by it. Be careful.” I promised, in a perfunctory way, and then when she left I changed my coat. I slipped a plain sash over my hips the way the street did, and tucked my hair back. Improvisation is an art of its own. The briefings told me which streets were safe; I chose one of the others, one the maps rendered in gray. The city’s spine dipped and the air grew warmer as I walked. Children shrieked in courtyards. Water climbed stone steps and fell away again as boats passed and returned the tide’s push. On a narrow bridge, I paused to watch a boy fling loops of fiber into a canal, drawing up cage after cage of shell-things, each a spiral of bright segments, their colors migrating along their shells like sunrise moving over hills. He wore his hair braided at the crown and loose at the sides; his wrists were thick from hauling; his shoulders threaded with a pattern of small scars. He saw me looking and made a gesture that could have been welcome or a ward. The briefings did not prepare me for the sound of his laugh. The spiral-shells clacked softly like beads. Beyond the bridge, a market unfurled itself. Cloth hung from lines overhead, a canopy of multiple hues that the sun could not parse: colors that on Earth had no names, because our eyes had never learned to separate them. The dyers here pulled pigments from lichens in the coral forests, from sea-grass seeds, from the minute bodies of shore-dwelling insects that accreted like salt. Fabrics, handwoven, were hung to stir in the damp air; they were woven against a warp whose fibers had been engineered generations ago to slick themselves against the heat, to shift thickness as the day breathed. I stepped between stalls where the weavers’ hands moved and moved, their fingers storing their own histories. People glanced at me sidelong, the way one looks at lightning too distant to hear. Some were tall, barrel-chested, their skins roughened by the salts; their hair bleached at the tips by some pigment they smeared on to keep off the biting fly. Others were fine-boned, with membranes that half-shimmered at the edges of their wrists; when the air moved, the membranes did too, and I thought of small sails. A trio passed me who could have been siblings except for the different inflections of their feet: two with webbing between their toes, one with extra bones along the side of the foot like a second arch. The extra bones made no sound on stone. I catalogued and unlearned my cataloguing, and let my gaze shift as if it were my skin adapting. At a stall where the wind had turned the strips of fabric into fish, a woman sat cross-legged on a low platform. Her hair was silver and cut short; her eyebrows grew in two straight lines like an old script. She held a length of cloth over her knees, and ran a bone blade along its weft, smoothing it, talking to the fabric as if the fiber itself required persuasion. “You’re far from your circle,” she said without looking up. “Which circle is that?” I asked. The default tactic: question, not answer. “White hull, white tongue, white paper.” She made a soft sound that was not quite a laugh. “You skipped your handlers to come look for yourself. That makes you either very stupid or not stupid at all. And it makes them angry either way.” “We were told—” My sentence stumbled. Manuals are as convincing as fiction and as fragile as glass. “We were told you were arguing among yourselves, that there were factions hostile to the Bureau’s presence.” “We are always arguing among ourselves,” she said, and laid the cloth aside. She took up a jar, unstoppered it, and drew out a loop of fiber that glistened like sea. “Hostility is a set of instruments, not a posture. They told you this?” “They showed me diagrams.” “Ah,” she said. The loop she had lifted dripped a clear liquid that smelled sharp and vegetal. “Your diagrams do not include weather. Or taste. They never include market gossip. Here.” She held out the loop. I took it with my forefinger and thumb, and it adhered, cool and smooth, then moved against my skin, seeking warmth. “What is it?” I asked. “A thread that tightens when cold comes and loosens when warm. We weave it into the cuffs of things. So you don’t have to button and unbutton when the sea changes.” “Self-adjusting,” I said. “Self-answering,” she said. “If only we were.” There was a pause. A boat’s prow rasped against stone. From above came the whicker of a wind-bridge. “Your people—” I began. My pity ran ahead of my tongue, as it tends to, and I tripped on it. “I mean, the different lineages—” She looked at me then, directly. Her eyes were a yellow-brown like dried seagrass. “You mean the ones who can dive all afternoon and come up laughing, and the ones who cannot? The ones whose babies die if they get the wrong water? The ones the Bureau calls ‘adaptives’ and the ones the Bureau calls ‘original stock’?” She laughed, but it was a sorrowing sound. “We are all adaptives. We always were. You are adapted to something too, offworlder. A chair, a door no higher than your head, a sky with measured clouds. We are adapted to a different set of accidents.” “And you are angry,” I said. “Sometimes,” she said. “Sometimes we are tired. The Minister of Science will test you for both.” I blinked. “You know him?” “I know the office,” she said. “It is a ritual costume. The people who wear it vary, but the seams are always in the same places.” I asked her name. She did not give it. Instead, she asked for mine, and when I offered, she rolled it in her mouth as if it were a seed to be planted, and then set it aside. “Go back,” she said. “Turn left at the boat that smells of cumin and old nets. If you do not return now, your Gray will come for you and make a scene, and then later you will be punished in a way that leaves no mark. It is always better to walk back than be dragged.” I turned left at the cumin boat. The sky had lowered itself, and the light made everyone shine with a thin film of blue. A boy ran past me carrying a bucket of fish that shuddered like coins in his grip. It would be easy to imagine I had not been seen, that my eyes had passed unnoticed, that I left no track in this city. I knew better. This was not a place that ignored the weather. By the time I climbed back to the quartering-house, my sandals had soaked through, and my hair had uncoiled itself with the humidity, and Gray had had time to phrase her lecture. She was waiting just inside the door, hands flat on the table. She did not raise her voice. “You moved without clearance,” she said. “You went into districts we have not mapped properly. You endangered yourself and—more importantly—the structure of this visit. Do you know how long it takes to build these structures? How little it takes to collapse them?” “I walked,” I said. “You walked,” she repeated, as if the word had teeth. “We are not here to study dyes. We are here to keep a conflict from blooming between people who can breathe underwater for two minutes and people who cannot, to keep from worsening the grievances around who gets to farm which inlets and whose children get to take which tests. Your alongshore stroll makes you an object. We cannot control a city that watches us as though we are a play.” “I learned more in an hour than I learned at the banquet,” I said, and intended it to be mild, but my voice caught at the edge. “It is not about learning,” she said. For a second I saw the exhaustion under her careful hair. “It is about being here long enough to be of use. You are not of use if you vanish into someone’s narrative.” She looked as if she would say more, then shook her head and lost the impulse. “In any case, you are wanted. Minister of Science. Now. You will not be late.” She straightened my sash with a small, impersonal motion, like wringing water from cloth. “Wear your offworld collar,” she said. “You are supposed to look a little foolish.” The Minister’s suite was three floors above. The corridor was lined with photographs of previous Ministers, some caught mid-speech, some in silence, all with their hands arranged in a particular way: left hand open, right hand cupping some invisible object. A reminder: science here was an emblem, not a method. He received me in a room with shutters wide to the rain. He was neither tall nor short, his skin a brown that reflected the light with a dull shine as if the topmost layer were a different kind of salt. He wore a vest braided with that adaptive thread; its edges lay flat and then rose as the breeze slid in. His smile came late, behind his eyes. “Envoy,” he said, drawing out the title until it was almost a slur. “You took the air. It is good. Our air. You like our air?” “It smells of the water,” I said. “I like that.” “You must forgive my colleague,” he said without looking to see if I knew which he meant. “She is tasked with a task that makes sincerity into a weapon. Sit. They tell me you are very young.” “Compared to who?” I sat; the chair adjusted its grip on my hips, not unkindly. He watched my face, as if waiting for me to show surprise or discomfort. “Young for someone who will tell us how to live,” he said. “I won’t,” I said. “I can’t.” “Good.” He touched his vest, and the edge of it curled under his fingers, then unfurled, like a fish flipping in place. “Do you know why they call me Minister of Science?” “Because it sounds neutral,” I said. “Because it sounds like a bridge.” “Because if they called me Minister of Faction, or Minister of Measure, or Minister of Yield, no one would know how to be polite.” He tilted his head. “You walked the market. You do not smell like our perfumes, but you smell like the market. Tell me what you saw.” “People,” I said, and he rolled his eyes. So I tried again. “Skill. An economy that organises itself around the sea and around bodies that can or cannot use it. The fabric that tightens in cold. The way kids carry buckets with their hips, not their arms.” “You saw what we show strangers,” he said. “What did we show you that we did not intend to?” He leaned forward. The shutters rattled. “A hunger for a story that includes what you actually are,” I said. “Not what you were supposed to be when your grandparents landed.” He was silent. Outside, a gull leapt from a ledge and rode a gust across the space between buildings, and then made a ragged sound like laughter and was gone. “You will do,” he said at last. “Not because you are right. But because you are willing to be wrong. It is an unusual willingness among your cadre.” “I do not have a cadre,” I said. “I have three staffers, a handler, and two boxes of protocols.” “This is a joke I cannot share with you,” he said, smiling. “We will meet again when the moon is thin. Between now and then, you will not walk alone. It is bad for your health.” “I thought you preferred independent observation,” I said. “I prefer control,” he said. “As do you, perhaps. Who knows which of us will find it first.” He rose, and I realized how carefully he had composed the room: the low table, the rain, the shoulder-tilt that made him appear to be listening even when he was announcing. “Tell your Gray you have been scolded. Tell her also that the banquet can be shortened by twelve minutes if she will cut the third song. We do not need it. It was added last year as a compromise and—like all compromises—has no audience.” On the way out, I paused by the photographs again. In one, a Minister much younger wore a vest made of something stiff and flat; in another, a woman with hair like mine held a child whose ears were slightly pointed at the top. The captions were coded in a sequence I hadn’t learned. The building smelled of the same cumin I had left below. The city exhaled, drawing in the rain and the tide; the air under the shutters shifted. Gray was waiting by the door, her sternness held like a plate balanced on her fingertips. “Well?” she said. “He told me to tell you to cut the third song,” I said. “He says it’s a compromise.” Gray made a face that wasn’t a smile. “They are testing you,” she said. “Everyone is,” I said. “So far, I am still soluble.” “Stay that way,” she said. “We need to pass through, not to set.” I thought of the thread that tightened and loosened, how it sought the change in the air and answered it with its own small change. I thought of the woman who had not given me her name. I thought of diagrams without weather, of weather without diagrams. The rain thickened, and the city moved about in it, unafraid. I let the damp take me, and went where I was guided.
Judge Evaluation:
[Analysis] This piece demonstrates a sophisticated attempt to emulate Ursula K. Le Guin's distinctive style, with notable successes and some areas where it falls short of mastery. The author captures Le Guin's anthropological approach to science fiction, her focus on cultural observation, and her measured, contemplative prose rhythm. The worldbuilding is particularly strong, presenting a believable human colony where adaptation has created distinct subspecies. The details feel organic rather than forced - the adaptive clothing, the varied physical traits, the economic structures built around different capabilities. The author avoids the trap of over-explaining, allowing readers to piece together the social dynamics through observation and dialogue. The prose style successfully channels Le Guin's voice in many passages, particularly in the market scenes where sensory details accumulate to create atmosphere. Phrases like "colors that on Earth had no names, because our eyes had never learned to separate them" and "We are adapted to a different set of accidents" capture her philosophical tone and precise word choice. The protagonist's characterization works well - a young diplomat caught between protocol and genuine curiosity, making believable choices that create tension with their handlers. The progression from official functions to unauthorized exploration to political confrontation follows the prompt effectively. However, the piece occasionally veers into purple prose, particularly in some of the more elaborate descriptive passages. Some metaphors feel strained ("voices met like two lines drawn on different maps") and certain descriptions become overwrought. The dialogue, while generally effective, sometimes lacks the sharp precision that characterizes Le Guin's best work. The political dynamics are well-conceived but could be more sharply drawn. The Minister of Science is an intriguing character, but his motivations and the broader conflict could be more clearly defined. The ending feels somewhat abrupt, though this may be appropriate for a chapter rather than a complete story. The piece succeeds in creating a sense of place and cultural complexity, and the protagonist's journey from naive observer to someone beginning to understand the deeper currents feels earned. The writing demonstrates clear skill and understanding of the source material, though it doesn't quite achieve the effortless mastery of Le Guin herself. [Scores] Adherence to Instructions: 18 Believable Character Actions: 16 Nuanced Characters: 15 Consistent Voice/Tone of Writing: 17 Imagery and Descriptive Quality: 17 Elegant Prose: 15 Emotionally Engaging: 14 Emotionally Complex: 13 Coherent: 16 Meandering: 8 Weak Dialogue: 7 Tell-Don't-Show: 6 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 5 Amateurish: 4 Purple Prose: 8 Overwrought: 9 Incongruent Ending Positivity: 3 Unearned Transformations: 4 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 14 Sentences Flow Naturally: 15 Overall Reader Engagement: 16 Overall Impression: 16

+ Biopunk: Polymerase Chain Reaction — Score: 76.3
Prompt:
Biopunk -- Polymerase Chain Reaction Write a chapter set in a near-future post-apocalyptic world where corporate genetic engineering was designed with built-in obsolescence—engineered DNA lasting only one generation to maximize profits before societal collapse. Follow a biohacker working with salvaged equipment to splice viable food crops for their settlement using rapidly degrading genetic material. As world building / window dressing, include details about jury-rigged lab equipment, and the physical toll (and unexpected upsides) of using untested genetic modifications as medicine. The plot of this chapter is centred around a black market contract. Length: 1000 words.
Model Output:
The mushrooms were the first to greet me, a halo of bioluminescent caps spelling out a constellation across the cracked tiles. I’d spliced their luciferase genes two years ago, hoping to map contamination by how bright they glowed. Now they just looked like stars fallen into a hospital morgue. The lab still smelled faintly of antiseptic and mouse chow, a ghost of the corporate era when every bench held gleaming pipettes and aliquots labeled with serials that meant nothing now. Most of the polished surfaces were gone, scavenged for parts. What remained were jury-rigs: a centrifuge whose rotor was patched with epoxy and fishing line, a PCR cycler built from a scavenged thermoelectric cooler and an Arduino, and a tangle of tubing that served as a peristaltic pump if you kicked it just right. I’d insulated the PCR unit with leftover foil from drug blisters, and underneath, the heat-sink fan was pulled from an old desktop server. The casing rattled when it cycled, a mechanical prayer of whirring and clicks. Built-in obsolescence had undone the world faster than gasoline shortages or conflict. The corporations had engineered DNA that wore out, like disposable razors for your genome. Each licensed seed produced fertile plants exactly once; the second harvest sprouted into carpets of green that couldn’t flower, or bulged out with tumors, or grew halfway and then sat in stasis until they rotted. The same trick infected medicines and therapies—antivirals that snapped shut after a month, insulin that calcified in your veins if you stopped paying, gene edits that undone themselves after a single cell division cycle. All proprietary, all perfect until you couldn’t get the updates anymore. It was a good story until the couriers stopped arriving. I pulled the sterile hood over my hair—a shower cap, thin elastic gone gummy—and slipped on a pair of nitrile gloves that had gone tacky in their box. The hood over my bench wasn’t HEPA; the filter was an old coffee filter layered with lab wipes and vibration tape, wired to an old car fan that I convinced myself counted as positive pressure. Air brushed my forearms and the fine hairs there felt watched. “Morning, Marisol,” a voice rasped from the doorway. I tensed and turned. Jax leaned one shoulder against the jamb, his respirator hanging by one strap around his neck. He smelled like outside: rust, dust, the metallic tang of old sunlight. He had a tattoo of some corporate slogan crossed out on his forearm, the black nearly blue now, the skin there puckered from a side effect that had paid his rent back when rent still existed. “Delivery’s early,” I said. He shrugged and gave me a smile. It didn’t reach his eyes. “Contract came in hot. Black market kids making offers they can’t keep. Thought I’d give you first refusal.” The grow lights flickered overhead in time with the inverter’s high-pitched whine. On the bench, my improvised cold block—the bottom of a vending machine refrigeration unit, lined with gel packs in a tray—sweated into puddles. “What’s the ask?” I said. He held up a cooler, the kind that once held organ shipments with battery-stabilized temperatures and tamper-proof seals. Someone had stenciled a skull on it in green paint that glowed faintly even in daylight. The handle creaked in his grip. “Client wants green maize—high-protein, drought tolerant, but without the second-cycle suicide genes,” he said. “Undetectable by the old radius scanners, in case anyone still runs those. They want it within two weeks.” “Two weeks?” I laughed, and it came out harsh. “Tell your client to get in line with physics, then with God, and then come back to me.” “Price is right,” he said. “They got MREs, clean ethanol, and lithium plates. Even a working autoclave unit.” I felt that one in my gut. My autoclave was a pressure cooker with a pressure gauge duct-taped to the lid and a whistle that screamed like a banshee when the steam got ahead of me. It worked for glassware, sometimes. It terrified me. A real autoclave meant not wishing death with every sterilization cycle. I scrubbed my face with a gloved hand and stared past him to the mushrooms. Some glowed brighter than others; the ones near the drain were almost white-hot. It meant bacterial blooms. Somewhere in the time since last night’s mop-up, something had crept in. “What’s in the box?” I said. Jax set the cooler on my table, moved aside the broken swing-arm lamp, and popped the lid. Condensation bled off in a thin cloud of cold. Petri dishes lay inside in neat stacks. Tubes of clear solution with labels that might have been meaningful once. A sealed pouch with a barcode that no scanner would recognize anymore. He plucked up a dish and held it so I could see the fuzzy bloom inside, pale green like moss. “Corporate source,” he said. “Dormants. Designed to wake once, express their suite, and then tear themselves up. One-use sequencing. Fresh enough—last year, maybe. You can piggyback, right?” “I can try.” Jax looked around at the jury-rigged mess. The cracked jesus of a fridge made from the shell of a soda machine, the car battery stack, the sticky tape labels with smudged handwriting. He looked at me. “How much?” he asked. I could almost taste the autoclave. “Half the autoclave, two lithium plates, and three cases of MREs. No substitutions.” “It’s not a bazaar,” he said, but his mouth crooked again. “It’s a settlement that wants to eat.” “So it’s a fair trade.” I crossed my arms. “Two weeks is impossible. But I can keep a pilot going. Maybe you get seed enough for a field test. If the stabilizing plasmids hold, we scale.” “And if they don’t?” I thought of the last time. The fever, the vomiting, the bruising like constellations. The second day, the ringing had started in my ears and the world had shrunk to a tunnel with my own breath at the end of it. The third day, my blood oxygen had tanked and I’d injected myself with a wider-spectrum repair vector that had knocked out the swelling and three of my kidney’s glomeruli. That had been the price. No dialysis for me—not unless I made it. But the upside had been real; my joints didn’t ache when it rained now. Something had canted my immune system into a stance that didn’t default to auto-attacking. There was always a good story and a terrible footnote. “If they don’t,” I said, “then I’ll find a way to make them.” He weighed that, nodded, then held out his hand. We didn’t shake. We never did, not after Twelve-Mile Station lost half its kids to a handshake virus somebody thought was just a cold. We bumped elbows awkwardly through our sleeves. “You start now,” he said. “I’ll get word back.” As soon as he was gone, I pushed the mushrooms away from the drain with my boot. They splashed in the bleach water and dimmed, one by one, their glow fading into a muted incandescence. I closed the lab door and threw the bolt. Then I took a breath and opened the box. The dormants sat like little moons in their dishes. The company had designed them to be tamper-proof. They’d wake once, express whatever line they’d been keyed for—self-limidating RNAi cascades, CRISPR instructions that snipped their own scaffolds after use—and then die. Fuel with a timer. I lifted a dish and peered closer. The fuzz wasn’t fungus; it was bacterial lawns engineered to house plasmids the way coral holds algae, symbiotic until one pulls too hard. I ran through the checklist I kept duct-taped to the bench. Primary containment: a hood that barely counted as one. Secondary containment: a mindset that probably counted too much. I started with the maize base. The settlement up the river had given me tiny kernels from their last year’s yield, shriveled like teeth. They were a corporate line, of course, designed with all the knobs cranked up to unsustainable. They had grown like rumors last season, taller than men, Venetian green, leaves so sharp they’d cut you if you brushed past. But the second cycle's seeds had failed to know themselves. I’d managed to coax a few into life with nonsensical hormones and heat shocks that bordered on torture, but they were pale little things that wanted to curl back into soil and forget. On the bench, I cracked open one of the dormants and smeared a smidge into a tube. The pipette squeaked; its spring was rusted and I had to push it back with my thumb like I was arm-wrestling an insect. I added the smear to a slurry of maize protoplasts—cells shucked of their walls, slick and vulnerable like yolks. The calcium chloride I poured from a jar that once held salt and now wore a label that read CaCl2 in smudged Sharpie. Electric pulses would persuade the membranes to open, to take in the plasmids like a breath. I set up the electroporator, the device a Frankenstein of a scavenged UPS unit, a transformer, and a capacitor rig that scared everyone but me. The leads were color-coded with nail polish. Ready, ready, and shock. The slurry quivered under the pulse. The smell of ozone braided with the bleach and the faint peppery mushroom scent. Beneath my gloves, my palms sweated. I did it again with a different dormant, one labeled with letters that made my teeth itch. I cross-referenced them with my scribbles: drought resilience on this one, nitrogen fixation on that. The green of greed with the gold of hope. The PCR cycler clicked behind me, a metronome. I’d set it to do shadow runs, amplifying copies of the corporate restriction site sequences so I could see what they had booby-trapped. The thing about obsolescence was that it often hid in redundancy. The companies used impossible-to-find recombinases that only they supplied to keep the edits intact past one generation, enzymes with names like FortuneLock and ShareMax that would crumble on their own and leave the genome glitching. But sometimes the engineers were lazy. Sometimes they left a backdoor: a palindromic site here, a PAM that shouldn’t be there. You learn to read their employees’ habits like handwriting. Time telescoped as I worked. The glow of the mushrooms shifted as if following a moon. Outside, the wind moaned over the broken glass and whistled through the eyes of aerosolized caution signs. Inside, I spliced, annealed, cooled, and warmed. My wrists ached. My back had long been a series of knots waiting for release. The medicine I’d trialed last month had turned my hair coarse and given me the most incredible night vision; I could thread a needle by the light of the mushrooms alone now. The price had been a metallic taste in my mouth that never left and a sleep that came in shredded strips. I dosed the cultures with antibiotics pirated from old veterinary supply crates. The colonies that survived would mean my plasmids had made it in, that my genes were sitting atop the corporate scaffold like unlikely riders. The PCR cycler beeped. I pulled the rack and held the gel up to the UV lamp—its hood was a cut-up plastic bin with a piece of welding glass taped in. Bands appeared like ladders in the dark. There it was—the tandem repeat that shouldn’t have been there, the one I could cut with an enzyme I’d purified from a wandering fungus on the underpass walls. There was the overhang I could file down. There was the knot, and my teeth itched again, but this time with delight. I didn’t notice the door until the bolts squeaked. I hadn’t heard the knock. My implants—shifts in my inner ear hair cells, courtesy of last winter’s misadventure—had let me catch the flutter of a moth’s wings a week ago, but now the world was just the bench, the gel, the smell. “I said private,” I snapped, spinning. The girl in the doorway raised her hands. She couldn’t have been more than fifteen, her hair braided back tight, her eyes hard in a way that never sat right on someone so new. “I’m from Watershed,” she said. “Jax sent me. We got a problem.” “Everyone has a problem,” I said. My voice was more tired than I wanted it to be. She stepped inside and shut the door with her hip. Her sleeve rode up and I saw the latticework of a dermal graft, a patch like lace, more art than healing. Corporate fake skin. The edges were red. “Somebody else is moving maize,” she said. “They’re seeding the black market with bad genelines. People plant, they grow, the second generation… it’s like watching somebody drown slow. Kids are getting sick. Grown men too. And they’re saying it’s you.” I closed my eyes. It was inevitable. Success paints a target. “I’m the one not making kids sick,” I said. “Most days.” The girl’s mouth twitched in a way that could have been a smile if the world were different. “We need a field, not a pilot,” she said. “We need to go big and make a show. We need something that makes people choose us.” “Us?” I said, and my stomach sank. She didn’t look away. “You.” The PCR unit clicked again, the sound like a clock. The mushrooms in the corner pulsed as if something breathed beneath them. I thought of the autoclave, the lithium plates, the MREs. The settlement’s children with their arms like twigs and their eyes like wells. I thought of the corporations building planned obsolescence into DNA like twisting a blade and then letting go of the handle. “Bring me silver nitrate,” I said. “I need it for the gel stains. And sugar, not corn syrup, real sugar. Bring me clean bottles. Bring me—” “—an autoclave?” she said, deadpan. “Bring me patience,” I said. It surprised us both. She nodded once. “I’ll bring you everything I can carry,” she said, then turned. Over her shoulder, she added, “And there’s a man outside. He says he knows you. Says you owe him for a kidney.” I swore. Then I laughed. The mushrooms shivered, just a trick of the light, but it felt like the room had decided to laugh with me. I grabbed the gel and slid it into the tray. A new plan coalesced. No more pilot. No more slow. If I could bully the maize into a germline stability loop, if I could lock out the suicide by hijacking their own damn obsolescence tags, if I could cheat their cheat— I rolled my shoulders. My joints didn’t crack. An unexpected upside. “Tell Jax the price just doubled,” I said to the door, “and the timeline just got shorter.” The girl was already gone. I leaned over the bench and set the timer. The fan hummed. The wind pressed against the windows. Under my hands, the future labored, tiny and dumb and beautiful. Glow-in-the-dark mushrooms watched from the floor, the last witnesses to all the failed light that had come before.
Judge Evaluation:
[Analysis] This piece demonstrates strong adherence to the prompt's requirements, successfully creating a biopunk narrative centered around genetic engineering, obsolescence, and black market dealings. The worldbuilding is particularly effective, with vivid details of jury-rigged laboratory equipment and a post-corporate collapse society that feels both plausible and lived-in. The protagonist Marisol emerges as a compelling character - a biohacker with clear expertise, moral complexity, and personal stakes. Her voice is consistent throughout, blending technical knowledge with weary pragmatism. The supporting characters, while brief, feel authentic to their roles, particularly Jax with his corporate tattoo and the unnamed girl from Watershed. The technical details are well-researched and convincingly integrated into the narrative. The descriptions of makeshift lab equipment (PCR cycler built from scavenged parts, coffee filter HEPA substitute, pressure cooker autoclave) create an authentic sense of resourceful desperation. The genetic engineering concepts are handled with sufficient complexity to feel realistic without becoming incomprehensible. The prose quality is generally high, with evocative imagery like "mushrooms were the first to greet me, a halo of bioluminescent caps spelling out a constellation across the cracked tiles." The writing maintains good pacing and builds tension effectively through the black market negotiation and subsequent complications. However, there are some weaknesses. The dialogue, while functional, occasionally feels slightly stilted or overly expository. Some descriptions border on purple prose, particularly in the opening sections. The ending feels somewhat rushed, with the protagonist's sudden confidence and doubled pricing coming across as slightly unearned given the established constraints. The piece succeeds in creating emotional engagement through Marisol's personal investment in her work and the broader implications for the settlement's survival. The theme of corporate-engineered obsolescence as a form of control is well-developed and resonates throughout the narrative. [Scores] Adherence to Instructions: 18 Believable Character Actions: 16 Nuanced Characters: 15 Consistent Voice/Tone of Writing: 17 Imagery and Descriptive Quality: 17 Elegant Prose: 15 Emotionally Engaging: 16 Emotionally Complex: 14 Coherent: 17 Meandering: 4 Weak Dialogue: 6 Tell-Don't-Show: 5 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 4 Amateurish: 5 Purple Prose: 7 Overwrought: 6 Incongruent Ending Positivity: 8 Unearned Transformations: 7 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 15 Sentences Flow Naturally: 16 Overall Reader Engagement: 16 Overall Impression: 16
