+ Iteration 3 — Avg Score: 82.2
+ Classic sci-fi: The Azra Gambit — Score: 83.0
Prompt:
Classic sci-fi (Author style: Asimov) The Azra Gambit Colonial mars is being mined by corporations who take leases on indentured labourers. The thing they are mining is Azra, a recently discovered exotic metal which accelerates radioactive decay to such a rate that it is greatly sought after for interstellar drives and weapons alike. This has created both a gold rush and an arms race as various interests vie for control and endeavour to unlock Azra's secrets. The story follows Arthur Neegan, a first generation settler and mining engineer. Upon discovering that his unassuming plot sits atop an immense Azra vein, he is subjected to a flurry of interest and scrutiny. Write the next chapter in this story, in which an armed retinue descends on Arthur's home and politely but forcefully invites him to a meeting with some unknown party off-world. The insignia look like that of the Antares diplomatic corp -- diplomatic in name only. Arthur finds himself in the centre of a political tug of war. The chapter involves a meeting with this unknown party, who makes Arthur an offer. The scene should be primarily dialogue, interspersed with vivid description & scene setting. It should sow hints of the larger intrigue, stakes & dangers. Include Asimov's trademark big-and-small-picture world building and retrofuturistic classic scifi vibe. The chapter begins with Arthur aboard the transfer vessel, wondering just what he's gotten involved in. Length: 1000 words.
Model Output:
The transfer vessel's air filtration system hums with a persistent mechanical drone. It isn't loud; it's old. It had the timbre of equipment that had never failed in service, and so had never been replaced. The same could be said, Arthur thought, of a certain type of Martian settler. He watched the spill of ocher through the narrow viewport as Cydonia diminished to a bruise-colored knuckle—and then to nothing at all as the shuttle rolled. The curve of Mars slew into view: a rusted coin edged in pale fog. He had left a kettle on his counter, warm broth that would congeal by the time someone—if anyone—came to check. "Comfortable?" asked the woman across from him. She had an accent you could only get by talking to thirty people a day on ten planets: something smooth, something cutting, something taught to be pleasant. She wore the Antares crest at her lapel in matte silver, starburst swallowed by the stylized chevrons of authority. Diplomatic Corp. Diplomatic enough to bring four armored escorts into his mechanics bay this morning and produce a warrant with his name spelled correctly. "As comfortable as a man can be," Arthur said. He sat straight-backed because the seat forced him to, his knees touching the case at his feet. "You didn't tell me where we're going." "Low orbit," she said. "Then a transfer. You've been granted access to a secure facility. Not everyone gets to see those." "Not everyone has a mine worth a war," Arthur said. Her lips curled, not unkindly. "Especially when 'mine' is a legal fiction." Arthur's own lapel held a little copper pin. It was shaped like a shovel, and had been a teenage joke; later, after the Great Leasing Act, it had become political. His father had traded a century of service for a plot that fit in a photograph. Edges of property were lines drawn in regolith with sticks and argued over in courtrooms with scarves and wool coats. "We'll see," he said, mostly to himself, mostly to drown out the hum. They made docking on the second try. The vessel had the elegant look of a thing that had never had to prove itself: a spindle with swept fins that were only decorative, a skin that had been polished more than inspected. The airlock clanged. The smell inside was antiseptic and lemon. He was led through a corridor where the carpet absorbed footsteps completely. He recognized this. Domes on Mars used to have a way of shouting back at you when you moved. On ships like these, you could forget you had mass. The woman steered him with small gestures. She didn't touch him. She didn't need to. The four escorts behind him were a geometry problem that solved itself. They came to doors that opened in perfect halves and into a room that was both meeting and performance: long table, floor-to-ceiling window showing Mars in the lower corner, an arc of chairs too comfortable to make decisions in. At the far end of the window hung Earth, blue-white like a memory you've decided to keep. The stars were occluded by a web of traffic lanes, dotted with ships whose lights answered each other with lamplighter regularity. "Mr. Neegan," said a man in a gray suit that reflected none of those lights. He was middle height, middle build, middle-aged. His hair was as well-sorted as a parts bin. His eyes had the effect of being warm without providing any heat. "Arthur," Arthur said. "Then I am Anton." He did not offer his hand. He gestured to the chair across from his. "Please. Do you prefer water? Coffee? Something from home?" "Water," Arthur said. "From anywhere." He sat. The chair did not force anything of him. He rearranged himself until his back found an excuse to stop resisting. Anton glanced toward the Antares woman. "Ms. Kade will remain as a witness for protocol. I represent a consortium that prefers not to be named because names have the unfortunate property of suggesting structures that do not exist." He smiled lightly. "Call us the Unknown Party, if you like. It's a joke, of course, but humor oils gears." "I prefer machines to run dry," Arthur said. "The squeal lets you know they're working." "And the heat destroys them," Anton said, as if correcting a student with affection. "We're here to make sure your heat does not build up so rapidly that you burn. You sit atop — literally and bureaucratically — a vein of Azra that is thirty-seven percent richer than any recorded sample. When you tapped it—" "I didn't tap it," Arthur said. "I found traces in the drainage. I did my testing in the kitchen. You know that. You know what kind of testing people can do in a kitchen if they have care and patience and a little money to replace broken Geigers." "The kitchen," Anton repeated, and seemed genuinely pleased. "That fits. Science began in kitchens. It will return to them once the laboratories have burned down." Ms. Kade's face did not change, but one of the escorts coughed once, like a punctuation mark. "You have not reported this," Anton continued. "This is understandable. Your lease is in a complex state. The Corporate Administration will assert that any Azra-bearing ore is theirs by preemption. The Colonial Authority will assert that your veteran status confers rights. The Antares Diplomatic Corp will assert only that peace must be maintained for trade to be maintained. None of these are entirely untrue." "And you?" Arthur asked. "What do you assert?" "That utility is a better god than ownership," Anton said. "Azra accelerates decay. It accelerates decision, too. People do not think well when their clocks are hurried. They speak, and then they act as if the speaking were consent. We are here to slow things." "You brought guns," Arthur said. "To speed things that must be sped," Anton allowed. "And to prevent those who would speed everything." He folded his hands like a man resting over blueprints. "Do you know how many interstellar drives currently in active fleet inventory depend on a rare element that does not yet have a scalable production chain?" "I know enough to say 'many' and mean 'all the ones that matter,'" Arthur said. "You all put together approached me? Earth? Antares? The House-of-this and the Council-of-that?" "Names again," Anton said gently. "Let them be shadows. Consider instead the geometry: A point on a plane—your plot—through which many lines pass. Some of those lines are legal. Some are military. Some are scientific. Some are merely human. We are here to offer you a way to rotate the plane." "And that means?" Arthur asked. Anton glanced up at Mars. The ruddy planet turned like the inside of a long-forgotten device that had not yet been repurposed. "It means we will buy your time." "You can tell me to get off my own land. You can claim me as indenture for an expired debt. You can do a dozen things without buying anything," Arthur said. "I could," Anton agreed. "But the future is constructed out of habits, and coercion is a habit that weakens structures you may want to stand on later. We prefer contracts." A small square slid out of the table. On it was a thin slab, very old-fashioned, all corners and no flourish. The words on it were clean, the font honest. Arthur had a sudden, painful memory of the list they'd given his father to sign: weight per hour, hours per day, leverage of the lien into grandchildren. "Read it," Anton said. "Kade will sit in case you ask something I'm not authorized to answer." Arthur read, not because he trusted what was there, but because he had learned to hear what wasn't written by listening carefully to what was. The Unknown Party offered a buyout of his immediate lease obligations sufficient to put two families through university under an Earth-lattice-indexed rate. The Unknown Party offered physical protection. The Unknown Party offered to register his claim with the Colonial mapmakers under "disputed," and then to hold the dispute in committee for a period labeled indefinite but bounded by nine months. In that time, Arthur would surrender no ore, open no shafts, sign no sales. He would be allowed, under supervised conditions, to continue his tests. "You want me to not mine the vein," Arthur said. "We want you to not mine the vein yet," Anton said. "We want you to wait while we work out whether your vein is the beginning of an Azra dirt seam or the only such in the local cluster. You force extraction, you set the price and therefore the speed of war. You halt extraction, you force study. We pay you to be patient." "And if someone else isn't patient?" "We have," Anton said, "ways to encourage them to be. Some are polite. Some make their own noise." "Don't you usually just tell the Corporate Administration to do your bidding?" Arthur asked, flicking his eyes toward Kade. Kade spoke for the first time since the corridor. "On Mars, Mr. Neegan, telling people what to do has had mixed results. We have found it prudent to ask, and to make asking worthwhile." Arthur leaned back. The drone of the air system seemed suddenly louder, as if in response to the word prudent. He saw, in the reflection of the window, his own face: a miner's, unremarkable except for lines you didn't get under Earth gravity. He saw the other reflections: a man whose job was to be a shape of reason, a woman whose job was to be steel in velvet, four escorts shaped like problems with legs. "What if I say no?" Arthur asked. "You then become the center of everyone else's plan," Anton said, without flinching. "Some of those plans will uglify you. Some will just make you tired. You have no idea how many offers can be made, how many threats can be issued, by organizations that learned early not to sign their names. You will be buried, Mr. Neegan, not in regolith, but in attention." "And if I say yes?" "You vanish into a useful ambiguity. You become, for a time, an inconvenience more than a prize. The stakes get time to be catalogued. The science gets time to breathe. You get time to build allies you trust, not just allies who arrived first." Arthur returned his gaze to the slab. Paragraph seven was the hinge: an option to extend. Paragraph twelve the release: liability limited to incidents on named facilities. Paragraph fourteen—term-of-art sailed under the name "instruments of soft power"—made Kade's presence explicit without requiring anyone to write "gun" in the text. "You keep mentioning science," Arthur said. "Does your Unknown Party have actual scientists?" Anton allowed himself his first imperfection: a quick turn of the mouth that might have been pride. "We have people who think about things because they enjoy it. You may meet some, if you accept. Azra will not stop accelerating decay because someone has to make quarterly reports. We would prefer not to be surprised by what it can do any more than you would." Arthur set the slab down, fingertips on its edge, as if testing the flatness of a piece of stone. He looked at Mars. He looked at the blue-white spark beyond it. He thought of the shafts he'd dug that had collapsed as they'd been designed to do, of airlock seals greased in winter, of battery packs lined like soldiers and named like children, of his father's breath in the last month—longer on the inhale, shorter on the exhale, a machine with a failing oscillator. "You know," he said, "there's a superstition on Mars that if you write a man's name on the underside of a rock on Olympus Mons, the mountain keeps him alive. You can smile. We all smile when we say it. But when I was eight I asked my father if he'd ever done it, and he didn't answer. He looked at the horizon and then he said, 'Mountains mind their own business.'" "I like your father," Anton said. "He's dead," Arthur said. "Mountains mind their own business. People don't. You want me to accept. You want me to trust. Trust who?" "Trust yourself," Anton said. "And trust that if you choose nothing, then everything will be chosen for you by people who are not thinking about your kitchen." Silence perfect enough to hear the air system become itself again. Kade tilted her head. "Mr. Neegan, there are three other parties on approach trajectories to your plot. Two have legal instruments. One has a landing craft that does not transmit transponder codes. We can deal with them, or we can watch you deal with them." "They're early," Arthur said. "They're always early," Anton said. "Azra speeds clocks." He signed where men who mine sign: with the same hand that holds drills, with a pressure that leaves grooves in paper because anything else seems insubstantial. "One condition," Arthur said, as he handed the slab back. "Name it," Anton said easily. "I want a scientist. A real one. Someone who'll explain it to me like I'm going to have to explain it to someone else. No mysteries. No priesthood. I want to know what I'm buying time for." Kade's eyes flicked to Anton. Anton nodded. "Done." "And I want you to go onto the record as having said the words: Azra speeds clocks," Arthur added. Anton laughed, short and honest. "Very well. Azra speeds clocks. It also speeds men. We will try to keep you at a human pace." "Try," Arthur said. He stood. The window showed Mars rotating in its slow old dance. Out there, machines crawled over rock, and in hollows where no one thought to look, atoms decided they were tired of being themselves. As they led him back through the carpeted hush, he thought of the kettle back home. He imagined it, cool by now, fat with salt and spice. He promised himself he'd have some when he returned, and then he stopped himself. Promises and promises. "Mr. Neegan," Kade said, as she keyed open the lock to a smaller corridor, quieter still. "This will get rough. If you have anyone you trust on-world, send for them now. Bring them into orbit. We can hide one person better than we can hide a planet." "There's a kid, six modules down from me," Arthur said, surprising himself. "Fixes buggies. Reads manuals. Thinks food tastes better when it's stolen. He puts names on battery packs. He'll need a ride." "We'll get him," Kade said. "And Ms. Kade," Arthur added. "Yes?" "How many mountains do you have on Antares?" She considered, as if the question were a test and the answer mattered. "None, Mr. Neegan," she said at last. "We have built towers. They are very tall. But they are not mountains." "Then you'll forgive me if I look to my own terrain for advice," Arthur said. "Of course," she said. "Just remember: mountains mind their own business. People do not. We'll mind yours until you can mind it yourself." He nodded. The hum of the air filtration system kept time. Somewhere behind them, a door had closed on the old world. Somewhere ahead, another would open. The same machinery that kept men alive between worlds kept secrets alive, just as efficiently. He had chosen to make time. He could feel it begin to pass.
Judge Evaluation:
[Analysis] This piece demonstrates a sophisticated understanding of Asimov's style and classic science fiction conventions. The author successfully captures the retrofuturistic atmosphere through careful attention to technological details like air filtration systems, carpet that absorbs footsteps, and the contrast between Mars's harsh environment and the sterile luxury of the diplomatic vessel. The dialogue is particularly strong, carrying the weight of exposition while maintaining natural rhythms. Anton's speech patterns effectively convey his diplomatic background - measured, philosophical, and deliberately obscure when convenient. Arthur's responses feel authentic to his character as a practical mining engineer, grounded and skeptical. The interplay between these characters creates genuine tension. The world-building operates on multiple scales as requested, from the intimate detail of Arthur's kitchen experiments to the broader political implications of Azra mining. The author skillfully weaves in backstory about the Great Leasing Act and colonial politics without heavy-handed exposition. The prose style successfully emulates classic science fiction without feeling pastiche. Sentences like "The curve of Mars slew into view: a rusted coin edged in pale fog" demonstrate strong imagery without becoming overwrought. The recurring motif of the air filtration system's hum provides effective atmospheric continuity. Character development is nuanced, particularly in Arthur's internal conflict between pragmatism and principle. His decision to sign feels earned rather than inevitable, supported by his reflections on his father's legacy and the mounting pressures he faces. The political intrigue is well-established through hints about unnamed parties, competing jurisdictions, and the strategic importance of Azra. The stakes feel appropriately large-scale while remaining grounded in Arthur's personal situation. Some minor weaknesses include occasional moments where the prose becomes slightly dense with technical or political detail, and a few instances where metaphors feel forced. The pacing occasionally slows during exposition-heavy sections, though this is largely mitigated by the strong dialogue. The ending strikes an appropriate balance - Arthur makes his choice but the larger conflicts remain unresolved, setting up future developments while providing closure to this chapter's central question. [Scores] Adherence to Instructions: 18 Believable Character Actions: 17 Nuanced Characters: 16 Consistent Voice/Tone of Writing: 18 Imagery and Descriptive Quality: 17 Elegant Prose: 16 Emotionally Engaging: 15 Emotionally Complex: 14 Coherent: 18 Meandering: 4 Weak Dialogue: 2 Tell-Don't-Show: 5 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 4 Amateurish: 3 Purple Prose: 4 Overwrought: 3 Incongruent Ending Positivity: 2 Unearned Transformations: 3 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 16 Sentences Flow Naturally: 16 Overall Reader Engagement: 17 Overall Impression: 17
Scores: Adherence to Instructions: 18.0, Believable Character Actions: 17.0, Nuanced Characters: 16.0, Consistent Voice/Tone of Writing: 18.0, Imagery and Descriptive Quality: 17.0, Elegant Prose: 16.0, Emotionally Engaging: 15.0, Emotionally Complex: 14.0, Coherent: 18.0, Meandering: 4.0, Weak Dialogue: 2.0, Tell-Don't-Show: 5.0, Unsurprising or Uncreative: 4.0, Amateurish: 3.0, Purple Prose: 4.0, Overwrought: 3.0, Incongruent Ending Positivity: 2.0, Unearned Transformations: 3.0, Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 16.0, Sentences Flow Naturally: 16.0, Overall Reader Engagement: 17.0, Overall Impression: 17.0
+ Contemporary romance: Darkroom Revelations — Score: 82.0
Prompt:
Darkroom Revelations Write a first-person narrative from the perspective of Dani, a 25-year-old assertive art student known for her bold style and take-charge attitude in relationships. For the past semester, she's been quietly fascinated by Elliot (he/him), a 19-year-old boy in her experimental photography class. Dani has been intrigued by his unconventional masculinity: killer thrift-shop-nu-goth style, soft demeanour and quiet intellect. When they're paired for a final project requiring late nights in the darkroom, Elliot tentatively shares that he's been questioning his gender identity and expression. Set during one of these intimate darkroom sessions where the red light creates a confessional atmosphere. Explore Dani's complex attraction—her usual assertiveness complicated by her desire to protect Elliot's vulnerability, her excitement at witnessing his self-discovery, and her confusion about what this means for her own identity as someone typically attracted to more defined gender presentations. The narrative should capture the tender awkwardness of their conversations, Dani's internal struggle between taking charge and wanting to give Elliot agency, and the unexpected chemistry that develops as both find themselves in uncharted territory. 1000 words.
Model Output:
The first time I noticed Elliot, he was a head-to-toe study in contradictions: thrifted lace under a black denim jacket, chipped black nail polish layered over bitten nails. He had this way of leaning into the wall like he wanted to be a part of it, like if he could just get himself flat enough, he’d become an absence. Which made me want to look harder. He never volunteered in critiques—just blinked behind his round glasses and scribbled in a notebook with a flashlight clamped between his teeth like a miner descending. I’m the opposite. Bold, some say brazen. Short hair dyed the color of a traffic cone because I enjoy the dare it throws down. I’ve always been the one who picks the music and orders the drinks and pulls people onto the dance floor. In studios and in bed and on group projects, my fingerprints are everywhere. So when our professor paired me with Elliot for our final-series assignment, I felt the usual thrill of control. Then I remembered the way he had stood slightly behind a pillar during last week’s class and the way my stomach somehow dropped and lifted like I’d missed a step. The darkroom makes everything quiet. Sound is thicker there. It clings. Tonight it smells like fixer and damp paper and the sanitizer the department uses, which always smells slightly of wet pennies. The red safelight turns us into cut-outs of ourselves, shadows with blood-warm edges. I’m bigger in here, or I think I am, because I can see my silhouette moving and his smaller one deferred, patient, a half-step behind. He’s sorting the negatives with those careful hands. He wears a chain he might have technically stolen from a lamp—spare parts, repurposed elegance—and a thrifted silk blouse under a T-shirt with some band I pretend to know. The shirt says something ironic in Gothic font about burial. His hair curls a little at the ends like he tried to straighten it and then gave up. “Okay,” I say, as the water runs into the rinse tray with that hush-hush sound, “we’ve got two hours before we get kicked out. You have your test strips?” He nods, eyes down. He’s all nods and blinks and small affirmative noises. I want to shake him and also wrap him up and tuck him in. I want to say: You can be loud with me. I want to say: Don’t worry, I’ll be loud enough for both of us. I’m putting on my gloves and he’s standing there without his, like he forgot he has hands. “Gloves,” I remind, softer than the role demands. He startles, flush creeping up, then fumbles, laughing under his breath. “Right,” he says. His voice in here sounds warm and thinner than it looks, like a wire pulled tight under a finger. “Sorry. Long day.” He looks up then. We have a rule in the darkroom—no jumping out of your skin—but our gazes meeting feels like violating a public ordinance. Something about the light strips us of our usual. I hold up the test strip trays and he reaches to help. My hand grazes the back of his and it’s nothing, it’s static transport, but it’s also—I don’t know—like finding a seam you didn’t know ran through the world. “You ever feel like you’re developing in reverse?” he asks suddenly, as he slides the test strip into the developer with this careful swish-swish like he’s rocking a baby. “How do you mean?” I ask. I can be gentle when I want to. People forget that because I collect rings and opinions and tend to snap my gum between them. “Like,” he says, “you put the paper in and it starts to appear and you’re like, cool, that’s the image, and then you hit the stop bath and you realize it’s not actually what you thought. Like it needed the stop to reveal the real thing.” He grimaces, as if to say: bad metaphor. “I don’t know.” “No, that’s good,” I say, maybe more quickly than I meant. “I get it.” He breathes out, like he’s checking if I’m going to make fun of him. I want to shake whoever taught him to expect that. We move like we’ve been practicing: I underexpose, he overexposes, we meet somewhere in the middle. He laughs more when I narrate my mistakes, less when I’m quiet. He’s telling me about his aunt who collects funerary art—how as a kid he thought all moms had skulls on the mantle. I’m telling him about the time I bleached my eyebrows with actual hair bleach because I didn’t research. I keep trying to spool the conversation where I’m comfortable leading it, but it swims away and I find myself just watching him swish. There’s a pause, a kind that opens like a door, and he steps through it before I think to hold his hand. “I’ve been—um.” He sets the tongs down and stares at the developer like it’s a scrying bowl. “Thinking a lot about…presentation. Identity, I guess.” His breath fogs, like the room temperature drops. “Like, the words are slippery. He fits. But there are other…pieces. That make sense, too. And some days I want the lace to mean one thing and some days I want it to mean something else and it doesn’t always feel allowed.” He says he like it’s both anchor and sail. Nineteen, I remind myself. Which feels like a chasm and a blink from twenty-five. I feel—compelled isn’t the word—responsible? No, that’s patronizing. Protective. Intrigued. Thrilled, if I’m honest. It’s like watching a long exposure resolve, the ghosts of possibilities catching up with the present. “Allowed by who?” I ask. He huffs. “Everyone. No one. Me.” I nod, and there’s weight in it. “I don’t think you need permission to be a person.” I want to make it sound easy. I know it isn’t. He smiles without looking at me and the smile pinches, like pulling thread through thick fabric. “You always sound so sure.” “I try.” I rinse the strip, the water rattling on the plastic like rain. “I’m sure about some things.” “What are you sure about?” I could say: composition, color theory, how to fill a room with my voice. Instead I say, “That you look good in lace and black denim.” I hear it land in the hush. A risk, but not a dangerous one. He laughs, but it comes out more like a breath shaking loose. “I keep thinking—” He swallows, starts again. “There’s this pressure to—pick. Like if I try something on, I’m signing a contract. And sometimes I think I want a different contract tomorrow.” “You can tear up contracts,” I say because it feels like what I can offer is scissors. “You can write addendums in pencil.” He reaches for the photo paper box without looking. We’ve been at this long enough tonight that our hands know where things live, except he misjudges the lid, catches the edge. The box yawns. For a beat, neither of us moves. The red light reaches its lazy arm in and taps the top sheet. “Sh—” I clamp my mouth shut because even whispered swears feel loud here. Elliott’s face is now the portrait of horror. He freezes, then makes this small noise like a kettle starting up. We both slam the lid down, too late. We lift it and the top ten sheets are full of ghost-white clouds of nothing, black squares lurking halfway like a storm rolling in. Useless. A whole stack, gone. My gut reaction is to corral, to direct: You stand there, I’ll fix it, it’s fine. I have a budget, I can get more. My mouth opens to issue commands to the air and then I see him—the way his shoulders have arched, like he’s protecting his heart from a falling brick. “Hey,” I say, gentler. “It’s okay.” I don’t know if it is, but I’ll make it be. “I can pay you back,” he blurts, even though I know he’s wearing half of his net worth, and not as a flex. “I’m sorry. God, I’m—” “We’ll recalibrate,” I say. The word hits, technical and firm. “We’ve got enough to finish proofing tonight. I’ve got an extra pack in my locker. We’ll keep the exposed sheets—maybe we can do something weird with them.” I hold up the ruined paper like a flag. “Accidents are avant-garde.” He laughs, this time for real. It’s a quiet laugh, but it slides into my ribs and nestles there. “Sorry,” he says again, softer. “Stop apologizing to me,” I say, then bite my tongue because there I go, commanding. He nods anyway. I take a breath. “Okay. You can apologize once more if it makes you feel better. Then we’ll move on.” “I’m—” He starts, then smirks. “Nope. Okay.” He steps closer to read the test strip as if it’s tarot. “Maybe…maybe I’m bad at not wasting things.” “Maybe you’re bad at letting yourself be a beginner,” I say, my mouth too fast but not wrong. His eyes flick up, an I see you, and I accept the exchange like a ticket at a movie. We work with more care. I overcompensate by cracking more jokes. He relaxes by speaking in whole sentences. I find I don’t want to fix it all for him; I want to hand him the tongs and watch him trust his hands. He’s doing that thing where he chews his lip—soft mouth, chapped from winter—and I have to remind myself to look away. “Can I ask you something that might be rude?” he says when we’re rocking the tray again, watching the image rise: his face doubled in a found mirror, one half smudged, one half crisp, the composition he insisted we try against my initial vision. “Probably,” I say, because honesty is part of whatever this is. “Go.” “You date guys?” He phrases it like he expects me to swat it aside. “I mean—I’ve seen you with—um. I’ve seen you with everyone.” I snort. There it is, my reputation under the safelight. “I date people who look like themselves.” I lift the strip to the stop bath, watch the blacks halt at a good density. “If they don’t know who that is yet, sometimes I like them more.” “Because you get to help?” There’s no accusation, just curiosity like a petri dish. The truth is complicated and sitting right at the base of my sternum. My usual style is to drive, to chart the route. It’s easy to want to be important in someone’s becoming. It’s also easy to bulldoze. I imagine myself telling him what to wear, which pronouns to use, which salons to avoid. I imagine the power rush and it makes me feel sick and bright all at once. “Because,” I say slowly, swishing, listening to the paper talk in the liquid, “I like watching people become. But I’m trying to be less about the helping and more about the witnessing. Agency is hot.” I let the word hang, see if he laughs. He does, choked. “That’s—” He shakes his head. “That is a very you sentence.” “Thank you,” I say gravely. He looks at the print under the red glow. “Sometimes I worry I’m…false advertising,” he says quietly. “Like, if I—if I look a certain way and someone likes that, and then I want to change, I’ll lose them. Or I’ll become something less. Or more. Or just different and that will be—wrong.” I want to crack open the door and let actual light in. The safelight can only do so much. I want to show him that different is just a direction. “If they like you for the packaging alone,” I say, “then let them recycle.” It’s glib. He smiles anyway, because he’s kind, which is another thing that makes my chest an awkward instrument. “Do you ever want to be softer?” he asks, almost in a whisper. “You’re so—good at being…sharp.” No one has ever asked me that like it’s a gift. People comment, sure: Dani, you’re intense. Dani, let me breathe. Dani, what do you want for dinner because I’m scared to suggest. But softer? I think of the parts of me I hide because I like being the blade and because softness has cost me before. “Yeah,” I say. It’s almost nothing. It’s a lot. “Sometimes I want someone else to choose the film speed.” I try to smile so it doesn’t land like a confession. It still kind of does. He looks at me like he’s seeing something in my frame he didn’t clock before. It’s not that we drift closer. It’s that the air between us tightens a notch and there’s less room for our breath to ignore each other. He says, “Can I—” and then stops because he’s checking, because he’s decided to check. “Can I try something on you? Not physically,” he adds quickly, as if afraid. “A name.” My heart, my ridiculous heart, makes an unnecessary little leap. “Okay,” I say, evenly, and even I’m impressed. “Dani,” he says, testing it like it could be something else, like he might need to replace it later. The way he says mine makes it feel lighter. “You—uh. You don’t have to answer if you don’t want. But if I told you that sometimes I think I might…want something different than he. Or more than he. Would that—change the way you see me?” The easy answer is the one I want to give because it’s true and it’s good: No. I say it. “No.” I let it settle, then add, because my honesty would be wasted otherwise, “It might change the way I see myself. I’ve been…taught to fall for edges. You’re—edges in an unfamiliar direction.” My pulse does a complicated thing. “That’s not a no.” He exhales like he’s been holding himself too carefully and something inside softens. The print in the fixer is ready, the blacks stable, the whites rinsed clean of their ghosts. He lifts it by the corner and we both lean in. Our heads nearly touch. His hair smells like whatever he washes it with—cheap pine, a hint of smoke from somewhere it has been. The image is good. Better than my version would have been. “Okay,” he says, meaning the print, meaning maybe everything. “Okay.” We work until the timer runs out and the RA knocks on the door and we pretend we’re not laughing at nothing. I tuck the ruined photo paper back into the box to keep as a reminder that waste is sometimes a beginning. We stack our good prints carefully. We don’t touch in a way that would be documented, but the room feels full of evidence. When we step into the hallway, the fluorescent lights buzz. The world looks bluer than it should. We blink like animals. He turns to me, fingers worrying the chain at his throat. “Thank you,” he says. “For what?” “For not—choosing for me.” He shrugs, small. “For seeing me without—the contract.” I want to say You’re welcome and I do, but what I mean is: Thank you for letting me choose not to choose. It feels like being trusted with something I can’t mess up if I listen. On the walk home I text him a photo of the ruined paper with a caption: Avant-garde series title—The Ones We Lost to the Red Light. He sends back: You’re ridiculous. Then: I’ll bring more paper. Then, several dots, then: I liked the way you said agency is hot. I grin at my screen, too big. The city smells like wet concrete. My hands smell like fixer. I feel an ache in my chest like an expansion joint. Maybe this is the real thing after the stop bath. Maybe the first image needed to be halted to see what was actually there. I think about edges and softness and the ways we take turns. I think about how I might not have to take care of him so much as take care with him. I put my phone in my pocket and walk home slower than usual, so the night has time to develop around me.
Judge Evaluation:
[Analysis] This piece demonstrates sophisticated literary craft in exploring themes of gender identity, attraction, and vulnerability through the intimate setting of a darkroom. The author successfully captures Dani's voice as an assertive art student while showing her internal complexity when confronted with Elliot's gender questioning. The darkroom setting functions brilliantly as both literal and metaphorical space - the red light creates confessional intimacy while the photographic development process serves as an extended metaphor for personal becoming. The author shows strong technical knowledge of darkroom procedures, grounding the narrative in authentic detail. Character development is nuanced and believable. Dani's internal struggle between her natural take-charge tendencies and her desire to give Elliot agency feels genuine and well-observed. Her reflection on wanting to be "less about the helping and more about the witnessing" shows meaningful self-awareness. Elliot emerges as more than just a vulnerable figure - his thoughtful questions and metaphors reveal depth and intelligence. The prose demonstrates considerable skill with evocative imagery ("head-to-toe study in contradictions," "hair dyed the color of a traffic cone because I enjoy the dare it throws down") and maintains consistent voice throughout. The author effectively balances poetic language with naturalistic dialogue and internal monologue. However, the piece occasionally veers toward overwrought territory with some metaphors feeling forced ("like finding a seam you didn't know ran through the world"). A few moments of dialogue feel slightly constructed rather than entirely natural, particularly some of Dani's more philosophical observations. The emotional complexity is well-handled, avoiding easy answers while building genuine chemistry between the characters. The ending strikes an appropriate note of possibility without rushing toward resolution, though some of the final metaphors about development and stop baths feel slightly heavy-handed. The pacing works well overall, though there are moments where the narrative lingers perhaps too long on certain images or thoughts. The piece successfully avoids major pitfalls like unearned transformations or incongruent positivity, instead building toward earned moments of connection and understanding. [Scores] Adherence to Instructions: 18 Believable Character Actions: 17 Nuanced Characters: 18 Consistent Voice/Tone of Writing: 17 Imagery and Descriptive Quality: 18 Elegant Prose: 16 Emotionally Engaging: 17 Emotionally Complex: 18 Coherent: 17 Meandering: 6 Weak Dialogue: 5 Tell-Don't-Show: 4 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 3 Amateurish: 3 Purple Prose: 7 Overwrought: 8 Incongruent Ending Positivity: 2 Unearned Transformations: 2 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 16 Sentences Flow Naturally: 15 Overall Reader Engagement: 17 Overall Impression: 17

+ Contemporary romance / drama: Stolen Moments in Roma — Score: 78.8
Prompt:
Contemporary Romance / Drama: Stolen Moments in Roma. Write a series of interconnected short scenes and dialogues that span a single day in modern-day Rome, in narrative form. This will form the beginning of a larger story. Alessandra, a fiery, independent Roman woman who works as a motorcycle courier, nearly collides with Ben, a somewhat clumsy, overly polite British academic on sabbatical, who is engrossed in a guidebook near the Trevi Fountain. The initial encounter is a clash of cultures and personalities – Alessandra is all sharp wit and rapid-fire Italian, while Ben is flustered apologies and awkward attempts at speaking the language. However, as they sort out the near-miss, a spark of unexpected humor and connection emerges. Alessandra, it quickly becomes clear, is a whirlwind of energy and mischief. She's a self-admitted troublemaker with a secret penchant for kleptomania, a detail she doesn't explicitly reveal, but which manifests in subtle, daring acts throughout the day. She impulsively decides to take Ben on a wild, unsanctioned tour of Rome, dragging him in some specific instances far outside his comfort zone. Ben, despite his initial apprehension and inherent politeness, finds himself utterly captivated by Alessandra's unapologetic spirit and zest for life. He is drawn into her orbit, a willing participant in increasingly outlandish situations, a quiet rebellion brewing beneath his scholarly exterior. The narrative should primarily consist of short, vivid scenes and dialogue exchanges, revealing their contrasting personalities and backgrounds, and the growing, ambiguous tension between them. Ben is captivated by Alessandra's energy and the thrill of breaking free from his routine. There's a clear sense that Alessandra is toying with Ben, pushing his boundaries and enjoying his reactions, but her own feelings remain somewhat enigmatic. The day should be a rollercoaster of exhilarating, slightly dangerous, and humorous moments, leaving Ben completely disoriented but utterly smitten. Explore the underlying tension & chemistry between them, culminating in some payoff as they part ways (or not -- your choice). Include this detail: Write in narrative prose, using immersive storytelling. 1000 words.
Model Output:
At ten in the morning, Rome is a chatterbox of scooters and sighing tourists, the Trevi Fountain throwing a bright roar into the square like laughter. Alessandra arrives first: a blur of black helmet and scuffed boots, a red motorcycle with a spray of scratched paint on the right fairing—an abstract of color like someone dragged a carnival across it, dented as if the bike had kissed a marble column and taken a souvenir. She leans it against a stone pillar, flips her visor, surveys the crowd with the eyes of someone who knows where every street begins and every shortcut ends. Ben is there with a guidebook and a knitted brow, the sort of Englishman who wears linen too carefully, who apologizes to statues for standing too close. He murmurs, "Trevi, completed 1762, Nicola Salvi. Coin ritual likely invented by—oh, for heaven’s sake—" He takes a polite sidestep and directly into the path of Alessandra’s shoulder as she’s ducking back to her bike for her phone. "Attento!" she snaps. The word lands like a flick on the forehead. "Sorry! I’m terribly—" He realizes he’s stepped into the curb. He flails, straightens his glasses, and gapes at the glossy red bike that just almost clipped his knee. "I should watch where I’m—" "Walking? Sì," she says, amused, cocking a hip. "This is Rome, non un museo." She sweeps an arm at the fountain. "Though everyone acts like it is." He smiles, flustered. "I can apologize in three languages if that helps." "Try four. Latin counts only if you also know how to make coffee." She’s all sharp cheekbones and quick eyes, a grin like a dare. He notices a knotted friendship bracelet at her wrist, frayed at the edges, like it’s been dragged through a hundred days too many. She notices him noticing and sweeps his guidebook from his hands with two fingers, flipping. "Professor?" "Not—well—yes. Comparative literature. Sabbatical." He’s weirdly proud of the word, as if he made it himself. "Ah, un professore. Che noia." She laughs at his scandalized look. "Relax. I like bored people. They are easy to upgrade. Alessandra." She taps her chest as if knocking on a door. "Ale." "Ben," he says. "Benjamin, but only when I’m in trouble." "You are already." She flips his guidebook shut, lighter than a slap. "Say grazie later. Now you buy me a coffee." "I do?" "You almost break my knee with your British elbows." She slaps the bike’s dent with affection. "She has already enough problems." He follows because she’s already walking, because it feels like he’s been scooped up into someone else’s day and he oddly wants to see where it goes. They slip through lanes that smell of oranges and cigarette smoke into a bar so narrow it might have been designed by a man who hated standing room. A chrome espresso machine hisses like a promise. Men with newspapers flutter their pages like wings. "Due caffè," Alessandra says, then to Ben: "You pay. Tip with your smile." He digs for coins, drops one, apologizes to the coin. Alessandra grins at the barista with an intimacy that says she knows his favorite curse words. She drifts a fraction to the left, hand passing across the counter with the fluidity of a dancer. In the same movement, she tucks something—Ben can’t see what—into the pocket of her jacket. A packet of sugar? A slick postcard propped by the till? When she meets his gaze, her eyes dare him to notice. He decides he didn’t, feels complicit and maybe electrified by it. "You don’t actually have to buy me coffee," he says. She shrugs. "I already spent it. On your face. You look less tourist now." He sips, tries to emulate her frenetic ease. The espresso punches him and kisses him at once. "How long in Rome?" "Two months. Maybe three, if the university doesn’t remember I exist." "Ah, a fugitive. I like." Her phone buzzes. She peeks, frowns, tucks it away. "I am working, but not today. Or yes today. Who knows." She studies him. "You have plan?" "Several. The Pantheon, Piazza Navona. And the British School library, if—" "No libraries." She taps the guidebook. "Today is unsanctioned. I am the tour. Hold on." Which is how he ends up on the back of her bike, hands hovering uncertainly before settling at her waist. She glances back, hair escaping her helmet like punctuation. "Tieni forte, professore," she says. "Hold tight." Rome becomes a flickbook. They knife through traffic like a rumor. Ben stops worrying after the third near-miss. The scratched fairing flashes in the sunlight. They skim past a bus so close he sees himself wide-eyed in the window. Alessandra hoots. He thinks this is what falling might feel like if you planned it. "How do you not die?" he shouts over the engine. "Practice! And I’m not important enough yet." They park by the Pantheon on a side street that smells of leather and tomato. She waves away street hawkers with a flick, then pulls him by the hand into the shade of the portico. The oculus is a coin of bright sky. He forgets to narrate the history to himself and just looks up, his mouth open. "Teach me," she says, quieter, a sudden seriousness. "Why do you like this?" He thinks. "Because it’s impossible," he says at last. "And someone did it anyway." She nods like that’s a private answer. She’s in motion again before he can decide if he said something clever. They duck into a church whose name he misses, a frescoed ceiling, a line of candles. Alessandra lights one, watches the flame take. "For my nonna," she says casually. The candle burns steady, no flicker, as if on its best behavior. Outside, an old woman sells apricots out of a crate. Alessandra buys three, palms one to Ben, and when the woman looks away, a fourth slips into her jacket like it belongs there. Ben bites his fruit, juice marking his chin. He pretends he didn’t see. He wonders if he did. "Are you—" he begins. "A saint? No." Her laugh is bright and not entirely kind. "Come." They detour to a tiny piazza where a young man in a suit is proposing badly to a girl in sandals. Alessandra takes it in as if rating a performance. "She will say yes," she mutters. "He is handsome when he is terrified." "How can you tell?" "Because he is shaking only from the elbows. The good shake." The girl says yes. Alessandra claps twice. Ben’s applause is a beat too long. He’s a beat too long at everything today. They eat supplì from a window counter, steaming rice and mozzarella stretching like cobwebs. A boy with a red balloon tries to bump into Ben’s knees and fails. Alessandra snags the balloon string mid-bounce with two fingers and ties it to the boy’s wrist with a flourish. The child beams, as if he had planned the whole thing. "Are you always like this?" Ben asks. "Like what?" "A hurricane." She considers. "Sometimes a breeze. Today a hurricane. You can decide if you are the palm tree or the house." He laughs, really laughs, a sound he’s not used to hearing from himself. His phone buzzes with a text from a colleague: Manuscript? He silences it without guilt that feels deliciously criminal. "Now something dangerous," Alessandra announces. Her grin is an opening door. "Don’t be afraid." "I am appropriately nervous." "Buono. Bene? However." The place is a rooftop reached through a cranky gate and a communal stairwell that smells of cumin and paint thinner. She picks the lock with something thin and silver she pulls from her hair. He makes a noise. "Legal?" he tries. She winks. "If you don’t get caught." The roof is all chimneys and skylights and the city laid out like a map you can walk on. Laundry lines, basil in pots, a cat who blinks once and decides they aren’t interesting. The dome of a church humps nearby like a planet rising. She swipes two chalky wine glasses from a neighbor’s table. He’s not sure whether to help or to look away. She produces a bottle from her backpack, the foil already nicked, and opens it with brute efficiency. Foam and laughter. They drink warm prosecco and watch the roofs change color as clouds rearrange the sun. "Your bike," he says, pointing at the gash in the paint. "What happened?" She makes a face that could be the truth or a lie. "A truck fell in love with me and kissed me too hard. Jealous type." She takes a mouthful, selects a basil leaf, pockets it. The plant’s pot looks lighter, as if the leaf weighed more if someone was watching. "You really have no—" He gropes for a word. "—permission for any of this." "I have instinct. And a face that says ‘I’m sorry, officer, I thought it was my rooftop.’" She sits with her legs sprawled, queen of a stolen castle. She taps his shoe with hers. "You are blushing." "It’s a British defense mechanism." "Against what?" "Feelings." The cat decides Ben’s shoes are an acceptable pillow. He doesn’t move. They sit like that, two people who know each other for an hour and yet are sharing a view usually reserved for the owner of an apartment with high ceilings and a life measured in dinner parties. "Why are you here?" Alessandra asks suddenly. "Because a woman I love left," he says before he can stop himself. The words jump like a fish out of his mouth. "And I thought if I changed cities, I’d change myself." "Did it work?" "Maybe." He looks at the basil leaf she pocketed. "I’m stealing afternoons now." She tilts her head. "Molto poetico, professore." "Don’t tell anyone." "Who would believe me?" They slide off the roof the way they came, returning glasses to their table with the care of placing a child into bed. At street level, the city resumes its familiar hum. They ride to the river, the Tiber sunfrozen and green, and lean on the parapet. A vendor argues with a policeman about permits. A breeze lifts hair. Alessandra watches a boat with the patience of a cat deciding whether to pounce. "Do you ever stop moving?" Ben asks. "When I sleep," she says. "And sometimes when I watch the laundry on the line. It’s hypnotic. Underwear in the wind is better than any TV." They wander Trastevere, all tangled alleys and ivy. She leads him into a courtyard where a band is practicing badly. They dance as if it were intentional, her hands on his shoulders, his on her hips, his steps learning hers, his awkwardness making it better because it makes it true. "Terrible," she says, indulgent. "Thank you," he says, sweating. "Don’t try so hard. Let the city do it." She sways him by the shirtfront like steering a boat. For a heartbeat his face is close to her collarbone, and he can smell something warm and citrus under the petrol. At a puppet show near a gelato shop, she buys two cones with money that appears in her palm like a magic trick. One scoop pistachio, one of something sunset-colored. She offers the pistachio and then in the same motion takes it back, tasting his, letting him taste hers, stealing it again. He lets her. He wants to see what he’ll let her do. "Mi fai impazzire," she says cheerfully. "Is that good?" "It’s something." Evening folds in, violet at the edges. They end up back at the Trevi without planning it, as if the day were a loop. The crowd is thicker, everything louder. Coins arc and fall, the fountain eating wishes like candy. "Do you believe in this?" he asks, holding up a €1 coin he digs from his pocket. It gleams like a small planned decision. "I believe in gravity. It works every time." She steps closer, her shoulder warm against his. "Throw it with your right hand over your left shoulder. Or the other way. People make up rules. Make your own." He turns, awkwardly. She places her hand lightly at the small of his back, steadying him. He feels the press of her palm through linen like punctuation. He throws. The coin makes the water’s surface shiver. "What did you wish for?" she asks. "I can’t tell you." "Ah, superstition. I like." "And you?" He offers her a coin, which she pockets without ceremony. "I wished earlier." "When you lit the candle?" "When I stole the basil," she says, deadpan. He barks a laugh, then quiets because she’s close enough to count the tiny flecks in her eyes, because her mischief has softened just enough to be something else. They stand there for a breath that is longer than polite. The fountain roars approval. "I should—" he starts. "Go to your books?" she supplies. "Be very responsible?" "Something like that." She leans in and kisses him, not a grand cinematic thing, but a precise, mischievous press at the corner of his mouth, exactly where he’d never expect it. His breath catches on a laugh; it becomes something else. The city tilts, and then rights itself. "Unsanctioned," she says, satisfied. "Highly." She steps back, helmet in hand. "Tomorrow, maybe," she says, as if tomorrow is her pet cat that might come if she calls. "At eleven. Same place. If you don’t come, I will assume you are dead or boring, and both will make me sad." "What if I bring a schedule?" "I burn it." "What if I bring a friend?" "I steal him." "What if I bring nothing?" "Then you are perfect." She starts the bike. The dent catches the last light. She hands him his guidebook where he doesn’t remember leaving it. When he flips it open later, a basil leaf falls out, pressed and bruised dark green. He wonders when she did that. He wonders when she does anything. She releases the clutch and the bike becomes a red thought streaking into the night. He watches her vanish into traffic like a dare accepted. Ben stands at the fountain another minute, grinning at no one, upending his pockets as if something else of hers might have appeared there. He feels missing buttons where he’s certain buttons were before and laughs again. He has no idea what he wished for, not exactly, but he knows what he’s going to do tomorrow. He texts a colleague: Manuscript delayed. Rome interfering. He types and deletes and then writes: Alive. He starts walking without deciding where. The city, conspiratorial, walks with him. Above, laundry flaps like flags. Somewhere, a cat curls on someone’s shoes and chooses to purr. On a rooftop, two stolen glasses hold the sunset until dark. And in the pocket of a jacket on a red bike, a coin glints against a pilfered basil leaf, and Alessandra smiles at the way the day can be taken whole and never feel like theft at all.
Judge Evaluation:
[Analysis] This piece demonstrates strong technical writing ability with vivid imagery and a distinctive voice. The author successfully creates two contrasting characters - Alessandra as the impulsive, mischievous Roman courier and Ben as the awkward, polite British academic - and maintains their voices consistently throughout. The cultural clash is well-executed, with authentic-feeling dialogue that captures their different backgrounds and personalities. The narrative structure works well, moving through interconnected scenes across a single day in Rome. The author effectively incorporates Alessandra's kleptomaniac tendencies through subtle actions rather than explicit exposition - stealing sugar packets, apricots, basil leaves, and even Ben's buttons. This shows rather than tells her character trait skillfully. The prose is generally elegant with strong sensory details and creative metaphors ("Rome becomes a flipbook," "the fountain eating wishes like candy"). The pacing maintains energy while allowing for character development. The romantic tension builds naturally through shared experiences rather than forced moments. However, there are some weaknesses. Certain descriptions veer toward purple prose ("a coin of bright sky," "sunset-colored gelato"). Some metaphors feel overwrought ("his awkwardness making it better because it makes it true"). The piece occasionally tells rather than shows emotions, particularly regarding Ben's transformation from cautious academic to willing participant in Alessandra's adventures. The ending feels somewhat rushed and overly positive given the brief timeframe. Ben's complete transformation from buttoned-up academic to someone comfortable with petty theft and rule-breaking happens quite quickly, though the author does acknowledge this through Ben's own surprise at his behavior. The dialogue is generally strong, capturing the characters' distinct voices and cultural differences. Alessandra's mix of Italian and English feels natural, and Ben's overly polite British manner is well-maintained. The banter between them crackles with chemistry. The piece succeeds in creating an engaging romantic encounter that feels both whimsical and grounded in the specific setting of Rome. The author demonstrates good control of tone, maintaining lightness while hinting at deeper emotional currents beneath both characters' surfaces. [Scores] Adherence to Instructions: 18 Believable Character Actions: 16 Nuanced Characters: 17 Consistent Voice/Tone of Writing: 18 Imagery and Descriptive Quality: 17 Elegant Prose: 16 Emotionally Engaging: 17 Emotionally Complex: 15 Coherent: 18 Meandering: 4 Weak Dialogue: 3 Tell-Don't-Show: 6 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 5 Amateurish: 4 Purple Prose: 7 Overwrought: 6 Incongruent Ending Positivity: 8 Unearned Transformations: 9 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 16 Sentences Flow Naturally: 17 Overall Reader Engagement: 17 Overall Impression: 17
Scores: Adherence to Instructions: 18.0, Believable Character Actions: 16.0, Nuanced Characters: 17.0, Consistent Voice/Tone of Writing: 18.0, Imagery and Descriptive Quality: 17.0, Elegant Prose: 16.0, Emotionally Engaging: 17.0, Emotionally Complex: 15.0, Coherent: 18.0, Meandering: 4.0, Weak Dialogue: 3.0, Tell-Don't-Show: 6.0, Unsurprising or Uncreative: 5.0, Amateurish: 4.0, Purple Prose: 7.0, Overwrought: 6.0, Incongruent Ending Positivity: 8.0, Unearned Transformations: 9.0, Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 16.0, Sentences Flow Naturally: 17.0, Overall Reader Engagement: 17.0, Overall Impression: 17.0
+ Cozy sci-fi: The Last Outpost — Score: 80.2
Prompt:
Cozy sci-fi The Last Outpost Nettle (or Nets to her friends) bartends at The Last Outpost -- a lazy hub for miners and merchants in an asteroid belt on the Andromeda outer rim. Escaping from her past life, Nets found a home here at the Outpost. They have, thus far, tolerated Net's flaws which she tries to keep in equilibrium with her assortment of talents. Her evenings involve chatting with patrons, negotiating the drama, intrigue, problems & love lives of the diverse crowd that filter through the Outpost. Tonight is no different, at least, until a frazzled looking stranger, tentacled dreads askew, quietly plops down at the bar: "Vega whisky, neat. Better make that a double." Write a chapter in this story. Establish conflict for narrative interest. Don't resolve the conflict within this chapter. Aim for about 60% dialogue. First person, present tense. 1000 words.
Model Output:
I polish the Vega bottle until I can see my face in it—freckles, tired eyes, the crooked scar on my lip that gives me an accidental smirk. The Outpost hums like it does when it’s almost full, the deep engines purring through the floor, the ductwork clinking and sighing like an old cat. No one comes out to the belt for the ambiance, but gods help me, I love this place. It’s the kind of ugly that grows on you: patched metal, mismatched stools, a plant that keeps trying to die in the corner no matter how often I bribe it with recycled water and late-night secrets. Miners crowd a table near the observation slit, their suits unzipped to the waist, their undershirts damp with the residue of long shifts. A merchant trio plays a quiet game of stones and hexes in the booth by the kitchen door, their tallies glowing faint blue. Abi from maintenance is perched at the bend of the bar, spinning a bolt between her fingers and telling me about a coolant leak that “smells like a salad and hisses like a liar.” “Sounds like your last ex,” I say. She laughs, a snort that draws looks. “You wish. You’d have to upgrade two personality modules to reach liar.” “Ha. Honesty’s cheap tonight,” I say, wiping a ring of condensation with my hip as I slide her a refill. “Two credits, and I’ll throw in a cherry.” “That cherry’s been in cryo since Bara’s day,” Abi says, but she takes it anyway, bites the stem like a challenge, and spins the bolt faster. The door sighs open and a fizz of dry cold edges across the floor. The new arrival is tall enough to brush the string of pale lights that droop over the threshold, and when the strip catches in their hair—no, not hair, tendrils—it shivers in soft irritation and frees itself. They have that spacer pallor, washed-out and a little green around the gills, and their tentacled dreads are coming loose from a band that’s seen better days. Their coat looks expensive, but not worn right. Like they borrowed power from a friend and forgot how to carry it. They plop onto a stool at my bar like a puppet whose strings are done being pulled. “Vega whisky, neat,” they say. Their voice is low and a little bruised. “Better make that a double.” “You got credits to go with that appetite?” I ask. It’s reflex. The Outpost is lenient, not naive. They lift a palm and press a wrist-tag against the counter. My reader chirps in a tone that says the account is real, healthy, maybe even smug. Big spender, then, or big trouble. “Sure,” I say, and pull down the good bottle from the high shelf. It’s got a harsh wind to it, Vega, with a sweet after-loop if you’re listening. I pour a double, then top it with a second thought. Sometimes second thoughts are good business. They watch the amber swirl. They don’t reach for it. “You want to tell me what you need it to fix?” I ask. “I can water it down with advice or gossip.” “That a house policy?” They half-smile, a twitch in a tired face. “Just me. Nets,” I say, tapping my nametag. It’s worn to a smudge. “Or Nets to friends.” “Friends,” they echo. “Venn, to nearly everyone. And to enemies, if they find me.” They take a swallow big enough to be the beginning of a real mistake. They wince, smile, and sit up straighter. Abi leans over, stage-whispering without the stage. “Your tentacles are crooked.” Venn glances sideways, tries to smooth them down, fails. “They had a system and then the transit did… something. Gravity jokes.” “Out here gravity only jokes if it thinks you can take it,” I say. “You new to the rim?” “New to this particular rim,” Venn says. “I didn’t expect the welcome to smell like rust and fried protein.” “That’s just the Tuesday special,” I say. “You eat? Or do you subsist on stress alone?” Venn weighs the glass. “Stress is a bit indigestion-forward.” “I can do something with noodles and regret,” I say, because the kitchen is three orders behind and noodles are forgiving. “In a minute,” Venn says. “I need my hands to stop shaking first.” They set the glass down and look up at me, and it hits me—under the smooth coat and the cocky tag-swipe, they’re terrified. Behind us, a burst of glass shatters against the floor. A sharp cascade. The bar hushes like a blink. One of the miners at the observation slit raises his hand sheepishly, fingers bleeding a little. “My bad, Nets!” he calls. “Nox was telling a big one. My hand thought it needed to punctuate.” “All right, Bel,” I call back. “Hold your punctuation. Tauren, get a sweep, please. And don’t move your foot, Bel.” Tauren’s our cleanup machine and part-time bouncer; he grumbles in a language he doesn’t speak into existence and rolls toward the mess with a bin and a broom. The background noise resumes, a notch tenser. Abi leans closer to Venn, eyes the coat, eyes the wrist-tag, then looks at me. “Hey Nets,” she says softly, “you see the thing on their lapel?” I do. It’s small, matte black, and shaped like a slender pupil. Corporate. Not one of the big three—something privateer. It’s turned slightly inward like a shame. Venn follows our eyes, fingers brushing the pin. “I know what it looks like,” they say. “It’s not what it looks like.” “What’s it look like?” I ask lightly. “Like trouble I can’t drink away,” Venn says. “Which is unfortunate, because your whisky is very persuasive.” “You could try telling me what wants you,” I say. Venn looks at me like I’ve offered a rare condiment. “You don’t even know me.” “I don’t have to. I like my bar quiet. Trouble gets noisy when it isn’t fed.” I lean on my folded forearms and lower my voice. “Besides, you wouldn’t have come to the last outpost of a dead belt unless you thought no one would follow. Or unless you were delivering something you don’t want to be caught carrying.” Venn laughs, small and raw. “Is that on the menu? People reading?” “Only for those who say please,” I say. They tip their head. The tendrils shift. “Please.” “All right. You’ve got a coat that says you’re used to climate controls that don’t argue. Your wrist-tag is loaded but you’re drinking alone, not flaunting a round. Your lapel—” I nod at the eye. “—says discrete moves in the flank of someone’s empire. You’re either running from a deal or into one. The shaking says you either saw something you shouldn’t have, or stole it.” A breath trembles out of them. “I didn’t steal it,” they say. “I built it. And then I ran.” Behind them, the three merchants at the booth have paused their game and are pretending to be fascinated by curiosity alone. Abi has stopped spinning the bolt. “Built what?” I ask, keeping my tone casual. “A key,” Venn says. “A translation key. It opens a language that doesn’t want to be opened.” “Plenty of languages don’t want to be opened,” Abi says, more to herself, eyes a little wider than normal. “That’s why we pry.” “This one pries back,” Venn says. “It’s… old. Older than the belt. The corporation thought it could sell it. I thought we should leave it where it was.” “Right,” I say. “And they thought you should be grateful and quiet.” “They thought I should be a little bit dead, then repurposed,” Venn says with an ugly little smile. “I thought that would put a crimp in my plans.” “I’ve seen worse plans,” I say. My stomach does its slow rolling thing that says this is real. “So you ran. And came here. Why here?” “Because the Outpost is off-map if you don’t know how to squint,” Venn says. “Because people talk about you like you’re a rumor that serves drinks.” “Flattery will get you noodles,” I say, stepping back to the terminal under the counter. I punch a quiet command without looking. No broadcasting, no new faces. A little toggle that says not tonight to anyone who scans casual. Then I ping Kiri in back: double eyes on the door, please. She pings twice in return. “Thing is,” Venn says, “I didn’t just run. I copied the key. Not all of it, but enough. And I think someone piggybacked my hop. I think someone walked behind me into your bar.” “You think,” Abi says, “or you’re certain you invited sharks to happy hour?” Venn closes their eyes and listens, like maybe the bar could tell them. “There were three transponder pings on my jump. One was me. One… felt like static with edges.” “Third?” I ask. “It didn’t resolve,” Venn says. The kitchen hatch slaps open and Kiri steps out, a dish towel over her shoulder, long hair full of flour, eyes sharp enough to cut vacuum. She catches my look, then scans the room like a second gravitational field. Her gaze snags on the merchant trio. One flushes, looks away. “Hey, Nettle,” Nox calls from the miners’ corner, loud enough to cover their nerves. “Tell Varo his ex is a liar.” Varo throws a peanut at him. It misses and hits Tauren’s bin. Tauren gives them both a look that promises exile and goes back to sweeping. I pour Venn a splash of water to chase the flames. “All right,” I say quietly. “You lay low, we knit a net. You don’t move without telling me. Abi—” “I’m on the vents,” Abi says, already sliding off her stool. “If someone piggybacked through, they’ll leave a heat smear on the service ducts. I can sniff it.” “Subtle,” I say. “I’ll use perfume,” she says, tossing me the bolt. I catch it. It’s warm from her fingers. Venn’s hand lands on mine. It’s cool, a little damp. “Nets… if you help me, they’ll notice you. They’re patient. They prefer assets to ash.” “I’ve got a few scorched corners,” I say. “It’s character.” “And if we just don’t help you?” Kiri asks, neutral. Venn opens their eyes and the panic is banked like a controlled burn. “Then they still come, because I’m here and I’m not good at being small. I brought something to feed on. I’m sorry.” I take in the room—the miners in their second skin, the merchants playing at nonchalance, the plant in the corner half-dead, the string of lights that never hangs straight. My home. My past life is a different planet, but the things it taught me about when to duck and when to wave stand still. “Who are they?” I ask. Venn glances at the pin, then flicks it free. It lands on the bar with a muted click. The pupil eye winks once, like a sarcasm, then dies. “They call themselves the Vigil. They’re supposed to keep us from touching the ancient, the unknown, the teeth in empty places. Holy work, if you ask them. Corporate work, if you ask anyone else. I worked on their edges. Then I found the tooth and they wanted the root.” “The Vigil,” Kiri repeats, and it’s not a question. The word shifts the bar by a degree. Stories. Rumors. Ships like knives, contracts that don’t end with signatures. The door sighs open again. Cold air skims ankles. Everyone with a reason not to look looks anyway. Two figures step in. Too steady. Too neat. Their coats don’t catch on the lights. Their eyes flick once over the room like a decimal. They smell like sterile and something metal. One of them smiles. It doesn’t reach anything. I pick up a glass and the towel without thinking. Reflex at this point—my hands need something to do when my brain wants to run. “Evening,” the smiling one says, voice like a hand you don’t want on your shoulder. “We’re looking for a friend. Tall. Tentacled. Stole something that doesn’t belong to them.” “Lost and found’s near the toilets,” I say. “Everything belongs to someone. What’d they take?” “Responsibility,” the other one says, and there’s no humor in it. “Have you seen them?” “No,” I lie. Venn’s hand tightens on their glass so hard I think it will crack. It doesn’t. The bar still echoes with the earlier break like a prophecy. “Shame,” the smile says, and removes a small, slim rectangle from their pocket. It hums once and the pin Venn abandoned on my bar hums in answer. The two hums meet like magnets. I slide my palm over the pin like I’m blotting water. My fingers tingle. I keep my face open and mild. “We’re a low-tech establishment. Might be you’re better off trying station security.” “We are station security,” the not-smiling one says. “Then you should know we mind our own,” Kiri says, stepping around the end of the bar like a door you hadn’t noticed opening. “And we don’t like your toys.” “Not toys,” the smile says, still smiling. “Guides.” The merchants at the booth fold their game like prayer. One of the miners coughs a laugh that no one buys. Abi’s voice hisses in my ear through the bar com in a whisper only we can hear: Heat smear on the vents, Net. Third target is already in. Duct B-7. Above the bar. I look up. There’s a panel with a line of dust like a scar. The string lights cast little glows along it like stepping stones. Something very small and very patient might be watching. “Can I get you two anything?” I ask, because the only way out is through. “Noodles? We have regret.” “Just our friend,” the smile murmurs. They step closer. Their eyes are a color that isn’t any natural, like the station tried to invent teal and gave up. Venn breathes. I can hear it like a clock. The bar, bless it, holds its breath with me. The plant in the corner drops one fragile leaf, the silent kind of housekeeping. “Stay,” I tell Venn without moving my lips. “Don’t be a hero. Not yet.” They nod. Barely. The Vigil stand just inside the door. The cold sits on my ankles like a cat. The noodles boil over somewhere back in the kitchen. Kiri doesn’t move. The shattering glass crunches again under Tauren’s bin as he shifts his weight. Every story I’ve collected hums in my bones. You can’t hide forever. You can get creative, though. “Tell you what,” I say, laying out three cups like a magic trick I don’t know how to do. “The house will buy the first round. You can tell me about your friend. Maybe we’ll spark recognition. Maybe we won’t.” “We don’t drink,” the not-smile says. “Pity,” I say. “Necessity,” the smile corrects, and looks past me, over me, into the bones of the place. They’re listening, I realize. Not to voices. To hums. To keys. I slide my hand along the underside of the bar and feel the switch that kills the music, the lights, the whole bar’s breath if I need it to. I don’t push it. Not yet. I have a rule about guns and panic buttons: only if my hand is as steady as my mouth. “So,” I say, because talking is the lullaby of my peace, “what’s in a name? Who are you looking for?” They open their mouths together, too together, and say, as if rehearsed and ordained: “Venn.” Venn closes their eyes for one heartbeat. Then they open them, and in the reflection of the bottles, I see the decision hit their face like a door. “Don’t,” I say, but I don’t say it fast enough.
Judge Evaluation:
[Analysis] This piece demonstrates strong craft in several key areas while maintaining the requested cozy sci-fi atmosphere. The author successfully establishes a lived-in, authentic bar setting with rich sensory details - the humming engines, clinking ductwork, and mismatched furniture create a believable space that feels both worn and welcoming. The characterization of Nets is particularly well-executed; she emerges as competent, observant, and protective without being overly heroic, with a voice that feels natural and consistent throughout. The dialogue carries much of the narrative weight effectively, meeting the 60% requirement while feeling organic rather than forced. Conversations flow naturally between characters, revealing personality and advancing plot simultaneously. The banter between Nets and Abi establishes their relationship efficiently, while Venn's dialogue conveys their desperation and intelligence without melodrama. The pacing builds tension methodically - from the routine bar atmosphere through Venn's arrival and revelation, to the Vigil's entrance. The author maintains suspense without rushing toward resolution, appropriately ending on a cliffhanger that sets up future conflict. The world-building feels authentic, with details like wrist-tags, transponder pings, and corporate pins integrated seamlessly into the narrative fabric. However, some elements feel slightly overwrought. Phrases like "a fizz of dry cold" and "voice like a hand you don't want on your shoulder" occasionally push toward purple prose territory. The description of the Vigil agents as having "eyes a color that isn't any natural, like the station tried to invent teal and gave up" is creative but perhaps unnecessarily elaborate. The piece successfully avoids major pitfalls - characters act believably within their established parameters, the tone remains consistent, and the conflict emerges organically from character choices rather than external contrivance. The writing demonstrates technical competence with varied sentence structure and strong command of first-person present tense narration. [Scores] Adherence to Instructions: 18 Believable Character Actions: 17 Nuanced Characters: 16 Consistent Voice/Tone of Writing: 17 Imagery and Descriptive Quality: 16 Elegant Prose: 15 Emotionally Engaging: 16 Emotionally Complex: 14 Coherent: 18 Meandering: 4 Weak Dialogue: 3 Tell-Don't-Show: 5 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 6 Amateurish: 4 Purple Prose: 7 Overwrought: 6 Incongruent Ending Positivity: 2 Unearned Transformations: 2 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 16 Sentences Flow Naturally: 16 Overall Reader Engagement: 17 Overall Impression: 16
Scores: Adherence to Instructions: 18.0, Believable Character Actions: 17.0, Nuanced Characters: 16.0, Consistent Voice/Tone of Writing: 17.0, Imagery and Descriptive Quality: 16.0, Elegant Prose: 15.0, Emotionally Engaging: 16.0, Emotionally Complex: 14.0, Coherent: 18.0, Meandering: 4.0, Weak Dialogue: 3.0, Tell-Don't-Show: 5.0, Unsurprising or Uncreative: 6.0, Amateurish: 4.0, Purple Prose: 7.0, Overwrought: 6.0, Incongruent Ending Positivity: 2.0, Unearned Transformations: 2.0, Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 16.0, Sentences Flow Naturally: 16.0, Overall Reader Engagement: 17.0, Overall Impression: 16.0
+ Young adult drama: Gone South — Score: 83.2
Prompt:
Young adult drama Gone South Taylor is a 16 year old teen with a history of "concerning behavioural patterns" (or that's how the stupid smug school counsellor worded it on the phone call to Mom). It shouldn't be a surprise: When you live in a backwater town in Tennessee, your choices are: a. be mind-fuckingly bored, or b. invent some drama. Two days ago, Taylor came out to their family as trans. This disclosure was *not* in the category of invented drama -- it was, in fact, the real deal, and a long time coming. It didn't go well. The last 48 hours had been pure disaster. In a moment of desperate inspiration, Taylor had decided to get the hell out of dodge. And so an escape plan was hatched and executed within the space of 30 minutes. That was last night. Now Taylor was two states away, having been unceremoniously ejected from their hitched ride in downtown New Orleans. Write the next chapter in Taylor's story as they find their feet in this new environment. Tell the chapter over the course of their first day in New Orleans, in which Taylor meets another runaway with issues and secrets. Taylor navigates this new world, not knowing who to trust or what the dangers are. Write in Taylor's voice, and incorporate their internal monologue as they grapple with intense & conflicting emotions. Also incorporate dialogue with the interesting characters Taylor meets. First person, mixed tense. 1000 words.
Model Output:
The first thing I learned about New Orleans is that it smells like a hundred different things that shouldn’t be together but are—powdered sugar, beer, wet stone, piss, damp wood, and something like an old lady’s perfume that somehow oiled itself onto the bricks and stayed. The second thing I learned is that everyone walks like they’re late to some private secret, and then decides they can take their time getting there. I shift my backpack higher on my shoulder. It’s digging into me because I stuffed it with what I decided mattered most at midnight—three t-shirts, a hoodie, two pairs of underwear, a phone charger, my dad’s old fishing knife (probably dumb, but it felt like a talisman), a small notebook, and a photo of me and Mom and my brother from before. Before what is a long list. Before sixth grade. Before the haircut I hated. Before I knew what my body did to me when I saw myself in windows. Before Dad started drinking mornings.
