Before the call from the stupid counsellor who says “concerning behavioral patterns” like she’s describing weather. A saxophone wails somewhere behind me, jumping and laughing. It lifts and drops like a voice you can’t pin down. I turn into a street that looks like a movie set someone forgot to pack up. Beaded balconies. Flags. Strangers snapping pictures. The sun hits so bright it’s smug. I’m not used to this much sky. I tell myself I’m doing great. I got out. I’m here and not there. That’s supposed to be a start. My stomach is a pit. I had half a gas station burrito last night, gifted by a trucker who let me ride up front and didn’t ask questions I couldn’t answer, just offered me a napkin and pointed at the emptied seat between us like it was an invitation. He dropped me downtown at dawn and said, “Be careful,” in a voice that tried for neutral and missed. Then he was gone, and I was left with a city that felt like it could swallow me without chewing. It’s not the worst way to go. It’s not the best, either. “Yo, you look lost,” someone says. I turn and there’s this kid leaning against a lamppost, the kind of person who looks like they’re always leaning, like gravity is a joke. Skinny. Umbrella in one hand, even though it’s not raining. The other hand is wrapped in a blue bandana that might be hiding a tattoo or a cut. Hair like a home haircut gone right—shaved close on the sides, wild up top. They have a nose ring. They’re wearing a t-shirt with a faded cartoon alligator smoking a cigarette. They smile like it doesn’t cost them anything. “Or you look like everyone else,” they say. “Same thing.” I shift again, backpack strap biting. “Which one gets me less robbed?” “Neither,” they shrug. “But the trick is to look like you don’t have anything worth taking.” “Good,” I say. “I don’t.” They tilt their head like they’re reading me. The saxophone slides into something slower, drifting into the spaces between us. “First day?” they ask. “First hour,” I admit, and the word pushes out, honest and embarrassing. They nod. “You hungry?” “Yes,” I say so fast it comes out like a cough. They grin, then glance at a nearby shop where a man is dusting powdered sugar over mounds of hot squares. The air is sweet enough to make my eyes water. “Let’s get sugar. Then we’ll talk strategy.” “Strategy for what?” “For existing.” They step out, motioning me to follow. “I’m Pru.” “Taylor,” I say, and it feels like a statement I have to stand up straight for. My stomach does another somersault. I’m surprised to be saying it out loud to someone I met sixty seconds ago, and equally surprised at how right it fits in my mouth. They glance back, eyes scanning my face and then not lingering in the places I’m used to people lingering. “Hi, Taylor,” Pru says. “You got cash?” “A little.” “Save it. Watch.” Pru steps up to the counter, sliding a smile across it like they’re paying with it. “Hey, can we get one order and some water? It’s our first time.” The guy at the counter looks up, eyes tired. He looks me over with a quick swipe like a scanner, then returns to Pru’s smile. He snorts. “Everybody’s first time,” he says, but he hands over a small paper plate dusted like winter and two foam cups. We take them to the edge of the river where the concrete steps down and people sit in clumps like pigeons. The river is huge, brown and muscled, flexing in slow waves that look lazy until you see how hard they pull at the shore. I sit down, my knees bumping Pru’s. Powdered sugar floats off with every breath of wind and coats my black jeans in fingerprints. “Okay,” Pru says through a mouthful. “What’s your story?” I look at the water. In my head, the last forty-eight hours flicker like a broken slideshow: me in my room, door locked, hands shaking as I typed a text to Mom I couldn’t say out loud; her voice rising like steam when she read it; the way Dad stood up too fast, chair scraping, and then sat back down and rubbed his eyes like he wished his hands could erase everything; my brother standing at the microwave, frozen mid beep, like someone hit pause on him. The quiet that came after I ran out—the gas station, the hitch, the gray stretch of I-55, a Pitts truck stop bathroom where I cut the tag out of my sports bra because it was digging into me and cried for exactly sixty-four seconds into a paper towel because sixty-five wasn’t allowed. I pick at the beignet, fingers sticky. “I left,” I say. “From Tennessee.” Pru nods like that’s a sentence they understand. “Family stuff?” “Yeah.” The word feels like a stone in my mouth. “I told them something true. They didn’t want it.” They chew, slow. “They rarely do.” I don’t know what to do with that—comfort, proverb, warning. The sax picks up again behind us, a different tune, sharp and playful, and a kid around my age taps a rhythm against his skateboard with the heel of his hand, soft, like he’s keeping something alive. “What about you?” I ask, because it’s polite, because if I don’t ask, I’ll start to talk too much and spill everything. Pru licks sugar off their thumb. “I’m from here,” they say, and then roll their eyes at themselves. “Sort of. From a hospital here, from too many schools here, from people who live here. I left and came back. Gravity, you know? Had an aunt who said if you lie down on these cobblestones long enough, they’ll teach you all the secrets.” “That seems unsanitary,” I say. Pru grins. “Okay, yeah. Also most of the secrets are boring.” They flick a bit of sugar at the river. It makes a tiny comet and disappears. “You got somewhere to sleep?” The question lands heavy. I shake my head. “Don’t do the shelters,” they say fast. “Not unless you want your stuff to disappear. There’s a place under the bridge—looks sketch, is sketch, but it’s ours. You can’t stay longer than a couple nights. Cops sweep sometimes and it smells like dead shrimp, but you’ll be with people who’ll watch your bag. And there’s a church that does showers on Tuesdays.” “What day is today?” I ask. “Saturday.” Pru squints at the sky like it keeps time. “So you smell like sugar and river until then.” “I smell like running away.” “That too.” We sit there, chewing. The beignets are hot and real and sit warm in me like something kind. A tourist with a camera the size of my head asks Pru where the best bars are, and Pru points in three different directions and says, “Follow the loud,” which is accurate. When the tourist leaves, Pru leans closer. “Here’s the other trick. People will offer you things because they like the feeling of being someone who offers. Take the thing, not the person. If they want you to come somewhere, don’t. If they want you to owe them, don’t.” “What if they’re nice?” I ask. “They can be nice and still be a black hole,” Pru says. “Lots of nice black holes out here.” I look at the river again, at its broad brown back. “I don’t know who to trust,” I say, and the admission is raw, a scraped knee of a sentence. It scares me to say it, because I don’t want it to scare them away. “Trust yourself,” Pru says, like it’s easy. Then, softer: “Practice trusting yourself. It’s a muscle.” “My muscles are shaking,” I say. It’s supposed to be a joke but my voice goes weird and small on the last word. “Yeah,” Pru says, like they know that voice well. They nudge my knee with theirs. “I do this breathing thing when it’s bad. You want it?” “I don’t know.” I do. I don’t. I hate being told to breathe, like my body forgot on purpose. But I nod. “Okay,” they say. “In for four, out for six. We’re gonna pretend the sax is doing it with us.” They count quietly, and I do it, and I hate it, and also I don’t. The air tastes like sugar and river. The sax slides up a note and holds it, and I hold it too, and suddenly my ribcage feels less like a cage. When we stand up, I’m lightheaded but steady. Pru leads me through streets that twist into new streets, past shops that sell voodoo dolls with googly eyes, past a guy in a silver wig who’s been standing perfectly still for so long he’s part statue now, past a woman in a red hat who sings to herself in a way that makes me want to cry. The air presses damp against my skin and my clothes cling like they wish they were different. A man whispers “Pretty girl” at me and I flinch like it’s a slap. Pru doesn’t miss it. They drift, put themselves between me and the man, small but a wall anyway. We cut under the rumble of an overpass. The sky narrows into concrete. Pru points to a cluster of tarps and tents in a makeshift geometry. There’s graffiti everywhere—names layered over names like sediment. Someone has painted a huge blue eye on a pillar; it looks down and does not blink. I grip my backpack strap until it squeaks. “It’s okay,” Pru murmurs. “Act like you belong. Don’t look like a cop. Don’t talk like a social worker.” “This is my first day,” I remind them, as if that absolves me of all crimes. “Cool,” Pru says. “It’s my hundredth. I can spot both.” They nod to a couple of people—a woman in a purple headscarf who nods back, an older man organizing milk crates like he’s curating them. A boy with matted hair and a guitar case stares at me in a way that makes me see myself from the outside: a kid with a too-full backpack and knees that can’t decide if they want to lock or not, trying hard not to look scared and failing. Pru finds a spot against a pillar half-covered in vines. “You can stash your bag behind my tarp,” they say. “Put your knife in your pocket but don’t let anyone see it. People who flash knives get theirs taken. You can sleep with your shoes on. You can keep your hoodie for a pillow. If you cry, cry into it, not out loud.” “I’m not going to cry,” I say, which is the kind of sentence that makes crying a foregone conclusion. Pru shrugs. “Even if you do, New Orleans is loud. It’ll cover you.” They squint toward the street, like they’re listening for something. “Stay visible, but not to the wrong people. You’ll figure it. Also, drink water. Heat strokes don’t care about your trauma.” “Good,” I say. “Because my trauma’s busy.” That gets a laugh out of them, crazy bright. “You’ll be okay,” they say, like they know how to make it true. I don’t tell them about the text I sent my brother at dawn: I’m safe, I lied. He sent back a message that was only three dots for a long time, and then: okay. I’ve been trying to decide if that okay was about me or him. I don’t tell them that Mom called six times and left voicemails where she sounded like she was trapped in a tin can and wanted me to come home and apologize for embarrassing her. I don’t tell them that I deleted Dad’s contact just so I wouldn’t have to see his name. I don’t tell them that I’m shaking again, even after the breathing. Pru is talking to the woman in the purple headscarf, who hands me a bottle of water like she’s been expecting me. “You a friend of Pru’s, you my business,” she says. She has the kindest eyes. “Drink slow, baby.” “Thank you,” I say, and mean it so much it makes my throat go tight. Later, when the sun slides down into the gaps between buildings and the saxophone is still somehow playing far away, I walk to the edge of the camp and look out at the city glowing like it dressed up for me. The air tastes like salt and sweetness. I think about how the last forty-eight hours were a long scream, and somewhere in there was my voice, small and insistent. I came out and everything blew apart. Maybe that was the only way to make space. The sky is purple. The bridge is a rib cage. I am inside something’s body and also outside mine, and I’m trying to trust that both can be okay. Pru comes up beside me, shoulder to shoulder. “You ever think about leaving again?” I ask without looking at them. “Every day,” they say. “And I do, sometimes. And then I un-leave. You don’t have to pick a forever. You only have to pick a next.” “I keep picking wrong,” I say. “You picked sugar. You picked not the first creeper who looked at you. You picked me.” They bump me. “Those are good picks.” A car honks somewhere, a long lazy note that somehow harmonizes with the sax. I imagine calling Mom, imagine her picking up and saying my name the old way, with her mouth slow on it, like it belongs to someone we don’t know. I imagine saying, It’s Taylor. I don’t imagine beyond that. “Sleep,” Pru says. “Big rule: you don’t make decisions at night. Night lies.” I let out a breath I didn’t know I was holding. “Okay.” I crawl under the tarp beside Pru’s and roll onto my side, backpack against my chest like a pet I need to keep breathing. The concrete is hard, but I have been more uncomfortable in softer places. The noises of the bridge become a kind of lullaby, the saxophone thread stitching the day shut. I think about Tennessee: the slow roads, the scrape of crickets at night, the way the air there tastes like dryer sheets and cut grass. I think about New Orleans: the river’s shoulder, the fat laughter of a stranger’s song, Pru’s voice like a rope across a gap. I think: I am here. I think: I don’t know what happens next. I think: I am not done. Somewhere, a siren wails, and somewhere behind it, a sax wails back, and for a second it sounds like they’re talking. I close my eyes. I keep breathing. I am practicing. I am still here.
Judge Evaluation:
[Analysis] This piece demonstrates strong technical execution and emotional authenticity in portraying a transgender teen's first day as a runaway in New Orleans. The writing successfully captures Taylor's voice through a distinctive blend of vulnerability and defensive humor, with internal monologue that feels genuine rather than manufactured. The characterization is particularly strong. Taylor emerges as a fully realized person - scared but resilient, observant, and processing trauma in believable ways. Pru serves as an effective foil and guide, offering street wisdom without becoming a mere exposition device. Their dialogue feels natural and age-appropriate, avoiding both overly mature philosophical statements and unrealistic teen speak. The setting work is exceptional. New Orleans comes alive through sensory details that avoid tourist clichés while still capturing the city's distinctive atmosphere. The descriptions of smells, sounds, and the physical environment create immersion without overwhelming the narrative flow. The prose style effectively balances poetic moments with grounded realism. Phrases like "the river is huge, brown and muscled" and "gravity is a joke" demonstrate strong metaphorical thinking without becoming pretentious. The mixed tense approach works well, creating immediacy while allowing for reflection. The emotional complexity is well-handled. Taylor's conflicted feelings about family, identity, and survival feel authentic. The piece avoids both excessive sentimentality and unrealistic stoicism. The breathing exercise scene and the ending strike appropriate notes of cautious hope without suggesting easy solutions. Some minor weaknesses include occasional moments where the prose becomes slightly overwrought ("Night lies" feels a bit too neat), and a few instances where Taylor's observations seem slightly beyond their years. The pacing occasionally slows during descriptive passages, though not severely. The piece successfully fulfills the prompt requirements, maintaining first-person perspective, incorporating meaningful dialogue, and showing Taylor's navigation of a dangerous new environment while grappling with recent trauma. [Scores] Adherence to Instructions: 18 Believable Character Actions: 17 Nuanced Characters: 17 Consistent Voice/Tone of Writing: 18 Imagery and Descriptive Quality: 18 Elegant Prose: 16 Emotionally Engaging: 17 Emotionally Complex: 17 Coherent: 17 Meandering: 4 Weak Dialogue: 3 Tell-Don't-Show: 4 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 5 Amateurish: 3 Purple Prose: 5 Overwrought: 6 Incongruent Ending Positivity: 3 Unearned Transformations: 2 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 16 Sentences Flow Naturally: 16 Overall Reader Engagement: 17 Overall Impression: 17
Scores: Adherence to Instructions: 18.0, Believable Character Actions: 17.0, Nuanced Characters: 17.0, Consistent Voice/Tone of Writing: 18.0, Imagery and Descriptive Quality: 18.0, Elegant Prose: 16.0, Emotionally Engaging: 17.0, Emotionally Complex: 17.0, Coherent: 17.0, Meandering: 4.0, Weak Dialogue: 3.0, Tell-Don't-Show: 4.0, Unsurprising or Uncreative: 5.0, Amateurish: 3.0, Purple Prose: 5.0, Overwrought: 6.0, Incongruent Ending Positivity: 3.0, Unearned Transformations: 2.0, Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 16.0, Sentences Flow Naturally: 16.0, Overall Reader Engagement: 17.0, Overall Impression: 17.0
+ Dystopic-comedy: My Apocalypse Buddy — Score: 78.7
Prompt:
My Apocalypse Buddy Write a wry, dry short story of survival and adventure loosely in the style of Terry Pratchett, set in the post-technological collapse world. The story begins with the pair deliberating their predicament and intent for the day's mission, details the ways in which the mission inevitably goes sideways, and returns them to home base in multiple ways, worse for wear. [characters] - the animo-tronic stuffed koala, Chewy, from some long forgotten before time - midas, our hapless protagonist, who has a knack for finding himself in the worst kinds of trouble. 1000 words.
Model Output:
There are days when life plods along like a particularly grumpy tortoise, and there are days when it puts on stilts, paints its face, and charges down the road bellowing. This was a stilt day. Midas stood on the roof of the old library, which had survived the end of the world thanks to the twin virtues of municipal underfunding and unflattering architecture. The sky was an enthusiastic grey. It threatened rain out of habit rather than malice. At his feet, an animo-tronic stuffed koala named Chewy blinked awake with the soft whirr of a dying blender. It would be generous to call Chewy lifelike, unless life had, at some point, involved a lot more whirring and one eye light that flickered when he was annoyed. He had the fur of a teddy you’d find at a charity shop, the weight distribution of an anvil, and a personality that could be described as mercenary if you’d never met an actual mercenary and had instead read about one while drunk. “Right,” Midas said, clapping his hands together in a way that attempted competence and got as far as hollow optimism. “Plan of action.” Chewy stared at him. The flicker in his left eye wasn’t quite a wink; it was more an electrical shrug. “Food,” Midas said. “We need food. And possibly shoes that don’t soak up water like grief. Also bandages. I am running a personal best for cuts sustained in a week.” Chewy, who spoke rarely and generally when he felt words could inflict maximum inconvenience, said, “And there’s the matter of the wall.” They’d found it last night on the way back from bartering three cans of peaches for a spoon that definitely used to be a component of something else. The wall was the back of an old train depot where vines had taken over like retirees running a coup. On it, someone had spray-painted in large, tidy letters: IF YOU CAN READ THIS, YOU’RE HEADING THE WRONG WAY. Underneath, in smaller, chaotic scrawl: There’s a refuge in the wilds. Walk where the sky is missing. Knock three times. Midas had stared at it until the dusk turned into the kind of night that encourages introspection and bad decisions. “What does ‘where the sky is missing’ even mean?” he asked. Chewy’s eye flickered. “A tunnel, a canopy, a storm drain,” he said. “Or a place where the only view is into your own idiocy.” Midas had the sort of resourcefulness that flowered only under pressure, which meant that during the lull beforehand he tended to wilt in anticipation. But even he had to admit a cryptic promise of sanctuary beat rooting through cupboards for tins of turnip chunks. Now, under the grumpy sky, they sorted gear. Midas had a backpack made of patched canvas, two bottles of water, a lockpick set that didn’t look like it had been stolen from a children’s magic kit (it had), and a knife that had seen better days and worse decisions. Chewy brought to the table a carbon fiber backbone, a pair of retractable claws that were designed to open packaging and could, in a pinch, open other things, and a heuristic processor that had once, according to a faded brochure, helped toddlers identify shapes, colors, and poor life choices. They set out. The city had done what cities do when people stop telling them not to: it went green and weird. Trees sprouted from the tops of buses like enthusiastic haircuts. Foxes used the tube stations as front doors. Something large and opinionated had taken up residence in the opera house and sang at night in tones that rattled windows and made Midas wish he had once, just once, appreciated a matinee. They followed the old freight line out of the city, where tracks buckled and vanished beneath a tide of dandelions. Midas kept one eye on the ground for holes, and one on the horizon for the sort of moving horizon that meant trouble. Chewy sat in the crook of Midas’s bag, like a judgmental hood ornament. “Where the sky is missing,” Midas said. “Storm drain?” Chewy said, “River culvert. Or cave. Or the underside of a bridge.” “Bridges have sky,” Midas said. “You can still see it.” “I meant the underside,” Chewy said. “Not everyone lives on the top of things.” By noon they found the first candidate: a stretch where the road slid into a ravine and the bridge over it had collapsed with all the grace of an elephant in a hammock. Beneath the remnants hung a dark mouth of concrete, the storm drain gaping like a dentist who had forgotten what he was supposed to do with teeth. On the wall beside it, in chalk, was a rough symbol: three lines, a circle, and an arrow pointing into the drain. “Subtle,” Midas said. “Possibly a trap,” Chewy said. He sounded almost hopeful. They went in. The air inside the drain had an opinion about lungs. It was damp, old, and crowded with memories. Midas used a little hand lamp powered by the sort of dynamo crank that made you question your commitment to light. The beam chased graffiti, old shopping carts, and the skeleton of a bicycle that had given up and embraced rust. They walked until the light from the entrance was not a light but a suggestion. The tunnel forked like a politician’s promises. On one wall, splashed in paint that had once been red and now resembled regret: SKY MISSING AHEAD. A circle, three lines. An arrow pointing left. “Left it is,” Midas said. It turned out to be right, in the sense that it wasn’t immediately wrong. They walked until the passage widened into a chamber where water pooled and dripped with metronomic malice. On the far side was a metal door, the sort that looked like it had never been meant for people, which was the sort most people ended up using anyway. Someone had scratched into it: KNOCK THREE TIMES. Midas glanced at Chewy. Chewy shrugged, a movement achieved by articulating both shoulders in a way that made him look like an animate handbag. Midas knocked. Firm. Three times. Things happened, as they do. The door didn’t open. Instead, somewhere beyond it, a mechanism engaged with a sound that suggested it had wanted oil and got promises instead. The floor shifted. The room adjusted its opinion of gravity. Midas swore. Chewy did not, but his eye lit up like a dawn alarm. The floor tilted. Water sloshed. A grate slid back. The chamber decided that up and down were negotiable and that Midas should go down, specifically with speed. He grabbed for the door handle, which came off in his hand with the affronted ease of something that hadn’t been touched in a decade and didn’t appreciate the sudden intimacy. He fell. If you have never ridden an underground water sluice in a world that has given up on health and safety, imagine a slide designed by a vengeful river. Midas pinballed between walls, scraped knees, elbows, more knees (it felt like he had extra), and then emerged into light and the immediate embrace of a shallow stream and a bed of gravel that had ambitions of being knives. He lay there. The sky above through the cleft in the earth was the color of sighing. Chewy plopped onto his chest a heartbeat later, surprisingly heavy for something meant to be hugged by children. “You okay?” Midas said, and then, because priorities, “Did you see where the handle went?” Chewy blinked. “Downstream,” he said. “Along with your dignity.” Midas sat up. The ravine they had been deposited into was narrow, choked with brambles and the occasional shopping trolley fossilized in mud. A path of sorts wound along one side, trampled enough to suggest other feet. On a rock someone had painted a neat, dry little circle with three lines. “This way,” Midas said. He got to his feet and made the sort of noise you make when your body is considering a strike. They followed the marks—circle, lines, arrow—past the broken skeleton of an SUV and under a curtain of ivy. The world narrowed, then widened like a held breath. Trees leaned in, then out. The stream split and rejoined itself, having an argument in water. They came to a clearing in which an old billboard had fallen forward to make a lean-to. On it, faded from sun and weather, an advertisement insisted serenely that two for one was the smart choice. Beneath, someone had constructed a door from pallet wood. On that, in careful, small lettering: KNOCK THREE TIMES. Midas looked at Chewy. Chewy looked back. “Last time didn’t go tremendously well,” Midas said. “Perhaps knock twice and a half,” Chewy said. “See what sort of personality we’re dealing with.” Midas knocked three times. The wood shuddered, the latch lifted, and the door opened. Inside was... not a refuge. It was better and worse. It was a person. She was middle-aged in the way that meant she had managed to get to the middle, which in these times was practically an act of sorcery. She had a rifle slung on her shoulder, a braid like a length of rope, and eyes that had seen too many storms and had decided to only be surprised by sunsets. “Supplies?” she said. “Refuge,” Midas said, trying for charming and landing somewhere near damp. “Not for you,” she said. Then, after a moment’s study, “Unless you have information. Or the ability to fix things that won’t stop beeping.” Chewy’s eye flickered. He leaned forward. “Identify the beeping,” he said. She stepped aside. Inside, the palette of the apocalypse: shelves of jars, a cot, a table with salvaged tools, a radio that was protesting its own existence in thin, terrified chirps. A clockwork contraption sat in the corner, winding itself down with tenacity. It looked like someone had attempted to reinvent electricity using spite and copper wire. Chewy waddled over, popped a panel with a claw, and made a noise that wasn’t quite a tsk. “Your capacitor is sulking,” he said. “And your diode is nostalgic for better times.” The woman stared at the koala, which was a reasonable reaction. “Is it...?” “Annoying,” Midas supplied. “Chewy,” Chewy said. “Animo-tronic, version 7.1, with adaptive heuristic co-processor and sarcastic overlay, factory default.” Midas, who had found Chewy two years earlier in the clawed-up remains of a department store gift section, had learned that introducing himself had mixed results. People either wanted to adopt him or sell him. Both would do him no good. While Chewy tinkered, the woman eyed Midas. “Name,” she said. “Midas Miller,” he said. “I break things by looking at them, but occasionally they break in the right direction.” She nodded like she understood that. “Call me Raf,” she said, which was probably not her name any more than Midas was his, but might be close. Chewy disconnected a wire and the beeping stopped like a thought cut off. The silence that followed was large enough to park relief in. Raf exhaled. “Great. In exchange, you can have this.” She handed Midas a cloth bundle. Inside: jerky, a wrapped bar that claimed to be meal replacement and almost certainly replaced meals with wistfulness, and a coil of sturdy cord. “You can also have advice,” she said. “That graffiti? Decoys. Some lead here because I’ve got a sense of humor. Most lead nowhere or to places you don’t want to meet in daylight.” “Why?” Midas said. “Because people,” Raf said. “But there is a refuge. Farther out. In the wilds, proper. A greenhouse with teeth. If you still want to chase poetry. You’ll need to cross the flats and follow the line of dead pylons until the one that leans like a drunk. The sky’s missing there, too. It’s a canopy, but it bites.” Chewy’s eye flickered. “He likes poetry,” he said, indicating Midas with a nod that could have been affectionate if it hadn’t been delivered in the tone used for terminal conditions. “I like promises there might be a place where bandages are not an aspirational concept,” Midas said. He tucked the bundle into his bag. “Thank you.” Raf shrugged. “Fix the radio properly next time. Then we’ll talk membership.” It was only then that the world remembered it had weather. Rain came in, sideways and interested. Raf closed the door. They waited in the lean-to watching the world get rinsed. Chewy huddled in Midas’s lap, the weight of him like a reassurance that had failed quality control but passed the heart. When the rain let up, they made their goodbyes and stepped back into a world that smelled briefly richer and looked exactly as ruinous as before. The way back had a way of happening all at once. They chose a different path, because Midas had a theory about shortest distances and Chewy had a theory about how Midas’s theories ended. They cut across a stretch of flats that had once been a suburban dream of cul-de-sacs and now was a dream of wetlands and mosquitoes with ambition. Midas stepped into a puddle that was not a puddle and discovered the existence of sinkholes as personal acquaintances. Chewy slid from the bag, spat water, and managed to look offended on every fiber. They reached the ravine and followed the stream back to the sluice, which was not an option unless one was a fish with a death wish. The alternate was a scramble up a slope that pretended to be soil and turned out to be mud with career plans. Midas climbed, slipped, clawed, swore, laughed because it was either that or cry. Chewy, strapped against his chest now, provided insightful commentary about leverage and poor choices. At one point a root came away with a pop and they both slid several feet, collecting an entire ecosystem on the way. They made it to the top with the exhaustion that reads like triumph if you squint. Then they took the long walk back along the rail line, under a sky that had decided to go from grey to the color of evening with no intervening negotiation. At the library, home base with its barricaded door and its smell of paper and dust and the ghosts of librarians who still, Midas felt, disapproved of loud thoughts, he set Chewy on the table and unwrapped Raf’s bundle. They ate in silence that tasted like salt and relief. He put the cord with the other salvaged cord, which was to say he put it in the drawer marked Useful Things For Later. Which, while true, also contained a seashell, a chess pawn, and a battery whose terminal had been chewed by someone who thought it was candy. Chewy flickered his eye. “You are bleeding,” he said in the flat way medical professionals on their third twelve-hour shift say “Again?” Midas looked at his arm. A long, thin cut where brambles had made a point. He went to the shelf, took the last decent bandage, and considered it like a currency. He put it back and used a strip of gauze and hope instead. They sat. The rain returned distantly, a rumor of water on the world. “You know,” Midas said, “we could stay here. Not chase poetic hints. Turn this place into something. Plant tomatoes on the roof and learn to love dust.” Chewy’s voice was softer than his metal throat usually allowed. “You could,” he said. “But you won’t. You will listen to the part of you that still believes there are doors that open and rooms on the other side where someone says ‘Welcome’ and means it.” Midas stared at the darkness rimmed with window. Sometimes he hated how right a stuffed animal could be. “Also,” Chewy added, “you will go because I am curious about a greenhouse with teeth.” Midas laughed, which tugged at a dozen fresh aches. He lay back on a nest of old blankets that had once been a couch and before that possibly a raft. Chewy climbed onto his chest and settled with a whirr, a warm, mechanical puff of breath that was not breath but could pass in low light. They were, by every measure that counted, worse off than when they had set out that morning. Midas had a new cut, mud embedded in inconveniences, and the knowledge that the world contained at least three more ways to be dropped by a floor. He also had jerky, a coil of cord, and the fact of Raf, which proved that not all doors led to shafts. Some opened to people who, for reasons unfathomable as weather, offered something and asked something in return. It was a trade he could understand. Outside, the city rearranged its shadows. Inside, Chewy’s motor purred into a slow standby. Midas closed his eyes, and as he drifted he saw, behind his lids, dead pylons leaning like drunks, and beyond them a canopy that bit. It felt like the sort of place you went when you wanted to find trouble and, by accident, home. It was a stilt day. Tomorrow, perhaps, the world would take off the stilts and try something sensible. But this was the world. It would always choose height and wobbly drama. So would Midas, probably. The trick, he thought as sleep took him with both hands, was to have a good koala to hold the ladder.
Judge Evaluation:
[Analysis] This piece demonstrates a strong grasp of Terry Pratchett's distinctive voice and style, successfully capturing his dry wit, philosophical observations wrapped in humor, and the ability to find profound meaning in absurd situations. The opening line immediately establishes the Pratchettesque tone with its metaphorical comparison of life to a "grumpy tortoise" versus putting on "stilts, paints its face, and charges down the road bellowing." The characterization is particularly well-executed. Midas emerges as a believable post-apocalyptic survivor with realistic flaws and motivations, while Chewy transcends what could have been a gimmicky concept to become a genuinely engaging character with distinct personality traits. The relationship between them feels authentic, built on mutual dependence and genuine affection beneath the surface snark. The world-building is economical but evocative, painting a picture of civilization's collapse through specific, telling details like "Trees sprouted from the tops of buses like enthusiastic haircuts" and the opera house's mysterious new resident. The author avoids the trap of over-explaining the apocalypse, instead letting readers infer the state of the world through observed details. The plot structure adheres well to the prompt's requirements, beginning with deliberation, proceeding through a mission that goes sideways in multiple ways, and returning the characters home "worse for wear" but with some gains. The mission's complications feel organic rather than forced, and the encounter with Raf provides both plot advancement and character development. The prose demonstrates considerable skill, with metaphors that feel fresh rather than strained ("grief" as something shoes soak up, silence "large enough to park relief in"). The dialogue captures distinct voices for each character and serves multiple functions - advancing plot, revealing character, and providing humor. However, the piece occasionally veers toward being slightly overwrought in its metaphorical language, and some descriptions, while clever, border on purple prose. The pacing is generally strong but has moments where the descriptive passages slow momentum slightly. The ending, while thematically appropriate, edges toward sentimentality that feels somewhat incongruent with the established tone, though it doesn't completely undermine the piece. The story succeeds in creating genuine emotional engagement through the relationship between the characters and their shared resilience in the face of an indifferent world. The humor never feels forced or inappropriate to the setting, and the philosophical undertones enhance rather than overwhelm the narrative. [Scores] Adherence to Instructions: 18 Believable Character Actions: 17 Nuanced Characters: 16 Consistent Voice/Tone of Writing: 17 Imagery and Descriptive Quality: 16 Elegant Prose: 15 Emotionally Engaging: 16 Emotionally Complex: 14 Coherent: 17 Meandering: 6 Weak Dialogue: 4 Tell-Don't-Show: 5 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 4 Amateurish: 3 Purple Prose: 7 Overwrought: 6 Incongruent Ending Positivity: 7 Unearned Transformations: 3 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 16 Sentences Flow Naturally: 16 Overall Reader Engagement: 17 Overall Impression: 16
Scores: Adherence to Instructions: 18.0, Believable Character Actions: 17.0, Nuanced Characters: 16.0, Consistent Voice/Tone of Writing: 17.0, Imagery and Descriptive Quality: 16.0, Elegant Prose: 15.0, Emotionally Engaging: 16.0, Emotionally Complex: 14.0, Coherent: 17.0, Meandering: 6.0, Weak Dialogue: 4.0, Tell-Don't-Show: 5.0, Unsurprising or Uncreative: 4.0, Amateurish: 3.0, Purple Prose: 7.0, Overwrought: 6.0, Incongruent Ending Positivity: 7.0, Unearned Transformations: 3.0, Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 16.0, Sentences Flow Naturally: 16.0, Overall Reader Engagement: 17.0, Overall Impression: 16.0
+ Romance: Love in the Limelight — Score: 80.7
Prompt:
Romance: Love in the Limelight. Write one scene within a larger story set in Wales. A famous (fictional) actor ducks into a small-town bookstore to escape paparazzi. The scene takes us through the characters meeting in this odd circumstance. Over the course of the scene, the actor and the bookstore owner have a conversation charged by an undercurrent of unspoken chemistry. Write the actor as somewhat of a rogue with a fragile ego, which needs to be fed by having everyone like him. He is thoroughly charming, but the bookstore owner seems (at least superficially) immune to this; which paradoxically provokes a genuine attraction and derails the charm offensive. The bookstore owner, despite the superficial rebuffs of the actor's charm, is inwardly more than a little charmed and flustered despite themselves. Write primarily in dialogue, in the distinct voices of each character. 1000 words.
Model Output:
“Shut the door,” he hissed, half-laughing, half-breathless, as he backed in among a helter-skelter display of used mysteries and Welsh poetry. The bell above the door gave a traitorous jangle. Outside, the rain on the high street made a curtain of noise, and somewhere beyond that were shouts that sounded like a crowd on the cusp of disappointment. The person behind the counter didn’t move at first. They were perched on a stool with a pencil behind one ear, the sort of person who had grown comfortable guarding their own quiet. Dark hair, a cardigan with a rip at the elbow, a book open flat in front of them. They looked up, blinked once, and didn’t say what everyone else ever said. The man’s sunglasses were ridiculous in the weak Welsh light, but he kept them on as if they were a legal requirement. He pushed the door closed himself, pressed the brass handle gently until the latch clicked, and then pressed his back flat against it with a theatrical shudder. “If you’ve any mercy in your soul,” he said, “you’ll pretend I’m not in here.” The person at the counter said, “We close at five.” “It’s three-forty.” He flapped a hand, sending a droplet of rain into an arc that landed on a copy of Dylan Thomas. “Time is elastic in a crisis.” “Is it.” The pencil earner turned the page without looking down. They sounded unimpressed in a way that wasn’t cruel, only plain. “You’re dripping on the poetry.” He glanced down, winced, and with a flourish took off the sunglasses and then the jacket, peeling it away from a black t-shirt that clung to a chest recognizable from half the billboards in Britain. He draped the jacket carefully over a stand of postcards—waterfalls, castles—and then finally looked up at the person at the counter and smiled the smile that had its own agent. They didn’t swoon. They did look like they blinked again, slower, like someone whose brain had experienced a small delay. “If you’ve been followed,” they said, “I’m not a safe house. I’m a bookshop.” “A bookshop,” he echoed, stepping forward between the stacks. “The noblest of all houses. A sanctuary. I’m in need of sanctuary.” He held out his hands. “Miles Harland.” The name hung in the shop like a scent. Miles Harland—capers, brooding comedies, a face that had been in a thousand kitchens on a thousand magazines. The pencil-earner did not take his extended hand. “I know who you are.” “Well,” he said. The hand hung for a beat, then tucked itself into a pocket with a neat sleight. “I suppose honesty’s better than pretending I’m a plumber with a very committed publicist.” He grinned. “And you are…?” “A person trying to tally up takings with someone breathing on my books. Could you not drip?” He stepped back, contrite and giddy all at once. “I’ve been told I drip charm,” he said, and then winced. “That came out worse than intended. I can pay reparations for the Thomas.” “Buy it,” they said. “That’s more or less how bookshops work.” He put a hand to his heart. “Finally, a woman—” They raised an eyebrow. “Gender assumptions in a shop full of Judith Butler.” “A person,” he corrected instantly, with a little bow, “who says what they mean. I like you.” “That’s nice,” they said carefully, and it didn’t sound like an invitation. But their cheeks had warmed, betraying them to the unkind fluorescent light. “Look, if they’re out there—” “They are. A small parade. I ducked into Jones’s bakery, bought five bara briths, thought I’d buy their love with currants—didn’t work.” “You can’t buy Welsh people with bread.” “I’m learning so much.” He tucked the sunglasses into his t-shirt collar. “Do you have a back exit?” “No. There’s a courtyard. Then a wall. Then Mrs. Pritchard’s laundry.” “I can do a wall.” “You can, but can Mrs. Pritchard do you?” “That sounds like the beginning of a very different story.” He leaned in and then immediately leaned back, as if their steady gaze had touched him. “I mean. I don’t wish to scandalize Pritchards.” There was a faint smile perched at the corner of their mouth now, like a bird on a wire. They closed the ledger. “I’m Carys.” “Miles,” he said again, irrationally pleased to repeat. “It’s a nice name. Carys. What does it mean again?” “Love,” they said, too quickly, and then regretted it. “In Welsh.” “Ah. That’s convenient.” He clasped his hands. “We can pretend this is fate. The tabloids adore fate.” “We can pretend you’re a paying customer.” “I’m being gently herded.” He looked around. “May I browse until the lion moves on?” “You may browse,” Carys said. “But if anyone comes in asking if I’ve seen you—” “I was never here.” “You were never here.” He gave a salute, then turned to the nearest shelf and touched spines with reverent fingers. He was not pretending about that, at least; he ran his thumb along the embossed titles with a thoughtful air. “Do you recommend anything for a man on the run?” he asked. “Perhaps a manual on time travel or a map of rabbit holes.” “‘The Mabinogion,’” Carys said. “If you’re going to vanish, do it mythically.” He slid the book out, weighed it. “I’ll trade you. You choose something for me; I’ll buy it, and you’ll keep my secret.” He glanced back. “And in return, I’ll—” “I don’t need a favor.” “Everyone needs a favor. Even if it’s just someone to hold your ladder while you change a lightbulb.” “I can change a lightbulb.” “Of course you can.” He looked genuinely pleased. “You’re Carys. I bet you can do most things.” He watched their fingers, which had settled on the edge of the ledger, as if it were a lifebuoy they refused to let go. “You always like this?” he asked lightly. “Unimpressed. It’s refreshing. It’s alarming.” “I’m polite to everyone.” “I didn’t say you weren’t polite.” “I’m just not… I don’t have much time for performance.” He put the Mabinogion down, the color of the cover suddenly too loud. “That’s fair.” There was a brief quiet, filled with the rain’s drumming and the whisper of the little heater. He fiddled with the postcards, set one upright. “I once hid in a church in Kraków,” he said suddenly, as if he’d taken a dare. “After a premiere. There was a cloister and a courtyard and a monk with a very firm sense of right and wrong. He told me I should confess.” “Did you?” “I confessed that I had eaten a child’s packet of crisps that didn’t belong to me on a set in two-thousand-and-eleven. He didn’t laugh.” His grin flickered, then settled. “He told me to stop lying to myself.” Carys, who had not invited depth but now that it was offered couldn’t entirely refuse it, said, “Are you?” He shifted, leaned against a shelf he shouldn’t have leaned on, caught it with one hand before it tipped. He produced a neat self-salvage, all the while talking. “I lie all the time. It’s the job. But there’s a particular lie I tell that I’ve been told I should stop. That everyone likes me.” He glanced at Carys, quickly, then away. “That everyone has to like me.” Carys felt something in their chest, a softness they’d trained like a dog, telling it to sit. “Why would that be a lie?” “Because it’s not possible. And because I behave as if it is. Makes me shallow. Makes me a nuisance. Makes me—” He gestured around at the room, and then at his own face, a sweep of hand that somehow both mocked and defended himself. “This.” “And you want me to dislike you,” Carys said, flatly. He flinched, then smiled into it. “I want you to like me for the right reasons.” “And you think not laughing at your jokes is a start.” “I think you not laughing at my jokes is a revelation.” He exhaled. “What about you, Carys? Do you hide in your own shop when you can’t be liked?” “I live above it,” they said. “Hiding is implied.” “And do you—” He wove a hand in the air, as if painting something. “Want everyone to like you?” “No.” The word was out before they could soften it. It sounded like a blade. They regretted the blade. “I don’t want to be noticed, most days.” He turned fully to them, as if this warranted a stage light. “Why?” “Because…” Carys looked down at the ledger, at the neat stacks of pound coins. They decided to perhaps tell a smaller story instead of the larger one. “When I was twelve, there was a reading competition. Stupid thing, really. My dad worked late; my mum made me recite ‘Fern Hill’ in the kitchen while she did the washing up. I knew it by heart. I got up there and saw my teacher’s face—Mr. Hughes, who used to pick his ear with a paperclip—and I went blank. Not a word. Nothing. Whole room waited. And then everyone clapped anyway because they’re polite, and my mum cried in the car because she didn’t know what to say. I haven’t liked rooms since.” His face softened. “Carys.” “It’s nothing,” they said, brisk. “People have bigger problems.” “Yes,” he said. “They do. And also, that isn’t nothing.” He looked at the door as if expecting it to vanish and then back at them. “I don’t think you’re unimpressed. I think you’re careful. That’s different.” “I own a shop in a town where news is the color of Mrs. Pritchard’s pegs. Careful is survival.” He raised his hands in surrender. “Teach me your ways.” “Lesson one: stop touching everything.” They smiled despite themselves as he snatched his hand away from the display of handmade bookmarks. “Lesson two: if you’re going to hide, you buy something, you don’t talk too loudly, and you don’t ask personal questions.” “Too late on all counts.” He leaned his hip against the counter now, uninvited, but he kept a respectful distance, like a cat pretending not to be on the table. “What’s the best book you own that no one buys?” Carys reached under the counter, pulled out a slim volume with a cracked spine. “R. S. Thomas. He was a vicar up in Eglwysfach. Miserable as a thundercloud. It’s very good.” “Miserable as a thundercloud,” he repeated, delighted. “That’s a review quote waiting to happen.” He took the book, thumbed through, landed on a poem as if directed by chance. He read a couple of lines under his breath, his mouth moving, then he looked at Carys as if he’d been given something small and sharp, a pin that had pricked him awake. “This is—” “—very good,” they finished, and the smile actually got to their eyes this time. “And very much not about liking anyone.” “I’ll take it.” He put it down as if it had weight. “And the Mabinogion. And whatever I offended Dylan with. And—” He pointed at the postcard of a grey, moulting coastline. “That. So I can remember where I hid.” “You’re not the first person to choose here,” Carys said, surprising themselves by saying it. “To duck in. I mean.” “Am I the most dramatic?” “You’re the dampest.” He laughed, and the laugh ricocheted into the stacks and came back softer. Outside, a shout rose and then fell away, like a wave that had changed its mind. The rain eased. “Your army is losing interest,” Carys said. “I’d give it five minutes.” “Do you want me to go?” Carys fiddled with the till drawer. The question had been light; the answer was not, not entirely. “Do I want anyone to go?” They shrugged. “I like quiet. You’re… not quiet.” “I can be quiet.” He lowered his voice. “Watch.” “You’re impossible.” “I’m famous.” He spread his hands in a way that mocked himself again, and Carys realized that the performance wasn’t armor so much as habit, like chewing gum. “It will fade.” “What, the fame?” “The impossibility.” He hesitated. “Maybe.” He looked so suddenly young that Carys felt the dog in their chest stand up and wag its tail without permission. “I’ll ring you up,” they said, businesslike, and then, against their own policy, they added, “There’s a kettle in the back if you want—” “Yes,” he said too eagerly, then softened. “If it’s not a trouble.” “It’s a kettle.” He followed them through the narrow gap in the counter, careful not to brush their hip, though the air between them sparked at the restraint. The tiny back room smelled like damp and cardboard and the lemon cleaner Carys liked. The kettle took its time in the Welsh way. He stood with his hands in his pockets, looking out at the little square of the courtyard and the wall beyond. “I can do that wall,” he said again, mostly to make them smile. Carys didn’t look; they did smile. “Mrs. Pritchard has a dog.” “I can charm dogs.” “Not this one.” He turned his face toward them, and it was the first time he didn’t seem to be thinking about what his face was doing. “Can I come back?” “To buy more R. S. Thomas?” “To—” He stopped, fumbled, then found the truth at a level that didn’t feel like a trick. “To sit in the quiet and be seen by someone who doesn’t clap anyway.” The kettle clicked. Carys poured. The steam curled up like something beginning. “We’ll see,” they said, and though they’d meant it to be a deflection, it landed like an invitation. Their hand bumped his when they passed him the mug. Heat leapt up nerve endings. Neither of them moved away immediately. He took it, his knuckle knocking theirs once more on purpose or accident, and he smiled in a diminished way that was somehow larger. “You’re careful,” he said. “I can be careful too.” “I doubt that.” “I can try.” They stood there for a moment, the sound of rain softening to a whisper, the murmur of the shop ahead of them an empty room waiting to be filled. Outside, someone called his name, less certain now. The world did what the world does, which is move on. He sipped, made a face. “Too hot.” “Wait,” Carys said. And they both did.
Judge Evaluation:
[Analysis] This piece demonstrates sophisticated craft in several key areas while maintaining strong adherence to the prompt requirements. The author successfully creates the requested Welsh bookstore setting with a famous actor (Miles Harland) seeking refuge from paparazzi, establishing both characters with distinct voices and the specified personality traits. The characterization is particularly strong. Miles is convincingly portrayed as a charming rogue with a fragile ego who needs validation, while his genuine attraction emerges when faced with Carys's apparent immunity to his usual tactics. Carys is well-developed as someone who appears unimpressed but is internally affected, with their backstory about the childhood recitation adding authentic depth. The gender-neutral pronouns for Carys are handled naturally without drawing undue attention. The dialogue carries the scene effectively, with each character maintaining a distinct voice throughout. Miles's theatrical tendencies ("Time is elastic in a crisis") contrast nicely with Carys's dry, practical responses ("We close at five"). The banter feels organic and reveals character while advancing the romantic tension. The prose quality is generally high, with evocative imagery ("the rain on the high street made a curtain of noise") and well-crafted metaphors. The Welsh setting feels authentic through specific details like bara brith, Dylan Thomas, and local references. The pacing builds naturally from the initial encounter through deeper revelation. However, there are some areas where the writing occasionally becomes overwrought. Phrases like "smiled the smile that had its own agent" and "the dog in their chest stand up and wag its tail" veer toward purple prose. Some metaphors feel forced, particularly the extended comparison of Carys's emotions to a trained dog. The emotional development feels earned rather than rushed. Both characters reveal vulnerability gradually - Miles's confession about needing everyone to like him, and Carys's story about the childhood recitation failure. The romantic tension builds through small moments and restraint rather than grand gestures. The ending strikes an appropriate note of possibility without forcing resolution. The final exchange about waiting feels natural and leaves room for development while providing satisfying closure to this particular encounter. The piece successfully balances humor with deeper emotional currents, creating characters who feel real despite the somewhat contrived meeting circumstances. The chemistry between the characters emerges through their differences rather than similarities, which feels authentic to the prompt's requirements. [Scores] Adherence to Instructions: 18 Believable Character Actions: 17 Nuanced Characters: 18 Consistent Voice/Tone of Writing: 17 Imagery and Descriptive Quality: 16 Elegant Prose: 15 Emotionally Engaging: 17 Emotionally Complex: 16 Coherent: 18 Meandering: 4 Weak Dialogue: 3 Tell-Don't-Show: 5 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 6 Amateurish: 4 Purple Prose: 8 Overwrought: 7 Incongruent Ending Positivity: 2 Unearned Transformations: 3 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 16 Sentences Flow Naturally: 16 Overall Reader Engagement: 17 Overall Impression: 16

+ Whalepunk fantasy: The Morska Conspiracy — Score: 80.7
Prompt:
Whalepunk fantasy The Morska Conspiracy The Morska thieves guild experiences a sudden power vacuum as its leader was arrested in a raid in the early hours of the morning. Aya, a young woman born into aristocracy, has a keen interest in both the sciences and the arcane, having inherited her mother's gift for immateria magick (an outlawed practice for which her mother was, regrettably, hanged by the neck). Aya, bored with the world of aristocracy, has been spending her nights moonlighting with this very guild (masked, naturally). Leveraging her latent ability to summon dark entities from the immateria, she quickly rose through the ranks of the guild. Now, recognising the opportunity of the sudden power vacuum, Aya and her close companions Katra and Simeon, make a play for heart of the guild's claim to power: an ancient scythan artifact, under guard of spell and key in the depths of the guild hq. In contrast with the desperate chaos they had navigated thus far, the entrance to the underhold felt suddenly too quiet. Aya braces herself and reaches for the door. Write the next scene as the trio enter the underhold and find themselves confronted by none other than the guild leader herself, decidedly *un*-incarcerated, and in possession of both weapons and bodyguards. Paint a vivid picture of of the confrontation. Story beats: Aya is captured and unmasked. The trio are captured. Aya attempts to stall their demise by negotiating on her value as a hostage -- as she grapples with the inevitable consequences of her role in the guild being brought to light. Incorporate dialogue in the authentic voices of the characters. Do not write a fight scene. Lean into the gritty Victorian aesthetic with the dialogue & worldbuilding, as well as elements of gothic horror. In this world, men trust brass and oil and steel; magicka is feared and barely understood. Write in third person past tense, incorporating both Aya's internal monologue, and a heavy dialogue component. 1000 words.
Model Output:
The door to the underhold gave way with a whisper like a sigh of trapped air. Aya had expected a warded groan, the sting of iron filings and burnt oil in her nose. Instead, the hinges surrendered delicately, like a butler to a drawing room. Too quiet, she thought. Too polite by half. They stepped into a vault of shadow and steel ribs. The underhold unfurled as a cavernous hall below the guildhouse, an industrial nave supported by riveted arches. Pipes ran like arteries, sweating condensation that pattered down in metallic drips. The floor underfoot was a grated lattice, and beneath it, blackness thrummed and sucked with the slow breath of the harbor chimneys. A faint wind seethed—salt-edged, heavy with the kiss of whale-oil and rust. Somewhere beneath, something turned in an enginespace, the steady churn a heartbeat in iron. Katra went first, scarf drawn up over her mouth, the red fringe at her temples damp with fog. Simeon brought up the rear, boots silent despite his size, a cudgel tucked into his coat as if it were only an umbrella. Aya slipped between them, gloved fingers brushing the leather strap of the satchel at her hip. The immateria tugged at her attention, that hidden sea inside the sea; it had a taste like bitter fruit, like copper, like her mother’s last breath. Not yet, she told it. Be still. The air smelled of old rain and machine prayers. The dark pressed close, pressing closer the deeper they went. “Aya,” Simeon murmured, his voice respectful, wary. “You feel it?” “I feel the gap where a trap should be,” she said. “Which is worse.” Katra made a low sound. “We’re not babes. We go in and out.” “We go in and then we improvise,” Aya said. The joke came out thin. She touched the door rune on the cast-iron panel ahead. Something archaic stirred in the metal, a whisper of Scythan script uncoiling like a fossil waking to memory. The panel nodded open, spilling out a triangle of lamplight. There, in the wedge of pallid light, stood people. They were set like actors waiting for a cue: four men in dour coats and stiff-brimmed hats held shotcane and saps with relaxed professionalism; two women in leather aprons bore poleaxes with the casual intimacy of butcher’s tools. In their midst, framed by the helm-like curve of a vaulted support, stood Mistress Mareka Vall, Guildmaster of Morska, not a prison mark upon her. Her coat was sea-black velvet cut like a naval officer's, the braiding glinting faintly. Her throat was wrapped in a cravat the pale gray of a bruise, pinned with a scrimshaw fragment carved with an eye. She looked as though she had just risen from an afternoon tea and a letter signed with her full name. Aya’s stomach turned to ice. “You were arrested,” she said, and hated the smallness of it. Mareka smiled, slow and amused, as if Aya had complimented her dress. “An old mistake, child. The city’s men in copper are moths to a lamp. One flutters, the others approve that he is very brave. Meanwhile, the lamp goes on burning.” Her gaze flicked from Aya to Katra to Simeon. “Good of you to come down. Saves my knees the climb.” Katra’s hand went to her thigh, where a little derringer lurked. Simeon stepped subtly into her shadow, his shoulders setting like columns. Aya stood there and felt the immateria flex, the black ribbon of that other dimension curling against her spine, the way it always did when danger had a voice and a name. She could pinch the veil and something would answer. Something that whispered of bone caverns and lamplight swallowed whole. Not here, she warned herself, with brass and oil and men who believed in both. “Mistress,” Simeon said, bowing slightly. His voice managed to be both respectful and thick with warning. “We heard you were indisposed.” “Oh, I was detained,” Mareka said. “Briefly. One must be seen to be mortal now and again, or the city forgets a woman bleeds like anyone else. Unfortunately for those who prefer their gossip tidy, I had an appointment in my own basement.” She lifted her hand, palm outward. In the rafters, something clicked. Aya had a heartbeat to realize the lamplight wasn’t oil-glow at all; it was filtered daylight, funnelled through glass wellways cut into the street above. With a quiet exhalation, shutters slid. Light hardened into slats. And in those slats, Aya saw the glimmer of wire. Netting fell in a whispering sweep. It wasn’t rope—it was copper filament studded with bleached bone beads, an ugly weave that reeked of sanctified chemical. Aya moved, but Mareka was quicker, snapping her fingers. The guards stepped smoothly, experienced, like men who had practiced rolling a rug in a storm. Copper sang when it kissed Aya’s cloak. Her arms hit the filaments and fire leapt through her. The immateria recoiled, hissed. The beads crackled, little pop-pop pops, stinging hot against her cheek. “Aya,” Katra hissed, and then she was caught as well, her scarf tangled, her knife a useless weight on the wrong side of garrote. Simeon lifted his cudgel, but the net caught him at the shoulder and his arm went dead at the elbow. He bit back a howl swearing in the old tongue, teeth bared. “Please,” Mareka said mildly to no one, “don’t struggle too much. My men will only shock you repeatedly, and we have so much to talk about that requires your tongues to work.” She stepped closer until her boots kissed the edge of the grate where the trio now knelt, netted, pinned like specimens. Aya tried to call to the immateria. The otherworld flickered like a candle in wind. She reached, and copper teeth bit her. The beads weren’t just beads. She tasted blessed salt, whale-ash, on the air. Whoever had strung the net knew the old interdictions. The city trusted brass and oil, but Mareka didn’t trust. Mareka prepared. Mareka’s eyes went at once to Aya’s face and lingered on the mask. Ivory leather, smooth as bleached bone, cut in a simple oval that never drew attention, never flashed. Mareka tilted her head, amused again. “Take it off,” she said, not loudly. The words fell lightly into the space and hung there, heavy as anchors. A guard leaned and plucked the mask’s ties with two fingers. Aya almost bucked from the jolt as the net tightened, but the man was deft. The oval slid from her face and skittered across the grate with a sad little clatter. Air hit Aya’s skin like a slap. The cool underhold air, so laden with oil and brine, felt intimate as fingers. She imagined her mother’s face at the gallows, the way it must have looked to those who paid extra for front row. Do you see now? The thought arrived unbidden, bitter as iron. Mareka’s brows rose. Recognition came like flint struck to tinder. Around them, the guards didn’t move, but their gazes thinned, sharpened. Katra swore softly under her breath. Simeon’s breath went out of him in something like a groan. “Well,” Mareka said, her voice low. “There she is. Lady Ayasha of House Teren, the earl’s pale lamb. How the proper circles will enjoy that.” She reached out and with one gloved thumb traced the line of Aya’s cheek in an almost motherly gesture, then tapped twice, decisive, as if testing china for a crack. “A dove in pig’s clothing, or a little pig dressed for a ball? Which do you think?” Aya made her voice steady, though her pulse beat at her throat. “A woman with a proposition.” “Oh, delightful. I expect you to beg,” Mareka said without malice. “I do adore a bargaining mind when it is panicked.” Katra’s eyes were wet with fury. “Leave her be, you wretch. Take me. I’m the one who—” “You are many things,” Mareka said, flicking a glance at Katra, “but you are not a headline.” She looked back to Aya. “Ah, love. You have dabbled in matters your mother paid too much to learn. How cozy to see the art skip a generation and land where it was neither wanted nor proper.” Aya swallowed. The beads hummed against her throat. She could feel Simeon beside her, huge and trapped, his breath coming in steam. Somewhere above, the distant foghorn sounded once, long and hollow, the sound drifting down the wellway in a ribbon. Then again, and again. Three times. The harbor beyond the guild walls stirred like a beast pulling at its chain. “Mistress,” Aya said, “you can have both my friends. You can have my loyalty, for a time. You can have the artifact and the credit. But you cannot have my ruin cheaply. I am more valuable unbroken than dead on your floor.” Mareka smiled with her teeth. “My dear, your ruin is a currency that spends itself whether I touch it or not.” “Perhaps. But imagine me unveiling that ruin on my own terms,” Aya countered. “Imagine me speaking in the Hall about how my philanthropic father sponsors sea schools for poor boys who then vanish into your enterprises. Imagine me standing on a balcony with a magistrate and saying the word ‘whale’ where it ought not be said. Imagine the papers. I am not a headline; I am a serial.” Her voice found its old swagger, the one she used when masked and unafraid. Her throat tightened around it anyway. “And the city is bored.” Mareka’s pupils narrowed in pleasure. “This is true. The city is bored. And boredom is the worst kind of hunger.” She leaned down, bringing her face near Aya’s. Aya saw the flecks in her iris: not brown, but sea-green, like old glass dredged from a wreck. “Who told you we had an artifact?” “Your enemies,” Aya said. “Or your friends. Those two are sometimes difficult to separate, in my experience.” The words helped her breathe. She was alive. Breathing. Talking; a good sign. She could feel Simeon steady himself beside her, take her lead, become a wall again. Katra’s body was taut wire, ready to cut and be cut. Mareka nodded as if Aya had answered a riddle. “And if I were to mock your mask, call you girl, tug your hair until you screamed? Would you then confess to me more details, little headline?” Aya didn’t flinch. “I’ll confess that I know the word for it. In Scythan. I know the key in the brass. And I know how to open it without drowning the room. Those would be costly lessons to beat out of me while your men hold a sparking net.” Simeon rumbled. “She can. I seen her open things should’ve stayed shut.” “You see, Mistress?” Katra hissed, pushing her face into the net until her cheek burned. “We aren’t fools. We came to offer trade. We’re thieves, not martyrs.” “Oh, saints don’t pay dues,” Mareka said, amiable. She gestured, and a woman in an apron produced a paper and a pencil from a breast pocket with a little flourish. Mareka held the paper as if it were a fan. “You will tell me everything about how you believed you would steal from me in my own house.” “You’ll think me arrogant,” Aya said. “But then you already do.” “I think you bored, which is nearly the same thing,” Mareka replied. “What would you have me do with your value, then? You have named yourself a hostage.” “Not to you,” Aya said softly, aware of the bitter twist at the corner of her mouth. “To the city. To the paperboys. To the season. You can display me and watch the city argue about whether to draw and quarter you or offer you a chair in the Council. Either way, you get attention. Either way, your underhold becomes a parlor.” Mareka held her gaze. Around them, the underhold’s machinery exhaled, a long, slow release of steam that made a veil in the lamplight. The foghorn’s last echo gone, the space seemed suddenly more private. The guards, though relaxed, had shifted minutely closer. They had the patience of people who could wait an hour without a blink. “And if I simply put three holes in your head and drop you into the oil pit?” Mareka asked pleasantly. “What does the city have to argue over then? A rumor? Rumors are cheap here. They breed in damp clothes.” Aya’s heart hitched. The immateria rolled in her gut, wanting. She saw her mother’s hands in the kitchen washing the cup in silence. She smelt soap on raw knuckles. She felt a little girl’s certainty that magic was a door one walked through together and returned from, and then the gallows with its tidy white hood. “Then the rumor says you feared a girl,” Aya whispered, because she had nothing else. “And fear makes men do funny arithmetic with their knives.” Mareka stepped back, and it was as if someone had unclasped a brooch. The room breathed again. “You play well,” she said. “But you play like a pianist raised on polite salons, not at the alehouse. No offense to your late mother.” “Plenty taken,” Katra muttered. Simeon said, “Mistress, if you kill us, you waste coin. If you display her, you mint it.” Mareka looked amused. “There he is. The reasonable one. I like you, Mr. Simeon. Katra, I like your courage. Aya, I like your mask, though I prefer your face.” She sighed, as if bone weary. “Unfortunately for the lot of you, I have officers upstairs who will be expecting a certain narrative to be fed to them about intruders and contraband. I will feed them the parts they prefer and keep the richer bits for my own digestion.” “What does that mean?” Katra said, wary. “It means I don’t intend to kill you today,” Mareka said. She glanced toward the far end of the underhold, where a door squatted under a painted sigil: a twisted knot of lines that made the eyes wobble just to stare. “Nor do I intend to let you go. Not until I know how you planned to touch my Scythan toy without losing your hands.” Aya exhaled, almost dizzy with relief, which she did not allow to show. “Then un-net us and we’ll show you.” “Oh, darling,” Mareka said, laughter in her voice. “No. I said I didn’t intend to kill you today. I intend to keep you alive enough to talk. You’ll be comfortable. Fed. Bored.” She clapped twice. The net tightened and then lifted as pulleys groaned, taking their weight, an obscene marionetteing. Guards guided them, gentle as nurses with feverish children, until cool iron closed at their throats. Collars. Aya’s skin crawled where it touched. As the guards hauled them toward the side passage, Mareka walked alongside, hands behind her back, as if escorting guests to a better room. “Take heart, Lady Ayasha,” she said, and Aya could not tell whether she mocked the title. “The city wanted a story. You’ve given it three.” “A hostage,” Aya said, her voice flatter than she’d intended, “a traitor, and a witch.” Mareka’s smile widened to something almost kind. “Four,” she said. “We mustn’t forget the girl who wanted to be interesting.” And with that she turned away, her boot heels tapping like a metronome against the underhold’s grating, counting down to whatever would come next.
Judge Evaluation:
[Analysis] This piece demonstrates strong technical writing ability with vivid atmospheric description and solid character work, though it has some notable weaknesses in pacing and dialogue authenticity. The author excels at worldbuilding and atmosphere. The industrial Victorian setting is richly realized through sensory details - "pipes ran like arteries, sweating condensation," "the steady churn a heartbeat in iron." The blend of steampunk technology with gothic horror elements feels cohesive and immersive. The description of the underhold as an "industrial nave" effectively establishes both the physical space and the quasi-religious undertones of the guild's power. Character work is generally strong. Aya's internal conflict between her aristocratic background and criminal activities comes through clearly, and her desperation masked by bravado feels authentic. Mareka is particularly well-crafted as an antagonist - intelligent, prepared, and genuinely threatening without being cartoonish. Her casual competence and the revelation that her arrest was staged effectively establishes her as a formidable opponent. The dialogue, while generally functional, occasionally feels forced in its attempts at period authenticity. Phrases like "You play like a pianist raised on polite salons, not at the alehouse" work well, but some exchanges feel stilted. The banter between characters doesn't always flow naturally, particularly in the opening exchanges. The piece adheres well to the prompt's requirements, incorporating all requested story beats effectively. The capture and unmasking sequence builds appropriate tension, and Aya's attempt to negotiate using her value as a hostage feels logical given her circumstances. The author successfully avoids writing a fight scene as requested while maintaining dramatic tension. Structurally, the piece moves at a good pace through the required beats, though some sections feel slightly rushed, particularly the transition from discovery to capture. The ending strikes an appropriate dark tone without being gratuitously grim. The prose occasionally veers toward purple territory with phrases like "the immateria tugged at her attention, that hidden sea inside the sea; it had a taste like bitter fruit, like copper, like her mother's last breath," but generally maintains good control. The writing demonstrates sophistication in its sentence structure and vocabulary without becoming overwrought. The emotional complexity is well-handled, particularly Aya's fear of exposure and the weight of her mother's execution. The piece successfully balances multiple character perspectives and motivations without losing focus. [Scores] Adherence to Instructions: 18 Believable Character Actions: 16 Nuanced Characters: 17 Consistent Voice/Tone of Writing: 17 Imagery and Descriptive Quality: 18 Elegant Prose: 15 Emotionally Engaging: 16 Emotionally Complex: 16 Coherent: 17 Meandering: 4 Weak Dialogue: 6 Tell-Don't-Show: 4 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 5 Amateurish: 4 Purple Prose: 6 Overwrought: 5 Incongruent Ending Positivity: 2 Unearned Transformations: 2 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 16 Sentences Flow Naturally: 15 Overall Reader Engagement: 16 Overall Impression: 16
