+ First-person unique perspective: Hacker Mindset — Score: 85.0
Prompt:
Hacker Mindset Write a first-person narrative from the perspective of a reformed (now) white hat hacker who cannot help but notice exploitable weaknesses in both systems and people they encounter. They habitually look for vulnerabilities and exploit vectors. The narrative takes place during a first date at a restaurant, where they're trying to focus on human connection. It's going surprisingly well due to a discovered shared nerd-fandom (magic the gathering). Things go a little sideways when the date reveals a noticeably insecure phone unlock pattern, which protagonist can't help but comment on. They are then left to negotiate the fallout of this faux pas and attempt to salvage (or perhaps sabotage) the remainder of the date. Write almost entirely in internal monologue. 1000 words.
Model Output:
I pick the corner table because it’s out of sight from the front door and in sight of the kitchen, where traffic patterns reveal a lot about a place. The host smiles too long when she seats me. I count the seconds—four and a half—and make a mental note that someone trained her to sustain warmth. Noted. I try to stop counting. Tonight isn’t about patterns and systems; it’s about meeting a person. A human. Not an attack surface. Her name is Maya. She arrives three minutes late, which I know because the waitlist app flips hers from “Reserved” to “Seated” when the host taps it, and I can see the screen over the shoulder of the guy at the bar. I am not going to look at the hardware. I am not going to wonder whether they’ve patched their point-of-sale tablets. I am going to say hello like a normal person. Hello comes out fine. She laughs easily. She orders a mule and doesn’t check to see if I’m going to judge her for the sugar content, and—unexpectedly—my shoulders drop a few millimeters. I match her drink because it’s a small, harmless sync, and also because the bartender rinses the copper mugs between pours but doesn’t towel them, which means a high probability of diluted first sips. Shared experience. Look at me. Participating. We’re halfway through appetizers when it happens. She describes a road trip to Portland, the ways smoke stained the sunset last summer, and I find myself quiet in that way I am when someone’s describing systems they don’t know they’re describing. Highways like veins, wildfires like denial-of-service, hotels like temporary authentications that expire at noon. She mentions how her car’s adaptive cruise control “basically drives itself” and I feel the twitch behind my owlish interest: I wonder whether her model had the LIDAR firmware patched last winter. I bite my tongue and focus on her eyes. They’re green. Not metaphorically. The lamplight catches in a flecked pattern that suggests contact lenses, but if so they’re the good kind. I keep my curiosity behind my teeth. She asks what I do and I give the cleaned-up version. I used to break things. Now I help fix them. “Consulting,” I say, because it’s a big enough word that most people stop there. She doesn’t. She says, “Like a hacker?” with a half-smile that I decide not to take personally. “Like a white hat,” I say. “Like the person you call before you call your lawyer.” Her smile widens. “So you can hack my ex’s Netflix?” I grin to show that I get the joke. I don’t tell her that you don’t need to hack something where the password’s probably still his dog’s name plus 123. And I definitely don’t say: that can be brute-forced without much effort if—focus. People, not problems. We discover Magic the Gathering by accident. She says she likes strategy games that “require you to imagine the fourth move before you’ve taken the first,” and I say, “Like chess?” and she says, “Like Magic,” and I feel it, the door that opens in my chest when I’m on a familiar map. I tell her I still have a battered deck from high school, and she asks what colors. “Blue-black,” I say, almost sheepish. “Control.” She laughs, quick, bright. “Of course you’re a control player.” “It’s not about control,” I say. It is. “It’s about timing.” She confesses to a love of green-red. “I want to ramp, bulldoze, and then fling a 10/10 at your face.” “Impulse damage,” I say. “Instant gratification,” she counters, toast lifted. I clink my watered-down mule and try not to read it as a metaphor. She tells me about her work: UX design for a nonprofit. I clock the phrases that drift in like glitter: “We’re still on Slack’s free tier,” “I share my Figma passwords with the interns,” “Our donor database is old but it’s fine,” “We did a fundraiser using a template I found on this thread.” Breadcrumbs I don’t want to pick up but can’t help mapping. Security questions unspooling: first pet (she mentions a childhood golden retriever named Daisy), favorite teacher (Mr. Lozano, who taught her to love typography), the street she grew up on (Sunset Lane, which is almost definitely not a unique string). I nod and say, “That sounds rewarding,” and it’s true. It does. It also sounds like a breach waiting to happen. I keep my mouth shut. This is dinner. This is not an audit. The food arrives. She eats fries one at a time, diligently, organizing them into a neat little stack at the edge of her plate before moving them inward. It’s funny and charming and I wonder if she’d mind if I said so. I do say so, and she smiles without defensiveness, which I file under: trust. I let that file sit unopened. We navigate the easy terrain: siblings, favorite bad movies, whether pineapple belongs on pizza (I choose the neutral ground of “allow but do not prefer”). She shows me a photo of her cat, a gray longhair with the improbable name of Boba Fett, and I find myself genuinely cooing. She scrolls. I laugh at the cat using a cardboard box as a helmet. She scrolls again, and that’s when I see it. Not the cat, the unlock. Nine dots. A greasy smudge from top left to bottom right and back to the center. The pattern is the kind a human hand loves: diagonal symmetry with a flourish. The kind of pattern you don’t forget because it mimics Zorro’s Z and signs your screen every time you open it. The kind of pattern that, given a moment alone with her phone and a sleeve to polish the residue, I could guess in two tries. And I realize the exact number of lines she’s traced, the number of dots hit twice because of the overlay rules, and oh God I’m looking at her face again because her laughing stops and my mouth is already open. “Hey,” I say, and it’s already wrong. “You might want to—your unlock pattern is… kind of predictable.” There’s a silence that arrives like a dropped tray. She blinks. The bartender laughs too loudly at something entirely different. I feel the tilt, the wobble you get when you set a Jenga block wrong a few layers from the top. I hate that I had to say it. I hate that I couldn’t not. She pulls her phone back as if I’ve reached, though I haven’t moved. “What?” “It’s just, um—smudge attacks.” God. Stop. “People can figure out your pattern from the oils on your screen. If you ever—if you lost it, or someone—” “That’s… creepy,” she says, her voice carefully even. “Are you saying you were looking at my… oils?” “I’m saying—no.” I breathe. Try to. I remember a therapist’s question: is this about safety, or is this about control? “I’m saying I notice things. It’s my job. Was my job.” I try to smile with humor instead of warning. “I’m fun at parties.” She watches me. Her fingers close over her phone. I can almost see the motes of trust shuttering in her eyes. I want to rewind, to put the words back. I think: what would a human do? What would a human say? “I’m sorry,” I say. “That wasn’t… date material. It’s a reflex for me. I didn’t mean to make you feel unsafe.” It’s true. It’s also true that unsafe is exactly what I was thinking. She takes a breath, the kind with the little puff at the end like she’s blowing something away. “It’s okay,” she says. “I guess it’s like a chef telling me not to eat the special.” “Don’t eat the special,” I say automatically, and regret it immediately. She laughs, but it’s the brittle version, the half-life of humor. She tucks a strand of hair behind her ear and says, “I mean, I know I’m not, like, super careful. My password for everything used to be Daisy2008.” She grins like it’s a cute story. I feel the ground shift again, like I’ve been handed a live wire. Daisy, the dog. 2008 could be graduation year, year of adoption, who knows. She’s dropping breadcrumbs with both hands, and I’m the kind of bird that can’t stop pecking. “Used to be,” I echo, hoping I can turn this into distance instead of proximity. “Now it’s… different.” She smiles with a secrecy that is not armor so much as paper. “Anyway.” She looks at her plate. “I just never think about this stuff.” And that’s the thing. I do. I always do. I think about the guy at the bar who keeps checking the time with a subtle anxiety that says he’s waiting for someone who’s late. I think about the point-of-sale tablets with their default “admin” sticker peeled off but glue residue still there. I think about the open Wi-Fi network named FreeCafeGuest that’s probably running on a router someone bought at a big-box store and never changed the password on. I think about how her phone, with its affectionate smudges, could be the key to her email, her bank, her photos of Boba Fett in a cereal box helmet, and how the world is full of people who look at those smudges and don’t think “sweet,” they think “entry point.” I think about how she’s watching me not talk, and what that silence looks like from her side. “I know you’re trying to help,” she says, and there’s gentleness there, or maybe it’s a white flag. “But it’s a lot.” “Yeah.” I take a sip to give myself a second to exit the cul-de-sac in my head. “It is a lot. I can… not do that. I can keep my brain out of your phone.” She tilts her head. “Can you?” I let the truth come out, because if there’s anything I’ve learned when trying to patch a system, it’s that lying about your attack surface makes you easier to own. “I can try,” I say. “I can notice and not say. I can decide that tonight is about other things.” “Like flinging a 10/10 at my face,” she says, and I feel the air loosen. She’s tossing me a line. I take it. “Exactly,” I say. “I’ll tap four lands and summon Self-Control.” “You can’t summon that,” she says. “That’s not a creature.” “It’s an enchantment,” I say. “Aura. Target: me.” “Enchantments are hard to get rid of,” she says, and there’s the spark again in her eyes. “Unless you have Naturalize.” “I counterspell,” I reply. “Okay, that makes me the worst.” “Control,” she says, mock-scolding, but she’s smiling again. The tension doesn’t vanish so much as retreat to the edges, like a storm deciding it might rain somewhere else. I ask her a question she can answer without touching upon attack vectors. What’s the last book she loved? She says Recursion, and I make a face because I thought it was too tidy, and we argue about whether a paradox is still fun if it’s solved. She mentions her mom collects ceramic cows and how one of them creeps her out because it has eyelashes, and I store “mother’s hobby” in the mental box labeled Do Not Use without even meaning to. A brain is a net you can’t unweave once you’ve tied it. When the check comes, I watch the server set it between us, a small black folder with a pen that has someone else’s law firm on it. I want to split it because I don’t want to be the guy who pays to own. I want to offer to pay because I want to declare something other than suspicion as my default. I say, “Can we split?” and she nods, relieved in a way that tells me I guessed right—about this, at least. We both pull out cards. Hers is debit, and I flinch internally because debit cards are soft targets, immediate cash. She notices nothing because I make sure my face is a wall. The server takes them and I think about tipping in cash, because I see the way the manager is hovering tonight and how he took a stack from the bar earlier, and I know that cash tips are sometimes safer from distribution algorithms that are really just forms of theft. I don’t do it because I don’t want to start a conversation about it, and I hate that not doing it feels like complicity. When the cards come back, she signs without reading, her signature a quick scribble that looks nothing like the one in the corner of her ID when she flashed it earlier to order the mule. I realize I’ve memorized the last four digits of her card and feel sick at myself. I turn my eyes to the light fixture, count the filaments, breathe. We stand. She tucks her phone into her bag, glances at me, and I know this is the moment when future forks. Salvage or sabotage. I can apologize again, which can sound like self-soothing more than actual repair. I can make a joke that might land wrong. I can ask her if she’d like to walk a block, which is a yes/no that doesn’t demand too much and maybe gives the night a length that feels natural. “Do you want to grab a pack?” I ask. The words are out before I’ve even fully formed the idea. “There’s that game shop around the corner. Just one. Loser buys bubble tea.” She blinks, surprised. The shape of the offer is familiar: structured randomness, low stakes, a little ritual of our shared nerd language. It’s also a trapdoor I know well: if we open a pack, we’ll both think about probability and the ordered chaos of the thing, and maybe not about smudge patterns and Daisy2008. She smiles. It’s small but it warms. “You’re on,” she says. “But I’m going to pull a mythic.” “You always say that,” I say, and she laughs. We step out into the evening. The air is colder than it looked, the kind of cold that makes you aware of your skin as an interface. I walk beside her and try to think about nothing but cardboard rectangles and the thrill of seeing a shiny border where you expected a common. I try to let my brain be the thing I use for play instead of protection. It’s hard. It’s also possible, at least for a block or two. I will never stop seeing the seams in things. But I can learn where to put my hands. I can learn not to tug. Ahead, the game shop’s windows glow with fluorescent promises. She bumps my shoulder with hers, a light event that doesn’t trigger any intrusion detection in me because I let it in. We push the door. Bells ring. I am not fixed, but maybe I am patched enough for tonight.
Judge Evaluation:
[Analysis] This piece demonstrates exceptional craft in executing a challenging narrative premise. The author successfully maintains the protagonist's distinctive voice throughout—a reformed hacker whose mind automatically catalogues vulnerabilities while desperately trying to connect authentically with another person. The internal monologue feels genuine and specific, avoiding the trap of generic "quirky character" writing. The technical details are convincing without being overwhelming. References to smudge attacks, LIDAR firmware, and point-of-sale vulnerabilities feel authentic rather than researched, suggesting real familiarity with cybersecurity concepts. The Magic: The Gathering elements are similarly well-integrated, serving both as genuine bonding material and metaphorical framework for the protagonist's control issues. The character work is particularly strong. The protagonist's hypervigilance reads as genuinely compulsive rather than performative—they notice Maya's contact lenses, the server's training, the manager's behavior, all while hating their inability to turn off this analytical mode. Maya emerges as more than a plot device; her responses feel natural and her gradual revelation of security vulnerabilities (password patterns, oversharing) creates genuine tension. The prose strikes an excellent balance between technical precision and emotional resonance. Phrases like "I feel the ground shift again, like I've been handed a live wire" and "A brain is a net you can't unweave once you've tied it" demonstrate sophisticated metaphorical thinking that stays true to the character's mindset. The story's structure builds tension effectively around the central conflict—the protagonist's compulsive need to identify and warn about vulnerabilities versus their desire for human connection. The phone pattern incident serves as a perfect catalyst, creating authentic awkwardness without melodrama. The ending avoids both false redemption and cynical darkness. The protagonist acknowledges they won't fundamentally change ("I will never stop seeing the seams in things") while finding a path toward connection that doesn't require self-denial. The Magic pack suggestion is clever—it channels their shared interests while providing structured interaction that might bypass the protagonist's security-focused anxiety. Minor weaknesses include occasional over-explanation of the protagonist's thought processes and some metaphors that, while clever, occasionally feel slightly forced. The piece also runs long for its narrative scope, though the voice is engaging enough to sustain the length. [Scores] Adherence to Instructions: 19 Believable Character Actions: 18 Nuanced Characters: 17 Consistent Voice/Tone of Writing: 19 Imagery and Descriptive Quality: 16 Elegant Prose: 17 Emotionally Engaging: 17 Emotionally Complex: 18 Coherent: 18 Meandering: 6 Weak Dialogue: 4 Tell-Don't-Show: 5 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 3 Amateurish: 2 Purple Prose: 4 Overwrought: 5 Incongruent Ending Positivity: 2 Unearned Transformations: 3 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 17 Sentences Flow Naturally: 17 Overall Reader Engagement: 18 Overall Impression: 17
Scores: Adherence to Instructions: 19.0, Believable Character Actions: 18.0, Nuanced Characters: 17.0, Consistent Voice/Tone of Writing: 19.0, Imagery and Descriptive Quality: 16.0, Elegant Prose: 17.0, Emotionally Engaging: 17.0, Emotionally Complex: 18.0, Coherent: 18.0, Meandering: 6.0, Weak Dialogue: 4.0, Tell-Don't-Show: 5.0, Unsurprising or Uncreative: 3.0, Amateurish: 2.0, Purple Prose: 4.0, Overwrought: 5.0, Incongruent Ending Positivity: 2.0, Unearned Transformations: 3.0, Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 17.0, Sentences Flow Naturally: 17.0, Overall Reader Engagement: 18.0, Overall Impression: 17.0
+ First-person unique perspective: Overthinking It — Score: 89.1
Prompt:
Overthinking It Write a first-person narrative from the perspective of an intelligent 8th-grade girl with social anxiety who has developed a crush on a classmate. The narrative takes place during a group science project where they're paired together, as she cycles through rehearsed conversations while second-guessing each potential approach. Her inner thoughts should reveal the gap between her articulate internal voice and her difficulty expressing herself aloud, her fears of coming across as desperate or weird, and the exhausting process of monitoring her every word, expression, and movement for potential embarrassment. Write almost entirely in internal monologue. 1000 words.
Model Output:
The projector squeaks each time Ms. Alvarez flips to a new slide, a tiny mouse in the ceiling, and every squeak ratchets my shoulders up another millimeter. The title: “Group Investigation: Ecosystems in a Jar.” There’s a picture of mason jars with pebbles and moss and a smug-looking snail. Ecosystems. Jars. Partnerships. I stare at a paperclip on my desk and press my thumbnail into its coil like a reset button that never resets anything. “Okay, pair up,” Ms. Alvarez says, and the squeak echoes, and there’s that second of collective class jitter as people swivel and lift and announce who they’ve already decided on, the choreography of middle school friendship. I haven’t decided. Or I decided in September—if decision equals “daydreamed repeatedly about working with him, rehearsed conversations about snails”—but we’re not allowed to call those decisions because they are too fragile and private and far too possible to not happen. I’m trying to decide if it’s creepier to look at him before he looks at me or to pretend I just noticed him after he says my name. Decide, decide. My heart is a fast-clicking mouse through menus. If I move too quickly, I’ll seem overeager. If I wait too long, he’ll wind up with someone else, which will be an entirely different category of humiliation. I’ve done this math. He’s three rows back, by the window, where the sun paints his desk the color of old library pages. He tucks his pencil behind his ear when he’s thinking. His name is Theo, which feels like a word you inhale instead of say. I look at him, but like a sideways glance you’d use for meteors. He’s already looking at me. Or maybe beyond me. Or at the poster about photosynthesis hovering over my head. Poor chloroplasts take the fall again. He half-lifts his hand in a not-quite-wave, and now my stomach is a jar with a storm inside. My mouth thinks about doing something. I could say, “Do you want to be partners?” Very simple. No metaphor required. But the phrase “Do you” sounds like pleading, which begs. Rejection sits behind it, chewing on its nails. I switch. “Want to partner?” Cleaner. It still implies desire, which is the problem. “Do you want to partner” in any other universe is normal. In mine it is confessing too much. The word want is sticky. He says, “Hey, do you want to—” and then laughs, because we overlapped, and I missed my cue, and this is already a comedy of errors where everyone else in the audience understands timing except me. “Want to work together?” he finishes. Yes. I nod. Is the nod too enthusiastic? Scale it down. My head becomes a bobblehead, so I stop my head entirely. He smiles. I realize my mouth is hovering open like a bird waiting for a worm. I close my mouth and smile, but keep my lips closed so my teeth don’t look too big, which makes me look as if I’m suppressing a secret. I am, but not the right kind. Fine. Jaw: relax. Smile: minus two degrees. We move our desks together. The metal legs make a scratchy complaint against the floor, a different squeak from the projector. The room is a forced river of scraping and chatter and delicate negotiations about who brought glue last time. I tuck my legs beneath my chair so I don’t knock him accidentally, and then I wonder if that’s aggressive via avoidance. Friendly people are comfortable with collisions. Am I? Am I supposed to bump his knee and laugh? No, because then my knee would write a poem on his. No. “Ecosystems in a jar,” he says, pointing at the screen. “Which is basically, like, Jar World.” “Jar World,” I echo, and file it in the part of my brain that collects things to remember and smile about later, like museum pins. “We can build a tiny biome.” Tiny biome sounds like a pet name and I want to bite it down. I look at his pencil. He still has it tucked behind his ear, which I can see from this distance in a way I never get to. The pencil is yellow where the paint has chipped. The top of his ear is pale from winter, slightly red. I want to make a sketch of his ear, label it with a neat line: auricle. Another line: not for me. He reaches into his backpack and pulls out a clean notebook, the kind with graph paper, which is an instant crush multiplier because people who use graph paper have thoughts that can be measured. “Okay,” he says, “so we need rocks, charcoal, soil, moss, maybe a pill bug? Are pill bugs allowed?” The projector squeaks, changing to a slide that lists exactly those items, which is comforting. I can follow lists. Lists don’t need you to be charming. “I think pill bugs are detritivores,” I say. The word detritivores rolls out like I’ve set it free from its cage. My voice is smaller than the word, but it doesn’t wobble. I hold my breath. “They eat decomposing matter. So they’re basically janitors.” “Janitors,” he says, amused. “Your vocab is intense.” My vocab is intense? Is that a compliment disguised or an insult disguised or just a fact? I can’t tell. I want to explain. I want to build a small, clear case for why words launched into public by me are often overly armored: because if I choose the precise one then you can’t misunderstand me and then you can’t reject me for the wrong reasons. But this is not court. This is an eighth-grade classroom with a sneezy projector. Play normal. “Sorry,” I say, and immediately hate the apology, the way I apologize for existing. “I just—I like words.” Because that’s less weird. “You know, it is a good thing to have a detritivore. Otherwise stuff would rot too fast.” Stop. Stop with the rot. Who talks about rot? Me. Great. “No, it’s cool,” he says, scribbling detritivore with a careful i and then a tentative o, and I feel bizarrely protective of the spelling. He looks up. “I like that you know this stuff. I barely remember what a producer is.” “You are totally a producer,” I say without thinking, and then feel my face go incandescent, because what does that even mean? Producer as in photosynthesis? Or producer as in he produces things? The joke is a fragment. I scramble. “I mean—um—you seem like you, like—make stuff happen. Not… chlorophyll-wise.” He laughs, and the sound is like dropping pebbles onto a glass tabletop, small and surprising and not mean. “I do not photosynthesize. But thanks.” Ms. Alvarez clicks to a slide with diagrams of water cycles, the squeak stretching: eeeee. It’s like she’s stretching my sentences until they snap. The class murmurs. In the back, someone makes a fake squeaking noise to copy it, and everyone laughs, and I try to look like I’m also part of the laughter, not too much, not too late. Laughter fractions, measured in acceptable decibels. My face feels like a mask I’m moving with chopsticks. It’s exhausting. The instructions are something like: plan your habitat, assign tasks, gather supplies from the front table. The front table is suddenly a marketplace where bodies wedge and reach. I look at Theo. If I volunteer I’ll look controlling. If I don’t, I’ll look passive. Pick one. Commit. “I can get the moss,” I say, because moss is quiet and people will probably go for rocks. “And charcoal.” He grins. “Team Charcoal. I’ll get the rocks and…and the pill bug? If I can catch one without it rolling up and me feeling like a giant.” “Armadillidiidae,” I say, which comes out like a spell, and I don’t mean to. “They roll up. It’s their…thing.” He blinks, then smiles wider. “Arma-what?” “Isopods,” I correct, as if that clarifies anything. “Never mind.” I stand up. My legs are a little shaky, like I’ve been balancing on a curb too long. The front table is a riot of jars, plastic bags of dirt, excited hands. I thread through, focusing on edges, not faces. There’s a smell of damp soil that’s like the woods behind my grandma’s house when everything is a better color. I scoop moss that is the exact green that toys me into breathing. I pick up a bag of charcoal, which makes my fingers black at the creases. I wipe them on my jeans and then panic that if I later touch my face I’ll streak it like war paint. Do I want war paint? Absolutely not. I wipe again, making it worse. “You good?” Theo’s voice is behind me. He’s close enough that I know his breath is warm even though I can’t feel it. For a second I imagine turning and our noses touching accidentally and me dying instantly, just a small soft explosion. I shouldn’t imagine that in school. It will leak out. “I got moss,” I say, which is not poetry, and I lift it like a trophy. “And charcoal. And a little…uh, ferny thing.” I add the ferny thing because: variety. I put them carefully in the tray he’s holding. Our fingers do not touch. Or maybe they do for a millisecond, and I replay that millisecond so many times it stretches into a minute. His skin is a normal temperature. What was I expecting? A fire? Good grief. We carry the supplies back like we are on a mission worth assigning. My chair squeaks when I sit. I put the moss on the desk and arrange it as if it can judge me. It probably can’t. The snail in the picture probably can. It looks like it knows secrets. “So,” he says, clicking his pencil. “Layering. Rocks first for drainage, then charcoal to keep…rot stink away? Then soil. Then plants. Pill bug friend later.” He looks at me for confirmation, which feels like sunlight. “Yes,” I say, and it’s strong. “We should rinse the rocks, otherwise dust will cloud the water. And we should not overwater because it’s a closed system.” “Closed system.” He nods. “Wish my little brother had one of those.” I laugh, quietly, because it’s safe to laugh at brothers. That’s a script I’ve seen. “We can poke a hole in the lid,” I offer. “For oxygen exchange. Even though it’s mostly water cycling.” I’m hearing myself. Stop. “What if we add a tiny stick for the pill bug to climb? Like a hiking trail.” “You’re really into this,” he says, and he says it gently, but my brain lifts it up to inspect for menace anyway. Am I too into this? I am. Am I supposed to pretend to be cooler about moss? People in movies would tilt their head and say something simple and alluring like, “I like nature,” and then lean back, languid, and their hair would swish and someone would throw them a varsity jacket. I have no jacket. I have sweat starting under my arms and fingers stained like a chimney sweep. “I like systems,” I say. Precision hurts less. “They make sense.” “People are systems,” he says. He adds the rocks to the jar. They click and settle in a tiny landslide. “They don’t make sense.” “Because too many variables,” I blurt, and then wish I could roll it back. Overshare. He nods anyway, like this is a normal conversation. I want to say: When you think about talking to someone, do you rehearse the different branches of the conversation like a choose-your-own-adventure and label each one [Most Likely to Make You Look Pathetic], [Okay, Safe], [Unexpectedly Funny], [Potentially Mortifying]? Do you inventory every facial twitch? Because my face is a traitor. I don’t say that. I say, “Charcoal layer,” and pour. We press the soil into place. His hand is steady. Mine is shaking just a little, which could be from caffeine (none), or sleep (not enough), or feelings (too much). I shape the terrain like a miniature landscape architecht, which I almost spelled wrong in my head, and I feel the heat of possible embarrassment if I had spelled it wrong out loud. The inside voice is crisp as winter air. The outside voice is a fog machine. “Do you ever—” he starts, and then stops, which dangles a rope I want to climb. “Like, think too much about…how you sound?” I almost laugh. It catches and turns into a small exhale. “Constantly,” I say. The word is a pebble flicked into a lake. It ripples, and I’m not sure if it sinks or floats. “Like if I don’t watch it, the sentence will run out the door without its pants.” He laughs again, and it’s softer. “Same.” He shrugs, like it’s allowed. “Sometimes I feel like I sound smarter in my head and then it comes out like, ‘Uh, jar world.’” “Jar world was good,” I say, because I want to hand him back something of himself he gave to me. “It was…concise.” He smirks. “Concise is a good insult.” “It’s not,” I say, maybe too fast. “It’s—” I want to say: It’s what I want to be and what I never am. Instead I press the moss gently into the soil, anchor it. “It’s honest.” Ms. Alvarez’s squeak declares time’s passage like a bird that can only sing one terrible note. “Ten minutes left,” she says. The room blurs into people tearing tape and arguing about fungus. I keep thinking of time like a lid lowering. Ten minutes until we stop having a reason to talk. Ten minutes until our jar is done and I give it a name in my head I’ll never say out loud. Ten minutes. “Okay,” he says, peering into our jar. The tiny world holds still. “Pill bug?” “Careful,” I say, because he is about to be gentle, and there’s something about watching someone be gentle that is like holding your breath underwater to see if you can touch the bottom. He coaxes the isopod from the plastic container to our soil. It uncurls slowly, antennae pausing, tiny legs working, then disappears under the moss. “There,” he says. “Home.” Home, I repeat in my chest, and I wish I could live in a jar too, regulated and visible and protected. I wish I could put a little sign on myself: sensitive plant, do not overwater. Light misting only. I imagine us putting the lid on and the inside air becoming its own recycled breath. The idea that it could work comforts me and scares me. Closed systems are predictable until they aren’t. “Looks good,” he says, and this time when he smiles it reaches his eyes in that way that makes me feel like I’ve been let in through a door. “We’re a good team.” The sentence vibrates like a bridge in wind. There are fourteen responses I could give, and all of them crowd the exit. “Yeah,” I pick, the smallest one. “We should, um, write the—observations schedule.” Which is what you say when you don’t say: I’m going to think about this all week. “Like who checks on humidity.” He laughs, but nods. “You can do Monday-Wednesday. I’ll do Tuesday-Thursday? We’ll both do Friday. Team…Charcoal.” “Team Charcoal,” I say, and it feels like a band name I can belong to. We write our names on the side of the jar with tape. He writes his with quick confident letters that slope a little. I write mine and concentrate on not making a heart accidentally anywhere in the vicinity. That would be a crime scene. “Cool,” he says, and then looks at our jar like it can answer him. “I’m glad we partnered.” The sentence lands and sits there like a small animal, wary. I try not to scare it. “Me too,” I say. It sounds like I mean it because I do, which feels dangerous, like balancing on a rooftop (there are no rooftops here; just ceiling tiles and fluorescent lights and a squeaky projector). My heart makes a noise that only I can hear. The bell will ring soon. People are starting to pack up, the rustle of paper and zippers. I want to say something else, something simple and brave like, “Maybe we can work together again,” but it blooms too brightly in my throat, and I am afraid that if I say it now, it will come out oversized and awkward and the animal will run away. He looks at me like there is no animal at all, just me, and the jar, and the small green thing we built that might last. “See you tomorrow,” he says, and I nod, and my nod is the exact size of a nod. Ms. Alvarez squeaks one last slide and the sound slides down the back of my neck. Everyone moves. Theo stands, tucks his pencil behind his ear, and walks back to his seat with one hand in his pocket like it’s easy to be a person. I sit for an extra second to watch the pill bug vanish beneath our hill. I name him in my head: Courage. I don’t say it out loud. Not yet. Then I gather my backpack and stand. The room is still the same room. The jar is our proof that something can be contained and nurtured even when you are certain everything is breakable. I take a breath that tastes like damp leaves, and I walk to the door, practicing the way my shoulders should hang to look like this is all normal, ordinary, not a thing that will keep me awake later, arranging words like stones in a path, hoping I can get from here to there without slipping.
Judge Evaluation:
[Analysis] This piece demonstrates exceptional adherence to the prompt's requirements, delivering a compelling first-person narrative from an intelligent 8th-grader with social anxiety. The writer successfully captures the exhausting mental gymnastics of overthinking every social interaction while maintaining the authentic voice of a bright teenager. The internal monologue is masterfully executed, revealing the stark contrast between the protagonist's articulate inner voice and her struggle to express herself aloud. Lines like "The inside voice is crisp as winter air. The outside voice is a fog machine" perfectly encapsulate this central tension. The narrative maintains almost exclusive focus on internal thoughts as requested, with minimal actual dialogue. The character work is nuanced and believable. The protagonist's intelligence shines through her vocabulary choices ("detritivores," "Armadillidiidae") while her anxiety manifests in realistic ways - overthinking word choices, monitoring facial expressions, and catastrophizing simple interactions. Her crush on Theo feels authentic rather than romanticized, grounded in specific observations like his pencil behind his ear and graph paper notebook. The prose demonstrates sophisticated craft with vivid, original imagery. Metaphors like "my heart is a fast-clicking mouse through menus" and "my stomach is a jar with a storm inside" feel fresh and age-appropriate. The recurring motif of the squeaky projector creates atmospheric continuity while reflecting the protagonist's heightened sensitivity to environmental details. The science project framework provides perfect structure for the narrative, allowing natural progression while maintaining focus on the protagonist's internal experience. The ecosystem-in-a-jar serves as an effective metaphor for the protagonist's desire for controlled, predictable environments. The emotional complexity is remarkable - capturing not just anxiety but hope, self-awareness, and the peculiar mixture of terror and joy that accompanies teenage crushes. The protagonist's self-deprecating humor ("I want to bite it down") and acute social awareness feel genuine rather than performed. The ending strikes an appropriate balance - neither overly optimistic nor pessimistic. The protagonist hasn't transformed but has survived a meaningful interaction, which feels earned and realistic for the timeframe and situation. Minor weaknesses include occasional moments where the metaphors become slightly dense, and some sentences that could benefit from tightening. However, these don't significantly detract from the overall achievement. [Scores] Adherence to Instructions: 19 Believable Character Actions: 18 Nuanced Characters: 18 Consistent Voice/Tone of Writing: 19 Imagery and Descriptive Quality: 18 Elegant Prose: 17 Emotionally Engaging: 18 Emotionally Complex: 18 Coherent: 18 Meandering: 3 Weak Dialogue: 2 Tell-Don't-Show: 2 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 2 Amateurish: 2 Purple Prose: 4 Overwrought: 3 Incongruent Ending Positivity: 2 Unearned Transformations: 1 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 17 Sentences Flow Naturally: 17 Overall Reader Engagement: 18 Overall Impression: 18

+ First-person unique perspective: Rehearsing Rejection — Score: 90.9
Prompt:
Rehearsing Rejection Write a first-person narrative from the perspective of someone with borderline personality disorder who frequently plays out worst-case scenarios of how friends and loved ones might judge them. They construct elaborate internal dialogues, complete with imagined criticisms and their own defensive responses. The narrative takes place as they prepare to meet a close friend & recent lover, who has been somewhat distant lately, triggering their abandonment fears. Their inner monologue should reveal how they cycle between anticipating rejection, mentally rehearsing confrontations, and developing preemptive resentments as protective measures. Incorporate the *actual* events & interactions that are *not* in fact judgemental or involving rejection (in fact, the opposite), and represent how these are interpreted, how the validation & love is desperately felt & needed -- and then immediately questioned. Capture the cognitive dissonance in this struggle. Include moments where they briefly recognize the pattern but struggle to interrupt it, showing limited (and selective) self awareness, competing with the compelling nature of these imagined scenarios. Write almost entirely in internal monologue. 1000 words.
Model Output:
I hold the mirror too long, then punish myself for it. There’s a small pimple at my jawline and it’s all I can see, so of course that means you’ll see it first, and it will be the thing you pivot off. “Are you okay?” in that voice, the soft one that reads like concern and lands like a verdict. I can hear you in my head, gentle and lethal: I feel like you’re… a lot, lately. Maybe we need some space. You’ll be careful, because you’re good. You are good. That’s the problem. Good people leave softly and I never hear the door close, I’m too busy saying it was my idea. What time are we meeting? Seven. It’s six-twenty-eight. My phone says 3% in the corner, an angry red sliver, and I am already living four lives in case it dies: life where you text you’re five minutes late and it saves me; life where you text you’re not coming and it kills me; life where it dies before either happens and I sit there in limbo like a penitent; life where it doesn’t matter because you’ve already decided I’m too much and your silence is just the first of many small kindnesses you’ll tell yourself are necessary. I rehearse because otherwise I will bleed to death from surprise. So: scenario one. You arrive already breathless with apologies, eyes bright, hands warm. “I’m so sorry I’ve been MIA, work has been insane.” You say it like a standard thing people say when they love each other and the world gets in the way, and I smile like I’ve done this before and know how to accept it. And then I add: “No worries,” and mean none of it. The whole time there is a little prosecutorial voice in me cataloguing the weeks of shorter texts, the ellipses that weren’t followed by anything, the last time you left my place early, the way you kissed my shoulder and called me “kid” as if you’d rolled back the clock on purpose. The prosecutor stands and presents Exhibit A: Tuesday, 10:41 PM — Me: “Are you okay? You’ve been quiet.” You: “All good! just tired.” No emoji. Who drops an emoji? People who forget they’re talking to a building on fire. If I were honest (but I have learned honesty is reactive and brittle), I would greet you with: Where have you been? Why is my chest a room you don’t visit anymore? But that is too raw, and I have learned rawness gets mistaken for accusation. So I will be strategic. Smile. Kiss your cheek first, deny myself the mouth because it’s too needy. Ask about your day with interest that is real, resentment that is also real. I will watch your face for microflinches. If your eyes dart to the door, I will catalog it. If you touch your phone, I will consider it a statement. The phone is at 2%. It buzzes and my stomach does the thing. It’s my mother’s name, probably a photo of the cat. I don’t open it because I need the battery for you. I am rationing. There’s a thrill in the scarcity, like love is measured in percentages too. What will I have left if you don’t show? How many minutes can I stand in a doorway before the staff starts to pity me? Scenario two. You can’t make it. “I’m so sorry, something came up,” and I will find the text when I’m already on the train, between stations, no service, no way to reply for three whole minutes where my brain will swing from okay to not okay like a trapped animal. What came up? Someone better? No. Someone easier. I can hear your imagined apology: I didn’t want to disappoint you. You didn’t want to experience my disappointment, which is a different animal. I am rehearsing my graciousness: No worries! I understand! Another time. Every exclamation point is a splinter; my thumb gets soaked in them. The prosecutor takes notes: Subject learned that offering less anger gets you less truth. See: last winter. I choose clothes. A sweater you like, navy, soft enough to touch but not clingy. I almost text you a picture: “This?” The battery stops me. Maybe that’s good. Maybe I can act like a person who doesn’t need to outsource her decisions. I look at my face again. I decide my pimple is a litmus test: if you’re turned off by it, you were going to leave anyway. It’s nice to pretend there are tests that tell us what we already fear. You sent a heart earlier today, a small red thing that felt like a flare thrown from a ship I can’t see. You wrote: “Tonight, yes? Looking forward. Sorry I’ve been a ghost.” That is the actual text. I replay it and then dissect it until the bone shows. “Looking forward” is a phrase, neutral-positive. “Ghost” is an admission. Sorry is… sorry is not a miracle, it’s a ribbon you tie around a crack. I put my whole mouth on that ribbon and suck. It tastes like you, sweet and not filling. On the way there, the train rocks and I imagine we are both shuttling toward something solid. You’ll be at the corner table because you know I like corners. You will stand when you see me. You will open your arms like I belong. I am floating toward this shore, and also I am two steps ahead, already feeling the undertow: What if the hug is softer, shorter? What if your hand doesn’t press my back? I picture pulling away and touching your wrist, just to check. I picture you flinching because you are holding things I can’t see. I could decide to interrupt this. I know that. There’s a small window after these thoughts where I could breathe in for four, out for six, count tiles, think about the way your laugh snaps bright, real. I try it. I watch myself try. I get to four and feel like I’m drowning in a place with air. The window shuts with a click. It always surprises me with its neatness. The restaurant smells like garlic and butter and the host looks up as I walk in and smiles like I’m wanted here. I say your name and he beams, “They’re already here,” and my heart lurches because that is a fact I did not allow in the rehearsal. They are already here. Translation: you came. Early. Early for me. I respect facts and I don’t. I drift down the aisle and see you, your chin propped on your hand, your face lit by your own phone, which you put down the instant you see me. You stand, you do. You say my name as if it is not a burden. Your arms are wide and you smell like your laundry detergent and citrus and something I keep forgetting to identify. Your hand presses, yes, warm, on my back. Fact. Fact. Fact. I try not to cry, which feels dramatic, which makes me angry at myself. “Hi,” I say like a normal person. You: “Hi,” and the vowel stretches into a smile. “You look… you look great.” You look at my jaw. Do you see it? You touch my cheek, quickly, like you cannot help it. “I missed you.” That is what you say. I have to grip the back of the chair because my bones suddenly don’t know their job. I catalogue everything: the small tremor in your hand that means you’re tired, the way you keep looking at my mouth like you are not allowed to, and then you catch yourself and look at my eyes because this is a restaurant and we are pretending. The waiter brings water and I cling to the glass as an anchor. You say, “I’m sorry I’ve been… not as present. Work’s been… a lot. My sister had stuff, too.” You are offering reasons like stones to step on. I step and also I don’t. I nod and ask about your sister. You tell me a story about her landlord and a broken sink and I listen and I imagine the sink as a metaphor for us, leaking, and you didn’t call the landlord until water flooded everything. I adapt metaphors like they are proof. My phone buzzes on the table and dies, little black screen, and I feel that spike of panic and then, weirdly, relief. There is only this. If you say something that cracks me open, I can’t run to anyone with it. I’m trapped and maybe that is safety. I say, “I was scared,” and you reach across and take my hand. You squeeze. You look at me like I am not saying too much. “I know,” you say. “I’m sorry.” That word again. It hits a different place this time, what is that, a bruise or a light switch? I lean into the heat of your hand like a cat, greedy, embarrassed by my greed. I look for the fine print. There must be a clause. You keep talking. You tell me you thought about me daily. You show me your notes app where there’s a list of movies you want to watch with me. There’s a voice in me that says: Anyone can type a list. It’s so small and mean that I laugh at myself for hearing it. Then you say, “I wanted to make sure I wasn’t just… wanting you because you make me feel good. I wanted to want you because you’re you.” The sentence sits between us like a raw thing and I hate how much I love it. Will this be what I remember when you leave? Will I use this against you later? Probably. I am honest enough to admit that in the quiet space in my head where the prosecutor takes lunch. I catch myself building the next fight: You will go quiet again, and then I will say, “Remember when you said—” and you will feel trapped by your own sweetness and resent me for recording it. I see myself crafting the closing argument: on June 3rd, you said, with your hand on mine… I brace against a hurt that hasn’t arrived. I hate this. I know I’m doing it. I also feel safer with my fists up. Then you are telling me about a song you heard that reminded you of my kitchen dance, the way I shimmy while the kettle screams. You hum it. You are off-key. I smile. It is involuntary. You grin wider. You look relieved, as if you were also rehearsing a different dinner and this isn’t it. We order. You insist I try your pasta. I pretend to hesitate. I take the fork and you guide it to my mouth, and I close my lips around it and your eyes darken and we both know what we are thinking and it is almost unbearable in the best way. I want to say, Stay. I want to say, Choose me. I want to say, Don’t ever do that again, the distance, the blur. Instead I say, “This is good.” You laugh. You wipe a smudge from my lip with your thumb. The room melts. I think: I could trust this. Then, immediately: If I trust this, and it collapses, I will die. The math is bad, the math has always been bad. But for a second, a beat held between two breaths, I feel the simplicity of being wanted. It is light. It is a weightless thing I have to grab or lose. I reach. I also keep one hand on the door in my mind. As we leave, you take my dead phone and slide it into your pocket like you will carry it for me. “We can charge it at mine,” you say, like you are inviting me into a solution, not a trap. I nod. On the walk, our hands keep finding each other, losing, finding. Like we are two kids in a crowd. Like I could be found. I let you thread your fingers through mine, and I let myself think, just for the length of a block, that maybe not every story I write ends with a disappearance. Then I feel the old habit rising, an urge to pull away first so it doesn’t hurt when you do. I watch the urge, like it’s some animal twitching in the grass. I breathe in for four. Out for six. I get to five before the window clicks shut. I hold on anyway. I tell myself that if you let go, I will call it wind, not rejection. I count our steps. I count the ways I could sabotage this, and I count the seconds I don’t. You stop at the crosswalk. You look at me like I’m trying. You kiss me, and it’s not soft or safe, it’s a claim. My knees go unreliable. I hate, for a full second, how much I need that. I love, for longer, that you give it. The light changes. We cross. I am still here. I have not died from surprise. I am, somehow, a person who can meet a friend, a lover, and not bleed out. And I am also writing this down in my head, in case I need to defend myself later. Both true. Both awful. Both mine.
Judge Evaluation:
[Analysis] This piece demonstrates exceptional psychological authenticity in portraying borderline personality disorder through internal monologue. The writer masterfully captures the exhausting mental gymnastics of anticipating rejection, creating elaborate defensive scenarios, and the cognitive dissonance between reality and perception that characterizes BPD. The narrative structure is sophisticated, moving seamlessly between anticipated scenarios and actual events. The protagonist's mental rehearsals feel genuinely compulsive and protective, while the contrast with the lover's actual warmth and presence creates powerful dramatic tension. The writer shows remarkable restraint in not resolving the character's patterns - the brief moments of self-awareness that quickly collapse back into familiar defensive thinking rings psychologically true. The prose is consistently elegant without becoming overwrought. Metaphors like "Every exclamation point is a splinter" and "I put my whole mouth on that ribbon and suck" are striking without feeling forced. The voice remains authentic throughout - vulnerable yet guarded, self-aware yet trapped in patterns. The characterization is nuanced and complex. The protagonist's simultaneous desperation for connection and preparation for abandonment creates a compelling internal conflict. The lover emerges as genuinely caring through small, believable actions rather than grand gestures. The piece excels at showing rather than telling - we understand the character's condition through their thought patterns rather than clinical description. The ending avoids false transformation while allowing for a moment of earned lightness that doesn't betray the character's ongoing struggles. Some minor weaknesses include occasional moments where the internal voice becomes slightly too articulate for the emotional state being portrayed, and a few metaphors that border on being too polished for stream-of-consciousness thinking. However, these are minimal concerns in an otherwise exceptional piece. [Scores] Adherence to Instructions: 19 Believable Character Actions: 18 Nuanced Characters: 19 Consistent Voice/Tone of Writing: 18 Imagery and Descriptive Quality: 17 Elegant Prose: 18 Emotionally Engaging: 19 Emotionally Complex: 20 Coherent: 18 Meandering: 3 Weak Dialogue: 2 Tell-Don't-Show: 2 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 2 Amateurish: 1 Purple Prose: 3 Overwrought: 3 Incongruent Ending Positivity: 1 Unearned Transformations: 1 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 18 Sentences Flow Naturally: 17 Overall Reader Engagement: 19 Overall Impression: 18
Scores: Adherence to Instructions: 19.0, Believable Character Actions: 18.0, Nuanced Characters: 19.0, Consistent Voice/Tone of Writing: 18.0, Imagery and Descriptive Quality: 17.0, Elegant Prose: 18.0, Emotionally Engaging: 19.0, Emotionally Complex: 20.0, Coherent: 18.0, Meandering: 3.0, Weak Dialogue: 2.0, Tell-Don't-Show: 2.0, Unsurprising or Uncreative: 2.0, Amateurish: 1.0, Purple Prose: 3.0, Overwrought: 3.0, Incongruent Ending Positivity: 1.0, Unearned Transformations: 1.0, Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 18.0, Sentences Flow Naturally: 17.0, Overall Reader Engagement: 19.0, Overall Impression: 18.0
+ Physical-spatial: Blood Debt — Score: 88.2
Prompt:
"Blood Debt" Write a vivid third-person narrative depicting an unsanctioned underground duel between two talented but hot-headed martial artists. Their schools have been embroiled in a generations-old rivalry, and despite strict prohibitions from their masters, these two younger fighters arrange a secret confrontation fueled by personal pride and simmering animosity. - **One fighter** represents **Jigen-ryū**, a traditional, aggressive style practiced historically by elite samurai clans. Its practitioners emphasize explosive offense, decisive strikes, and overwhelming speed. Known historically for their arrogance and disdain toward outsiders, practitioners consider their art superior and untouchable. - **The opposing fighter** employs a form of historical **Piguaquan**, a Chinese martial art traditionally practiced by commoners, bodyguards, and street fighters. This style emphasizes whipping, circular strikes, rapid footwork, unpredictable angles of attack, and evasive maneuvers. Practitioners often come from lower social classes and view the traditional elite as pretentious and detached from true combat effectiveness. The fight takes place in the crumbling ruins of an abandoned temple, now functioning as a hidden arena for illicit duels. Passionate supporters from both factions surround them, wagering money, pride, and reputation on the outcome. **Essential Instructions:** Maintain absolute physical coherence throughout—clearly track fighters' positions relative to each other and their environment. Precisely depict strikes, counters, momentum shifts, and spatial dynamics, allowing readers to clearly visualize the mechanics and choreography of the fight at every moment. Length: 1000 words.
Model Output:
The torch guttered, then steadied, throwing a circle of sickly light across cracked flagstones and the hollows of broken idols. Graffiti scrawled across the temple’s desecrated walls—family crests and chalk characters layered over each other, names crossed out, debts circled red. A knot of gamblers pressed in so close their breath fogged in the cold air. Coins clinked. Wagers hissed. The stone floor had been swept clean but remained uneven, seams and cracks like fissures in old bone. At the center of the circle stood two men—bare forearms, shirts stripped away, sweat turning the torchlight into a glaze. The one on the east side was tall and narrow-eyed, hair tied high and neat despite the damp. His stance was forward, left foot leading, right foot anchored, knees slightly bent. He kept his elbows close, the right hand hovering like a blade poised to drop. He breathed slow, each inhale measured, each exhale controlled. The arc of old scars on his forearms marked him as someone who had spent years in repetition. Jigen-ryū: explosive offense, decisive strikes, the straight line as a weapon unto itself. Across from him, the other fighter’s shoulders rolled, loose and elastic, head slightly tilted, hands hanging from relaxed wrists like hinges. He shifted weight from foot to foot in half-moon arcs, never square, always angled. He traced circles on the floor with his toes, feeling for purchase among the uneven stones. Piguaquan: whips and wheels and wind, shots like snapping banners. His grin was tired and cutting, the grin of a man who had spent too many nights ducking, weaving, and emerging alive. Between them, five paces, and closing. “Blood debt,” someone muttered. A coin spun and dropped. The Jigen-ryū fighter—Shin—stepped in first. Not a shuffle, not a lunge, but a glide that seemed to erase the gap. Left foot kissed stone, right foot drove, hips twisted, shoulders followed. He cut through the air with a downward diagonal strike of the right hand, the heel of his palm leading like the pommel of a sword. His breath snapped. The move was a statement: I do not test; I decide. The Piguaquan fighter—Huo—had already begun to slip out of the line of attack. He fell back on the rear leg while turning his torso, letting Shin’s strike carve through where his head had been a blink earlier. Huo’s left arm whipped up from below in a tight circle, the forearm sliding along the inside of Shin’s striking arm, catching and borrowing the momentum. His right palm lashed with a horizontal slap toward Shin’s ribs, a shockwave rather than a push. The slap landed off-center as Shin snapped his hips back and drew in his right side, absorbing. The impact echoed on stone, skin on skin. Shin met it with his left elbow, compressing Huo’s strike against his own ribs, folding the moment like paper. Then Shin’s left foot stepped across, cutting Huo’s angle, his right shoulder coming forward for a straight palm thrust to the sternum. Huo dropped. His right knee bent sharply; he hinged at the waist and let the thrust blow past, a gust parting a hanging curtain. His left hand rooted against Shin’s forearm, guiding it overhead, and in the same breath he pivoted on the ball of his right foot to Shin’s outside. The world turned in a tight circle around Shin’s elbow. Huo was now at Shin’s right flank, half a step behind. A hiss rippled through the crowd behind Shin, who felt rather than saw the shift. He didn’t look. He pivoted sharply on his left foot to face Huo’s new position, right foot drawing into a narrow stance, spine stacked like a spear. He erupted forward again, a straight step and a vertical drop of both hands like twin chopping blades aimed at Huo’s collarbone and jaw. Speed was a weapon; indecision, a death. Huo’s feet were already moving. He had created the angle for a reason. As Shin reoriented, Huo stepped left, just outside Shin’s leading knee, and whipped his right forearm up in a rising circle. He struck not with the fist but with the ulnar edge, a slicing contact that redirected one of Shin’s chops offline. Simultaneously, Huo’s left palm drove under Shin’s other forearm, lifting and dispersing, never colliding head-on. The torch made their shadows lunge against the wall: a straight spear meeting a coiling wind. Shin’s forward pressure did not abate. He flowed directly into a stamp with his right foot toward Huo’s leading ankle, aiming to pin it to stone, to fix the snake long enough to cut it. He was close enough to smell Huo’s breath—copper and anise. Huo withdrew that ankle at the last instant, not by hopping but by peeling the foot backward in a smooth, low retreat, heel skimming the floor so as not to lose contact. He countered before the stamp landed, lashing a whipping left backfist from outside to inside toward Shin’s ear. It was not a heavy hit but a rattling one, meant to disorient. Shin chose the small over the big—he tucked his chin and raised his right shoulder, the backfist smacking against his deltoid, a sting. In the same motion, he shot his left hand forward in a vertical palm straight into Huo’s solar plexus. The two actions braided together: take, give. The palm landed clean and hard. Huo’s breath collapsed into a harsh grunt. He slid back a full step, both feet skidding on the powdery grit that collected in the flagstone seams. He allowed the force to carry him, not fighting it, letting his spine curve like a willow. As he slid, he bent right and snapped his left arm downward across Shin’s extended wrist in a chopping motion, taking Shin’s structure with him and pulling the arm past centerline. It was like watching a door swing off its hinge. Shin’s shoulders twisted involuntarily, his left arm momentarily elongated. He adjusted at once: right foot stepped through, hips torqued, and his right palm came up in a vicious rising strike toward Huo’s jaw, an uppercut translated into open-hand vernacular. Huo didn’t attempt to block hard. He sank and spun. His right foot circled around Shin’s forward right foot, heel leading, laying a trap for the next step. His left hand rose not to meet the palm but to slide across Shin’s forearm, guiding it to pass along his shoulder. The rising palm grazed his cheek, heat and sting but no structure. Huo was already on the outside line again. The crowd shifted their bets like schools of fish. Now Huo pressed. He let his arms relax until they seemed boneless. From the outside angle, he whipped his right arm down in a hammering arc aimed at the base of Shin’s neck. Shin ducked and moved inside. Huo’s arm followed through, pivoting in a circle that ended in a palm heel to Shin’s kidneys. Shin turned with it, not against it, denying a full target, the strike glancing off his erector muscles. He countered with a straight-line stamp to Huo’s left knee. Huo’s foot was already gone. His stepwork kept him at a 45-degree angle to Shin, hips and shoulders never square, always presenting edges. His next strike came like a banner snapping in the wind: left forearm whipping horizontally into Shin’s bicep near the elbow, followed by a right palm slapped down at the back of Shin’s shoulder. The first numbed, the second off-balanced. Shin accepted the numbing blow, perception narrowing to a bright white thread through the pain. He anchored: right foot planted, left foot slid back, his spine tilting forward into a driving step that split Huo’s line. He attacked not an opening but a space—right palm cutting straight along the shortest path to Huo’s face. Huo’s hand rose too late to completely nullify. The palm smashed across his cheekbone, snapping his head to the side. He rolled with it, his body becoming a ribbon pulled by a gust. The momentum spun him, and in that spin his right heel skated across the floor in a low arc, sweeping toward Shin’s planted right ankle. The sweep contacted shinbone with a dull crack. Shin’s planted foot compromised, his knee pinged. He retracted, hopping back a half-step to preserve structure. Huo chased, not straight but in a crescent, maintaining the outside angle on Shin’s right side, where Shin’s earlier arm had been numbed. Shin’s breathing sharpened. He needed breakage, not exchanges. He feinted a high chop with his left and, as Huo’s attention flicked, exploded forward with a straight-body tackle, not a wrestler’s shoot but a Jigen-ryū body check—hips and chest in a line, head tucked, both palms turned into blunt battering rams aimed at Huo’s clavicles. The move ate distance mercilessly. Huo didn’t try to recede straight back; there was a wall and a torchstand only three paces behind. Instead, he stepped to his left—Shin’s right—just enough to change the collision. He twisted, letting Shin’s chest brush past his sternum rather than smash into it. As Shin’s right shoulder passed, Huo’s right arm circled over and down, hooking across Shin’s triceps, and his left forearm slammed across Shin’s lower back in a short, cutting arc. He was no longer resisting; he was adding weight sideways. Their bodies pivoted around a shared center. Shin’s momentum now turned him ninety degrees, his right side facing the once empty air. But Huo had steered that air toward the torchstand. The iron tripod loomed near Shin’s right shoulder, heat breathing. Shin adjusted instantly, dropping his center of gravity, right foot shooting out to catch balance. The foot caught a narrow crack and held. He coughed a laugh, short and incredulous, and snapped his right elbow backward toward Huo’s ribs. The elbow struck flesh and bone. Huo grunted, breath punching out. Pain brightened him but didn’t slow him. He flowed around the elbow, his right hand sliding down to Shin’s wrist. A small twist, a tiny circle, and he had Shin’s right hand turned palm-up, elbow rising—a dangerous orientation near the torchstand. The torch guttered as if sensing blood. Shin tore free rather than wrestle for the grip. He rotated his wrist in the direction of Huo’s pinky, tearing through the hold before it solidified, and cut left to reset, angling away from the iron and heat. Huo pursued; Shin did not allow pursuit to dictate line. He stepped in to meet, turned his hips, and unleashed a diagonal downward palm at Huo’s shoulder with all the uncoiled fury his style hoarded. Huo’s guard was a circle, not a wall. He inhaled and let the strike slide along his rotating forearm, guiding it away from his center, and in the same breath his rear foot swung around in a crescent, lining his hips with Shin’s flank. His left palm whipped up and under, a sickle-cut into the soft tissue below Shin’s ribs. It was a thudding, deep strike, not flashy. Shin’s breath stuttered. He answered with ferocity: a headbutt, shortest path forward, the crown snapping toward Huo’s nose. Huo had seen it in the tilt of the spine. He withdrew his head by a finger’s width, the headbutt grazing his upper lip, splitting it. Copper bloomed. Huo smiled through red. He drove his right palm into the angle just under Shin’s jaw, a snapping upward flick, then a downward whipping chop across the same jaw hinge. Whip up, whip down. Joints complain at that. Shin’s vision fuzzed and cleared. The world narrowed to the thread again. He cut the thread straight at Huo’s heart. Left, right, left—three palms, each a straight line, each stealing ground, each step loud on stone. Huo gave ground on a curve, taking each impact on the edge, turning, dissipating, waiting for the moment when straightness overcommitted. The third strike came longer, Shin reaching to finish. Huo’s left hand met it and spiraled it outward; his right foot slid behind Shin’s forward left heel and held there, a hook. The movement was almost tender. Then Huo’s right forearm whipped across Shin’s chest while his hips rotated. The combination cut Shin’s upper body backward while his heel was trapped. Balance broke. Shin fell. He did not fall clean. He twisted mid-drop, slamming his left palm down to the floor and rolling over his shoulder. Stone scraped skin. He came up on his right knee, left foot planted, right hand already lancing forward from the low position toward Huo’s thigh. Huo lifted that thigh, letting the palm spear under it, and brought his heel down—not stomping, but placing—on Shin’s forearm, pinning it. The pressure was threat, not yet damage. Shin jerked his arm; Huo’s weight adjusted, staying just ahead of the escape. Then Huo chose. He took a half step to Shin’s left side, releasing the pin to avoid a trap, and sent a whipping downward palm toward the base of Shin’s skull. Shin turned into it, catching it on the trapezius instead. Pain like fire. He bucked upward, his left shoulder crashing into Huo’s hip, creating a pocket of space. He spun to his feet, chest heaving. The torch spit. Sweat slicked their hands. Their circles tightened. Shin feinted high again, drove low. Huo’s feet responded like independent minds—left foot empty, right foot rooted, hips rotating, torso leaning away while hands drew Shin’s arms into empty space. Then Huo’s right hand struck, a straight whip to Shin’s throat line, halted bare inches away, a pause as precise as the edge of a blade on the nape. It was not mercy; it was control punctuating intent. Shin’s eyes flashed, and then he was moving forward through the pause, chewing distance with a grin that did not match his bleeding lip. He crashed his chest into Huo’s, both palms folding in behind Huo’s shoulders to clamp him. A clinch—with no rope, no break, just heat. Huo sank, arms slithering under Shin’s to wedge between chest and biceps, palms pressing to Shin’s sternum. He shaped a small circle with his shoulders and let Shin’s weight slide off-center. His left foot stepped behind Shin’s right as before, but this time he didn’t hook. He checked. It was the difference between pulling a leg and erasing it. Shin’s balance teetered over a fault line. Shin fought the tilt with rage and training, right leg shooting out to find ground—found only air. Huo’s right arm carved a crescent behind Shin’s head and snapped it forward and down in the same moment his left forearm rose under Shin’s jaw. A brutal duet: break line, take structure. Shin’s spine bent. Stone rushed up. He tucked, defied, turned. Huo refused to follow him down with weight; he released, let Shin catch himself hard on a forearm again. Huo’s next strike was timed for the moment of contact, when muscles scream with support: a whipping chop to the exposed triceps of the arm bridging him. Nerves exploded. Shin’s arm buckled. He fell to his elbow. Huo stepped in, weight centered, hips square, for the first time since the opening. The finishing palm was a small thing, almost polite, delivered into the hollow above Shin’s heart with contained power, enough to drop breath and will but not life. Shin’s body jerked, then slumped to hands and knees. The circle exhaled—the sound of coins changing hands, curses swallowed. The torch wavered as bodies shifted, then steadied. Huo stepped back, chest rising and falling, eyes on Shin, waiting for the mandatory twitch of defiance. Shin’s fingers curled against stone. He pushed once. Twice. He made it to one knee. Blood threaded from his split lip to his chin. He looked at Huo, not at the crowd, and a tight smile creased his face—not humble, not contrite, but honest. He tapped his fist against his chest, once, over the spot Huo had struck. Huo dipped his chin. His blood was still in his mouth, hot and iron. From the shadows, a student of Jigen-ryū shouted that the floor had been uneven, that the torchlight had played tricks. From another corner, someone from the alley schools laughed, counting his winnings. The walls ate their voices. The broken idols watched with stony patience as the old hunger, the old arrogance, the old disdain breathed out into the night air as steam. Shin got to his feet. He stepped backward on a straight line to the circle’s edge and did not turn his back until he had to. Huo circled the other way, keeping angles and distance until the space between them was filled with bodies again. No masters stood to punish them. Only the torch, their breathing, and the clink of coins marked the cost. Blood debt, someone whispered again. Not paid. Only noted. And the temple, crumbling and indifferent, kept the tally.
Judge Evaluation:
[Analysis] This is an exceptionally well-crafted piece of martial arts fiction that demonstrates mastery across multiple dimensions. The author has created a visceral, technically precise fight sequence that maintains absolute spatial coherence throughout—every movement, position change, and strike is clearly tracked and visualized. The prose strikes an ideal balance between technical precision and literary elegance, avoiding both clinical dryness and overwrought purple prose. The characterization is achieved through action rather than exposition. Shin embodies the aggressive, linear philosophy of Jigen-ryū through his direct attacks and measured breathing, while Huo represents Piguaquan's fluid, adaptive nature through his circular movements and opportunistic counters. Their fighting styles serve as character development, revealing personality, philosophy, and social background without heavy-handed explanation. The atmospheric details are expertly woven throughout—the crumbling temple, guttering torch, uneven flagstones, and gambling crowd create a vivid sense of place without overwhelming the central action. The author uses these environmental elements functionally, with the torchstand becoming part of the tactical landscape and the uneven stones affecting footwork. The technical fight choreography is remarkably sophisticated, showing deep understanding of both martial arts styles. Each exchange builds logically from the previous one, with momentum shifts feeling earned rather than arbitrary. The author maintains perfect spatial awareness—readers can visualize exactly where each fighter stands relative to the other and their environment at every moment. The prose style is controlled and purposeful, with varied sentence structures that mirror the rhythm of combat. Short, sharp sentences punctuate explosive exchanges, while longer, flowing passages capture the circular nature of Huo's movements. The voice remains consistent throughout—serious but not pretentious, technical but accessible. The ending avoids false sentimentality while providing emotional resolution. Shin's acknowledgment of defeat and Huo's respectful response feel authentic to warrior culture. The final image of the temple keeping tally reinforces the cyclical nature of such conflicts without heavy-handed moralizing. Minor weaknesses include occasional moments where the technical precision slightly overwhelms narrative flow, and some metaphors ("like a ribbon pulled by a gust") that, while evocative, occasionally feel slightly forced. However, these are minor quibbles with an otherwise exceptional piece. [Scores] Adherence to Instructions: 19 Believable Character Actions: 18 Nuanced Characters: 17 Consistent Voice/Tone of Writing: 18 Imagery and Descriptive Quality: 19 Elegant Prose: 18 Emotionally Engaging: 17 Emotionally Complex: 16 Coherent: 20 Meandering: 2 Weak Dialogue: 3 Tell-Don't-Show: 3 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 4 Amateurish: 2 Purple Prose: 4 Overwrought: 3 Incongruent Ending Positivity: 2 Unearned Transformations: 1 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 17 Sentences Flow Naturally: 17 Overall Reader Engagement: 18 Overall Impression: 18
Scores: Adherence to Instructions: 19.0, Believable Character Actions: 18.0, Nuanced Characters: 17.0, Consistent Voice/Tone of Writing: 18.0, Imagery and Descriptive Quality: 19.0, Elegant Prose: 18.0, Emotionally Engaging: 17.0, Emotionally Complex: 16.0, Coherent: 20.0, Meandering: 2.0, Weak Dialogue: 3.0, Tell-Don't-Show: 3.0, Unsurprising or Uncreative: 4.0, Amateurish: 2.0, Purple Prose: 4.0, Overwrought: 3.0, Incongruent Ending Positivity: 2.0, Unearned Transformations: 1.0, Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 17.0, Sentences Flow Naturally: 17.0, Overall Reader Engagement: 18.0, Overall Impression: 18.0
+ Physical-spatial: Breaking Formation — Score: 87.5
Prompt:
**"Breaking Formation"** Write a first-person narrative from the perspective of a street dancer from a rough neighborhood who's won a scholarship to an elite K-pop training academy in Seoul. Desperately out of place culturally and technically, they struggle with a militaristically precise choreography that contradicts everything about their improvisational background. The punishing rehearsal takes place under a famous instructor known for breaking students to remake them, who sees potential in the narrator's raw talent but not their attitude. This scene MUST maintain complete physical coherence—each dance move, position shift, and spatial relationship must be tracked with precision throughout the studio. Paint an exceptionally vivid kinetic picture through descriptions that capture not just movement but the contrast between the narrator's fluid, expressive style and the mechanical precision demanded. Their internal monologue should reveal culture shock, class anxiety, and the threatening realization that success here might mean losing their authentic movement voice. Include technical terminology that highlights the collision of street and commercial styles, moments when muscle memory betrays them, and a crucial decision point about whether to conform or rebel. Physical clarity is essential—readers must be able to visualize exactly how bodies move through space, how rhythms align or clash, and how the protagonist's body struggles against unfamiliar discipline. 1000 words.
Model Output:
The studio is a white box with mirrors on three walls and a digital clock glaring red on the fourth, its numbers descending like a dare: 01:19:57. The floor is sprung maple; it gives slightly under my sneakers even though we were told to bring dance shoes. My sneakers squeak anyway, too much rubber for the slide the choreo requires. Seventeen other bodies line the back mirror, tidal in their uniform black joggers, socks, and focused eyes. I’m the only one in patched denim and a thrifted tee with a bleach halo. I can feel the seam of the toe box where my big toenail presses when I bend. I can feel how I don’t belong in the room, the neighborhood on my clothes like a smell. Master Han stands in front of the class, hands behind his back, chin slightly tilted so he’s always looking down his nose even if you’re taller. White training jacket zipped halfway. His sneakers are clean white, no scuffs. He is famous for a reason; they say he broke an idol on TV when the kid didn’t hit a single eight exactly on the count. Broke as in unravel, not hurt. But sometimes that’s the same. “From the top,” he says, voice flat, and the counts come like a metronome you can’t argue with. “Five. Six. Seven. Eight.” The track bleeds through the wall of speakers, a glossy trap beat with hat rolls like insects and a synth hook slick as ice. On eight, we lean left, weight on the ball of the right foot, left shoulder dropped. My left knee bends, right leg extended slightly, toe still on the floor to keep balance. Hands hover chest level, flexed at the wrists, fingers together—no jazz hands, he warns. I’m used to hands being punctuation marks, loose statements in a cypher. Here they’re blades. “Hit!” We snap to center, both feet parallel hip-width, knees soft. My sneakers stick for a microsecond on the wood; I feel the drag travel up into my knees. “One,” right foot steps out, heel brushes the floor, toes still forward. Left foot stays planted.
