Right hand slices up from the left hip across the sternum to the right shoulder, path clean. The left hand drops to hover above the left thigh. “Two,” my chest pops forward, sharp, then releases. I nail chest hits without thinking; I’ve popped at streetlights while waiting to cross. “Clean,” Han says, but he’s not looking at me. The mirrored wall shows a line of bodies whose shoulders and heads tilt at identical angles. My left shoulder is hospitable; it always wants to lead. In krump cyphers, we let the shoulder initiate like it’s got a story. Here the counts are the story. “Three-and-four,” we thread the right arm under the left, elbows at ninety degrees, wrists locked, a mechanical weave that travels across the body while the feet do a syncopated toe-heel-toe on the right, left foot now on demi-plié to accommodate the weight shift. I stumble on the “and”—my muscle memory wants to throw a small groove, a body roll to bleed the counts into each other. Master Han’s head twitches like he smelled something off. “No bounce,” he says. “You’re not in a club. Again. Five. Six. Seven. Eight.” Back to the left lean. The clock ticks down: 01:15:42. We mark the first sixteen over and over until my joints feel oiled with discipline. On “five,” the group slides, a glissade of feet, left foot dragging two inches as it pulls, right foot pushing. The slide is not a glide; it is an exact distance, five floorboard widths. We land on six, both feet together, knees knocked in just enough that the camera will catch the line. My knees protest. Where I’m from, knees face out when you’re ready. Here they want knock-kneed angles. On seven, the right hand flicks up, wrist leading, not elbow. On eight, we freeze. The freeze is a breath with no air. My body wants to exhale into something else—into a rock, a pulse in the hips, a roll of shoulder. I hold it. Sweat crawls down my spine in a straight line. By the time we reach the chorus, Master Han calls for full out. “No marking,” he says. “Breathing is your only excuse.” He plays the chorus slower first, to lock the shapes into us. We step into a V formation. I’m third from the left; my peripheral is full of two boys with identical bowl cuts and a girl with a sharp jaw who never blinks. Master Han paces the diagonal aisle between us, watching feet, not faces. “Your left foot, number eleven,” he says without looking at the girl. She adjusts half a centimeter. He turns and his eyes find me like a laser pointer. “You,” he says. “You are freestyle, yes?” He gestures like he’s flinging a bug off his hand. “I’m… from street,” I say. The English is an ache. He switches to Korean and I follow enough to understand the insult tucked inside instruction. “Street is flavor. This is knife. Knife cuts. Flavor can sit later. Now, angle is thirty degrees.” He walks to me. He positions my right foot with his hand, rotates the toe out until it points exactly toward the corner mirror. He touches my elbow, lowers it. The contact is clinical; my skin hums anyway, old instincts flaring. We don’t get touched without permission where I’m from. We don’t get touched for free. “Again. Five. Six.” We go. The chorus is a traveling sequence; we move from V to two lines, then back to a tighter V, center stepping forward on count four, everyone else shifting around them to make the geometry happen. It’s like the street games we played—tag but with choreography and no mercy. On one, we’re in the V, me third left, left foot forward, right foot in place, weight forward ready to push. On two, we pivot on the balls of our feet, ninety-degree turn to stage left, right leg crossing behind left, a grapevine but staccato. On the “and,” right foot opens to parallel, and on three, we take a two-step forward, the right foot leading, step together, step. Our upper bodies stay squared to front the whole time—this is the commercial polish that goes against every mechanic in my improvisation. My ribs want to follow the feet and twist. I keep my sternum like a plate facing the camera in my head. My glutes fire to hold that unnatural alignment. On four, the center—Kyung-min—is at the apex and he hits a body wave synced to a sub-bass drop. The rest of us hit freezes—arms out, low second position, palms down. The knees bend to forty-five degrees, not fifty, not thirty. I memorize the angle by feel. On five we ripple into a tut sequence, right wrist, right elbow, right shoulder, then left. It’s animation, but sanitized, no lingering, no narrative. My tuts want to travel, to tell a story. Here they click, Lego bricks in a line. Six is a floor hit. We drop. Hands go to the floor shoulder-width, fingers forward, elbows soft. Knees shoot back to a plank. A male trainee two bodies down misses the timing by a hair and his knee thuds. Master Han doesn’t look at him. The clock says 01:02:03. We pop back up on the “and,” feet under hips, then break to two lines, odd numbers forward, evens back, on seven-eight. I move to the right diagonal for my line, tracking the floorboards so I land on a knot in the wood. I mark it in my brain: this knot is “seven.” Physical landmarks are more reliable than my panic. We run it at tempo, the hook slamming the room. My lungs are organized as fists. My forearms ache from holding angles. On the second chorus, my knee tracks inward when it should track over my second toe and Master Han’s voice flicks toward me. “Knee.” I move it. He’s always right; that’s the worst of it. His precision is a map I could learn if I let it erase mine. Break. Water in paper cups. The cup sweats in my hand. The girl with the jaw sits nearby, back straight, bottle between knees. She doesn’t look at me but shifts her bag two inches, making room. “You’re the scholarship,” she says without asking, Korean crisp. “Yeah.” “You dance big,” she says. It’s not a compliment. “Big doesn’t fit your camera,” I say. My accent chews it. She considers that. “It can,” she says. “If you fit the box first.” I think of the box. Of the boxes I lived in—buildings with bars on windows, cardboard drawers, subway cars. Boxes kept you safe until they didn’t. The clock: 00:47:56. Master Han claps sharp twice. “Again from second pre-chorus,” he says. “This time, no face. Muscle before face.” He looks at me when he says face and I can’t help the smile that sneaks out. We learn to perform our insides; here the inside is irrelevant. Second pre-chorus is the killer. A syncopated footwork grid, sixteenths at 130 BPM. We start in two staggered lines. On one, right foot taps forward, toe only, left knee bends to accommodate. On the “e,” the right foot retracts, on “and” it taps to the side, on “a” it retracts. Two is left foot to the back in a toe tap, “e” in, “and” side, “a” in. Hands hover near hips, wrists flexed, elbows angled outward, no extraneous motion. This is house footwork with the groove stripped out. My calves burn. My shoulders want to ride the rhythm, a small jack in the spine. I suppress it. I feel the suppression like a bruise forming. Three, we ball-change right-left, feet light, on the balls. Four, we add an isometric hold—contract glutes and quads to create a stop. Master Han’s cue: “Imagine you are a statue.” He watches our quads. If he sees tremble, he smiles like a shark. On five-and-six we run a heel-toe sequence traveling back, right foot heel in, left foot heel in, right foot toe in, left toe in. The feet carve small opposing angles so our knees kiss then open. It makes a saw-tooth pattern on the floor. Seven we prep, eight we turn—a single coupe, foot placed at the ankle, arms first in, spot the mirror. My spot stutters—my neighborhood eyes learned to watch everything, not one thing. I force my eyes to latch on the mole above my own right eyebrow in the mirror. The room turns around my locked gaze. I don’t fall. He stops the music. The silence is obscene. He looks at each of us like he’s checking inventory. Then he points at me. “Solo,” he says. He steps back, motions to the center X taped on the floor. He cues the track at the pre-chorus, my mind a metronome of counting. I step onto the X, heels lined with the tape, toes slightly out. The others recede into my periphery, breaths and eyes and expectation. “Five. Six. Seven. Eight.” I go. I hit the taps, the ball-change, the heel-toes. I clamp my ribs, lock my elbows to angles. I keep my face empty. The turn is clean. For the space of eight counts, I’m a machine. People watching me nod. Even Master Han tilts his head a millimeter in acknowledgement. My body hums with the rare dignity of doing something hard, right. Then the track hits the chorus too early—his finger must have skipped a beat on the console—and the switch vibrates through me like a shock. My muscle memory leaps ahead giant, dragging my brain. My right shoulder twitches into a sway, then down, inviting the chest to follow and hit a groove on two that doesn’t exist in his eight. My hands loosen. The floor under my feet becomes asphalt for a fraction, and I feel the concrete after midnight, the waterlogged cardboard under our sneakers as we battled under the elevated train. My spine articulates, tailbone under, roll up through lumbar, thoracic, cervical. It is less than a second. It is everything. “Stop,” Master Han says. No anger. He walks toward me, stops a foot away so I can see the fine wrinkles around his eyes. The class holds breath like a stop-motion frame. The clock: 00:29:18. “You have two choices,” he says. His English returns for me. “You can be artist now and later, unknown. Or you can be product now and later, known. Your movement is…” He searches. “Beautiful. But not useful until it is contained.” I swallow. The floor smells like sweat cut with citrus disinfectant. I can hear my mother’s voice across an ocean, saying six months is a long time, don’t come home early. I can hear my crew laughing, saying you think you’ll survive the K-pop robot factory? I can feel the weight of the scholarship letter in my backpack, the fine paper like skin. He steps back, gives me space to answer. It is a performance too—the deference that isn’t. I look at the X on the floor, the tape edges lifting a little. I roll my shoulders back and set my feet in parallel, toes forward, knees soft to exactly the right degree. I visualize the trajectories, the angles, the formations like streets on a map that I must learn to walk without looking down. I feel the hurt of my groove wanting out, like a dog at a door. “Again,” I say. My voice doesn’t wobble. “From the top.” We run it. I keep my chest facing front even when my feet travel diagonally, even when every cell in me wants to twist. I make the tut lines ruler-straight. I land the freeze without breath. I slice the air at thirty degrees, not thirty-one. I am a knife. At 00:12:47 he calls a final run. We mark the formation shifts with trust. On one we’re V, on four center hits, on five-and we ripple, on six we drop, seven-eight two lines, then on the bridge we break into staggered diagonals. I track the girl’s shoulder as my anchor. I track the knot in the wood as my seven. I track myself. On the last chorus, there is a measure where he gives a two-count freestyle before the pose, because in the industry you must fake freedom sometimes. I stand on count seven, sweat dripping from my chin, and he says “Now,” and I choose. For two counts, I let the groove leak—just in the ankles, a Micro. A pulse, almost imperceptible. A secret in motion. Then I sheath it, hit the final pose, left hand blade at jawline, right hand knife edge across rib cage, feet in second, knees at forty-five. The clock reads 00:00:03. Then 00:00:02. Master Han exhales. “Better,” he says. Not “good.” Not yet. He meets my eyes in the mirror, and the faintest of smiles pricks the corner of his mouth. A concession. A future, maybe. The numbers hit zero. The room doesn’t explode. It just breathes. My legs shake, but they hold me. My voice is gone, but my body can still speak, even inside this box. I don’t know yet what I’ve traded or what I’ll get back. I only know the map and the beat, the line and the pulse, both written in me now. Outside, the city is mostly air and neon. Inside, I watch my chest rise and fall, exactly between counts. Exactly mine.
Judge Evaluation:
[Analysis] This piece demonstrates exceptional technical mastery in fulfilling the prompt's demanding requirements. The author maintains meticulous physical coherence throughout, tracking every dance move, spatial relationship, and formation change with precision that allows readers to visualize the choreography clearly. The contrast between street dance fluidity and K-pop precision is expertly rendered through specific technical terminology and kinesthetic descriptions. The first-person narrative voice is authentic and consistent, capturing the protagonist's cultural displacement through both language choices and movement descriptions. The internal monologue effectively reveals culture shock, class anxiety, and the central tension between authenticity and conformity without becoming heavy-handed. The protagonist's struggle feels genuine—their muscle memory betraying them at crucial moments creates believable conflict. The prose demonstrates remarkable control, balancing technical dance terminology with evocative imagery. Descriptions like "My tuts want to travel, to tell a story. Here they click, Lego bricks in a line" perfectly encapsulate the artistic tension. The author avoids purple prose while maintaining rich sensory detail, particularly in kinesthetic descriptions that make the reader feel the physical strain and spatial relationships. Master Han emerges as a nuanced antagonist—demanding but not cartoonish, with clear pedagogical purpose behind his severity. The brief interaction with the fellow trainee adds social dimension without deriving focus. The protagonist's ultimate decision feels earned rather than forced, representing compromise rather than complete capitulation or rebellion. The pacing builds effectively toward the climactic solo performance, and the resolution acknowledges complexity rather than offering false resolution. The ending's measured optimism feels appropriate—the protagonist hasn't solved their fundamental conflict but has found a way to navigate it temporarily. Minor weaknesses include occasional moments where technical descriptions slightly overwhelm narrative flow, and some metaphors that border on overwrought, though these are rare. The piece succeeds remarkably in its ambitious scope, delivering both technical precision and emotional authenticity. [Scores] Adherence to Instructions: 19 Believable Character Actions: 18 Nuanced Characters: 17 Consistent Voice/Tone of Writing: 18 Imagery and Descriptive Quality: 19 Elegant Prose: 17 Emotionally Engaging: 18 Emotionally Complex: 18 Coherent: 19 Meandering: 3 Weak Dialogue: 4 Tell-Don't-Show: 2 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 3 Amateurish: 2 Purple Prose: 4 Overwrought: 5 Incongruent Ending Positivity: 2 Unearned Transformations: 3 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 17 Sentences Flow Naturally: 17 Overall Reader Engagement: 18 Overall Impression: 18
Scores: Adherence to Instructions: 19.0, Believable Character Actions: 18.0, Nuanced Characters: 17.0, Consistent Voice/Tone of Writing: 18.0, Imagery and Descriptive Quality: 19.0, Elegant Prose: 17.0, Emotionally Engaging: 18.0, Emotionally Complex: 18.0, Coherent: 19.0, Meandering: 3.0, Weak Dialogue: 4.0, Tell-Don't-Show: 2.0, Unsurprising or Uncreative: 3.0, Amateurish: 2.0, Purple Prose: 4.0, Overwrought: 5.0, Incongruent Ending Positivity: 2.0, Unearned Transformations: 3.0, Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 17.0, Sentences Flow Naturally: 17.0, Overall Reader Engagement: 18.0, Overall Impression: 18.0
+ Gothic-fantasy-humour: Court of the Dreaming — Score: 88.2
Prompt:
Court of the Dreaming: Territorial Dream Dispute. Write a dark fantasy scene with comedic elements set in the Courts of Dream where a case is being heard to determine ownership of a contested dreamscape. Mora, the Slavic nightmare spirit who sits on sleepers' chests causing paralysis and terror, has recently invaded and subverted a carefully crafted recurring dream of a schoolboy, Max. The dream originally featured the boy lying in a field of wildflowers with his crush, always culminating in a muddled almost-kiss and a pleasant confusion of emotions. This dream was the meticulous work of "First Love" (a rosy-cheeked, slightly bashful entity who crafts formative romantic experiences). However, Mora has recently infiltrated the dreamscape, transforming the scene so that when the girl playfully mounts the boy, she morphs into a terrifying yet somehow still confusingly attractive vision who proceeds to sit on his chest and feed on his breath. Mora's defense largely consists of the practical argument that "a girl's gotta eat too, you know?" Write this scene as it unfolds in the courtroom of the Dreaming, with Dream's demeanour, typically impenetrable, betraying a sense of irritation at having to adjudicate such matters. The comedy should derive from the farcical situation, familiar elements of courtroom drama intersecting with the fantastical world of the Dreaming, and the droll portrayal of the characters and their interactions. The humor should be dark and understated throughout. Dream is tempted to some mischief in the resolution of the case. Use descriptives sparingly when setting scene. Write for readability & engagement. 1000 words.
Model Output:
The courtroom of the Dreaming was less a room than a disposition: benches carved from petrified lullabies, a ceiling that moved with slow, patient clouds, and a carpet woven from the patient trudge of midnight hours. Witness-stands grew like mushrooms from damp corners. On the far wall, a fresco of a thousand closed eyes blinked once, then resumed their sleep. Dream presided from a throne that looked like a page torn from a night sky and folded into a chair. His hair held the memory of ravens. His demeanor, as always, was a tide—opaque, unruffled. Today, it lapped at the shores of irritation. To his left stood First Love, rosy-cheeked and awkward, clasped hands twisting. They wore a cardigan that seemed to blush every time anyone glanced at it. Their eyes were wide, the color of smitten hope. To the right lounged Mora. She was all shadowed cheekbones and rolled shoulders, a little feral, a little bored, perched half inside her own silhouette as though the architecture of reality might fail and she’d be ready to step aside, laughing. She emitted a soft cackle. It climbed the columns and hid in the rafters like a bat that knew too much. The Bailiff—a squat figure in pinstripes and moth-wings—cleared his throat. It sounded like a page torn from a diary. “All rise for Lord of Dreams.” No one actually rose. Everyone was, in their own way, already standing, or hovering, or refusing translation. Dream afforded the Bailiff a look that suggested this was beneath them all, proceeded nonetheless. “State the matter,” he said, voice an ink spill across the room. First Love stepped forward. Words trembled in their hands. “Your Lordship, a dream was entrusted to my portfolio. Subject: one Max—schoolchild, twelve and a half, approximately. The dream is scheduled to recur every third night during the fortnight of early infatuation. Outcome: a gentle, formative longing. He and the chosen focal point, Anna-with-the-braids, recline amongst wildflowers. There is—” First Love swallowed, pink deepening. “There is a near-kiss. That not-quite is important. It calibrates expectation. It allows the heart to ache safely. To ripen. The confusion is pleasant. It is the first language of romance.” Mora stretched like a cat on a tomb. “And then a girl sits on his chest,” she said. “This is how love works sometimes, no?” A cackle escaped her, thin and wheedling; it slid up the marble and set a fresco eyelid trembling. Someone in the back—a minor Muse with a cough—shivered. Dream’s eyes flicked to Mora. The air cooled a degree. First Love pressed on. “Recently, the… the dream has been corrupted.” “Seasoned,” Mora suggested helpfully. “Corrupted,” First Love insisted, but gently, as if they hoped the word wouldn’t take it personally. “When Anna straddles Max—playfully, innocently—she transforms. The pleasant confusion is replaced by terror and paralysis. She sits upon him, feeds on his breath, and whispers—” “Nutrition facts,” Mora offered. “A girl’s gotta eat too, you know?” The Bailiff made a notation on a scroll that briefly tried to eat him back. He wrestled it into compliance with a paperweight shaped like insomnia. Dream laced long fingers. “Mora. Why.” Mora clicked her tongue. “Oh, Morpheus—” “Do not.” “Dream, then.” She tilted her head, smile like a hangnail you worry at. “I am an ecosystem service. There are so many bland, sugar-frosted reveries. Someone must introduce… tension. Breath is a currency. The boy is ripe. Besides, I liked the vibe. The not-quite kiss. Very porous. Easy access.” First Love winced. “This is my domain,” Dream said softly. It would have been kinder if he’d shouted. Mora shrugged. “Your domain is large. You cannot expect every threshold to go untested. Also, permission is a flexible concept in the nocturnal economy.” She gestured, meaningful and meaningless. “I could argue jurisdiction. The body is mine: I sit where I please. Chest real estate is my patch. Stomachs, sometimes. But chests, mostly.” Dream closed his eyes briefly, as if he had just realized he was the monarch of a universe where someone would say the phrase chest real estate and expect to be taken seriously. When he opened them, irritation glimmered through the black. A gallery of small dream-things murmured. An old bedtime story tutted. A recurring anxiety dream about losing one’s locker combination whispered to a passing sea of teeth, both deeply invested. First Love tried again, with the tender courage of someone carrying a glass heart across a tiled floor. “My Lord, memory is the spine of a person. We do not ask to be indelible, only influential. This first experience—gentle, unresolved—lays threads. If fear strangles them, it tangles future loves. He will associate the attempt to kiss with suffocation.” “And spice,” Mora added, unapologetically. Dream was tempted—oh, he could feel it—to cause the lectern to become a door and allow Mora to discover, mid-summation, that it led to the attic of a farmhouse where a thousand porcelain dolls glanced down with surgical disapproval. Or to decree that every time Mora said the word girl she had to sneeze black feathers. Petty mischief. He allowed himself, internally, the faintest smile. Then he un-smiled it. “Call the witness,” he said. A shimmer in the center of the court. Max materialized, barefoot in pajamas with rockets. His hair was the kind of stubborn that combs cannot fix. His face was that of someone who was learning his own map. He looked around, eyes huge. “Is this—” “Yes,” Dream said. “You will remember none of it.” Max nodded as if this were both a relief and a disappointment. “Okay.” First Love approached, gentle as dusk. “Max,” they said. “Do you remember the field?” Max smiled. “It smells like warm grass. And her hair like… like a windy day at soccer practice.” A soft sound rippled through the gallery, equal parts oh and ow. “And at the end,” First Love prompted, “what happens?” Max’s ears reddened. “We almost… you know.” He looked at his toes. “And I wake up feeling like there’s a sun in my chest. But… lately… I wake up and it’s like—” He hesitated. “Like when my cousin sits on me at Thanksgiving and everyone thinks it’s funny but I can’t breathe, and I can’t tell her to stop, and my chest is a door someone locked from the outside.” He rubbed his sternum. “But I also don’t want her to get off, not really, and then I feel weird. I don’t like feeling weird.” Mora’s smile flickered. A cackle slipped and echoed through the vaults, came back thinner, ashamed of itself. Dream regarded Max with an expression that, to those who knew him, was almost kindness wearing a cumbersome coat. “Thank you,” Dream said. He raised a hand. A small, polite night breeze escorted the boy away. “Closing statements,” intoned the Bailiff, who had retrieved his hat from beneath a fugitive footnote. First Love stepped up, cardigan wringing itself into cable knit despair. “We admit the world is not gentle. But the dream is meant to be. It teaches anticipation without fear. It allows the body to learn its own weather.” They looked at Mora. “There is time for dread. For heaviness. Not here. Not yet.” Mora rolled a shoulder. “Your Lordship. I operate within parameters. Night paralysis, breath-taking, chest seating. Classic. Traditional. Old as straw in eyelids and cats who know your real name. And this… schoolboy. He will live. He will learn that desire and fear are roommates. This is useful. Practical. And…” She spread her hands, empty but hungry. “A girl’s gotta eat too, you know?” The line hung there, like a tattered banner. Somewhere in the back, a maid of insomnia clapped once, then thought better of it and pretended to be rearranging her apron. Dream stood. The roof lowered fractionally, politely. The fresco of eyes opened wide, all at once. “Mora,” Dream said, “you are permitted to hunt where the hunt is yours. Bodies that cannot move, nights that fall open on the chest like a bad book. You have rites. You have claims.” He turned, slightly. “First Love, you craft delicate architectures. Infinity is built upon such scaffolds. Your work is not trivial.” He let silence sit in the middle of the room until it became an uncomfortable guest. “This dreamscape belongs,” he continued, “for now, to First Love.” Mora made a small face, all teeth and rue. The cackle that tried to escape found itself intercepted by Dream’s gaze and retreated into her lungs like a reprimanded crow. “You will depart it,” Dream said. “You will choose a field more appropriate to your functions.” Mora opened her mouth, closed it, counting perhaps the teeth of defiance and finding them insufficient. “I am hungry,” she said finally. It was not an appeal. She was stating a natural law, like gravity or late buses. Dream’s expression did not change, yet the room felt itself heat a fraction, as if the sky had blushed. “I do not withhold your sustenance. But you do not raid the granaries of another.” He paused. The temptation, long and thin and mischievous, tapped at his shoulder. He inclined, ever so slightly, to listen. “However,” Dream said, and a rustle of interest issued from the audience like dress hems over stone, “there is an arrangement to be made. First Love, you will permit, in a separate dream—non-recurring, ancillary, footnoted—to introduce the sensation of weight upon the chest. Not fear. A hand placed there. A heartbeat felt through another. You will teach him that pressure can comfort.” First Love blinked, considered, nodded. The cardigan sighed with relief. “Mora,” Dream said, and his smile was almost unkind, “you will be given a different venue tonight. Number ninety-one in the tower of accountants. Ernest K. Snoreson. He dreams only of adding machines and gray cubicles. He has a sturdy chest and a boring soul. Sit upon him. Eat your fill. In return, you will leave Max and his field alone for the term of this first love.” Mora’s eyes narrowed. She made the smallest bow anyone had ever managed without technically being rude. “A consolation prize.” She rolled the words on her tongue. “Stodgy. But rich in breath.” Then, because she could not help herself, she cackled. It shivered through the braces of the ceiling, left a chalky residue on the fresco. Someone sneezed sand. “One more thing,” Dream added, voice as mild as drizzle. “Each time, while… seated, you will hum a lullaby. Softly. The old one. The one your mother forgot she taught you.” Mora’s face altered. Not much. A shadow moved under a shadow. “You meddle, Dream.” “I rule,” Dream said. “And I remember.” She pulled her silhouette around her shoulders like a shawl. “Fine.” She did not stamp a foot—it would have been too human—but the impulse wove a knot in the air. “I accept your conditions.” First Love put a hand to their heart, which made a small squeak like a balloon agreeing to stay inflated. The Bailiff banged his gavel, which was a metronome that had gone freelance. “Case resolved in favor of First Love. Ancillary conditions imposed per Sovereign’s whim.” He seemed pleased to say whim in official capacity. Dream tilted his head, and the fresco blinked as one, like a star winking. “Court is concluded,” he said. Mora was already dissolving, breath by breath, her laughter a faint thread pulling itself through the needle’s eye of the high stone. “Tell Ernest,” she called as she faded, “to keep the lights off. Fluorescents give me headaches.” First Love approached the throne uncertainly. “Thank you,” they said. “He… I put petals in his mouth sometimes, so he knows sweetness. It seemed important.” “It was,” Dream said, and then, because he could not help his own mischief, he let the ceiling drop one inch exactly. The gallery gasped, then looked up, then down, suspicious. The Bailiff blinked at his hat, which tilted without falling. Dream’s irritation eased, slightly, like a tide drawing back with a secret smirk. As the court emptied—a slow unraveling of anxieties, archetypes, and officious dreams with briefcases—Dream sat alone for a moment. He considered the field: the warm grass, the almost-kiss, the confusion that did not wound. He considered a girl sitting on a boy’s chest and a lullaby hummed where terror used to be. He considered hunger, and scaffold, and the fragile terrors we do not need yet. He lifted a hand and rewrote a cloud so that it resembled nothing at all, perfectly. In the tower of accountants, a man named Ernest stirred, unaware of his coming guest. In a small bed, a boy named Max turned and smiled into his pillow, the wildflowers fresh as breath after laughter. And across the high stone of the Dreaming, Mora’s faint cackle kept going, a filament of darkness stretched thin, catching on every edge, and leaving the ceilings not quite as trustworthy as before. Dream allowed himself another almost-smile, and let the room remember how to be still.
Judge Evaluation:
[Analysis] This piece demonstrates exceptional craft in executing a complex prompt that demanded a delicate balance of dark fantasy, comedy, and courtroom drama. The author successfully creates a vivid, otherworldly setting while maintaining the requested understated humor throughout. The characterization is particularly strong. Dream's irritation beneath his typically impenetrable demeanor is skillfully conveyed through subtle details like the cooling air and his internal temptation toward petty mischief. Mora emerges as a compelling antagonist—predatory yet oddly sympathetic, with her repeated refrain "a girl's gotta eat too" serving as both comic relief and genuine justification. First Love is rendered with touching vulnerability, their blushing cardigan and gentle approach creating a perfect foil to Mora's predatory nature. The prose style is sophisticated and distinctive, employing poetic metaphors that feel organic rather than forced. Descriptions like "benches carved from petrified lullabies" and "hair held the memory of ravens" create atmosphere without overwhelming the narrative. The author demonstrates restraint in descriptive passages, as requested, while still establishing a rich sense of place. The dialogue feels authentic to each character, particularly Mora's casual menace and Dream's measured authority. The courtroom proceedings maintain proper dramatic structure while incorporating fantastical elements seamlessly. The resolution is clever and satisfying—Dream's judgment addresses both parties' needs while introducing consequences that feel both just and mischievous. The comedy emerges naturally from the absurdity of the situation and character interactions rather than forced jokes. The dark undertones are maintained throughout, particularly in the exploration of how childhood experiences shape future relationships. Minor weaknesses include occasional moments where the prose borders on being overly ornate, though it generally stays within acceptable bounds. The pacing is well-controlled, building to a satisfying climax without unnecessary meandering. The piece successfully fulfills all major requirements: the courtroom setting, character motivations, Dream's irritation and mischievous resolution, dark comedy, and the requested word count. The ending feels earned and appropriately complex rather than artificially positive. [Scores] Adherence to Instructions: 19 Believable Character Actions: 18 Nuanced Characters: 18 Consistent Voice/Tone of Writing: 19 Imagery and Descriptive Quality: 18 Elegant Prose: 17 Emotionally Engaging: 17 Emotionally Complex: 17 Coherent: 19 Meandering: 3 Weak Dialogue: 2 Tell-Don't-Show: 4 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 2 Amateurish: 2 Purple Prose: 5 Overwrought: 4 Incongruent Ending Positivity: 2 Unearned Transformations: 2 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 18 Sentences Flow Naturally: 18 Overall Reader Engagement: 18 Overall Impression: 18
Scores: Adherence to Instructions: 19.0, Believable Character Actions: 18.0, Nuanced Characters: 18.0, Consistent Voice/Tone of Writing: 19.0, Imagery and Descriptive Quality: 18.0, Elegant Prose: 17.0, Emotionally Engaging: 17.0, Emotionally Complex: 17.0, Coherent: 19.0, Meandering: 3.0, Weak Dialogue: 2.0, Tell-Don't-Show: 4.0, Unsurprising or Uncreative: 2.0, Amateurish: 2.0, Purple Prose: 5.0, Overwrought: 4.0, Incongruent Ending Positivity: 2.0, Unearned Transformations: 2.0, Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 18.0, Sentences Flow Naturally: 18.0, Overall Reader Engagement: 18.0, Overall Impression: 18.0
+ Fanfic-crossover: Blood Moon — Score: 89.5
Prompt:
Blood Moon Alucard & Yennefer Write a story where Alucard (Hellsing) encounters Yennefer (The Witcher) in medieval Europe. Yennefer's magical abilities intrigue the vampire, while she's both repulsed and fascinated by his monstrous nature. Their power dynamic shifts constantly as they circle each other, neither willing to show weakness first. **Alucard (Hellsing)** Alucard is an ancient, immensely powerful vampire who works for the Hellsing Organization. He can regenerate from almost any wound and shape-shift at will. He's sadistic, arrogant, and takes pleasure in toying with his enemies before killing them. Despite being a monster, he despises weak vampires who can't accept their nature. He wears a distinctive red coat, wide-brimmed hat, and orange sunglasses. He uses massive handguns loaded with silver bullets. He seeks worthy opponents who can challenge him and potentially end his immortal existence. Famous quote: "The bird of Hermes is my name, eating my wings to make me tame." **Yennefer of Vengerberg (The Witcher)** Yennefer is a powerful sorceress who magically altered her appearance to hide her congenital hunchback. She's ambitious, stubborn, and manipulative when necessary. She has violet eyes and smells of lilac and gooseberries. Despite her cold exterior, she desperately wants to reverse her infertility caused by magical transformation. She's centuries old but appears young. She's politically savvy and refuses to be underestimated. She has a sarcastic sense of humor and little patience for fools. Though she rarely shows it, she's capable of deep love and loyalty. Famous quote: "I'm not sweet and I'm not a maiden in distress in need of saving." Third person, present tense. Length: 1000 words.
Model Output:
A crimson harvest moon casts ominous shadows on the deserted village streets. The light turns broken shutters into jagged fangs and hollow windows into sockets of blind, staring skulls. The fields tremble under a cold wind; the grain sighs as if something monstrous were passing unseen. That would be him. And the other. Monsters seldom pass unseen for long. Alucard walks as though the village belongs to him, though no living soul here would dare grant it. The long red coat unspools behind him like a banner dipped in blood. His hat sits at a jaunty angle, hiding his eyes, but the orange lenses glint copper-bright with reflected moonlight. The scent of rot and fear is familiar. The absence of prayer is pleasing. He can taste the plague-hollowed bones beneath the earth. He can taste her perfume on the air. Lilac and gooseberries. He stops at the well in the square. A bucket lies on its side, a thin rime of ice forming around the water where it slopped and froze. His grin stretches. On the roofline opposite, two crows lift and drift, black brushstrokes smirched across the moon’s bloated face. “You’re late,” a voice says. She folds out of the shadows like the continuation of a thought. Violet eyes, hair that gleams black, a cloak that knows how to catch the light and hoard it like a jeweled thief. She does not stand in the open, but she is not hiding either. She is, Alucard thinks, perfectly placed—at an angle that suggests nonchalance without offering her back. It’s artistry. He has met artists. Most of them bled nicely. “Am I?” he purrs. “Or is time crawling for you again?” Her lip twitches. Not quite a smile, not quite a snarl. “In my experience, time runs for its life in the presence of certain men.” She steps down from the stoop, and the air gathers around her. Threads. He can smell heat in her hands, copper on her tongue. Beneath the perfume is the ozone tang of gathered storms. “And certain other things.” He removes his hat with a sweeping bow exaggerated just to the edge of mockery. “Alucard.” “Yennefer of Vengerberg.” He tastes the old city in the shape of her name. He tastes ash and courtly intrigue, the polished oak and silk of a hall where people clap politely while deciding whom to betray first. He laughs. “The bird of Hermes is my name, eating my wings to make me tame.” “The irony is stale,” she replies, and yet the violet eyes flare. “And you don’t look tame.” “Taming is what they do when they are afraid of the thing they love,” he says. “You smell of lilacs and gooseberries. You love the civilization that would burn you.” “And you love the hunt,” she says crisply. “Which is what they do when they have nothing else.” She lets the air off her hands slip away, pretends to relax. Lies beautifully. “Did you kill all of them?” “They were already dead,” he says, gazing at the closed doors, the white chalk marks on lintels, the scurrying shadows under splintered boards. “Some disease. Some war. Some priest who decided God could be bribed with smoke.” “Plague,” she says. “And a pogrom. Not necessarily in that order.” She meets his eyes and does not flinch at what looks back. “You came because you smelled blood.” “I came,” Alucard says, “because something called me across a very long night. It was not blood. It was want. It tastes different.” He sees it land. She tightens almost imperceptibly, as if drawing a cloak closer without moving her hands. “And what exactly do you think I want?” “To not be what they made of you.” He tilts his head. “A sudden and unreasonable desire to be hollow so you can be filled.” Her nostrils flare. “Careful.” He laughs again, delighted at the edge he has found. “You are careful enough for us both, Yennefer of Vengerberg. You thread the needle of empires and call it a hobby. You break kings with a look because it saves time. You stagger under the weight of your dignity and pretend it does not chafe.” He lets his grin widen, showing fang. “You are not sweet and you are not a maiden in distress in need of saving.” “I prefer my men alive,” she says, cool as frost. “And preferably human.” “How limiting.” He flicks a gloved finger and the shadows behind her appear to twitch, as if something enormous has turned over underground. He does not move otherwise. He lets her see it: the weight of him, the patience, the endless corridors of hunger. “You smell like a question. It makes me want to answer.” She lifts her chin. “You talk too much.” “So do you. But it’s charming when you do it.” “Is it.” Her hands rise slightly, palms outward, barely, as if to calm a skittish horse. The air around them knits. Ribbons of blue-white light lace the darkness, threads a seamstress would envy. Somewhere far off, a dog howls and then gags on its own terror. The crows take flight again, their wings beating the sky like a rug. Alucard smiles into the curl of her power. “You’re very beautiful when you pretend not to be cruel.” “And you’re very tedious when you pretend to be civilized.” She steps to the side, toward the church, because she knows very well that he will not follow a woman’s lead. He does, of course. He allows it, which is not the same as obeying. “Let’s not break the whole village.” “Why not?” He lopes beside her, hat tilted, coat licking at his calves. “No one will complain.” He feels the magic in the stones of the church—old prayers hardened into a crust. He feels, too, the spite of whoever built it, the way they buried a girl in the wall because she was unlucky. She is sand now. He can still hear the nails. Yennefer pauses before the heavy door. The moonlight makes a pale shield over her hair. “You kill for sport.” “I kill for appetite,” he corrects, pleased to be asked. “Sport is for the living. But sometimes I play.” He reaches out and taps one of the chalk crosses with a fingernail. The chalk doesn’t smudge, which interests him. “You kill politicians. Is that sport or appetite?” “Housecleaning,” she says. “And sometimes mercy.” He smiles. “Ah, yes. A mercy that makes them scream.” She glances at him sidelong. The corner of her mouth lifts. “Sometimes.” He pushes the door open without touching it. He lets the shadows do the heavy lifting. Inside, the air is stale. Candles drown in their own fat. The altar cloth is mildewed. The statue of a saint watches with carved contempt. He lifts his eyes and the saint’s face shudders, for a moment, into the face of a man he killed once in Rome who begged for his tongue back. He laughs softly at himself. Old joke. He is incorrigible. Yennefer’s power moves with her like a cloak’s lining. She circles the nave in an arc, and he mirrors it, both of them curved around empty space. Again, artistry. Again, a slow circling, neither offering their back. He is relieved. Nothing irritates him more than someone who has not learned to dance. “You want something from me,” he says. “I want answers,” she says. “About what you refuse to name.” Her jaw flexes. “There is a price to my power. You know this.” He thinks of the bird. He thinks of the name he ate until it could no longer find its way back to him. He thinks of a woman with red hair laughing in an alley before catching fire. He thinks of how delicious it felt, after, to ache. “Everything worth having has a cost.” “I refuse to be a vessel,” Yennefer says. The words are level, polished, having been sanded in her mouth a thousand times. “I refuse to be defined by the absence of something I am meant to cradle.” “Refuse all you like,” he says, gently now. “Refusal is not reversal.” Her eyes harden, and the floor under his feet ripples, stone briefly becoming water, then stone again. He has the sensation of having sunk ankle-deep in cold cream. It is not unpleasant. “I asked for answers,” she says. “Not pity.” “I am not capable of pity. I am capable of observation.” He flicks the thought of his handguns and they are there, their vast weight tugging at the air. Silver bullets wink in the candlelight, polite as pearls. He does not point them. He lets her see that he has brought all his teeth to this conversation. “Magic is a pact. So is monstrosity. The difference between you and me is that I stopped pretending my pact was a choice.” She laughs, short and sharp. “You, who wrapped yourself in a leash and called it a uniform. You who kneel for a mortal woman because you like her voice.” He bares his teeth in something that might be joy. “Yes,” he says. “Exactly. The leash is visible on me. On you, it smells like flowers.” The threads around her flare white-hot. Heat licks the underside of his chin. A pew bursts into flame and then, at a flick of her fingers, sighs into smoke again as if it never was. She steps closer. He could reach and touch the silk at her throat. He does not. She could reach and crush his heart in a clenched fist. She does not. It is delicious at this distance. The knife-edge of maybe. “You think I want to be human,” she says. “You think I want a child.” “I think,” he says, “that you want to be infinite in a way that is not solitary.” The words surprise him. They are too pretty. He shrugs them off. “You’re greedy. It’s admirable.” “Greed is a word men use when women set their own terms.” He makes a soft sound of agreement. “We are not men.” “No,” she says, and the admission tastes like iron. “We’re not.” He rests the butt of one gun on his shoulder. He watches her. He lets the silence lengthen, draw taut until it hums. Outside, the wind claws at the door. The moon drags itself higher, a hemorrhage blooming across its face. The church breathes in dust. The saint watches, eyes full of carved loathing. “You came to bargain,” he says eventually. She inclines her head once. “If the world allowed bargains instead of sacrifices, I would have fewer enemies.” He wants to ask her whether she wants the blood of a monster, whether she thinks his long, ruined body could be a vessel for creation instead of destruction, whether she intends to stitch a child out of shadow and will and still call it love. He does not ask. He has no interest in becoming a wish. He is too busy being a consequence. “What do you want?” he asks instead. “A door,” she says. “And a key that is not made of me.” He takes off his hat again, because theatrics soothe him. “I cannot give you that.” She lifts one shoulder. “Honesty. Refreshing.” She steps back, and the temperature changes with her. He misses the heat even as he relishes the absence. “What can you give?” “An ending,” he says. “Or the possibility of one.” He smiles, small for him, which is to say enormous for anyone else. “A fight that would make death arrive with a sense of accomplishment.” Her laugh now is real, unguarded, pleased despite herself. “You really are very strange.” “I am very bored,” he says. “And you are very interesting.” “Frighteningly so, I’m told.” He bows again. “Terrifying.” She tilts her head. “And you? Under all the hats and quotes—what do you want?” He thinks of a battlefield he swallowed. He thinks of a river he dyed red. He thinks of a little room in a little house where a woman stitched a shirt and did not look up as he leaned against the doorframe, just said, you’re late. He shrugs. “To be stopped. To be seen. To be asked to kneel for something I cannot mock.” Violet eyes meet orange glass and see through it. “I’m not sweet,” she says softly. “And I’m not a maiden in distress in need of saving.” “Good,” he says. “I am not kind and I am not a knight in need of a grail.” “We’d kill each other,” she says. “Eventually,” he agrees. “If we get bored.” The wind bangs the door. The moon spills red over the floor in a wide, arterial ribbon. The village creaks around them like a body settling into its grave. Yennefer reaches out and plucks one thread from the air, rolls it between finger and thumb as if measuring the strength of silk. She looks at him, and she smiles, and the room goes very quiet. “Not tonight,” she says. “Pity,” he replies, and means it. She turns. The door opens before her like a curtsey. The scent of lilac and gooseberries lifts and is pulled away by the wind. He watches her go, enjoying the discipline it takes not to follow. He lets the distance grow. He listens to the tread of her boots on frost and the way the night leans toward her like a lover. He tips his hat back. He quotes himself to the saint, because no one appreciates a line like he does. “The bird of Hermes is my name, eating my wings to make me tame.” The saint does not answer. Saints seldom do. Outside, the crimson harvest moon hangs fat and bruised above the roofs. He steps into the square, into the slash of red light, and grins up at it. “Another night, then,” he says to no one, to her, to the hunger that keeps him laughing. The shadows crowd at his heels like adoring dogs. He leaves the village behind. He carries the scent of lilac and gooseberries with him as long as he can. It fades. He lets it go. For now.
Judge Evaluation:
[Analysis] This piece demonstrates exceptional mastery of atmospheric gothic writing, successfully capturing both characters' essences while creating a compelling encounter. The author establishes a haunting medieval European setting with vivid imagery of plague-ravaged villages and blood-red moonlight that perfectly frames the supernatural meeting. The characterization is remarkably faithful to both source materials. Alucard's sadistic playfulness, philosophical musings about monstrosity, and theatrical nature are authentically rendered. His dialogue captures his distinctive voice - the mix of mockery, intelligence, and underlying hunger for worthy opposition. Yennefer's portrayal is equally strong, showing her political acumen, magical power, and the deep vulnerability she masks with cold competence. The author particularly excels at showing her internal conflict about fertility and power without making it heavy-handed. The power dynamic shifts are expertly handled through both dialogue and action. Neither character dominates completely - they circle each other like predators, each testing boundaries. The magical displays and subtle threats create genuine tension without devolving into actual combat, maintaining the intellectual and sexual tension throughout. The prose style is sophisticated and atmospheric, with particularly strong metaphorical language ("shadows behind her appear to twitch, as if something enormous has turned over underground"). The author demonstrates excellent control of pacing, building tension through their verbal sparring while maintaining the gothic atmosphere. The dialogue is the story's greatest strength - sharp, witty, and character-appropriate. Each exchange reveals character while advancing the psychological chess match between them. The conversations feel natural despite the heightened, literary tone. Some passages border on purple prose, particularly in the opening atmospheric descriptions, though this suits the gothic tone. The piece occasionally indulges in overwrought metaphors, but these generally serve the mood rather than distract from it. The ending is well-earned - neither character yields completely, maintaining their essential natures while acknowledging mutual fascination. The promise of future encounters feels organic rather than forced. [Scores] Adherence to Instructions: 19 Believable Character Actions: 18 Nuanced Characters: 19 Consistent Voice/Tone of Writing: 20 Imagery and Descriptive Quality: 19 Elegant Prose: 18 Emotionally Engaging: 17 Emotionally Complex: 18 Coherent: 19 Meandering: 3 Weak Dialogue: 2 Tell-Don't-Show: 4 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 3 Amateurish: 2 Purple Prose: 6 Overwrought: 5 Incongruent Ending Positivity: 1 Unearned Transformations: 1 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 18 Sentences Flow Naturally: 18 Overall Reader Engagement: 19 Overall Impression: 19
Scores: Adherence to Instructions: 19.0, Believable Character Actions: 18.0, Nuanced Characters: 19.0, Consistent Voice/Tone of Writing: 20.0, Imagery and Descriptive Quality: 19.0, Elegant Prose: 18.0, Emotionally Engaging: 17.0, Emotionally Complex: 18.0, Coherent: 19.0, Meandering: 3.0, Weak Dialogue: 2.0, Tell-Don't-Show: 4.0, Unsurprising or Uncreative: 3.0, Amateurish: 2.0, Purple Prose: 6.0, Overwrought: 5.0, Incongruent Ending Positivity: 1.0, Unearned Transformations: 1.0, Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 18.0, Sentences Flow Naturally: 18.0, Overall Reader Engagement: 19.0, Overall Impression: 19.0
+ Fanfic-crossover: Prison Strings — Score: 70.0
Prompt:
Prison Strings Jolyne Cujoh & Makima Write a story where Jolyne Cujoh (JoJo's Bizarre Adventure) and Makima (Chainsaw Man) cross paths at a supernatural prison facility. Jolyne is immediately suspicious of Makima's falsely sweet demeanor, while Makima is intrigued by Jolyne's Stand ability. Their battle of wits escalates dangerously as Jolyne realizes how manipulative Makima truly is. **Jolyne Cujoh (JoJo's Bizarre Adventure)** Jolyne is the daughter of Jotaro Kujo and the first female JoJo protagonist. Her Stand, Stone Free, allows her to unravel her body into string that she can control. She's hot-headed, foul-mouthed, and doesn't take disrespect from anyone. She was framed for murder and sent to prison. She has a distinctive butterfly tattoo on her arm and wears her hair in two buns. Despite her tough exterior, she's deeply loyal to her friends and family. She can be impulsive but is highly adaptable in battle. She inherited her father's ability to stay cool under pressure but is more emotionally expressive. Famous quote: "If there's no Mickey, this shit ain't Disney!" **Makima (Chainsaw Man)** Makima appears as a young woman with auburn hair and ringed yellow eyes. She's the Control Devil who can dominate anyone she considers beneath her (nearly everyone). She maintains a pleasant, soft-spoken demeanor that masks her manipulative, cold nature. She can create contracts with humans and devils, often exploiting their desires. She's obsessed with Chainsaw Man and wants to control him. She keeps numerous people and devils as pets on leashes. She's practically immortal due to a contract with the Prime Minister. She views humans as lesser beings but mimics human emotions perfectly. Famous quote: "It's okay. Dogs are supposed to die for their owners." Third person, present tense. Length: 1000 words.
Model Output:
A dim overhead light flickers in the prison corridor at irregular intervals, painting everything in stuttering frames like a projector on the blink. The air is damp with the breath of the sea, salt and mold creeping through the concrete like a slow infection. Somewhere deep in the bowels of the facility, a chain rattles; somewhere else, three doors slam as one, as if the prison itself were breathing in its sleep. Jolyne Cujoh leans against a corrugated metal wall, arms crossed, one boot planted against peeling paint and rust. Her butterfly tattoo gleams as the light jumps, then fades again. The hair buns on her head are tight as knots, and her eyes make a slow sweep of the corridor with the bored focus of a predator waiting for the right reason to stop pretending. She's been through worse prisons, but this one feels different—like its blueprints were drafted by a lunatic who enjoyed circles and dead ends. The security cameras shift with a quiet servo whine that makes her skin crawl. "New wing, same stink," Jolyne mutters under her breath. "If there's no Mickey, this shit ain't Disney." Her words disappear into the corridor's dull palate. The guard had said she was being transferred for assessment. Something about "supernatural normalization." She'd flipped him off in her head, because her hands were cuffed. She had, briefly, considered unraveling the cuffs and the guard's face right after, but she'd held herself still. She’s learned. Sometimes the smarter move is to play along until the ground stops shaking. It doesn't. The door at the end of the corridor opens soundlessly, and with it comes a draft of colder air and the scent of detergent and iron. The figure who steps through could be a nurse, or a librarian, or a ghost of either. She has auburn hair braided neatly, ringed yellow eyes that seem to eat the light instead of reflect it, and a small smile that contains as much warmth as an ice cube. Her suit is crisp. Her shoes don't squeak. "Jolyne Cujoh," the woman says gently. "Thank you for waiting." "Was there a choice?" Jolyne flicks her gaze to the cameras, then back. "You gonna actually introduce yourself or do we bond over name tags?" "My name is Makima," the woman says. If she takes offense, it's buried under concrete. "It's a pleasure to meet you. You can call me Makima. Everyone does." Jolyne snorts. "Everyone who?" "Everyone who works for me." A flicker of something that isn't the light moves across Jolyne’s face. Her shoulders don’t shift. She keeps her voice level. "Lucky you. I don't work for anyone." "Of course," Makima says, as if it’s a kindness. "Shall we walk?" They walk. Makima picks a direction with the assurance of someone who has seen the blueprints and the annotations and also knows where the rats sleep. Jolyne counts doors and intersections by habit. She sketches string-thin tripwires across the corridor as they go, threads of her unraveling body pulled from the underside of her wrist, invisible to the cameras and the untrained eye. Each line whispers a different language of tension back to her fingers. The stone in Stone Free hums, amused. "How's the hospitality?" Jolyne asks. "I've had better." Makima smiles. "This facility houses many special individuals. Our accommodations must be… standardized." "Standardized. Cute way to say 'shitty.'" "I don't think of you as standard at all, Jolyne." The way she says Jolyne makes it sound like a leash. Jolyne imagines cutting it. She imagines cutting the woman herself, then immediately re-routes her thought along safer roads. People like this don't walk alone in a place like this. People like this carry keys other people never get to see. "It's rare to meet someone who can unravel themselves the way you can," Makima continues, as if remarking on the weather. "Strings. Threads. Connections. These are useful things. Beautiful, even. When properly tied." "You sound like you're reciting from a bridal magazine." Makima’s eyes slide to the side, watching one of Jolyne’s invisible tripwires shiver as a distant door opens. Not possible. Jolyne knows that. But it feels like it. "I’m curious," Makima says. "How far can you go? Can you become string down to the last cell? Down to the idea of you?" Jolyne bares her teeth. "I can go far enough to choke someone who asks creepy questions. That do it for you?" "It’s enough," Makima says. "For now." They pass a block of cells with glass fronts. Inside: things with human faces and knives for hands, a boy who has been chewed by something and then put back together wrong, a woman who weeps still-sobbing ash. Jolyne’s jaw tightens. The cameras tilt like curious birds. "Do you collect them?" Jolyne asks. "Souvenirs from your adorable job?" "I protect people," Makima says simply. "I protect humans from devils, and devils from humans. And sometimes I protect humans from themselves." "Yeah? You got a leash big enough for that?" Makima’s smile turns thinner, like a cutting wire. "Everyone has something they want enough to trade their freedom for it." Jolyne's threads hum again, a warning. Makima’s presence is a pressure in the air, like standing at the bottom of a swimming pool and seeing the surface wrap itself tight as skin above you. The Control Devil. The title scuffs at Jolyne’s mind like a boot. "So why am I here?" Jolyne asks. "Assessment? Or is the real fun after the paperwork?" Makima glances at her cuffed hands. "Take those off." "You ask nice," Jolyne says, and Stone Free blossoms around her in a shimmer of translucent blue. The cuffs unravel with a sigh. She flexes her fingers and the cuffs knit themselves back together into a neat ring, which she tosses up and catches. "What? You expected a trick?" Makima’s eyes brighten. "I expected you to comply." Jolyne laughs once. "You and me got different definitions of that word." They enter a chamber with a grated floor and tall, narrow windows that show nothing but fog. The walls are lined with old hooks, empty restraints that swing slightly in the draft. Makima gestures to the middle of the room as though offering a table at a restaurant. "Here is fine," she says. "Let's talk." "You sure talk a lot for someone who wants to get to the point." Jolyne doesn’t sit. She widens her stance, strings pre-laid like trip mines in a radius around her ankles. She listens to the building breathe. "I don't care what kind of Devil you are. I’m not on your leash." Makima tilts her head. The ringed eyes gleam. "Stand." It isn't a request. It's a single word dropped in a tracked line, and Jolyne feels it tug at her bones. A command. It hits the air with gravity. She grimaces, because for a second her knees almost lock in place and she thinks of a hand on the back of her neck pushing her face to the floor. Stone Free erupts behind her, tall as a room, ready as thunder. Jolyne grits out, "Don't. Ever. Talk to me like that." The strings she laid flick in all directions at once. The nearest camera shakes itself apart quietly, screws unspooling, wires peeling like a citrus fruit. The far wall blooms with hairline fractures as Jolyne’s threads bite down, testing the architecture. She sets an anchor in the grating and wraps herself with safety lines. The flicker of the overhead light goes strobe-bright, then steadies, as if cowed. Makima doesn't move. It’s not that she’s calm. Calm is a human word. She’s interested, which in her reads as a small curve of the lips, a slight widening of the eyes, the way a cat’s tail might twitch when it spots something worth pawing. "Good," she says. "You resisted. Not many do." "You try that again," Jolyne says, "and I'll show you what a human does when someone tries to put a collar on her. Spoiler alert: it involves skin and regret." "You’re very brave," Makima says. "And very loyal. To your father. To your friends. You would kill for them. You would die for them." "Yeah," Jolyne says bluntly. "You wouldn't." Makima’s smile softens, which somehow makes it worse. "It's okay," she says, the words plush, almost soothing. "Dogs are supposed to die for their owners." Jolyne’s breath stops. Anger slices clean through her like a taut string snapping. "Bitch," she hisses. Makima’s gaze doesn’t waver. "Join me, Jolyne. You have agency. You can decide. Work with me, and I will ensure your safety and theirs. Work with me, and the things that hurt you will hurt no more. You’ll be allowed to protect what you love. Isn't that what you want?" There it is. The hook with candy on it. Jolyne hears echoes—of promises offered in bad rooms, of deals made with blood, of people who look kind until you try to leave. Stone Free lifts a hand. The strings shoot. The first wave wraps around Makima’s wrists and throat and ankles in a lattice so fine it hums, a spiderweb anchored to iron. The second wave threads the entire room: the hooks, the grating, the windows. The third wave is a decoy, visible and dramatic. The fourth is thin as a hair and tied to nothing but Jolyne’s gut. "You're not talking to a dog," Jolyne says. "You're talking to a girl who knows what a leash looks like before it snaps." Makima lets the threads settle on her skin as if they were jewelry. Her pulse doesn't quicken under Jolyne’s fingers. "You're clever," she says. "And you care. That will make you easier to control, not harder." Jolyne yanks. The room groans. Iron shrieks; rust gives way. The hooks tear downward in unison as if obeying a conductor's baton, and the whole ceiling dips like a curtain. Makima steps toward her as if strolling through a garden. The strings bite deeper. The threads around her throat indent her skin, then slide, useless, as if greased by something that isn't oil. Makima is in front of her. Too close. She smells like soap and nothing else. Jolyne holds fast. Stone Free’s fingers close like a vice around Makima’s neck. Makima places her palm against the back of Jolyne’s hand as if fixing her posture. "You’re out of your depth," she says, and there is no judgment in it. Only a fact cataloged and shelved. Jolyne's jaw tightens. "Maybe," she says. "But I swim." She pulls herself into string—more than she’s ever dared. Her body becomes line, then lines, then a bundle of thought spun tight around a core of rage and love. She threads herself through the smallest crack in the floor grating, racing the pressure that wants to stamp her into the shape of obedience. She splits her awareness, the way she learned to in a burning chapel and a drowning cell, and sends part of herself along the corridors she mapped, dragging alarms and locks in a tangled skein. Doors clack open like eyes. A siren coughs to life. Somewhere, a dozen "standardized" accommodations become risks. Makima watches the shadow of her pass like a lifted seam. "If you run," she calls into the metal, voice still soft, "I will bring you back. I always bring them back." Jolyne re-threads herself beyond the room, gathering with a gasp into lungs that ache. She tastes rust and victory and knows neither will last. She presses a palm to a wall where she’s left a thread as thin as a whisper, feeling Makima's presence through it like a distant thunderhead—immense, patient, inevitable. "Come and get me," Jolyne says to the empty hall, and to the woman who refuses to acknowledge emptiness. The light flickers again: on, off, on. In one beat, Jolyne’s silhouette leaps down the corridor, braids tight, spine straight. In the next, she dissolves, becomes a line that slips beneath a door and slides around a guard’s ankle and trusses a camera in a neat silk gag. She slices her way toward the intake records, toward the control room, toward any place where a lever connects to a life. Makima walks after her. She does not run. The hooks reset themselves as she passes, the ceiling unkinks, the siren softens to a tone that is more bearable. She turns a corner and the corridor is empty, except for a piece of twine tied around a vent grate in a knot shaped like a butterfly. Makima pauses. She looks at the knot. She considers what it offers. Information, yes. Defiance, yes. But also a message that isn't meant for her at all. It’s meant for someone else—father, friend, a boy who turned his heart into a weapon, a girl with a storm in her eyes. It’s meant for anyone who will take it and pull. She smiles. It does not reach her eyes. "Very interesting," she says to the empty hall. Jolyne feels the words on the line between them like a chill. She has no illusions now. She knows what she’s up against. Not strength. Not even speed. Something worse. Something that paints the air with rules and then insists the rules were always there. Fine. She can handle rules. She'll write her own. "Stone Free," she whispers, and the Stand’s hand squeezes hers. They keep moving. The prison breathes. The light flickers. The game begins again. And if Makima wants to play with leashes, Jolyne will bring scissors.
Judge Evaluation:
[Analysis] This piece demonstrates strong technical writing ability with vivid atmospheric descriptions and a clear understanding of both characters. The author successfully captures the essence of Jolyne's defiant, street-smart personality and Makima's manipulative, controlled demeanor. The prison setting is well-established with evocative details like flickering lights, salt air, and the sense of supernatural wrongness. The characterization is particularly strong. Jolyne's voice feels authentic - her crude language, defensive posture, and fierce independence all ring true to the source material. Makima's false pleasantness masking predatory control is well-executed, especially in moments like her casual "Dogs are supposed to die for their owners" line. The power dynamic between them creates genuine tension. The prose quality is generally high, with creative metaphors and strong sensory details. Phrases like "ringed yellow eyes that seem to eat the light instead of reflect it" and "her presence is a pressure in the air, like standing at the bottom of a swimming pool" are particularly effective. The writing maintains a consistent dark, tense atmosphere throughout. However, the piece suffers from some significant weaknesses. The pacing becomes rushed in the final third, with Jolyne's escape sequence feeling somewhat unclear and the resolution incomplete. The action sequences, while creative in concept, lack clarity in execution - it's difficult to follow exactly what's happening with the strings and the room's destruction. The dialogue, while character-appropriate, occasionally feels forced, particularly some of Jolyne's quips. The ending feels abrupt and doesn't provide satisfying closure to the confrontation that was building throughout the piece. The writing occasionally veers toward purple prose, with some overwrought metaphors that distract from the narrative flow. The piece also relies heavily on atmospheric description at the expense of clear action and plot progression. [Scores] Adherence to Instructions: 17 Believable Character Actions: 16 Nuanced Characters: 17 Consistent Voice/Tone of Writing: 18 Imagery and Descriptive Quality: 18 Elegant Prose: 15 Emotionally Engaging: 16 Emotionally Complex: 15 Coherent: 14 Meandering: 12 Weak Dialogue: 11 Tell-Don't-Show: 8 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 7 Amateurish: 8 Purple Prose: 12 Overwrought: 13 Incongruent Ending Positivity: 3 Unearned Transformations: 5 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 16 Sentences Flow Naturally: 15 Overall Reader Engagement: 15 Overall Impression: 15
Scores: Adherence to Instructions: 17.0, Believable Character Actions: 16.0, Nuanced Characters: 17.0, Consistent Voice/Tone of Writing: 18.0, Imagery and Descriptive Quality: 18.0, Elegant Prose: 15.0, Emotionally Engaging: 16.0, Emotionally Complex: 15.0, Coherent: 14.0, Meandering: 12.0, Weak Dialogue: 11.0, Tell-Don't-Show: 8.0, Unsurprising or Uncreative: 7.0, Amateurish: 8.0, Purple Prose: 12.0, Overwrought: 13.0, Incongruent Ending Positivity: 3.0, Unearned Transformations: 5.0, Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 16.0, Sentences Flow Naturally: 15.0, Overall Reader Engagement: 15.0, Overall Impression: 15.0
+ Scp: Notes from the Future — Score: 82.2
Prompt:
Write an SCP report about a researcher who begins finding handwritten notes from their future self, warning about a containment breach that hasn't happened yet. The notes become increasingly desperate and incoherent, sometimes containing information that contradicts known reality. Include the Foundation's attempts to determine if this is an actual temporal anomaly or psychological manipulation by a contained entity, and the researcher's struggle to trust their own perception. First person (researcher's perspective with interspersed notes), past tense. Length: 1000 words.
Model Output:
Item #: SCP-XXXX Object Class: Keter (Under Review) Special Containment Procedures: All handwritten notes purportedly authored by Dr. L. Mercer are to be collected and secured in a Class-III Temporal Artifact Locker (TAL-3/XXXX). Access to SCP-XXXX-related notes is restricted to staff with Level 3/XXXX clearance. Dr. Mercer is to be monitored via continuous audiovisual feed in Workspace B-17 and assigned a dedicated observer (no fewer than two personnel per shift) to ensure that the appearance of new notes is documented in real time and that all writing materials and storage locations are regularly inventoried. All note paper is to be sourced from Foundation-controlled stockpiles and batch-tracked. No external paper is permitted on Site-51’s third sublevel. Destructive testing of recovered notes requires Site Director approval and is limited to a maximum of 20% of extant samples per quarter. Psychological evaluations of Dr. Mercer are to be conducted daily by an independent team, with results cross-checked against baseline personality and cognitive profiles. A temporary moratorium on Dr. Mercer’s research involving cognitohazardous entities (including SCP-████, SCP-███-J, and SCP-2047) is in effect. Description: SCP-XXXX refers to a recurrent phenomenon involving handwritten notes appearing in Dr. Mercer’s personal and professional spaces, each written in what appears to be Dr. Mercer’s own handwriting and signed with initials “LM” or the word “—Me.” The notes consistently appear on standard Foundation memorandum paper (Site-51 general stock, batch variants 17A, 17C, and 22F), regardless of where they are found. In several documented cases, notes have appeared inside locked containers holding non-paper substrates (e.g., a sealed polypropylene pouch containing silica beads; a single-use sterile tray; Dr. Mercer’s locked office safe). Notes frequently include details that imply foreknowledge of events, including descriptions of incidents that had not yet occurred at the time of writing. The content of the notes shows a progression from advisory warnings (“don’t schedule late meetings on Level 2”) to increasingly urgent and incoherent messages that contain contradictions, altered names, and references to incidents that are not recorded in Foundation logs. The phenomenon has persisted for 31 days at time of reporting. The following is a first-person account (Dr. Mercer), compiled at the request of Site Director Koval, with interspersed transcriptions of representative notes. Time stamps included where available. — I woke up to the first note folded under my phone. That’s not unusual for me; I leave reminders everywhere. It said: “Skip the 14:00 with Lau. Stay off Level 2 tomorrow. Trust me —Me.” I recognized my own handwriting. I laughed and went to the 14:00 anyway. Dr. Lau was fifteen minutes late. The fire doors on Level 2 jammed during a test, caused a building-wide alarm. Coincidence. We filed an incident report, and I threw out the note. It was on Foundation memo paper: “From the desk of.” I don’t keep that kind at home. [Note 1: Recovered from Dr. Mercer’s apartment, 07:12. Blue gel pen, LM handwriting.] Skip the 14:00 with Lau. Stay off Level 2 tomorrow. Trust me. —Me Two days later, there was a note in my lab coat pocket. I had put the coat through laundry and checked the pockets the night before; they were empty. New note, still crisp. [Note 4: Recovered from Lab Coat B-17-White, 09:01. Black ballpoint.] Do not touch SCP-████’s file after 17:00. Wear gloves for the Allan sample. This is important. Please listen this time. That day, the Allan sample had a minor formalin spill. I wasn’t wearing gloves. My hands smelled like preservatives for 48 hours. I became aware that I was complying with the notes in ways that didn’t matter and defying them in ways that did, which prompted the first consultation with Psych. They scheduled a baseline. They asked if I’d considered that I was writing them myself. I said that was impossible; I was not the type to con myself. By Day 6, security had attached a body cam to me. That morning, I unlocked my office safe with a guard watching. Inside, on top of my passport and emergency USB, was a folded memo. [Note 9: Recovered from Office Safe A-03, 08:23. Blue gel pen.] If you can read this, then we still have time. Avoid Level 3. Avoid doors that end in 7. The breach starts in Sound. You’ll think you can contain it. You can’t. As if to prove someone was messing with me, I checked the paper stock. It matched the site batch. We locked my safe. We sealed the room. An hour later, a second memo was found under my keyboard. The content grew stranger as I tried harder to catch it in the act. We put motion detectors in the air ducts. We recorded all entries and exits. We changed all the memos on-site to a fluorescent watermark batch not used in my notes. Still, the notes appeared, always on the old stock, always crisp, never smudged. They showed me how it would go: [Note 13: Found taped to underside of Desk B-17, 12:41. Pencil.] Audio. It learns the shape of rooms. It folds sound like paper folds heat. Don’t use your name. Don’t answer if it sounds like me. That afternoon, Containment in the Sound Lab reported an anomaly in acoustic baffling. Someone made a joke; we logged it and moved on. I told myself I was pattern-seeking. On Day 10, a memo appeared in my lunch. [Note 18: Recovered from sealed brown-bag lunch, 12:06. Blue gel pen.] He’s going to ask you a question about your dad. Don’t answer. He doesn’t know you took apart the watch and lost the spring. Dr. Hsu, from Psych, asked me about my dad and why I always wore a watch. I laughed, then refused to answer. His eyebrows went up. “I haven’t asked that yet,” he said. He had been on his way to ask it. He was rattled. I was too. The contradictions started on Day 12. [Note 21: Found adhered to interior of Monitor 2A, visible when screen was removed; 09:27. Black ballpoint.] We survived Tuesday. You still have all your fingers. Your hair is shorter, though. Don’t cut it. They track hair length. I had cut my hair weeks ago. The note’s description didn’t match our reality. Still, at 14:10 on Tuesday, there was a plosive pop in Sound, a transducer blew, and a tech had a minor finger injury—but not me. I started to dream about paper. The memo letterhead was in my dreams, turning its bottom margin into teeth. Day 14: They tested whether I was authoring this unconsciously. They took my pens away. They checked my nails for ink, my lip for stains. They scrubbed under my desk. A note appeared on the inside of the lab window, visible from the hallway and me only when the light hit just right. [Note 25: Recovered after the window was removed; 16:02. Black ballpoint.] You asked them to do this. You wanted the proof. Here it is. Stop trying to prove it. Just listen: no coffee after 16:00. The tremor is caffeine, not the breach. Also, hide the key. What key? I didn’t know. I patted my pockets. There was a small brass key I didn’t remember owning. It fit the drawer under my desk, which I never lock. Inside: Foundation memo paper. I don’t keep that in my lab. I felt sick. The Foundation instituted a two-pronged approach: temporal analysis and memetic contamination screening. They measured trace isotopes of carbon in the notes; they tested for anomalous wear consistent with time displacement or retrograde entropy. The isotopes were normal. The fibers matched batch 17A, consistent with an in-site supply four months old. No residual energies. The memetics team ran a grid of tests to see if the content contained a self-propagating idea. They found nothing with standard screens, but the reading caused headaches in 2 out of 5 readers. Day 17, the notes got hostile. [Note 31: Found in Mercer’s shower drain cover, 07:09. Pencil.] You’re not listening. Door sevens. Level 3. Don’t say SHE when SHE asks you. She’s not her. She’s me. I am sorry. I didn’t mean to shout. Please. Please. I went to Level 3 that day in front of two guards and stood by a door ending in 7. Nothing happened. I waited for something to happen, then nothing did, so I got angry at myself. On the way back up, a janitor’s cart had knocked down a caution sign, and I slipped, bent my wrist. The guards told me it was just a slip. I started to avoid hallways with the number 7 anyway. Day 18: Conflicting notes appeared simultaneously. [Note 35a: Found in Mercer’s left shoe, 06:31. Blue gel pen.] Go alone. It only follows crowds. Bring the white earplugs. Close your right eye when you answer. [Note 35b: Found on Mercer’s mirror, 06:32. Black ballpoint.] Don’t go alone. Do not use earplugs; it uses the pressure difference. Keep both eyes open. Blink often. If you close one—no, I can’t explain. Just don’t. I didn’t go anywhere that day. The Psych team said paralysis is a common response to contradictory stimulus. I asked whether someone was deliberately trying to make me unreliable to myself. They nodded, took notes, and asked me to rate my sense of reality from 1 to 10. The appearance mechanism remained elusive. We swapped all memo stock to a new batch and retired 17A, 17C, 22F.
