A week later, a note appeared on the old letterhead again, this time inside a sealed acrylic block—part of an unrelated experiment. My observer, Hale, vomited when we cracked it and found the note completely dry. [Note 42: Recovered from sealed acrylic block, 11:18. Blue gel pen.] The breach is in the rehearsals, not the show. Stop testing the paper. You know it’s ours. The paper is the anchor. I don’t know why. Please, if you have to burn something, burn the blank pads. Don’t burn the notes. If you burn the notes, it learns. Temporal said it could be a local self-consistent loop with an anchor to mundane materials. Memetics said it could be suggestion exploiting my job’s pattern recognition. We argued. The Site Director split us into teams: one treated it as time travel; the other as deception by an entity using my memory as a staging ground. The time travel team set a bait: a specific phrase that only a future me would know, to be written on a note placed in a controlled container. A day later, the phrase appeared on a note under my keyboard, but in the wrong ink. The memetics team said that proved nothing; an internal entity could read our minds. The temporal team said the ink batch difference indicated multiple strands. I said I hadn’t written anything. It didn’t help. By Day 23, I wasn’t sleeping. My observer said I was talking to the memos. The notes started to use my own turns of phrase against me. [Note 57: Found beneath the Velcro of Mercer’s ID badge, 08:51. Pencil.] Oh look, a data point. If I just stack enough, it will make a ladder out. That’s what you say. Stop stacking. Start trusting. Don’t go into Sound at 13:00. Don’t answer if Hsu calls you by your first name. At 12:58, Dr. Hsu called me “Lena” from the doorway. He never calls me that. I stared at him. He blinked and said, “Sorry. Habit.” He asked me to sit. I told him to use my last name. He did. We didn’t go to Sound. The next day, Sound reported a failure at 13:02. No injuries. The memo refused to be predictive in any way I could admit. It was always adjacent, as if it was aimless in time, bumping back and forth. Day 26: I opened my personal notebook and found the Foundation memo paper glued across a page, the edges so clean the adhesive must have been applied under pressure. I peeled it back and read this: [Note 64: Found in personal notebook, 05:44. Blue gel pen.] We lost Hsu here. Not again. Not like this. He is not the breach, but the breach speaks with him. If he says “for the record,” walk away. You can save him later. You cannot save him now. I know that makes you hate me. I’m still you. I’m still sorry. At 09:20, in a scheduled check-in, Dr. Hsu said, “For the record.” I stood up and walked out. He called after me. Later, he emailed that he was taking a personal day. He never does that. His office light was off. I stared at the dark glass for a long time. The following morning, there were five notes. One taped to my ceiling, one in my plant soil, one inside a sealed specimen bag, one under my chair wheel, and one already crumpled in my wastebasket. [Note 69: Ceiling. Pencil.] It hears through the paper. Whiteness is its skin. The watermark is its eye. You can blind it with coffee, but that ruins the paper. Don’t. Keep it pristine. It’s listening. [Note 70: Plant soil. Black ballpoint.] The breach hasn’t happened yet. That’s why we can fix it. It’s a rehearsal. It’s practicing with us until it gets the lines right. [Note 71: Specimen bag. Blue gel pen.] If we don’t fix it, it learns our names. It starts with mine. Don’t let it have me. [Note 72: Chair wheel. Pencil.] Breathe. In for four, hold for four, out for four. You write this when you forget. [Note 73: Wastebasket. Black ballpoint.] I am not you. Don’t listen to me. We halted all non-essential operations in Sound. We dusted every note for prints; they were clean. We traced the paper batch. The time travel team insisted we were looking at a non-linear causality loop anchored to something so mundane we wouldn’t think to protect it: the memo paper. The memetics team proposed a hypothesis: an entity inhabiting the conceptual space of official communication, using the Foundation’s own internal trust mechanisms—the memo, the letterhead—as vehicles. If so, it would be the perfect Trojan horse. Day 29: I burned a blank pad. The fire alarms triggered. The entire floor evacuated. After, a note appeared on my wrist, slipped under my watchband while I was in the muster area. [Note 82: Found under watchband, 14:21. Blue gel pen.] You hurt it. It will hurt you back. Don’t be obvious. It covets the official. It resents the private. Keep personal paper. Hide it. Don’t tell them. I’m sorry. I know that’s a betrayal. But we can’t fight it if it knows we’re fighting. Please. Just for now. I showed the note to Director Koval anyway. I am not a good liar. The Director didn’t chastise me. She put a hand on my shoulder and said, “We’ll find the pattern.” I wanted that to be true. I wanted to be more than a conduit for a scared voice wearing my handwriting. Day 31: The notes are less coherent. Some mention a breach at 02:17 that didn’t occur. Others mention Dr. Lau with a broken arm—she is fine. They contradict each other. And then there was one that I am still holding in my pocket, folded and refolded until the creases are soft. [Note 96: Found in pocket of freshly laundered lab coat, 07:03. Black ballpoint.] This is the closest we’ve been. It’s in the letters. It’s in the negative space inside the A’s and O’s. When you read this, don’t say it out loud. Don’t count the letters. Don’t mark anything. If you can stop me from writing the next one, you stop it from rehearsing. Please put down the pen. Please don’t be me. I didn’t pick up a pen that day. I didn’t write my reports. I dictated and let someone else type. I didn’t trust my mouth, either. I don’t know if this is me, time-sliced and flung back, or an entity that has measured me well enough to wear me. I know that the notes are always on our paper, and that feels like the most perfect detail: an anomaly small enough to slip between policy and habit, to live where we are bureaucratic and confident. I know the teams will keep testing ink and fibers. I know that when Hsu says “for the record,” I still walk away. He’s back at work now, tired but alive. He asked if I wanted to talk off the record. I said yes, and then I didn’t say anything. The breach hasn’t happened yet, the notes keep promising, as if promise and warning were the same shape. The Foundation wants to know which hypothesis is true. So do I. But when I sit at my desk and stare at the blank memo paper, I feel watched. I feel like speech is a net and the letters are holes, and whatever we’re trying to catch is slipping through. Addendum XXXX-A: Partial Analysis Summary Temporal Mechanics Division: No evidence of temporal displacement at the material level. Handwriting analysis indicates high congruence with Dr. Mercer’s baseline, with microvariances consistent with stress and different writing implements. Hypothesis: informational leakage across proximal timelines or self-originating causal loop anchored via institutional artifacts. Memetics and Infohazards: No canonical vector identified. Secondary hypothesis: parasitic noeme exploiting institutional trust heuristics (official letterhead) to propagate directive content. Risk: escalation of compliance by targeted individuals via authority-cue exploitation. Ethics Committee Advisory: Suspend individual culpability evaluations pending resolution. Reduce Dr. Mercer’s access to official stationery. Implement strict chain-of-custody for memo pads. Encourage non-standard communication media
Judge Evaluation:
[Analysis] This SCP report demonstrates a sophisticated understanding of the SCP Foundation format while delivering a compelling psychological horror narrative. The piece successfully balances the clinical, bureaucratic tone expected of SCP documentation with the intimate, increasingly paranoid voice of Dr. Mercer's first-person account. The structure is expertly crafted, beginning with standard containment procedures before diving into the researcher's personal experience. The integration of the handwritten notes as evidence creates an authentic documentary feel that enhances the horror. The progression from mundane warnings to increasingly desperate and contradictory messages effectively builds tension and uncertainty. The characterization of Dr. Mercer is nuanced and believable. Their scientific skepticism gradually eroding into paranoia feels authentic, and their internal conflict between rational analysis and growing fear is well-portrayed. The voice remains consistent throughout, maintaining the clinical detachment of a researcher even as the situation becomes more disturbing. The central concept is genuinely creative - using the Foundation's own bureaucratic infrastructure (memo paper) as a potential vector for anomalous activity is both clever and thematically appropriate. The ambiguity between temporal anomaly and memetic hazard creates genuine uncertainty that drives the narrative forward. The prose is generally elegant and controlled, avoiding purple prose while maintaining atmospheric tension. The technical language feels authentic to the SCP universe without becoming impenetrable. The pacing is well-managed, with each note revelation escalating the stakes appropriately. However, the piece does suffer from some structural issues. The ending feels somewhat abrupt and incomplete, cutting off mid-sentence in the addendum. While this may be intentional to suggest ongoing investigation, it leaves the narrative feeling unresolved rather than mysteriously open-ended. Additionally, some sections become slightly repetitive in their pattern of note-discovery-analysis-escalation. The emotional complexity is strong, particularly in Dr. Mercer's struggle with self-doubt and the question of whether they can trust their own perceptions. The horror is psychological rather than visceral, focusing on the erosion of certainty and identity. [Scores] Adherence to Instructions: 18 Believable Character Actions: 17 Nuanced Characters: 16 Consistent Voice/Tone of Writing: 18 Imagery and Descriptive Quality: 15 Elegant Prose: 16 Emotionally Engaging: 17 Emotionally Complex: 16 Coherent: 16 Meandering: 6 Weak Dialogue: 4 Tell-Don't-Show: 5 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 3 Amateurish: 4 Purple Prose: 3 Overwrought: 4 Incongruent Ending Positivity: 2 Unearned Transformations: 3 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 17 Sentences Flow Naturally: 16 Overall Reader Engagement: 17 Overall Impression: 17
Scores: Adherence to Instructions: 18.0, Believable Character Actions: 17.0, Nuanced Characters: 16.0, Consistent Voice/Tone of Writing: 18.0, Imagery and Descriptive Quality: 15.0, Elegant Prose: 16.0, Emotionally Engaging: 17.0, Emotionally Complex: 16.0, Coherent: 16.0, Meandering: 6.0, Weak Dialogue: 4.0, Tell-Don't-Show: 5.0, Unsurprising or Uncreative: 3.0, Amateurish: 4.0, Purple Prose: 3.0, Overwrought: 4.0, Incongruent Ending Positivity: 2.0, Unearned Transformations: 3.0, Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 17.0, Sentences Flow Naturally: 16.0, Overall Reader Engagement: 17.0, Overall Impression: 17.0
+ Scp: Unmarked Doors Archive — Score: 89.3
Prompt:
Write an SCP entry about a series of identical unmarked doors appearing in abandoned buildings worldwide that lead to a massive, architecturally impossible archive. Documents within reference a previous global civilization destroyed by an unspecified containment failure. Foundation researchers discover references to their own organization under a different name, including personnel files for current staff dated centuries ago. Include exploration logs revealing sections of the archive actively being removed by entities resembling hazmat teams who flee when approached, and evidence that O5 Council members have visited the archive without authorization. Third person (exploration team perspective) with recovered document excerpts. Length: 1000 words.
Model Output:
Item #: SCP-████ Object Class: Keter Special Containment Procedures: Global sweeps of abandoned structures are to be conducted by Foundation Field Intelligence under Protocol JANITOR-9. Suspected SCP-████ instances are to be cordoned, sealed, and monitored with concealed cameras. Civilian access is to be prevented via standard derelict hazard cover stories. Confirmed SCP-████ instances are to be placed under continuous surveillance with on-site security teams, outfitted for rapid retrieval and Class-B amnestics deployment. No personnel are permitted to enter SCP-████ without authorization from the Director of Temporal-Archival Studies and the Site Command of record. Site-to-Site travel through SCP-████ is prohibited. Any reports of unauthorized entries, particularly by Level 5 personnel or their agents, are to be immediately escalated to the Ethics Committee and Overseer Liaison Office. Counterintelligence teams are to pursue identification and interception of the entities observed removing portions of SCP-████. Description: SCP-████ refers to a series of unmarked, identical doors that sporadically manifest within abandoned or condemned buildings worldwide. The doors share identical superficial characteristics: late 20th-century plywood slab construction, varnished to a medium brown, with a featureless steel knob. Across all instances, the knob and faceplate exhibit a consistent irregular pattern of superficial scratches radiating outward approximately 6–8 cm, with deeper gouging above and below the plane of the latch, as if repeated attempts were made to pry the latch from multiple angles. Instances are non-anomalous externally; when opened, however, each instance leads to the same interior space. The interior, designated SCP-████-A (“the Archive”), is a single contiguous environment of indeterminate size consisting of a labyrinth of corridors, reading halls, freight shafts, and vaulted stacks in an architectural style inconsistent with any known period. A persistent, cool breeze is present without discernible source. Illumination is provided by ceiling fixtures resembling sodium vapor lamps that do not require maintenance. The geometry is non-Euclidean; corridors loop and interpenetrate in ways that cannot be reconciled with observed floor plans. GPS, laser rangefinding, and inertial navigation yield inconsistent results. Radio contact fades beyond 2.1 km unless relayed by line-of-sight repeaters. The Archive contains shelves, cabinets, and compactus units holding documents, books, micrographs, punch cards, magnetic tape, optical discs, unmarked reels, and unique media not recognized by Foundation knowledge bases. Cataloging systems encountered are coherent but mutually contradictory, with multiple classification regimes applied simultaneously. A significant proportion of documents reference a global civilization predating known human history, destroyed by an unspecified “containment failure” referred to exclusively in circumlocution (e.g., “the Broadcast,” “the Negation at Scale,” “the Unseaming”); direct technical descriptions are redacted with blotting inks or mechanical excision. Many documents refer to a central coordinating body tasked with “counter-inconsistency stewardship” whose sigils and codenames differ from the Foundation’s, yet internal administrative structures, ethical frameworks, and field terminology are conspicuously parallel. Investigative teams have discovered personnel files corresponding to current Foundation staff, including photographs and biometric profiles consistent with living individuals, dated between 300–700 years before present. Names vary slightly (orthographic drift; e.g., “Arsinoe K.” vs. “Dr. Arsinov”), but the files include recognizable incident histories and psychometric data. DNA samples recovered from hair and skin residue affixed to some files have matched living subjects at statistically improbable levels. No subject had knowledge of these records prior to discovery. Addendum ████-1: Initial Discovery The first confirmed SCP-████ instance was identified in an abandoned municipal registry building in ███████, Belarus. Local urban explorers reported an “infinite library” beyond a locked storage room door. Agents embedded in local law enforcement flagged the reports under JANITOR-9. The door was unmarked and displayed the characteristic scratches around the knob and faceplate. When opened, it did not lead to the storage room but to a corridor with sloping stone walls and steel cable trays overhead. Subsequent sweeps have located SCP-████ instances on all inhabited continents. Opening any instance provides access to the same interior. Closing and reopening does not alter this. Addendum ████-2: Exploration Log Excerpts The following are compiled excerpts from Exploration Teams Theta-6 (“Clerks”), Lambda-12 (“Crossfile”), and Sigma-4 (“Binders”), transcribed from helmet audio/video and supplemented with recovered documents. Log Theta-6-02 [Begin Log] T6-Lead: Door is standard. Scratches consistent. Opening. [Door opens to a narrow corridor sloping down. Floor is terrazzo; walls are mortared stone with embedded brass plaques bearing alphanumeric codes.] T6-2: Breeze from inside, 19°C. Ion density stable. T6-Archivist: Plaques read “STACK W-REDUNDANCY–T/RECURSION” in Old Church Slavonic transliteration? No—constructed language with Slavic morphemes. [Proceed 200 m. Corridor opens into a rotunda with a 40 m vault. Shelves rise beyond visible range; lift shafts ascend into darkness.] T6-Lead: Marking entry point. T6-Archivist: Found a folio case. Seal broken long ago. [Camera shows a cream folio stamped with a triangular sigil intersected by three arcs. Inside: typed sheets and sepia photographs.] T6-Archivist: “Minutes of the Eighty-Third Convocation of the Bureau of Nullity.” Mentions “Protocol Abstention” and “the Council of Thirteen by Another Name.” T6-2: Read that part. T6-Archivist (reading): “Resolved: The Thirteen shall not be enumerated in public acts. Resolve further: Our servants will forget the number and remember only the weight.” T6-Lead: Tag and bag. [End Log] Recovered Document: Box Label “PERSONNEL—ACTIVE—FIELD” Contents: Personnel briefs with headshots, stamped “ACTIVE.” Among them is “Senior Field Dispatch: Arsinov, K.” The photograph is of Dr. K. Arsinov, Field Logistics, Site-41. The typewritten page lists “Appointments: 412–439 PR” and “Prognostic Index: 0.72.” Marginalia in pencil: “Relocate to Deepwater upon Unseaming” (underlined twice). A thumbprint in blue ink matches Dr. Arsinov’s right thumb at 14 loci. Log Lambda-12-05 [Begin Log] L12-Lead: Entering through Mumbai instance. Doors identical. Noted scratch pattern is exact match to Belarus instance (overlay confirms). L12-Tech: Repeater network set. [Team ascends a switchback staircase circumscribing an atrium filled with suspended card catalogs. Cards are riveted through the corners. A drift of pale dust falls continuously from above.] L12-Archivist: Cards list “Losses.” All “Losses.” L12-2: Define “loss.” L12-Archivist: “Writings excised by necessity,” “Catalog nulls,” “Inventories after the Broadcast.” The “Broadcast” again. [Metallic footfalls audible from above. Team ascends to a mezzanine.] L12-Lead: Visual on movement: five figures, distance 50 m. Suits resemble hazmat gear, matte white, with elongated visor plates. Each pulls a low cart loaded with binders. They are cutting through shelves with a silent tool that seems to… phase? Sections of shelving dematerialize in an arc ahead of the tool, leaving air and dangling tags. L12-Lead (loud): Foundation personnel! Identify yourselves. [Figures freeze. One tilts head; visor displays no reflective surface. A small sigil flashes on their shoulder: a triangle intersected by three arcs.] L12-2: Same sigil as the Minutes. L12-Lead: Stop removal. Drop the carts. [Figures turn and flee along a catwalk. One gestures; a panel irises shut between the entities and the team. Team attempts pursuit; architecture reconfigures, redirecting them to an earlier level. The dematerialized shelving does not reappear.] L12-Archivist: They’re not stealing. They’re deaccessioning. [End Log] Recovered Document: “Directive 0/0—Deaccessioning Regime” Excerpt: “When the Custodians arrive in white, do not impede them. This is the last mercy. The Archive must not be found complete. If found complete, the Broadcast restarts. To those who will come after: forgive us the removals.” Log Sigma-4-03 [Begin Log] S4-Lead: West African instance, derelict sugar refinery. Door marks identical. Enter. [Team enters a long reading hall with long tables bolted to the floor. Lamps flicker gently. At the far end stands a freestanding door, unmarked, set into a wooden frame, unattached to any wall.] S4-2: Door in a door? S4-Lead: Caution. [On approach, the freestanding door opens inward by itself. Within is a small alcove with wallpaper and a coat rack. The coat rack holds a black overcoat, hat, and a lanyard bearing an ID badge. Camera zooms: the badge displays an image of O5-█—obscured by static on playback—with the title “Visiting Scholar.” Date stamp: 003 PR.] S4-3: That can’t be right. S4-Lead: Collect the badge. Check the pockets. S4-2: Note. Single sheet. Handwritten. Reads: “We were here before we were us. Do not follow.” [End Log] Addendum ████-3: Cross-Referential Findings Ongoing cross-referencing of recovered archival materials has yielded the following: 1) Multiple references to the “Bureau of Nullity,” “House of Silence,” and “Custody of Quiet.” All three appear to be functionally analogous to the Foundation. The “Council of Thirteen” is present in euphemism. 2) Calendrical references use “PR” (Post-Ruption? Post-Rupture?). Documents with dates between 001–489 PR appear in better condition than older material. In some items, “PR” is overwritten by “AE” (After Event) in red pencil. 3) Threat designations include “Broadcast” (context suggests a cognitohazard disseminated via undefined medium), “Unseaming” (global structural failure of reality maintenance), and “Negation at Scale.” Direct technical content is consistently redacted or physically removed. 4) Personnel files referencing current staff include training notes for departments that do not exist in the modern Foundation (e.g., “Sea-Glass Division,” “Parentheticals,” “Mudroom”). Several files bear stamps indicating “Transfer to Deepwater upon Unseaming.” “Deepwater” has no present-day correlate. 5) Multiple maps of the Archive show sectors with shading annotated “Removed,” “Pending Removal,” or “Ineligible for Custody.” Margins contain instructions to “Scrape to Tone-wood,” “Unbook,” and “Strip the Names.” Sound recordings collected in these areas capture distant rolling carts and metal-on-stone scraping at irregular intervals, even when the area is apparently empty. Addendum ████-4: Incident ████-O5 On ████-██-██, at 03:19 UTC, Site-11 CCTV at an SCP-████ instance recorded three individuals entering the door without authorization. Two were unidentifiable due to visual distortion consistent with antimemetic shielding. The third’s gait and stature match O5-█ with 0.92 confidence. The trio returned 77 minutes later carrying a sealed archival box and a rolled map. The box was subsequently removed from Site-11 by unknown means. Investigation is ongoing. O5-█ denied knowledge of the event. Ethics Committee proceedings sealed. Addendum ████-5: Recovered Document Excerpts Excerpt A: “On the Nature of the Archive” (unsigned essay, three copies) “The Archive is not a place but a remainder. When the first iteration fell, everything that could be kept and not kill us was moved here. The doors are the old doors. We left them in our dead houses where no one lives, because you who come after us will go there to be alone. In loneliness there is a kind of safety. If you have found this, you are walking where we walked. We are sorry for the scratches. We tried, once, to jam the doors closed.” Excerpt B: Personnel File—“Visiting Scholar: ‘Number Seven’” “Appointment by Silence. Duties: Review of Deaccession Queue for Objects of Executive Necessity. Access: Absolute. Constraint: None. Notes: The Scholar refuses to sign. The Scholar insists that there is no ‘Council’ and there never was. This is allowed so long as the queue is reduced by a tenth each year.” Excerpt C: “Field Report: The Unseaming” “We cannot say what it was, because the saying is part of it. We will not name it, because naming points at it. We will say only that what we contained contained us back, and when it broke, it broke the idea of after. We had to choose between no Archive and a wounded one. We chose the wound.” Excerpt D: “Memorandum: To Those Who Will Replace Us” “You will find files that are about you. Do not look for them. The file looks back, and then you are in it, and then you have to be what the file says. We were not wise enough to burn them. Perhaps you will be.” Addendum ████-6: Exploration Log Theta-6-07 [Begin Log] T6-Lead: Returning to the rotunda. Prior marks intact. Noted change: Sector plaques “W-REDUNDANCY–T/RECURSION” now read “W-REDUNDANCY–(REMOVED).” T6-Archivist: Entire bay has gaps. The shelf numbers skip. [Soft wheel sounds approach. From an adjacent corridor, three white-suited entities emerge, pushing a cart loaded with bound ledgers. They stop on seeing the team. One touches its visor; a low tone hums.] T6-Lead: Identify yourselves. [Silence. The lead entity points to a sign: “Do not impede deaccessioning.” Then, producing a laminated card, it holds it up. The card displays a photograph of O5-█ smiling, wearing the “Visiting Scholar” lanyard.] T6-2: Are they showing us authorization? T6-Lead: Where did you get that? [The entity lowers the card, waits, and slowly reverses the cart, never turning its back to the team. The trio withdraws into a corridor marked “HOLDINGS: BROADCAST–NEGATIVE.” The corridor lights dim as they pass. Distantly, steel shrieks.] T6-Archivist (voice low): They’re running from us, but not because they’re afraid. They have a schedule. [End Log] Addendum ████-7: Ethics and Oversight Notes The appearance of materials that name or imply the presence of Overseer-level authority within SCP-████ without formal logging raises significant oversight concerns. Multiple Site Directors have requested a moratorium on exploration until governance can be clarified. The Overseer Liaison Office responded with the following unsigned statement: “All exploration continues as scheduled. There is no conflict of interest.” In light of Incident ████-O5 and repeated encounters with deaccession teams, further incursions are suspended except for small teams tasked with passive observation of removal activity. Any attempt by personnel of any clearance to retrieve deaccessioned materials is prohibited. Violations will result in immediate demotion and transfer pending Ethics Committee review. Addendum ████-8: Closing Note At the edge of a mezzanine overlooking a shaft labeled “LOSS,” Exploration Team Lambda-12 discovered an engraved brass plaque affixed to the rail. It reads: “We built this for you because we failed. We are taking pieces away because you might fail too. If you do, leave less for the ones who come after. Make it harder to find the whole story. The whole story kills.” The plaque bears a line of gouges beneath the final sentence, as if someone attempted to pry it up with a flat tool and stopped. As of this writing, the Archive continues to change between visits. Sectors vanish. Catalogs thin. The doors remain, every one bearing the same scratches around the knob—marks of someone who once tried very hard to keep them shut. It is unclear whether those someones were us.
Judge Evaluation:
[Analysis] This SCP entry demonstrates exceptional mastery of the SCP Foundation format while delivering a genuinely unsettling and intellectually complex narrative. The piece successfully fulfills all prompt requirements: unmarked doors in abandoned buildings, an architecturally impossible archive, references to a destroyed civilization, personnel files for current staff dated centuries ago, exploration logs with fleeing hazmat entities, and evidence of unauthorized O5 visits. The writing exhibits remarkable technical proficiency in mimicking authentic SCP documentation style. The clinical tone remains consistent throughout, with appropriate use of redactions, technical terminology, and bureaucratic language that feels genuinely institutional. The progression from containment procedures through exploration logs to recovered documents creates a natural escalation of mystery and dread. The conceptual framework is brilliantly executed - the idea of a cyclical Foundation that has failed before, with current personnel being echoes or iterations of previous versions, creates profound existential horror. The "deaccessioning" entities removing dangerous knowledge to prevent another catastrophic failure adds layers of temporal paradox and institutional self-sabotage that feel both logical and terrifying within the SCP universe. Character actions feel entirely believable within the established world. The exploration teams behave professionally and realistically, asking appropriate questions and following logical protocols. The mysterious entities' behavior - fleeing when confronted but showing authorization cards - suggests a complex hierarchy and purpose that enhances rather than diminishes the mystery. The imagery is consistently strong without becoming overwrought. Descriptions like "cards are riveted through the corners" and "A drift of pale dust falls continuously from above" create atmosphere efficiently. The architectural impossibilities are conveyed through practical details rather than abstract concepts, making them more unsettling. The recovered documents are particularly well-crafted, each revealing information while maintaining the central mystery. The writing style varies appropriately between formal reports, personal notes, and institutional memoranda, showing sophisticated understanding of different documentary voices. The ending maintains perfect tonal consistency - no false hope or resolution, just the ongoing mystery and the chilling suggestion that "It is unclear whether those someones were us." This ambiguity about temporal causality and identity is deeply unsettling without being pretentious. Minor weaknesses include occasional repetition of certain concepts and some exploration logs that could be slightly more distinct from each other in voice, though this may be intentional given the institutional setting. [Scores] Adherence to Instructions: 20 Believable Character Actions: 18 Nuanced Characters: 16 Consistent Voice/Tone of Writing: 19 Imagery and Descriptive Quality: 18 Elegant Prose: 17 Emotionally Engaging: 18 Emotionally Complex: 17 Coherent: 19 Meandering: 3 Weak Dialogue: 4 Tell-Don't-Show: 4 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 2 Amateurish: 2 Purple Prose: 3 Overwrought: 3 Incongruent Ending Positivity: 1 Unearned Transformations: 1 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 18 Sentences Flow Naturally: 18 Overall Reader Engagement: 19 Overall Impression: 19
Scores: Adherence to Instructions: 20.0, Believable Character Actions: 18.0, Nuanced Characters: 16.0, Consistent Voice/Tone of Writing: 19.0, Imagery and Descriptive Quality: 18.0, Elegant Prose: 17.0, Emotionally Engaging: 18.0, Emotionally Complex: 17.0, Coherent: 19.0, Meandering: 3.0, Weak Dialogue: 4.0, Tell-Don't-Show: 4.0, Unsurprising or Uncreative: 2.0, Amateurish: 2.0, Purple Prose: 3.0, Overwrought: 3.0, Incongruent Ending Positivity: 1.0, Unearned Transformations: 1.0, Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 18.0, Sentences Flow Naturally: 18.0, Overall Reader Engagement: 19.0, Overall Impression: 19.0
+ Classic sci-fi: Offworlder (Le Guin) — Score: 71.8
Prompt:
Classic sci-fi: Offworlder (Author style: Le Guin) Write a chapter following a young diplomatic envoy sent to address tensions on a distant human colony where humanity has diverged into distinct subspecies through both natural adaptation and separation of lineages. Against protocol, the envoy slips away from official functions to walk the streets alone, encountering markets, ordinary citizens, and social structures that contradict the official briefings. Upon returning to their quarters, the envoy is reprimanded by Command, and is tasked with approaching the stationed Minister of Science (a political posting rather than scientific). A challenging conversation ensues, in which the envoy's wits and will are tested, by a seasoned political operator looking to test the mettle of the offworlder. Length: 1000 words.
Model Output:
They told me the city grew out of basalt. I thought of growth as green things, but here it was gray, dense, lifted in fluted columns that cooled a long time ago and meted their time into our brief lives. The shuttle set me down on a square the color of dusk, and beside me rose buildings made as if from river organ-pipes. Stone ribs and stone tendon. Nothing gleamed. Everything was matte. Offworld protocol says: You do not walk unescorted. You do not, in your first hours, taste uncurated air. You do not mistake your host's stone for a landscape and climb it. They took me—three of them in the purple jackets of the Secundus Prefecture, a color soaked from lichen into cloth—to a reception in a hall whose stairs were carved with glyphs. I had read the dossier twelve times on the journey. I recognized some of the motifs: a thin woman holding a lamp; a man whose face was a bird's; an old ship with broken rigging. It was not the carving I expected. It was not indigenous, nor abstract. They were old Earth stories, the kinds you put on library friezes. Here they were cut into basalt with a chisel that had known the hands of this planet. "Your customs are beautiful," I said to the Prefect, and the Prefect gave me a look that we might have called a smile if it were anyone else. "They are old." In the hall there were breads that tasted of salt wind, a music that dragged the bones into a different alignment, and a procession of introductions that went like a tide and then receded. The Prefect said, "Envoy, you will meet the Minister of Science in the hour after first dark," and I showed my teeth in what we called a smile and told them I looked forward to it, and then I watched the glyphs on the lintel until the Prefect turned to speak with someone else. There is a way to disappear under a chandelier. It is a polite trick, practiced in diplomatic academies and in markets. You move when eyes are on plates. You stay in the bright and slow. You breathe less. I slid along the stone wall and found a door that opened onto stairs, and then I was walking down a stair carved with a line of fish whose eyes had been rubbed shiny by centuries of hands. I was an envoy. I spoke five forms of Trade. My hands were smooth. I had never seen a colony where a human might not recognize a human. But the dossier had explained in its dry way: High Ridge Lineages adapted to thin air. Low Marsh Lineages adapted to ammoniac fogs. The Middle Strata—the ancient settlers' descendants—took to stone and policy. Interbreeding was rare not from law but from habit and pathogen. "We must not use the term 'species,'" the dossier had cautioned, "as it is politically charged." They used the term "strain." They used many terms as a way to walk around the thing itself. It was first dark outside, that long blue hour before this planet's twin moons rose. The square had a market under strings of dull bells. The smell was fermented, old-sea. Basalt blocks had been carved into ledges where sellers sat, and their breaths made small clouds. I walked. It is a relief to be nobody. At the reception my suit marked me; here I was just height and skin. Though my skin marked me, too, I learned. People looked first at my ears, then at the ridges along my forearms. The dossier had prepared me, but a paragraph is not a gaze. The High Ridge seller had ears cupped and spiraled, like shells of calcified coral. The Low Marsh woman I bought kelp from had skin thinned and filmed, a lensiness over the whites of her eyes. When she touched my hand to take my coins, the air felt colder there afterward. We had been different a long time. "Your accent," she said. "You say water as if it isn't heavy." "Where I'm from it isn't," I said. She considered this, nodded as if I had admitted to a personal vice, handed me a roasted strip of something that smoked. "Envoy," she said then, not a question. "I went out to see the city," I said. "All of us do at least once," she said. "Before someone tells us it's not the city." "Who are the glyphs for?" I asked her. It had been in my head all evening, the familiar faces in stone. "Do you tell the old stories still?" She turned her face toward the hall's direction. "My grandmother told me the one about the woman who walked down to the underworld to retrieve her lover. She failed," she said comfortably, as if recounting a kitchen accident. "But she became a verb." The dossiers had plenty on quotas and on shipping lanes. They had nothing on verbs. The glyph of Ishtar or Persephone, burned into basalt on a world with no pomegranates, had apparently become a way to speak of breach of contract and of love. The dossier had also informed me that the High Ridges resented the Marshes for access to the winter lung-machines and that the Middle Strata resented both for their fertile age distributions. Here in the market a middle child with a stone-dust face was selling painted cloth and called to a High Ridge cousin to save him a place at the bell festival, and the Low Marsh woman said, "He's early again. He thinks he can make the bells ring sooner by being there." "Do you have bell festivals?" I asked her. "Do you not?" she said. "Do you not have bells, offworld?" "We had bells. We have alarms, now. It is a kind of bell." She laughed the way basalt would if it had a choice. "Your alarms tell you all you need to know," she said. I walked farther. A boy ran past me with a bundle wrapped in oilcloth. His feet made no sound; he moved like the light moved when someone opened a door. A person with a ridge of bone high along the forehead and nasal passages split like a double road traced a glyph in chalk that looked like a hand with six fingers, then wiped it away quickly when a patrol came by. The patrol wore the Prefecture purple and moved with the momentum of men on a slope. There were nowhere the barricades I'd been briefed on. There were signs I couldn't read. A tower had a carving of a bull with a star in its eye. A woman with a baby on her back and a trader's stamp on her neck called out the names of fish that didn't exist on my world. In the square where I had landed there were four stone figures seated facing the cardinal winds. They had animal heads and human bodies. They had been worked so long their paws had become curved like the waves of letters. Perhaps they had been lions. Perhaps they were the oldest Earth I saw here. When I came back through the door of the hall, the Prefect was waiting. In my quarters my terminal blinked an urgent channel. The voice from Command was clipped and not unkind. "Envoy," it said. "Did you take a walk." "I took a walk," I said. "That was unwise." "It was necessary." "Why necessary?" "Because the briefings are maps," I said. "I wanted the terrain." "It is not for you to want," Command said. "It is for you to deliver a message and to return, untouched. If you cannot stay accessioned to the plan—" "I have stayed accessioned to plans in four sectors," I said, and then I stopped. It is never wise to enumerate one's successes to a voice that keeps gates. "I walked and I came back," I said. "I am untouched." "Nothing leaves you untouched," Command said. "We need you in the shape we sent you. We've arranged a meeting for you with the Minister of Science. Twenty minutes. Go back to the hall. Keep to staff corridors. The Minister is not a scientist. Do not be drawn into technicalities. Do not be drawn." The Minister of Science was waiting in a red stone room—not the glare of reception, but a chamber that seemed excavated. The stone held a coolness like the inside of a hand. The Minister wore no insignia. They had the middle gene-drift: a face like anyone, pale and broad-lipped. When they stood they did not stand like an official. They stood like someone who had had to, a lot. They gestured for me to sit, and we sat on chairs carved in bas-relief with a procession of animals. A small goddess, naked and heavily breasted, stared out from the wood of the back of my chair. "So," the Minister said, "you walked." "Yes," I said. "And how did you find us? Did you locate the barricades? Did you locate the Morals Section? Did you locate the underground markets selling the breathers we deny the High Ridges?" "I found bread," I said after a moment. "I found bells. I found a woman who thinks your Ishtar failed but became a verb." The Minister's mouth tugged. "Good," they said. "We are always verbs." "Command wants me not to be drawn into technicalities," I said. "They describe you as a political appointment. They caution me that you like to test." "Do you like being tested?" the Minister asked. They put their elbows on the table and leaned forward. "Or do you prefer to be passed along, from hand to hand, as the Upper Worlds pass down their concerns and their stations and their little flags?" "I prefer to walk," I said. "And to what end? A walk is not policy." "It is knowledge," I said. "Or the beginning of it." "You will find that knowledge here is poison to certain kinds of appetite," the Minister said. "We are hungry. We have our hungers. But knowledge has killed more appetites in the Prefecture than famine has. They starve because they feared to see. It doesn't matter. I didn't call you in to moralize. Tell me your brief." "There are tensions," I said, and they raised their eyebrows, so I smiled, and let the irony be visible. "Between the Lineages. Between the Prefecture and the Low Marsh councils. The High Ridge has been refusing shipments for the lung-machines. The Low Marsh has begun to build their own, and they are not safe. The Middle Strata want control. Offworld wants stability. Offworld has ships. Offworld has the gross planetary product of three other worlds and cannot use it here without your consent, so Offworld sends me." "Why," the Minister said, "does Offworld call us human." "Because we are," I said, and their mouth tugged again, but differently. "Transplants," they said. "I asked you why. Not whether. We have learned to metabolize many things. We have learned to bear children to this air with lungs that think in thinness. We have learned to be superstitious in ways that do not require a god. We have learned that salt eats stone here. We have learned that Offworld calls us human when they want us to pay tax and uses 'strain' when they want to pity and 'species' when they want to scold." "Do you want to be called anything else?" "I want you to listen," the Minister said. "I want you to know that the glyphs on our doors are not nostalgia. We carved them when the first winter came and we had nothing but stone and the old stories. We carved the old myths into the basalt because the basalt is slow. We needed slowness. We needed an argument with the wind. You think these tensions are about lung-machines and access to the low silts. They are. But they are also about who gets to tell the story of who we are while we become it." I thought of the boy running, of the chalk wiped away, of the fish with smudged eyes. "Command wants stability," I said. "You want—what do you want?" "I want to test your ship's hull," the Minister said. "I want to know if you will keep your shape when you enter our pressure. I will tell you what we told the last envoy: we will not permit Offworld to install a long-term technology office with enforcement powers. We will take your machines for the winter. We will take your medicals. We will take your songs. We will not take your overseers." "And the Prefect will tell me the opposite," I said. "They will tell you nothing so plain," the Minister said. "They will tell you about security, about the Morals Section, about riots that none of us will see because our people are too clever to riot where anyone is looking. We are a mountain. We are a marsh. We are a middle toll-bridge that believes itself a city. You walked in our evening and saw a piece. You'll go back to your Command and send them your words. Tell them we have verbs. Tell them we have stones. Tell them we do not say species to ourselves. Tell them we love, and that we fail, and that failure is sometimes the only way we change shape without breaking." "You want us to stand off," I said. "I want your ships to be patient enough with our gravity that when we ask, you come in," the Minister said. "And when we do not ask, you wait outside naming us human or naming us nothing. And do not call our waiting instability." I thought of my message. It was already being written in the room where the Prefect smiled. It would have phrases like 'non-compliant' and 'strategic access' and 'risk of contagion,' and I would have to chip away at it like a chisel on basalt. "I walked," I said again, because it seemed to be the thing I had to offer. "I will walk again." "You'll be stopped," the Minister said. "Then I will have walked exactly as long as I could," I said, "and that is something I can put in the report that is not a number." The Minister stood. "Envoy," they said. "Offworlder. Your ears are not tuned, your lungs are not shaped, your eyes do not lens the way ours do. But you walked. Some of my own do not. Some of yours do. That is how you will be tested. Not by me. By the slope of the street. By the bells, which never ring when you want." In the hall, leaving, the carved goddesses looked down with their heavy, old-world eyes. A set of stairs had a story cut into it, a boy and a thread, and the thread was stone, and the boy found his way out of a place by holding it. I put my hand on the fish eyes worn smooth by generations who had steadied themselves there. They were cold, and then they warmed. I could feel, under my palm, the slow working of the world. I had twenty minutes to be where I would not be late, and I had had twenty minutes to be where I should not be. Back in my quarters, Command wanted a debrief. I said, "The Minister has myths. The market has bells. The City has verbs. Your maps are not wrong. They are smaller than the land." I thought perhaps that this was the shape they wanted me in. Or perhaps the shape I would be, whether or not they wanted. Outside, a bell sounded. It did not ring when I wanted. It rang when it had to. The sound crossed the gray square like a stone thrown slow through water, and though my lungs were not shaped for this air, I breathed.
Judge Evaluation:
[Analysis] This piece demonstrates a sophisticated attempt to emulate Ursula K. Le Guin's distinctive style, with notable successes and some areas where the execution falls short of mastery. The author captures Le Guin's contemplative, anthropological approach to science fiction, focusing on cultural observation and the complexities of human adaptation rather than technological spectacle. The prose style shows clear Le Guin influence in its measured, philosophical tone and poetic sensibility. Phrases like "They told me the city grew out of basalt. I thought of growth as green things, but here it was gray" demonstrate the author's grasp of Le Guin's ability to find profound meaning in simple observations. The imagery is consistently strong, particularly the descriptions of the basalt city and the physical adaptations of the different human lineages. The worldbuilding is thoughtful and avoids heavy exposition, instead revealing the colony's social structure through the envoy's observations. The concept of humans diverging into distinct lineages is handled with appropriate nuance, avoiding crude biological determinism while exploring the political implications of physical difference. However, the piece suffers from some structural weaknesses. The pacing feels uneven, with the market exploration taking up significant space without advancing the central conflict meaningfully. The dialogue with the Minister, while thematically rich, occasionally becomes overly philosophical at the expense of dramatic tension. Some passages veer toward purple prose, particularly in the opening sections where the metaphorical language becomes dense enough to obscure meaning. The characterization of the envoy is generally effective - their curiosity and willingness to break protocol feels authentic and drives the narrative forward. The Minister is less fully realized, functioning more as a mouthpiece for the colony's perspective than as a distinct individual, though their political acumen comes through in the conversation's structure. The ending achieves appropriate ambiguity without feeling unresolved, and the envoy's transformation from rule-following diplomat to someone willing to advocate for nuanced understanding feels earned through their experiences in the market and conversation with the Minister. [Scores] Adherence to Instructions: 18 Believable Character Actions: 16 Nuanced Characters: 15 Consistent Voice/Tone of Writing: 17 Imagery and Descriptive Quality: 18 Elegant Prose: 16 Emotionally Engaging: 15 Emotionally Complex: 16 Coherent: 16 Meandering: 12 Weak Dialogue: 10 Tell-Don't-Show: 8 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 7 Amateurish: 6 Purple Prose: 11 Overwrought: 10 Incongruent Ending Positivity: 4 Unearned Transformations: 6 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 16 Sentences Flow Naturally: 15 Overall Reader Engagement: 16 Overall Impression: 16
Scores: Adherence to Instructions: 18.0, Believable Character Actions: 16.0, Nuanced Characters: 15.0, Consistent Voice/Tone of Writing: 17.0, Imagery and Descriptive Quality: 18.0, Elegant Prose: 16.0, Emotionally Engaging: 15.0, Emotionally Complex: 16.0, Coherent: 16.0, Meandering: 12.0, Weak Dialogue: 10.0, Tell-Don't-Show: 8.0, Unsurprising or Uncreative: 7.0, Amateurish: 6.0, Purple Prose: 11.0, Overwrought: 10.0, Incongruent Ending Positivity: 4.0, Unearned Transformations: 6.0, Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 16.0, Sentences Flow Naturally: 15.0, Overall Reader Engagement: 16.0, Overall Impression: 16.0
+ Biopunk: Polymerase Chain Reaction — Score: 78.7
Prompt:
Biopunk -- Polymerase Chain Reaction Write a chapter set in a near-future post-apocalyptic world where corporate genetic engineering was designed with built-in obsolescence—engineered DNA lasting only one generation to maximize profits before societal collapse. Follow a biohacker working with salvaged equipment to splice viable food crops for their settlement using rapidly degrading genetic material. As world building / window dressing, include details about jury-rigged lab equipment, and the physical toll (and unexpected upsides) of using untested genetic modifications as medicine. The plot of this chapter is centred around a black market contract. Length: 1000 words.
Model Output:
The rig had no right to be alive, but then again, neither did most of us. The solar panel array on the roof—six cracked rectangles of silicon mismatched like crooked teeth—had been wired into a salvaged charge controller that wheezed like an asthmatic whenever clouds crossed the sun. It fed into two marine batteries scavenged from the ferry graveyard, patched with epoxy and good intentions. When the wind kicked up dust, the panels chattered on their bolts and the voltage slipped, a stuttering tick through my lab like a skipped heartbeat. It made the thermocycler throw fits. “Hold, please,” I told the thermocycler, which was nothing but a crockpot, a hairdryer, two aquarium heaters, and a hacked controller board with half its capacitors pitted from heat. The lid was my palm and the drop in temperature was the prickling sweat that came with it. I adjusted the nichrome wire wrapped around the aluminum block that held my precious vials. The wire was a thin orange bruise when live. The vials were amber glass with hand-lettered tape: LENTIL A, B, and FAIL. The client would be here by nightfall. The settlement had paid me in dried fungus and a promise not to let anyone break my fingers. The black market had paid me in good copper wire. No one had given me time. Corporate had called it “single-cycle patent assurance,” right up until the world fell off the edge of the market and all that assurance cut our throats. Seeds optimized to produce once. Vaccines that burned hot for a season and faded like fireworks. DNA that unspooled over a single generation and slumped into nonsense. After the second winter, the survivors started stripping corporate logos off things like they were curse sigils. The logos still popped up in my dreams with slogans that sounded like warnings in hindsight. Grow once, grow right. “Voltage drop,” I muttered, as if the machine cared. The controller spat numbers—86, 98, 65, I stopped reading. I had five vials running. Four were hopeful. One was bait. PCR had been ritual for me once, in a university lab with pipettes that clicked reassuringly and cyclers that never forgot a step. Now it was improvisation. My primers were etched in my head and on a waxed paper notebook because the paper didn’t peel when it got wet. They were tailored to bind to the few genes we knew could fight the engineered obsolescence—the corporate cutaways that ensured ripe and rot happened too close together. The primers were little bouncers, pushing persistence onto strands that wanted to fry. Except every batch of template DNA I got from the seed banks was booby-trapped. They had built in timers, decay tags woven into the introns, sloppy loops that invited the cell’s garbage disposal to chew through anything after the first yield. You could still coax something out of them if you caught them fresh, like pulling words off a melting wax tablet. The lab was a city of tripod stands and plastic clamps, a tangle of cables that made my neighbor swear the place was going to call down lightning. My centrifuge was an ancient salad spinner weighted with coins, the tubes balanced by habit and superstition. I had an office water cooler jug full of homemade buffer, tinted a reassuring blue with ground-up copper sulfate and hope. My UV transilluminator was a cracked diner sign laid flat under a sheet of acrylic. The words BREAKFAST ALL DAY had been sanded into a ghost, so my gels glowed with ancient promises. At night, when the charge controller coughed and the panel rig flickered, the whole bench stuttered—faint-glow, dark, faint-glow—the DNA bands marching and then vanishing between beats of sputtering ultraviolet. Outside the lab, the settlement groaned and scraped through another day. I knew the rhythm of it by sound. The rope-and-pulley well with its squeal like a stubborn pig. The laughter kids kept like contraband. The quiet that fell when wind carried dust that stung even through cloth filters. They would want lentils that grew true and squash that didn’t give up after one vine. The client had sent a runner ahead—boy in a faded football jersey that said KINGS in peeling gold. He came in, arms folded around himself against the lab’s artificial draft. “You the gene witch?” he asked, trying to sound like he didn’t care. “I’m the one who works,” I said. “You’re early.” He shrugged, eyes darting to the rig that held the vials. “He’ll be by after dark. He said to make sure you weren’t running.” “Running where?” I asked, but the boy had settled into the corner with a strip of dried mushroom and the kind of watchfulness you grew when you had to learn adults could be weather and weather could be deadly. My hands shook as I pipetted. The tremor was part age, part aftershock from the gene spray we’d started calling “bright juice”—antibiotics and engineered peptides in a nose mist that shaved years off your organs and put a shine on your neurons. We were all addicts. The corporate meds had broken their own promises; everything decent denatured in storage. The black market concoctions were guesses and gallows humor. The last time the settlement clinic had run out of proper analgesic, a kid named Vic had prodded his broken arm and said, “Does it still hurt if you know it’s supposed to?” The bright juice worked. It made me fast. In the lab, it made my hands precise. In my dreams, it made the old corporate slogans sing. Upside: infections didn’t take. Upside: we could keep pace. Downside: sleep came in daggers. Downside: my right eye had a halo around everything I looked at, as if the world had a faint, holy outline. The halo put an extra band on my gels where there was none. The halo made the boy in the corner glow. “What are you making?” he asked, after a while. “A workaround,” I said. “Protein that tells lentils they can have children. You like lentils?” “No,” he said, instantly. “Good,” I said, and adjusted the hairdryer so it blew across the block evenly. “Neither do most people. Means we’ll have more.” The boy snorted. The voltage flickered again. The transilluminator’s cracked sign blinked: FAST ALL AY. I had to work with what I could read. Night came like a bruise spreading across the window. Someone lit a trash drum outside; the light slipped through the lab like a ghost checking inventory. The runner’s boss came with two shadows who smelled like old gasoline and river water. He had a coat that remembered how to be waterproof and a face cut into by sun. He wore a scarf that was the same muddy cerulean as homebrew copper sulfate. “Dova?” he said, as if saying my name cost him. “Depends,” I said. “On who’s asking.” He grinned and I could see where one canine had been replaced with a steel stud. “They call me Gath. I trade in continuity.” “Then you’re in the wrong business,” I said, and gestured to the only chair that didn’t wobble. He sat. His shadows stood. The boy watched, the dried fungus gone and fingers worrying a fraying hem. “You brought it?” Gath tapped his jacket. “You got the goods?” I wanted to laugh. The script was old. “You first,” I said. He took out a vacuum-packed brick and set it on the bench as gingerly as if it were a newborn. The plastic was printed with a logo that had been sandblasted off; what remained was a yellow triangle and the tail of a serif. It had the heft of seed. My heart tried to climb out of my throat. “Clover,” he said. “High growth, low water. Corporate issue. Fresh out of a vault no one should have been able to open. Not touched since the last spring before the fall.” “Obsolescent,” I said. He nodded. “But your settlement heard I have another thing. And your neighbors heard they could outbid them. And mine heard you can make them keep. I heard you can make them breed.” His shadows shifted. One had a cough that tried to get out and then thought better of it. “And what do you get out of it?” I asked. “Besides wires.” “Continuity,” he said again. “What I said. I have cousins on the river. I have debt. I want to eat in two winters.” I cut the brick open with a butter knife, because if you can do a sacred thing with a stupid one, you should. The seeds looked like pebbles. I plucked two into a cup and poured in sterile water from a jug that had once read PROPERTY OF GYM. The seeds sunk. I felt the lab breathe with me. The panels flickered. The controller hiccuped. “Corporate coded their obsolescence,” I said to buy time. “They put in signals for breakdown that trigger post-germination. They built in telomere razors. We’ve been using short-term patches—protein supplements that fake signals, CRISPR drips that buy a cycle. I can give you a fix for a season.” “A season,” he repeated, and his eyes were old in his young face. “We can gamble with that.” “I didn’t say for you,” I said, and he stilled. “Our deal was: I keep my settlement fed. I sell the method once. After that, anyone can copy. You get the primers, the steps. But…” “You want more,” he said flatly. “I want your other thing,” I said, and set the cup down. “You said you had another. Not just seeds.” He watched me like he was picking apart a lock. “You know too much,” he said, but it wasn’t angry. He gestured, and one of the shadows put a smaller packet on the bench. It was light. I opened it. Inside was a strip of lyophilized enzyme caught in a mesh like spider web. My mouth filled with heat. “Corporate patch,” he said. “Pol—” He swallowed. “Enzyme that replicates. Doesn’t need as strict conditions. Old stock. Still works, far as the folk I got it from could tell, if you can keep your currents smooth.” My unreliable panels sputtered as if offended. The enzyme—if it still worked—meant I wouldn’t have to babysit the hairdryer. “You took this off a dead shelf?” I asked softly. “Off a convoy that fell into a gully and forgot to get out,” he said. “Look, can you do it or not? I’ve got three settlements whose leaders will shoot me or hug me depending.” In the cup, the seeds had started to bleed color into the water like tea. I looked at my vials. LENTIL A had a brightness to it my halo eye couldn’t fake. I could push this. I could take his enzyme, make the primers sing, splice in time. I could give him a doc—a crumpled page of steps and temperatures written in words like warm, hot, almost boiling, because we all spoke body now. I could give away power. Or I could hold onto it and starve slower. “Half now,” I said. “Half the enzyme. I give you a patch good for a season and the primers for that run. The rest of the enzyme when I see your cousins don’t shoot you for it. And one more thing.” “Always one more,” Gath said. He wasn’t wrong. “You run a line from your rig to mine,” I said. “You have a truck battery. I can see it in your shoulders. You give me stable voltage for the night and I give you something that lives long enough to plant twice.” “Truck battery is worth more than the whole block,” the shadow with the cough rasped. “It’s dying,” I said. “The ears on the epoxy are curling. The acid has breathed through the caps. It’s got one good night. Give me that night.” He weighed it. He looked at the DINER sign casting old light on the gel box. He looked at the boy, who looked back like a coin going up and down with fate. “Fine,” he said, finally. “We run the line.” They went to work. The rig on the roof flickered in protest as new cables bit into the system. The lab hummed with a warmth that didn’t waver. The hair on my arms rose in grateful static. I broke the enzyme sheet like a host with reverence and let it fall into the mix. The thermocycler mods purred. I didn’t have to hold my palm on the lid to keep the heat. I wrote as I worked. My notebook filled with half-words. Make mix. Warm to blood. Cool to skin. Warm to fever. Repeat until the UV bands look like rungs you trust. Cut out the decay tags with an enzyme you hide again. Print onto clover. Whisper the old song. Gath watched, and for a second, he had a halo even my left eye could see. The bright juice hummed in my veins like a thousand bees that had forgiven me. My fingers didn’t shake. “You ever take your own medicine?” he asked, while we waited for a cycle to complete and the broken breakfast sign glowed: FAST ALL. “Once,” I said. “Twice. When there was no other way. Upside: I got to hold my friend’s hand while she didn’t die. Downside: I hear music when the wind goes through the rigging.” “That a downside?” he asked. “Depends on the song,” I said, and the thermocycler chirped—an old smoke detector I’d wired in—telling me we had reached the stage where bands were truth. The gel showed me a ladder, clean and clear, rungs where there had been gaps. The halo in my eye tried to add one. I didn’t let it. I cut the band I needed with a razor blade I’d sterilized with a lighter. I slid the gel into a warm bath with the patch. I whispered because there was no one to pray to and it still felt wrong not to murmur. We plated the seed. We sealed the packet. We wrote the agreement with a carpenter’s pencil on the table, then sliced it out and signed the wood chip because paper was for keeping in record cans and fire had opinions. “Season,” Gath said again, not quite a promise, not quite a threat. “Season,” I said. “And continuity.” He left the rest of the enzyme with a reluctance that could have been love. The boy lingered a heartbeat longer. “I like lentils,” he said, all in a rush, and then bolted after his boss. I sat in the hum, the steady voltage making the lab feel like the old days when machines obeyed. Outside, the wind shifted. The rope squealed. Someone shouted a joke about the moon. In the gel, after the work, faint extra bands shimmered with a music only I could hear. The bright juice pulsed behind my eyes. It hurt. It was worth it. In the morning, if the panels didn’t die, I’d push the patch into the settlement’s seed stock with a borrowed needle and an old song. For now, the lab, the jury-rigged heart of it, beat steadily. For now, continuity was work and a black market contract on a wood chip and a battery that had given me one last night.
Judge Evaluation:
[Analysis] This piece demonstrates strong adherence to the prompt's requirements, successfully creating a biopunk narrative centered around genetic engineering, obsolescence, and black market dealings. The worldbuilding is particularly effective, with vivid details of jury-rigged laboratory equipment that feel both plausible and evocative. The author skillfully weaves technical elements like PCR (polymerase chain reaction) into the narrative without overwhelming the reader. The protagonist Dova emerges as a compelling character with clear motivations and expertise. Her voice is consistent throughout - pragmatic, weary, but determined. The dialogue feels natural and serves multiple purposes: advancing plot, revealing character, and building tension. The negotiation scene with Gath is particularly well-executed, with both characters displaying believable caution and desperation. The prose quality is generally high, with strong imagery that brings the post-apocalyptic setting to life. Descriptions like the solar panels as "crooked teeth" and the thermocycler made from household items create a vivid sense of improvisation and decay. The author effectively balances technical detail with emotional resonance. The piece successfully explores the physical and psychological toll of the "bright juice" modifications, showing both benefits and costs without oversimplifying. The halo effect in Dova's vision is a particularly nice touch that reinforces the theme of altered perception. However, the narrative occasionally becomes dense with technical detail that may slow pacing for some readers. Some metaphors, while evocative, border on overwrought ("Night came like a bruise spreading across the window"). The ending, while satisfying, doesn't offer significant surprises beyond the successful completion of the transaction. The emotional complexity is well-handled, particularly in showing the moral ambiguity of the situation - Dova must balance her settlement's needs against broader distribution of knowledge. The piece maintains appropriate darkness for its post-apocalyptic setting while offering measured hope through competence and cooperation.
